# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define emulation-based testing. List any two advantages over software simulation.**

**Model Answer:**
Emulation-based testing is a hardware-assisted verification technique in which the RTL design is mapped onto a reconfigurable multi-FPGA emulator that executes the design at MHz speeds, while a software testbench drives it via SCE-MI transactors.

**Two advantages over software simulation:**

1. **Speed:** Operates at 0.5–5 MHz versus 100 Hz–1 kHz in simulation, achieving $10^3$–$10^6$× speedup, enabling OS boot-up and full firmware validation.
2. **Real-world interfacing:** Allows real hardware peripherals and target systems to be connected for true hardware-software co-verification.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of SCE-MI in emulation?**

**Model Answer:**
SCE-MI (Standard Co-Emulation Modeling Interface) is an Accellera standard that defines the communication protocol between a software testbench (running on the host) and the hardware DUT (running on the emulator). It enables transaction-level communication, allowing high-level commands from SystemVerilog/UVM to be translated into pin-level events at the emulator boundary, ensuring portability of testbenches across different vendor emulators.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical hardware emulator. List the major functional blocks and explain their roles. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) in the context of inter-FPGA communication. How does TDM ratio affect the effective emulation speed? Derive the relation between FPGA clock, TDM ratio, and effective frequency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Architecture of a Hardware Emulator — Block Diagram:**

The major functional blocks are:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Host Workstation** | Runs the UVM/SystemVerilog testbench, compiles design, controls the emulator. |
| **SCE-MI Transactor Layer** | Translates high-level transactions into pin-level signals and vice versa. |
| **Emulator Backplane** | A high-speed interconnect fabric (MGT/SerDes based) that connects all FPGAs. |
| **FPGA Array** | Multiple FPGAs that hold the partitioned, mapped, and routed design. |
| **Clock and Reset Network** | Distributes global clock/reset to all FPGAs with skew control. |
| **Probe and Debug Block** | Captures internal signal states for waveform reconstruction. |
| **Memory Mapper** | Maps DUT memory accesses to external SRAM/DRAM accessible to the host. |

**Workflow:** The host compiles the RTL → partitions it → maps onto the FPGA array → the testbench drives transactions through the SCE-MI transactor → the backplane delivers them to the appropriate FPGA → the FPGAs run in lockstep → results and probe data flow back to the host for analysis.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Time Division Multiplexing in Inter-FPGA Communication:**

When the design is partitioned across $P$ FPGAs, the signals crossing the partition boundary (the *cut*) often exceed the number of physical I/O pins available between FPGAs. To overcome this pin limitation, multiple logical signals are **time-multiplexed** onto a single physical wire using a TDM scheme.

**Derivation:**

Let $f_{\text{FPGA}}$ be the FPGA's internal operating frequency and $R$ the TDM ratio. In one FPGA cycle, the system must transmit $R$ logical samples. Therefore, the design's effective clock (the rate at which one full set of cut signals is delivered) is:

$$
f_{\text{eff}} = \frac{f_{\text{FPGA}}}{R}
$$

**Effect of increasing $R$:**

- **Higher $R$** → more signals share fewer wires → less interconnect bandwidth required → **lower $f_{\text{eff}}$** → slower emulation.
- **Lower $R$** → fewer signals share each wire → more physical channels needed → **higher $f_{\text{eff}}$** → faster emulation, but more pins consumed.

**Trade-off Example:**

If $f_{\text{FPGA}} = 100$ MHz and $R = 10$, then $f_{\text{eff}} = 10$ MHz. If we double $R$ to 20, then $f_{\text{eff}}$ drops to 5 MHz. Emulator vendors therefore select $R$ to balance cut size and emulation speed for the target design.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Compare and contrast **Transaction-Based Emulation** and **In-Circuit Emulation** with suitable diagrams. Mention one specific use case for each. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** An SoC of $90 \times 10^6$ gates is to be emulated on a platform with 6 FPGAs, each holding $18 \times 10^6$ gates. The inter-FPGA cut is 4000 signals of 8-bit width. The FPGA runs at 200 MHz. Compute (i) the number of partitions, (ii) the TDM ratio if each channel is 64 bits wide, (iii) the effective clock frequency, and (iv) whether real-time emulation at 100 MHz is achievable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Transaction-Based Emulation | In-Circuit Emulation |
|---|---|---|
| Stimulus source | Software testbench (UVM) | Real target hardware |
| Interface | SCE-MI transactors | Physical connectors (speed adapters) |
| Speed | Slower (kHz range), decoupled | Closer to real-time |
| Setup | Easier, software-driven | Harder, needs target board |
| Use case | Protocol compliance, regression | HW/SW co-verification, OS bring-up |
| Debug | Transaction-level visibility | Real I/O waveforms |

**Use cases:**

- **Transaction-Based:** Validating a USB 3.0 controller's link layer by generating UVM transactions at the host.
- **In-Circuit:** Booting Android on an emulated mobile SoC with a real LCD, real modem, and real touch sensor.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:**

$$
N_{\text{gates}} = 90 \times 10^6, \quad P_{\text{FPGA}} = 6, \quad C_{\text{FPGA}} = 18 \times 10^6
$$
$$
S_{\text{cut}} = 4000, \quad w = 8 \text{ bits}, \quad f_{\text{FPGA}} = 200 \text{ MHz}, \quad f_{\text{target}} = 100 \text{ MHz}, \quad N_{\text{channel}} = 64
$$

**(i) Number of Partitions:**

$$
P = \left\lceil \frac{90 \times 10^6}{18 \times 10^6} \right\rceil = \lceil 5 \rceil = 5
$$

**Result:** $P = 5$ partitions (fits within 6 available FPGAs).

**(ii) TDM Ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{S_{\text{cut}} \times w}{N_{\text{channel}}} = \frac{4000 \times 8}{64} = \frac{32{,}000}{64} = 500
$$

**Result:** $R = 500$.

**(iii) Effective Clock Frequency:**

$$
f_{\text{eff}} = \frac{f_{\text{FPGA}}}{R} = \frac{200 \text{ MHz}}{500} = 0.4 \text{ MHz} = 400 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Result:** $f_{\text{eff}} = 400 \text{ kHz}$.

**(iv) Real-Time Achievability:**

$$
f_{\text{eff}} = 400 \text{ kHz} \quad \ll \quad f_{\text{target}} = 100 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Result:** Real-time emulation is **NOT achievable**. The TDM configuration produces an effective clock that is $\approx 250\times$ slower than required. To achieve 100 MHz:

$$
R_{\max} = \frac{f_{\text{FPGA}}}{f_{\text{target}}} = \frac{200 \text{ MHz}}{100 \text{ MHz}} = 2
$$

The designer would need to reduce the cut size dramatically, use faster SerDes links, or accept slow-time emulation with transactor-based bridging.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing emulation with FPGA prototyping:** Emulation has full debug visibility, automated partitioning, and SCE-MI compatibility. FPGA prototyping is a one-shot physical board. Marks deducted if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** $f_{\text{eff}}$ must carry Hz/kHz/MHz explicitly. Marks lost for bare numerical values.
> 3. **Wrong TDM formula direction:** Some students write $R = N_{\text{channel}} / (S_{\text{cut}} \times w)$ — this inverts the meaning. Remember: $R$ = *logical bits per physical bit*, always $\ge 1$.
> 4. **Skipping the real-time check:** KTU expects the final feasibility verdict, not just the number. A 7-mark question explicitly asks for "whether achievable" — answering only $f_{\text{eff}}$ loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing SCE-MI with standard SystemVerilog DPI:** SCE-MI is specifically the co-emulation interface; DPI is a general Verilog-C linkage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Emulation** = hardware (multi-FPGA) execution of a real design model driven by a real testbench in (near) real time.
- **Speedup** over simulation: $10^3$ to $10^6$×.
- **SCE-MI** is the standard bridge between software testbench and emulator.
- **Partitioning** is mandatory; cut size $S_{\text{cut}}$ directly drives TDM ratio $R$ and effective clock.
- **TDM relation:** $f_{\text{eff}} = f_{\text{FPGA}} / R$. Higher $R$ = slower emulation.
- **Two operating modes:** Transaction-based (testbench-driven, kHz) and In-Circuit (real peripherals, near real-time).
- **Use cases:** SW boot-up, OS bring-up, regression acceleration, protocol compliance, HW/SW co-verification.
- **Real-time feasibility:** Requires $R \le f_{\text{FPGA}} / f_{\text{target}}$; otherwise transactors stretch/stunt timing.
- **Visibility:** Better than FPGA prototyping, worse than simulation — probe-based sampling is used.
- **Vendors (KTU context):** Cadence Palladium, Synopsys ZeBu, Siemens Veloce.
- **Limitation:** Setup/compile time is hours-to-days; not suitable for last-minute corner-case exploration.
- **Inter-FPGA bandwidth** requirement: $B_{\text{req}} = S_{\text{cut}} \times w \times f_{\text{eff}}$ — must fit the backplane's SerDes capacity.
- **Memory:** DUT memory is mapped to external SRAM/DRAM via memory mapper block.
- **Debug:** Probe logic + waveform database; not full VCD like simulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon Prototyping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Silicon Prototyping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Silicon Prototyping** is the engineering methodology of fabricating a preliminary, fully-functional version of an Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC) or System-on-Chip (SoC) on real silicon (or an equivalent hardware platform) to validate the design's functional correctness, performance, and manufacturability **before** committing to high-volume, full-mask production. It serves as the critical bridge between Register-Transfer Level (RTL) simulation-based verification and mass production.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, silicon prototyping encompasses both **full-custom ASIC prototypes** (limited-run wafer fabrications) and **Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)-based prototypes** that emulate the final ASIC behavior with high fidelity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** Silicon Prototyping is a *pre-silicon-to-post-silicon* validation step. It is *not* a replacement for simulation; rather, it is a complementary hardware-acceleration technique to catch bugs that escaped software-based verification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of silicon prototyping like building a **scale model of a skyscraper** before constructing the actual building.

- **RTL Simulation** = The architect's 2D blueprints and computer wind-tunnel tests (cheap, fast, but you cannot walk through the rooms).
- **Silicon Prototype** = A 1:1 scale mockup or a small-batch, fully functional mini-version of the building (expensive, slower, but you can experience it, find structural issues, test the elevators).
- **Mass Production (Tape-out)** = Constructing the full skyscraper for thousands of tenants. If a flaw is found here, the cost is catastrophic.

Just as civil engineers build models to test elevators and fire escapes, VLSI engineers build silicon prototypes to test real-world hardware behavior, software stacks, and corner-case bugs at near-real speed (**MHz to GHz clock ranges**).

### 1.3 Why is Silicon Prototyping Needed? (The "Why")

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Limitation of Simulation:** A software simulator runs at **~1 kHz effective speed** for a complex SoC. A silicon prototype runs at **50–200 MHz** — that is a **~100,000× speedup**. This allows real boot-up of operating systems, real execution of firmware, and real hardware-software co-debug.

| Issue in Simulation | How Prototyping Solves It |
|---|---|
| Slow execution of long testbenches | Real-time execution at MHz speeds |
| Cannot run real OS / driver software | Boots actual Linux, Android, RTOS |
| Misses real silicon timing bugs | Captures crosstalk, IR drop, clock skew |
| Cannot validate I/O peripherals | Tests real cameras, USB, PCIe, DDR |
| Misses concurrency / race conditions | Real parallel hardware execution |

### 1.4 Classification of Silicon Prototyping (KTU Taxonomy)

According to the PEEVT635 syllabus structure, silicon prototyping is broadly classified into:

1. **FPGA-Based Prototyping** (most common, cost-effective)
2. **ASIC-Based Prototyping** (using structured/semi-custom ASICs)
3. **Emulation Systems** (commercial — Palladium, Veloce — not in KTU scope typically)
4. **Virtual Prototyping** (purely software — QEMU, SystemC TLM)

This module will focus primarily on **FPGA-based silicon prototyping**, the industry workhorse for academic and small-to-medium scale projects.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Silicon Prototyping Flow (Structured Logic Steps)

The end-to-end flow from RTL to a working silicon prototype follows these well-defined stages:

1. **Design Partitioning** — Split the large SoC into smaller sub-modules that fit into available FPGAs.
2. **RTL Modification & Adaptation** — Convert ASIC cells to FPGA primitives (e.g., dedicated clock buffers, Block RAMs, DSP slices).
3. **Pin Multiplexing & Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)** — Map the massive pin count of an ASIC (often 1000+ pins) into the limited I/O of FPGAs (typically 200–500 pins).
4. **Synthesis & Place-and-Route (PnR)** — Target the FPGA vendor's library (Xilinx Vivado / Intel Quartus).
5. **Board Bring-up** — Configure the FPGA bitstream onto the hardware board.
6. **In-System Debug & Validation** — Use logic analyzers, Integrated Logic Analyzers (ILA), and ChipScope.
7. **Software/Hardware Co-verification** — Boot firmware, run real workloads, validate I/O.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Partition?** A modern SoC may have **50–100 million ASIC gates**, but a high-end FPGA holds only **~10–20 million ASIC-equivalent gates**. Partitioning is unavoidable.
- **Why Pin Multiplexing?** ASICs use **~1000+ I/O pins**; FPGAs offer **~200–500**. Without TDM, you cannot connect them.
- **Why TDM?** Time-Division Multiplexing allows multiple internal signals to share one physical FPGA pin, switched at a faster internal clock rate.
- **Why FPGA primitives?** ASIC standard cell libraries are not available for FPGAs. Hard macros (BRAM, DSP, PLL) must be inferred or instantiated.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ASIC-to-FPGA gate ratio | $G_{FPGA} \approx G_{ASIC} \times 0.4$ | gates | FPGA uses 4-input LUTs, so a 6-transistor NAND becomes ~1 LUT |
| 2 | TDM Ratio | $R_{TDM} = \dfrac{F_{int}}{F_{ext}} = \dfrac{\text{Internal fast clock}}{\text{External slow clock}}$ | dimensionless | Higher ratio = more signals multiplexed |
| 3 | Number of FPGAs required | $N_{FPGA} = \left\lceil \dfrac{G_{total}}{G_{per\_FPGA}} \right\rceil$ | integer | Round up to nearest whole FPGA |
| 4 | Prototyping speed-up factor | $S = \dfrac{f_{prototype}}{f_{simulator}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $10^3$ to $10^5$ |
| 5 | Total verification time | $T_{verify} = T_{RTL\_sim} + T_{FPGA\_prot}$ | hours | FPGA phase is shorter due to speed |
| 6 | Clock domain crossings | $N_{CDC} = \binom{N_{clk}}{2}$ | pairs | $N_{clk}$ asynchronous clocks in the design |
| 7 | Throughput of TDM bus | $\Theta = W_{bus} \times f_{ext}$ | bits/sec | $W_{bus}$ = bus width |
| 8 | Cost per prototype iteration | $C_{iter} = C_{FPGA\_board} + N \times C_{FPGA\_chip}$ | currency | FPGA boards are non-recurring (NRE) |
| 9 | Memory bandwidth requirement | $BW = f_{clk} \times W_{data}$ | bits/sec | Critical for DDR prototyping |
| 10 | Partitioning cut size | $C_{cut} = \dfrac{E_{cut}}{E_{total}}$ | ratio | Lower is better; minimizes inter-FPGA wires |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **TDM ratio** $R_{TDM}$ is the most-tested formula in this topic. Always show units. TDM ratio must be an integer for clean time-slot alignment.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Silicon prototyping is the backbone of:
- **Mobile SoCs (Apple, Qualcomm Snapdragon, MediaTek Dimensity)** — validate AI engines, 5G modems, ISPs.
- **Automotive ADAS chips (NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ)** — must run real perception algorithms pre-tapeout.
- **Data-center accelerators (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium)** — boot Linux and ML frameworks pre-silicon.
- **Aerospace & Defense FPGAs** — radiation-tolerant by-design prototypes.

Without prototyping, the **respin cost of a modern SoC** exceeds **$5–10 million per mask set** — making pre-silicon bug detection economically essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of FPGAs Required

**Problem:** A SoC has a total gate count of $G_{total} = 80$ million ASIC gates. A single FPGA can hold $G_{per\_FPGA} = 12$ million ASIC-equivalent gates. Calculate the minimum number of FPGAs required.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

We use the partitioning ceiling formula:

$$N_{FPGA} = \left\lceil \dfrac{G_{total}}{G_{per\_FPGA}} \right\rceil$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$N_{FPGA} = \left\lceil \dfrac{80 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 10^{6}} \right\rceil$$

Perform the division:

$$N_{FPGA} = \left\lceil 6.666\ldots \right\rceil$$

Apply the ceiling operator (round up to the next integer):

$$N_{FPGA} = 7 \text{ FPGAs}$$

**Conclusion:** A **7-FPGA** configuration is required. The 7th FPGA will be only ~67% utilized, which is acceptable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: TDM Ratio Calculation

**Problem:** An ASIC has 2000 internal signals that need to be mapped to an FPGA prototype with 250 available I/O pins. The internal FPGA logic runs at $f_{int} = 200$ MHz. Calculate the required TDM ratio and the effective external sampling rate.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Find the multiplexing requirement:**

$$\text{Multiplex factor } M = \left\lceil \dfrac{S_{int}}{P_{avail}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{2000}{250} \right\rceil = \lceil 8 \rceil = 8$$

So 8 internal signals must share 1 physical pin.

**Step 2 — Compute the TDM ratio using the formula:**

$$R_{TDM} = M = 8$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective external frequency:**

$$f_{ext} = \dfrac{f_{int}}{R_{TDM}} = \dfrac{200 \text{ MHz}}{8} = 25 \text{ MHz}$$

**Conclusion:** Each external pin carries 8 time-multiplexed signals. The receiver samples at **25 MHz**, demultiplexing them back to 200 MHz domain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher $R_{TDM}$ reduces pin count usage but increases receiver complexity and latency (latency = $R_{TDM} / f_{int}$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Time-to-Market (TTM) Savings Analysis

**Problem:** A design requires $T_{sim} = 600$ hours of simulation to reach 95% coverage, and the remaining 5% (real-world bugs) takes another $T_{prot} = 40$ hours on an FPGA prototype. Simulation runs at $f_{sim} = 1$ kHz effective; FPGA runs at $f_{FPGA} = 50$ MHz. Show why FPGA prototyping is faster for the last 5% of bugs.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent FPGA time to run the same simulation workload:**

$$T_{equiv} = T_{sim} \times \dfrac{f_{sim}}{f_{FPGA}} = 600 \times \dfrac{10^{3}}{50 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{equiv} = 600 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} = 0.012 \text{ hours} = 43.2 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Add the actual FPGA prototype time:**

$$T_{total\_FPGA} = 0.012 + 40 = 40.012 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with simulation-only:**

$$T_{total\_sim} = 600 \text{ hours (assuming it can even find those bugs)}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup factor:**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{total\_sim}}{T_{total\_FPGA}} = \dfrac{600}{40.012} \approx 15\times$$

But more importantly, simulation **cannot find the last 5% of real-world bugs** (silicon-accurate timing, I/O, OS interaction) — so the speedup is effectively **infinite** for those bug classes.

**Conclusion:** FPGA prototyping is **15× faster** for the equivalent workload AND finds bugs simulation physically cannot.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: FPGA vs. ASIC vs. Emulation Prototyping

| Parameter | FPGA Prototype | ASIC Prototype | Commercial Emulator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost (NRE)** | Low (~$1K–$50K) | Very High (~$500K–$5M) | Very High (>$1M) |
| **Speed** | 50–200 MHz | Real silicon speed (GHz) | 1–10 MHz |
| **Capacity** | 1–8 FPGAs (multi-board) | 1 ASIC, full capacity | Up to billions of gates |
| **Debug Visibility** | Excellent (ILA, JTAG) | Limited (scan chains) | Excellent (full visibility) |
| **Turn-around time** | Days to weeks | Months (mask fab) | Hours (compilation) |
| **Power modeling** | Approximate | Real silicon accurate | Software-modeled |
| **Use case (KTU)** | Academic, small SoCs | Pre-production validation | Large SoC, software dev |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams:** When asked to choose a prototyping method for a 5-million gate SoC on a student budget, the answer is **multi-FPGA prototyping**, not ASIC.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Silicon Prototyping Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RTL Design Verilog VHDL] --> B[Design Partitioning]
    B --> C[RTL Adaptation for FPGA]
    C --> D[Synthesis FPGA Vendor Tool]
    D --> E[Place and Route]
    E --> F[Bitstream Generation]
    F --> G[FPGA Board Configuration]
    G --> H[Board Bring-up and Debug]
    H --> I{Validation Pass?}
    I -- No --> J[Fix RTL and Iterate]
    J --> A
    I -- Yes --> K[Software Co-verification]
    K --> L[Mass Production Tape-out]
```

### 4.2 Multi-FPGA Prototyping Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FPGA_A["FPGA Board 1"]
        A1[CPU Subsystem]
        A2[Memory Controller]
        A3[Inter-FPGA Bridge TDM TX]
    end
    subgraph FPGA_B["FPGA Board 2"]
        B1[GPU ISP Pipeline]
        B2[DSP Audio Engine]
        B3[Inter-FPGA Bridge TDM RX]
    end
    subgraph FPGA_C["FPGA Board 3"]
        C1[Peripherals USB PCIe]
        C2[DDR PHY]
        C3[Clock Management]
    end
    A3 -- High Speed Serial LVDS --> B3
    B3 -- Serial Link --> C3
    A1 -.GPIO.- A2
    B1 -.GPIO.- B2
    C1 -.GPIO.- C2
```

### 4.3 Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM) Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[S1 Internal Signal 1] --> MUX[Time Multiplexer M equals 8]
    B[S2 Internal Signal 2] --> MUX
    C[S3 Internal Signal 3] --> MUX
    D[S4 Internal Signal 4] --> MUX
    E[S5 Internal Signal 5] --> MUX
    F[S6 Internal Signal 6] --> MUX
    G[S7 Internal Signal 7] --> MUX
    H[S8 Internal Signal 8] --> MUX
    MUX -- Single Physical Pin 200 MHz --> P[External FPGA Pin]
    P -- Sampled at 25 MHz --> DEMUX[De-multiplexer]
    DEMUX --> R1[S1 Recovered]
    DEMUX --> R2[S2 Recovered]
    DEMUX --> R3[S3 Recovered]
    DEMUX --> R4[S4 Recovered]
    DEMUX --> R5[S5 Recovered]
    DEMUX --> R6[S6 Recovered]
    DEMUX --> R7[S7 Recovered]
    DEMUX --> R8[S8 Recovered]
```

### 4.4 Verification Pyramid showing Prototyping Position

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Static Linting and Formal] --> P2[Dynamic Simulation]
    P2 --> P3[FPGA Prototyping]
    P3 --> P4[Emulation]
    P4 --> P5[Silicon Validation]
    P5 --> P6[Mass Production]
    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P2 fill:#b3e5fc
    style P3 fill:#81d4fa
    style P4 fill:#4fc3f7
    style P5 fill:#29b6f6
    style P6 fill:#0288d1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the pyramid:** Each level is **slower to set up but faster to run** and exercises the design with **more realistic stimuli**. FPGA prototyping is the **sweet spot** for cost-vs-accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define silicon prototyping. List any two advantages of FPGA-based silicon prototyping over software simulation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Silicon prototyping is the process of implementing an ASIC/SoC design on real silicon or an FPGA-based hardware platform to validate functionality, performance, and software stack before committing to high-volume production.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Speed:** FPGA prototypes run at **50–200 MHz**, providing up to **$10^5$× speedup** over software simulation, enabling real boot-up of OS and firmware.
2. **Silicon-accurate bug detection:** Captures real timing issues (crosstalk, IR drop, clock jitter) and hardware-software race conditions that simulation cannot model accurately.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM) in the context of FPGA prototyping? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** TDM is a technique where multiple internal signals of a design share a single physical FPGA I/O pin by sampling them at different time slots of a faster internal clock.

**Necessity:**
- Modern ASICs have **~1000+ I/O pins**, but FPGAs offer only **~200–500 I/O pins**.
- TDM allows fitting a high-pin-count ASIC into a lower-pin-count FPGA.
- The TDM ratio is given by $R_{TDM} = f_{int} / f_{ext}$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the complete FPGA-based silicon prototyping flow with a neat block diagram. Discuss the challenges in each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The FPGA-based silicon prototyping flow consists of the following stages:

| Stage | Activity | Key Challenge |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Partitioning | Divide the SoC into FPGA-fitting blocks | Minimize inter-FPGA cut size |
| 2. RTL Adaptation | Replace ASIC macros with FPGA primitives | Memory mapping (BRAM), clock tree differences |
| 3. Pin Multiplexing | Apply TDM to fit pins | Latency and skew management |
| 4. Synthesis | Use Vivado/Quartus | LUT overflow, timing closure |
| 5. PnR | Place and route within FPGA | Congestion, hold-time violations |
| 6. Board Bring-up | Program bitstream, test I/O | Signal integrity at high speed |
| 7. Debug | Use ILA, ChipScope | Limited probe visibility |
| 8. SW Co-verification | Boot OS, run drivers | Driver-hardware mismatches |

**Key Challenges:**
- **Clock Domain Crossing (CDC):** Multiple FPGAs introduce new asynchronous boundaries.
- **Memory bandwidth:** External DDR must match ASIC bandwidth requirements.
- **I/O voltage mismatch:** ASICs may use 0.8V core; FPGAs use 0.85V–1.2V.
- **Debug visibility:** Internal signals are not directly observable.

---

**(b)** A SoC contains **120 million ASIC gates**. Each FPGA can implement **15 million ASIC-equivalent gates**. The design has **2400 internal signals** that need external observation through FPGAs having **300 I/O pins** each. The internal FPGA clock is **240 MHz**.

Compute:
- (i) The number of FPGAs required.
- (ii) The TDM ratio per FPGA.
- (iii) The external sampling frequency.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of FPGAs:**

$$N_{FPGA} = \left\lceil \dfrac{G_{total}}{G_{per\_FPGA}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{120 \times 10^{6}}{15 \times 10^{6}} \right\rceil = \lceil 8 \rceil = 8 \text{ FPGAs}$$

**(ii) TDM ratio per FPGA:**

Assuming signals are distributed uniformly (2400 / 8 = 300 signals per FPGA):

$$M = \left\lceil \dfrac{S_{per\_FPGA}}{P_{avail}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{300}{300} \right\rceil = 1$$

So $R_{TDM} = 1$ (no TDM needed in this case — every signal gets its own pin).

If signals were concentrated: e.g., 1200 signals on 1 FPGA → $M = \lceil 1200/300 \rceil = 4$.

**(iii) External sampling frequency:**

$$f_{ext} = \dfrac{f_{int}}{R_{TDM}} = \dfrac{240 \text{ MHz}}{1} = 240 \text{ MHz}$$

(no degradation in this case)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget the **ceiling operator** in FPGA count. Always round UP — a partial FPGA does not exist. Also, distribute signals *evenly* across FPGAs to avoid pin congestion on a single FPGA.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast FPGA-based prototyping with ASIC-based prototyping. Mention two scenarios where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | FPGA Prototyping | ASIC Prototyping |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low ($1K–$50K) | Very High ($500K–$5M) |
| **Lead Time** | Days–weeks | Months |
| **Performance** | 50–200 MHz | Real GHz speeds |
| **Capacity** | Limited by FPGA logic | Full ASIC capacity |
| **Power Modeling** | Approximate | Real silicon-accurate |
| **Debug** | Excellent | Limited (scan/BIST) |
| **Iteration** | Quick (reprogrammable) | Slow (re-mask required) |

**Preferred Scenarios for FPGA:**
1. **Academic / student projects** — budget-constrained, fast iteration.
2. **Early software development** — boot Linux on a pre-silicon SoC.

**Preferred Scenarios for ASIC:**
1. **Final silicon-accurate validation** before mass production tape-out.
2. **Performance-critical prototypes** where real GHz speed and power data are mandatory (e.g., mobile SoC final check).

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Design Partitioning** in multi-FPGA prototyping. What are the key metrics to evaluate a good partition? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Design Partitioning is the process of dividing a large ASIC design (often 50–100M+ gates) into smaller sub-blocks, each of which can fit into a single FPGA, while minimizing inter-FPGA communication overhead.

**Partitioning Strategies:**
1. **Functional Partitioning** — group by logical function (CPU, GPU, DDR).
2. **Structural Partitioning** — group by hierarchical boundaries.
3. **Timing-driven Partitioning** — group to meet timing closure.

**Key Metrics for a Good Partition:**

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Cut Size** | $C_{cut} = E_{cut} / E_{total}$ | Minimize (< 5%) |
| **Logic Balance** | Variance of gate counts across FPGAs | Minimize (< 10%) |
| **Timing Closure** | Max delay across partition | Minimize |
| **Pin Utilization** | Pins used / Total pins per FPGA | 70–80% (avoid congestion) |
| **Clock Domains** | Number of CDCs introduced | Minimize |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When asked about partitioning, students often miss the **cut-size metric**. The cut size (number of signals crossing FPGA boundaries) directly determines the **TDM ratio** and thus the prototype's speed. A poor partition = slow prototype.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in TDM ratio problems (always state MHz or dimensionless).
> 2. **Confusing emulation with prototyping** — emulation is software-driven, prototyping is hardware-real.
> 3. **Missing the ceiling operator** when computing number of FPGAs.
> 4. **Not mentioning debug tools** (ILA, ChipScope, JTAG) — these are syllabus-mandated.
> 5. **Ignoring power estimation** differences between FPGA and ASIC.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Silicon Prototyping = pre-tapeout hardware validation** on real hardware.
- **Two main types:** **FPGA-based** (cheap, fast) and **ASIC-based** (expensive, accurate).
- **Key formula:** $N_{FPGA} = \lceil G_{total} / G_{per\_FPGA} \rceil$.
- **Key formula:** $R_{TDM} = f_{int} / f_{ext}$.
- **Prototyping speed advantage:** $10^3$ to $10^5$× over simulation.
- **Partitioning** is the most critical step — minimize **cut size** and **CDC** count.
- **Pin multiplexing (TDM)** is mandatory when ASIC pin count exceeds FPGA pin count.
- **Flow:** RTL → Partition → Adapt → Synthesize → PnR → Bitstream → Board → Debug.
- **Debug tools:** ILA (Xilinx), Signal Tap (Intel), JTAG boundary scan.
- **Cost advantage:** FPGA prototype costs **<$50K** vs. **>$1M** for ASIC respin.
- **Limitations:** FPGA cannot model ASIC-accurate IR drop, clock jitter, or process variations.
- **Co-verification:** FPGA prototypes boot real OS (Linux, RTOS) and run production firmware.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate Level Verification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gate Level Verification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Gate Level Verification (GLV)** is the rigorous, multi-strategy engineering process of mathematically and functionally proving that the synthesized **gate-level netlist** (post-synthesis, post-DFT, post-ECO) is logically equivalent to its corresponding **Register Transfer Level (RTL)** reference design, under all valid input stimuli and operational corner conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Gate Level Verification is the verification of the gate-level netlist against the RTL using simulation, formal equivalence checking (EC), and linting to catch synthesis-introduced mismatches, X-propagation, and unintended optimizations.*

Mathematically, for two designs $M_1$ (the RTL/Reference) and $M_2$ (the Gate-Level Netlist/Implementation), GLV aims to prove:

$$
\forall x \in \text{Inputs:} \;\; \big( M_1(x) = M_2(x) \big)
$$

Where $M_1(x)$ and $M_2(x)$ represent the output bit-vectors observed over all reachable states.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Translation Bureau" Analogy**
> Imagine you wrote an original recipe in **English** (the RTL — high-level, behavioral), and a translator converted it into a precise **molecular chemistry formula** in a lab notebook (the Gate-level netlist — low-level, structural). Gate Level Verification is the act of having a **third-party food critic** ensure that the molecular formula, when actually cooked, produces *exactly* the same dish as the English recipe describes.
>
> - The translator (Synthesis Tool) may **shorten** sentences (sequential optimization).
> - The translator may **add extra spices** that weren't in the original (DFT scan chains).
> - The translator may leave some words **ambiguous** (the notorious 'X' — unknown state).
>
> GLV is the exhaustive audit confirming **no flavor was lost or changed**.

## 1.3 Why GLV is Indispensable in Modern VLSI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** GLV is *non-negotiable* in the **Tape-out Ready (Tape-out Gate)** sign-off checklist for any SoC/ASIC chip below **7 nm**. Approximately **70% of all silicon re-spins** historically originate from bugs introduced or escaped during synthesis — bugs only catchable by GLV.

The principal causes of RTL-to-Netlist divergence include:

1. **Sequential Optimization**: Retiming, state encoding, redundant register removal.
2. **Combinational Optimization**: Constant propagation, dead code elimination, technology mapping.
3. **DFT Insertion**: Scan chains, BIST controllers, compression logic.
4. **Power Intent Realization**: Clock gating, power gating, multi-Vt swapping.
5. **Engineering Change Orders (ECOs)**: Late manual netlist edits.

## 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics in GLV

- **Equivalence Coverage**: **100%** for combinational cones; **> 95%** proven sequentially.
- **X-Optimization Ratio (XOR)**: Ratio of unresolved X-bits after simulation, target $XOR \to 0$.
- **Miter Circuit Mismatch Count**: Target $= 0$ across all corner cases.
- **Proof Time (Formal EC)**: Bounded between **10 minutes to 24 hours** depending on design size.
- **Setup/Hold Slack**: Verified via **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** as a complementary step.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Verification Stack

GLV operates across a layered strategy stack, each layer catching what the previous one misses.

### Layer 1 — Dynamic Simulation (Zero/Unit Delay)
Reuses the RTL testbench. The testbench is connected to a gate-level model through **PLI/VPI/DPI** interfaces. Stimulus is applied and outputs are compared.

### Layer 2 — Formality-Based Equivalence Checking (Formal EC)
Pure mathematical proof using Boolean SAT/SMT solvers. No testbench is required.

### Layer 3 — Linting & Structural Checks
Checks for undriven nets, multi-driven nets, floating buses, asynchronous reset issues.

### Layer 4 — X-Propagation Analysis (X-Bounding)
Ensures unknown states ('X') do not propagate into observable outputs.

## 2.2 Core Mechanisms of Equivalence Checking

> [!NOTE]
> **The Miter Circuit** is the fundamental construct of Formal Equivalence Checking. It XORs every corresponding output bit of the two designs and ORs the results. A single '1' at the miter output **PROVES inequivalence**.

### 2.2.1 Combinational Equivalence Checking (CEC)

Used when the **sequential behavior (flip-flop boundaries)** is identical between $M_1$ and $M_2$. Each flip-flop output is treated as a pseudo-primary-input.

| Property | Mathematical Representation |
|---|---|
| Output Equivalence | $M_1^k(x) \oplus M_2^k(x) = 0 \quad \forall x, k$ |
| Miter Output | $Y_{\text{miter}} = \bigvee_{k=1}^{N} (O_1^k \oplus O_2^k)$ |
| Equivalence Proof | $Y_{\text{miter}} \equiv 0$ (Boolean constant) |

### 2.2.2 Sequential Equivalence Checking (SEC)

When the **state register count or ordering differs**, CEC fails. SEC unrolls both designs $k$ time-frames and creates a *product machine*.

$$
S_{\text{product}} = S_1 \times S_2, \quad \vert S_{\text{product}} \vert = \vert S_1 \vert \cdot \vert S_2 \vert
$$

## 2.3 SAT-Based Equivalence Checking

A Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) solver is invoked on the miter circuit. The problem reduces to:

$$
\text{Is the CNF of } Y_{\text{miter}} \text{ satisfiable?}
$$

- If **UNSAT** → Designs are equivalent.
- If **SAT** → A counter-example trace is generated, exposing the divergent behavior.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_{\text{miter}} = \bigvee_{k=1}^{N} (O_1^k \oplus O_2^k)$ | Miter output for $N$ output bits | Boolean $\{0,1\}$ |
| 2 | $\text{CNF} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{M} C_i$ | Conjunctive Normal Form of miter | Logical clauses |
| 3 | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{P} t_i$ | Total GLV runtime | seconds |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{ec}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{proved}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Equivalence coverage | ratio $[0,1]$ |
| 5 | $X_{\text{bound}} = f(X_{\text{init}}, C_{\text{clk}})$ | X-propagation boundedness | Boolean lattice |
| 6 | $\delta_{\text{state}} = \vert S_1 \vert - \vert S_2 \vert$ | Sequential state register delta | integer $\in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 7 | $C_{\text{cut}} = \sum_{j=1}^{K} 2^{n_j}$ | Cutpoint combinatorial explosion | exponential |
| 8 | $P_{\text{match}} = 1 - \dfrac{F_p}{F_t}$ | Simulation match probability | ratio $[0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Notation Rule:** Always express absolute value or set cardinality as $\vert \cdot \vert$ (never the bare pipe `|`) to avoid parser failure in LaTeX environments.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-Silicon Sign-off**: Catches RTL-Netlist divergence **before** wafer fabrication, saving **\$1M–\$10M** per re-spin.
- **ECO Validation**: After manual gate-level edits, GLV confirms no regression.
- **DFT Closure**: Verifies scan stitching has not altered functional behavior.
- **Power-Intent Validation**: Confirms clock-gating logic does not corrupt functional data.
- **Safety-Critical Silicon** (Automotive ISO 26262, Medical IEC 62304): Mandated by functional safety standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Two Designs to a SAT Instance

This is the foundational derivation that every KTU examiner expects in an ESE question.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Designs

Let $M_1$ be the **Reference (RTL)** and $M_2$ be the **Implementation (Netlist)**.

$$
M_1 : \mathbb{B}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{B}^m, \quad M_2 : \mathbb{B}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{B}^m
$$

Here $\mathbb{B} = \{0, 1\}$, $n$ = number of primary inputs, $m$ = number of primary outputs.

### Step 2 — Construct the Miter Circuit

For every corresponding output bit $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, m\}$, XOR the two outputs:

$$
D_k(x) = O_1^k(x) \oplus O_2^k(x)
$$

Aggregate all disagreements through a single OR gate:

$$
Y_{\text{miter}}(x) = D_1(x) \vee D_2(x) \vee \dots \vee D_m(x)
$$

If $Y_{\text{miter}}(x) = 1$ for some input vector $x$, then a mismatch is found at output bit $k$.

### Step 3 — Convert Miter to Boolean Formula

Define $\Phi(x)$ as the Boolean formula of the entire miter:

$$
\Phi(x) = \bigvee_{k=1}^{m} \big( O_1^k(x) \oplus O_2^k(x) \big)
$$

Using De Morgan's law and XOR expansion:

$$
a \oplus b = (a \wedge \overline{b}) \vee (\overline{a} \wedge b)
$$

Substitute into the formula:

$$
\Phi(x) = \bigvee_{k=1}^{m} \Big[ \big(O_1^k(x) \wedge \overline{O_2^k(x)}\big) \vee \big(\overline{O_1^k(x)} \wedge O_2^k(x)\big) \Big]
$$

### Step 4 — Transform to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) for SAT

Apply Tseitin's transformation to convert $\Phi$ into an equisatisfiable CNF:

$$
\Phi_{\text{CNF}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{M} C_i
$$

where each $C_i$ is a disjunction of literals (variables or their negations). This linear-sized conversion enables polynomial-time solver processing.

### Step 5 — Invoke the SAT Solver

The SAT solver determines satisfiability:

$$
\text{SAT}(\Phi_{\text{CNF}}) = \begin{cases} \text{SAT} \rightarrow \exists\, x : Y_{\text{miter}}(x) = 1 & \text{(Inequivalence found)} \\ \text{UNSAT} \rightarrow \forall\, x : Y_{\text{miter}}(x) = 0 & \text{(Equivalence proven)} \end{cases}
$$

If **SAT**, the solver returns the satisfying assignment $x^*$, which is the **counter-example** exposing the divergence.

### Step 6 — Unrolling for Sequential Equivalence

For sequential designs, unroll $T$ time-frames and analyze the product machine:

$$
\Phi_{\text{seq}}(x_0, \dots, x_{T-1}) = \bigvee_{t=0}^{T-1} \bigvee_{k=1}^{m} \big( O_{1,t}^k \oplus O_{2,t}^k \big)
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

Consider two combinational designs:

$$
M_1(x_1, x_2) = x_1 \wedge x_2, \quad M_2(x_1, x_2) = \neg(\neg x_1 \vee \neg x_2)
$$

By De Morgan's theorem, $M_1 = M_2$. The miter is:

$$
Y_{\text{miter}} = (x_1 \wedge x_2) \oplus \neg(\neg x_1 \vee \neg x_2)
$$

Truth-table analysis confirms $Y_{\text{miter}} \equiv 0$. A SAT call returns **UNSAT** → **Equivalent**.

## 3.3 X-Propagation Bound Computation (Analytical Matrix)

Given initial X-states at primary inputs:

$$
X_{\text{init}} = \{x_3 = X\}, \quad X_{\text{others}} = \{0, 1\}
$$

The X-propagation function through a logic cone is:

$$
X_{\text{out}} = f_{\text{cone}}(X_{\text{init}}, \text{Control Signals})
$$

| Control Signal | AND Gate Behavior | OR Gate Behavior | X-Status at Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C = 0$ | $0 \wedge X = 0$ | $0 \vee X = X$ | **Controlling value applied** |
| $C = 1$ | $1 \wedge X = X$ | $1 \vee X = 1$ | **Non-controlling; X propagates** |
| $C = X$ | $X \wedge X = X$ | $X \vee X = X$ | **Fully unresolved** |

The bounding algorithm iteratively assigns $X \in \{0, 1\}$ in both polarities and re-checks functional impact, computing:

$$
\Delta X_{\text{obs}} = \vert \text{Observability}(X=0) - \text{Observability}(X=1) \vert
$$

If $\Delta X_{\text{obs}} > 0$, the X **matters functionally** and must be resolved.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gate Level Verification Flow Architecture

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps every component interaction in a production GLV pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RTL Source Code VHDL Verilog] --> B[Logic Synthesis Tool]
    A --> C[Formal Reference Model]
    B --> D[Gate Level Netlist]
    D --> E[DFT Insertion Scan BIST]
    E --> F[Post DFT Netlist]
    F --> G[Power Intent UPF CPF]
    G --> H[Final Gate Level Netlist]
    C --> I[Equivalence Checker Formal EC]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Proof Engine SAT SMT}
    I --> K{Equivalence Decision}
    K -- Equivalent --> L[GLV PASS Signoff Ready]
    K -- Counterexample Found --> M[Debug Trace Generation]
    M --> N[RTL or Netlist Patch ECO]
    N --> B
    H --> O[Dynamic Simulator PLI DPI]
    A --> O
    O --> P{X Optimisation Analysis}
    P -- X bounded --> Q[Simulation PASS]
    P -- X leaks to output --> R[X Resolution Required]
    R --> S[Initial Value or Tie Cell Fix]
    S --> H
    L --> T[Tapeout Ready Silicon Fabrication]
```

## 4.2 Miter Circuit Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PI[Primary Inputs x1..xn] --> M1[M1 Reference Design RTL]
    PI --> M2[M2 Implementation Design Netlist]
    M1 --> X1[XOR Gate Output Bit 1]
    M2 --> X1
    M1 --> X2[XOR Gate Output Bit 2]
    M2 --> X2
    M1 --> Xm[XOR Gate Output Bit m]
    M2 --> Xm
    X1 --> OR[OR Aggregation Gate]
    X2 --> OR
    Xm --> OR
    OR --> MIT[Y Miter Output]
    MIT -- 1 --> CEQ[Inequivalence Counterexample Found]
    MIT -- 0 --> EQ[Equivalence Proven PASS]
```

## 4.3 Sequential Equivalence Unrolling Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[State Registers of M1] --> C1[Combinational Logic of M1]
    S2[State Registers of M2] --> C2[Combinational Logic of M2]
    PI[Primary Inputs] --> C1
    PI --> C2
    C1 --> S1
    C2 --> S2
    C1 --> O1[Output Bit Stream M1]
    C2 --> O2[Output Bit Stream M2]
    O1 --> UC[Unroll Comparator T Timeframes]
    O2 --> UC
    UC --> RES{All Timeframes Match}
    RES -- Yes --> SP[Sequential Equivalence Proven]
    RES -- No --> SF[Sequential Inequivalence at Timeframe T minus 1]
```

## 4.4 X-Propagation Cone Analysis Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Initial X Bit Source] --> G1[AND Gate]
    C1[Control Signal C1] --> G1
    G1 --> G2[OR Gate]
    X --> G2
    C2[Control Signal C2] --> G2
    G2 --> OUT[Observable Output]
    G1 --> OBS[Observability Check]
    G2 --> OBS
    OBS --> BND{X Bounded Decision}
    BND -- Bounded Safe --> OK[No Functional Impact]
    BND -- Unbounded --> FAIL[X Affects Functional Output]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Gate Level Verification. Why is it necessary even after functional verification at RTL has passed?**

**Model Answer:**
Gate Level Verification (GLV) is the process of verifying that the **gate-level netlist** produced after synthesis, DFT insertion, and ECO changes is functionally equivalent to the original RTL design.

It is **necessary** because synthesis tools perform optimizations such as retiming, constant propagation, dead code elimination, and technology mapping. Additionally, DFT logic (scan chains, BIST) and clock-gating logic are inserted at the gate level. None of these transformations are present in the RTL, so RTL-only simulation cannot detect bugs introduced by them. GLV ensures functional equivalence before silicon fabrication, preventing costly re-spins.

*(3 marks: definition 1, necessity 2)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**What is a miter circuit? How is it used in formal equivalence checking?**

**Model Answer:**
A **miter circuit** is a comparator circuit constructed by XORing every corresponding output bit of the Reference design (RTL) and the Implementation design (Gate-level Netlist), and ORing all the XOR results. The single output is called the **miter output** $Y_{\text{miter}}$.

$$
Y_{\text{miter}} = \bigvee_{k=1}^{m} (O_1^k \oplus O_2^k)
$$

In formal equivalence checking, the miter is converted into CNF and fed to a SAT solver. If the solver returns **UNSAT**, the miter output is identically zero, proving equivalence. If **SAT**, a counter-example vector $x^*$ is returned, exposing the divergent behavior along with its triggering input stimulus.

*(3 marks: miter definition with equation 2, SAT usage 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the complete Gate Level Verification methodology. Differentiate between **Combinational Equivalence Checking (CEC)** and **Sequential Equivalence Checking (SEC)** with suitable diagrams. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a **miter circuit**. Derive the SAT-based equivalence checking formulation step-by-step starting from the two designs. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Gate Level Verification Methodology** consists of four stages:

**Stage 1 — Setup:** Load the RTL as the Reference and the gate-level netlist as the Implementation. Both designs must have matched top-level ports and clock/reset domains.

**Stage 2 — Compare Point Mapping:** Identify corresponding flip-flops, primary inputs, and primary outputs between the two designs using name-based or hashing-based mapping.

**Stage 3 — Verification:**
- If the **register count and boundaries match**, **CEC** is applied. Each register output is treated as a pseudo-primary-input. Combinational cones are compared independently.
- If the **register count differs** or registers have been merged/split, **SEC** is applied. Both designs are unrolled over multiple time-frames, creating a *product machine* whose state space is $S_1 \times S_2$.

**Stage 4 — Debugging:** If a counter-example is found, trace the divergent signal path back to the synthesis optimization or DFT insertion responsible.

| Parameter | CEC | SEC |
|---|---|---|
| Register Boundary | Identical | May differ |
| State Space | Single timeframe | $\vert S_1 \vert \times \vert S_2 \vert$ |
| Complexity | Polynomial in fan-in | Exponential (state explosion) |
| Use Case | DFT-free synthesis | Retiming, scan stitching, ECO |

*(7 marks: methodology 3, comparison table with explanation 4)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Miter Circuit Construction:**

A miter circuit XORs each corresponding output bit of $M_1$ (RTL) and $M_2$ (Netlist). The XOR outputs are aggregated through an OR gate.

*(Insert the miter schematic from Section 4.2 here during the exam)*

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For each output bit $k \in \{1, \dots, m\}$:
$$
D_k(x) = O_1^k(x) \oplus O_2^k(x)
$$

**Step 2:** Aggregate:
$$
Y_{\text{miter}}(x) = \bigvee_{k=1}^{m} D_k(x)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute XOR with its Boolean expansion:
$$
D_k(x) = \big(O_1^k \wedge \overline{O_2^k}\big) \vee \big(\overline{O_1^k} \wedge O_2^k\big)
$$

**Step 4:** Convert via Tseitin transformation to CNF:
$$
\Phi_{\text{CNF}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{M} C_i
$$

**Step 5:** Invoke SAT solver:
- **UNSAT** → $Y_{\text{miter}} \equiv 0$ → **Equivalence proven**
- **SAT** → counter-example $x^*$ exposed → **Inequivalence detected**

*(7 marks: miter sketch 2, derivation steps 5)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Alternate]`

**(a)** Discuss the challenges of **X-propagation** in gate-level simulation. How does the X-bounding algorithm resolve these challenges? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of **SAT solvers** in formal equivalence checking. Discuss the Tseitin transformation and CNF conversion with an example. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

**X-Propagation Challenges:**

In gate-level simulation, the value **'X' (unknown)** can arise from uninitialized flip-flops, multi-driven buses, or asynchronous reset races. Unlike RTL, the gate-level model may propagate X to observable outputs, causing:

1. **False Failures:** Testbench compares output with expected value but sees X, leading to spurious mismatches.
2. **Masked Real Bugs:** X can mask actual functional errors because the comparison logic (XOR/equality) treats X as a non-match.
3. **Simulation Inefficiency:** The simulator must consider both polarities of X ($X=0$ and $X=1$), doubling the simulation load.

**X-Bounding Algorithm:**

The bounding algorithm operates as follows:

1. Identify all X-sources in the netlist.
2. For each X-source, run **two simulations**: one with X=0, one with X=1.
3. Compare both output waveforms. If both produce identical observable outputs, the X is **bounded** (functionally inert).
4. If outputs differ, the X is **unbounded** and must be **resolved** by adding initial-value assignments, tie-cells, or RTL-side fixes.

Mathematically:
$$
\text{X-Bounded} \iff \forall i \in X_{\text{sources}} : \text{Out}(X_i = 0) = \text{Out}(X_i = 1)
$$

*(7 marks: challenges 3, algorithm with math 4)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Role of SAT Solvers:**

SAT (Boolean Satisfiability) solvers are the workhorse engines of modern formal equivalence checking. They solve the decision problem of whether a Boolean formula in CNF has a satisfying assignment.

In GLV, the miter circuit's output is expressed as a Boolean formula and converted to CNF. The SAT solver's verdict determines equivalence:

| SAT Solver Verdict | Interpretation | GLV Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **UNSAT** | No input assignment can make the miter output equal 1 | Designs are **equivalent** |
| **SAT** | An assignment exists that triggers the miter | Designs are **inequivalent**; counter-example generated |

**Tseitin Transformation:**

Tseitin's algorithm converts any Boolean formula into an equisatisfiable CNF in **linear time and size** by introducing auxiliary variables for each sub-formula.

**Example:**

Given $\Phi = (a \wedge b) \vee c$:

- Introduce auxiliary variable $d$ for $(a \wedge b)$.
- $d \leftrightarrow (a \wedge b)$ becomes $(d \vee \neg a \vee \neg b) \wedge (\neg d \vee a) \wedge (\neg d \vee b)$.
- $\Phi$ becomes $(d \vee c) \wedge (d \vee \neg a \vee \neg b) \wedge (\neg d \vee a) \wedge (\neg d \vee b)$.

This CNF is then fed to the SAT solver for analysis.

*(7 marks: SAT role 3, Tseitin with example 4)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in GLV Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Miter Equation** — Students often describe the miter verbally but omit the mathematical expression. KTU examiners award **2 marks exclusively for the equation** $Y_{\text{miter}} = \bigvee (O_1^k \oplus O_2^k)$.
>
> 2. **Confusing CEC and SEC** — A frequent error is stating that SEC unrolls *one* time-frame. The correct answer is that SEC unrolls **$T$ time-frames** and analyzes the **product machine** with state-space $\vert S_1 \vert \cdot \vert S_2 \vert$.
>
> 3. **Ignoring X-Propagation** — Many students write only about simulation and formal EC, forgetting the dedicated X-bounding step. This costs up to **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Mis-stating SAT Verdict** — Writing "SAT means equivalent" is the most common fatal error. Correct mapping: **UNSAT → Equivalent**, **SAT → Inequivalent (counter-example)**.
>
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams** — A labelled miter diagram in Question A(b) carries **2 marks** separately. Omitting it costs full marks for that sub-part.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Gate Level Verification**

- **Definition:** GLV = RTL-vs-Netlist equivalence proof.
- **Why needed:** Synthesis, DFT, ECO, power-intent introduce changes.
- **Miter Circuit:** XOR each output, OR all XORs. $Y_{\text{miter}} = \bigvee (O_1^k \oplus O_2^k)$.
- **CEC:** Same register boundaries, single time-frame, polynomial complexity.
- **SEC:** Different register count, $T$ time-frame unrolling, exponential state-space $\vert S_1 \vert \times \vert S_2 \vert$.
- **SAT Verdict Mapping:** **UNSAT → Equivalent**; **SAT → Inequivalent + counter-example**.
- **Tseitin Transformation:** Linear-size CNF conversion for SAT input.
- **X-Propagation:** 'X' = unknown; must be bounded to $\{0, 1\}$ at observable outputs.
- **X-Bounding Algorithm:** Run dual simulation with X=0 and X=1; if outputs match, X is bounded.
- **GlV Tools (Industry):** Synopsys Formality, Cadence JasperGold, Siemens Questa Formal.
- **Sign-off Criterion:** **100% equivalence coverage** for combinational cones; proof on critical sequential paths.
- **Cost of Skipping GLV:** \$1M–\$10M per silicon re-spin; potential safety-certification failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sign Off Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sign Off Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign-Off Simulation** is the *final, gold-standard verification phase* in the VLSI design flow that uses production-grade (silicon-accurate) libraries, parasitic extraction data, and worst-case operating corners to mathematically guarantee that the synthesized design will function correctly, meet timing, consume bounded power, and remain reliable after fabrication. It is the **last gate** before the GDSII is handed to the foundry.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, sign-off simulation is a **closure activity** that converts a "functionally correct" RTL design into a **"manufacturable and silicon-correct"** netlist by exercising the design under conditions that mirror real silicon behavior.

### Categories of Sign-Off (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Sign-Off Type | Primary Tool/Method | What it Validates |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing Sign-Off** | Static Timing Analysis (STA) with PrimeTime/Tempus | Setup/Hold, Max-Delay, Min-Delay across all PVT corners |
| **Power Sign-Off** | PrimePower, Voltus, RedHawk | Dynamic power, leakage, IR-drop, EM |
| **Functional Sign-Off** | Gate-Level Simulation (GLS) with SDF | Logic correctness with real delays |
| **Formal Sign-Off** | FormalVerify, Conformal | RTL-vs-netlist, netlist-vs-netlist equivalence |
| **Physical Sign-Off** | Calibre, IC Validator | DRC, LVS, ERC, Antenna, DFM |
| **DFT Sign-Off** | Tessent, DFTCompiler | Stuck-at, ATPG, BIST coverage |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **PEEVT635** course, the focus is primarily on **Functional Sign-Off (Gate-Level Simulation)** and its relationship to **Timing Sign-Off**, both of which are high-weight topics in the KTU University examinations.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English)

Imagine you are building a **commercial passenger airplane**.

- **RTL Design** = Building a *scale model* and a *blueprint* in a wind tunnel.
- **Functional Verification** = The *aviation regulatory tests* where the model proves it can *fly* in principle.
- **Synthesis** = Manufacturing the *real* airplane using *real aluminum*.
- **Sign-Off Simulation** = The *final, expensive, all-encompassing test flights* using:
  - Real fuel (real cell libraries with accurate delays)
  - Extreme weather (PVT corners — Pressure = Voltage, Velocity = Temperature)
  - Heavy luggage (parasitic resistance/capacitance)
  - Thousands of pilots (test vectors / regression suite)

> Once sign-off is passed, the design is **"signed off"** — meaning the lead engineer takes *legal/technical responsibility* that the chip will work in the customer's hands. **No more changes are allowed** without a fresh sign-off cycle.

---

## 1.3 The Sign-Off Methodology in Numbers (Key Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner loves these **bold metrics** — they frequently appear in 3-mark and 14-mark answers.

- **Corner Count:** Modern SoCs are verified across **20–50+ MMMC (Multi-Mode Multi-Corner) scenarios**.
- **Coverage Target:** Functional coverage must exceed **95%**, code coverage > **98%** for sign-off.
- **SDF Resolution:** Sign-off SDF uses **AIFT (Actual Instantaneous Floating-point Time)** resolution, not the integer ps of pre-layout.
- **Hold-Time Slack:** Must be **non-negative** across **all** corners; typical sign-off safety margin = **+50 ps**.
- **X-Propagation Tolerance:** Less than **0.01%** of simulation cycles may contain unresolved X's.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Sign-Off Simulation Cannot Be Skipped

RTL simulation is **zero-delay** and **idealized**. Synthesis introduces:
1. **Real gate delays** (combinational + sequential)
2. **Wire delays** (RC parasitic extraction)
3. **Clock skew & jitter**
4. **Glitches, races, and hazards**

RTL simulation **cannot detect** any of these. **Only sign-off simulation can.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If your design passes RTL simulation but **fails** in silicon, **70% of post-tapeout bugs are timing-related** (per industry defect analyses). This single statistic is *why* sign-off simulation exists.

---

## 2.2 The Sign-Off Flow — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Netlist Generation:** Synthesized gate-level netlist (`.v` or `.vhdl`) from synthesis tool.
2. **SDF Generation:** Standard Delay Format (`.sdf`) file produced by static timing analyzer with parasitic-aware delays.
3. **Library Preparation:** Sign-off cell library (`.lib`) containing **min/typ/max** timing arcs.
4. **Testbench Reuse:** Same SystemVerilog/UVM testbench used for RTL, re-targeted to gate-level.
5. **SDF Back-Annotation:** Engine reads SDF and applies per-pin/integer delays to the netlist.
6. **Corner Sweep:** Same regression run repeated across all PVT corners.
7. **X-Optimization & Reset Verification:** Specialized checks for unknown propagation and power-on reset behavior.
8. **Coverage Closure:** Functional + code coverage targets must be hit.
9. **Sign-Off Report:** Master document with corner matrix, slack distribution, coverage plots.

---

## 2.3 The Three Pillars of Sign-Off Timing

| Pillar | Definition | KTU Jargon |
|---|---|---|
| **PVT Corners** | Process (FF/SS/TT), Voltage (1.08V/0.9V/0.81V), Temperature (-40°C / 25°C / 125°C) | "Process-Voltage-Temperature variations" |
| **OCV / AOCV** | On-Chip Variation / Advanced OCV — accounts for intra-die delay mismatch | "Derating factors" |
| **SI / Noise** | Signal Integrity — crosstalk, delta-delay, glitches on victim nets | "Crosstalk delta-delay" |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exam-grade formula sheet** for Module 1 Sign-Off. Use `\vert` for absolute values (NOT the pipe symbol) to keep tables intact.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Setup Time Check (Launch-Edge) | $T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + T_{skew}$ | Must hold for **all** paths to **all** corners | ps, ns |
| 2 | Hold Time Check (Same-Edge) | $T_{cq} + T_{comb} \geq T_{hold} + T_{skew}$ | Must hold for **all** paths to **all** corners | ps, ns |
| 3 | Slack (Setup) | $\text{Slack}_{setup} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su} + T_{skew})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ (sign-off: typically $\geq +50$ ps) | ps |
| 4 | Slack (Hold) | $\text{Slack}_{hold} = (T_{cq} + T_{comb}) - (T_{hold} + T_{skew})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ | ps |
| 5 | OCV Derating (Setup) | $T_{derated} = T_{nominal} \times (1 + \delta_{ocv})$ | $\delta_{ocv} \in [0.02,\,0.10]$ typical | dimensionless |
| 6 | OCV Derating (Hold) | $T_{derated} = T_{nominal} \times (1 - \delta_{ocv})$ | Used pessimistically for hold checks | dimensionless |
| 7 | SDF Back-Annotation Delay | $D_{annotated} = D_{cell} + D_{net} + D_{ocv}$ | Per-pin delay applied at simulation time | ps |
| 8 | Effective Clock Period (Worst) | $T_{clk,eff} = T_{clk} - T_{jitter} - T_{skew,max} - T_{setup,margin}$ | Net available time for logic | ns |
| 9 | Glitch Width (Crosstalk) | $W_{glitch} = \Delta V \cdot C_{victim} / I_{driver}$ | Must be $< T_{hold}$ of next stage to be harmless | ps |
| 10 | X-Propagation Probability | $P_X = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P_{X,i}$ | Probability that an X survives $N$ gates | dimensionless |
| 11 | Functional Coverage Ratio | $C_f = \dfrac{\text{Hit Bins}}{\text{Total Bins}}$ | Sign-off threshold: $C_f \geq 0.95$ | ratio |
| 12 | Code Coverage (Line) | $C_{line} = \dfrac{\text{Executed Lines}}{\text{Total Lines}}$ | Sign-off threshold: $C_{line} \geq 0.98$ | ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table breaks the table renderer. In KTU answer sheets, simply write $x$ with the word "absolute value" or use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX, but **never** the bare pipe character.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs (Apple, Qualcomm):** Sign-off is run across **>100 PVT corners** before tapeout.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Functional safety requires sign-off at **ASIL-D** with redundant cross-checking.
- **Aerospace / Defense:** Radiation-hardened designs add **SEU (Single Event Upset)** corners.
- **IoT / Ultra-Low-Power:** Multi-Voltage sign-off is mandatory (DVFS-aware timing closure).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Setup-Time Slack Equation for Sign-Off

We derive the canonical setup-time check used in *every* sign-off timing report.

### Step 1 — Define the Path

Consider a single-bit data path launching at FF (Flip-Flop) **FF1** and capturing at FF **FF2**, clocked by the same clock domain `CLK` with period $T_{clk}$.

Let:
- $T_{cq}$ = Clock-to-Q delay of FF1
- $T_{comb}$ = Total combinational delay (cells + wires)
- $T_{su}$ = Setup time requirement of FF2
- $T_{skew}$ = Clock skew between FF2 and FF1 ($T_{skew} = T_{clk\_FF2} - T_{clk\_FF1}$)
- $T_{jitter}$ = Cycle-to-cycle clock jitter

### Step 2 — Identify the Critical Launch-Capture Edge Pair

The data launched by FF1 at edge $n$ must arrive at FF2 **strictly before** the capturing edge $n+1$ minus the setup time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Launch Time at FF1 output} &= n \cdot T_{clk} + T_{cq} \\
\text{Arrival Time at FF2 input} &= n \cdot T_{clk} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} \\
\text{Required Time at FF2 (capture edge)} &= (n+1) \cdot T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Inequality

For **correct capture** we need:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Arrival Time at FF2} &\leq \text{Required Time at FF2} \\
n \cdot T_{clk} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} &\leq (n+1) \cdot T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

Subtract $n \cdot T_{clk}$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cq} + T_{comb} &\leq T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Account for Jitter (Sign-Off Refinement)

Subtract $T_{jitter}$ pessimistically from the right-hand side (jitter only hurts timing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cq} + T_{comb} &\leq T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} - T_{jitter} - T_{margin}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Define Slack

Rearrange to isolate the **slack** (positive ⇒ timing met, negative ⇒ violation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup Slack} &= \big[T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} - T_{jitter} - T_{margin}\big] - \big[T_{cq} + T_{comb}\big] \\
\text{Setup Slack} &\geq 0 \quad \text{(Sign-off condition)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $T_{margin}$ is a sign-off-only safety buffer (typically **50 ps to 100 ps**) that is **NOT** present in pre-synthesis RTL timing budgets. This is the *quantitative* difference between "RTL works in simulation" and "silicon is guaranteed to work."

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Hold-Time Check with OCV

### Step 1 — Same-Edge Capture

Hold time is checked at the **same** clock edge (no $T_{clk}$ allowed), so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Arrival Time (FF2)} &= T_{cq,FF1} + T_{comb,FF1 \to FF2} \\
\text{Required Time (FF2)} &= T_{skew} + T_{hold,FF2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Hold Inequality

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cq,FF1} + T_{comb} &\geq T_{hold,FF2} + T_{skew}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply OCV Derating (Pessimistic)

For hold, we want the data to arrive **as late as possible** and the clock to arrive **as early as possible**. So we **derate upward** the data path and **derate downward** the clock path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cq,FF1} \cdot (1 + \delta_{ocv}) + T_{comb} \cdot (1 + \delta_{ocv}) &\geq T_{hold,FF2} + T_{skew} \cdot (1 - \delta_{ocv})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Hold Slack

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hold Slack} &= \big[T_{cq,FF1} \cdot (1 + \delta_{ocv}) + T_{comb} \cdot (1 + \delta_{ocv})\big] - \big[T_{hold,FF2} + T_{skew} \cdot (1 - \delta_{ocv})\big] \\
\text{Hold Slack} &\geq 0 \quad \text{(Sign-off condition, ZERO jitter — hold cannot use clock period)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Trap:** Unlike setup, hold checks **DO NOT** benefit from a longer clock period. Many KTU students incorrectly add $T_{clk}$ to hold equations, losing **3 marks** immediately.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (RTL vs Sign-Off)

| Parameter | RTL Simulation | Sign-Off Simulation | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delay Model | Zero / ideal | SDF back-annotated with parasitics | Reveals real timing |
| Library | Generic `&cells` | Sign-off cell lib (`.lib` w/ TT/FF/SS) | Real silicon delays |
| Corners | 1 (typical) | 20+ MMMC | Reveals corner failures |
| X-Handling | Mostly ignored | Actively checked (X-prop, X-merging) | Catches real silicon Xs |
| Reset | Not modeled | Power-On-Reset (POR) modeled | Catches cold-boot bugs |
| Speed | Fast (10x–100x faster) | Slow (huge vectors) | Schedule pressure |
| Testbench | Same UVM | Same UVM, re-targeted | Reusability |

---

## 3.4 X-Propagation: Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a 4-stage ripple carry chain has, at power-up, one register stuck at X (because reset has not yet propagated). Each stage has $P_{X,i} = 0.5$ chance of propagating the X.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X \text{ (after 4 stages)} &= (0.5)^4 = 0.0625 \\
P_X \text{ (after 8 stages)} &= (0.5)^8 = 0.0039 \\
P_X \text{ (after 16 stages)} &= (0.5)^{16} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sign-off verdict:** If 16 stages deep, the X-propagation probability is **0.0015%**, well below the **0.01%** tolerance. If only 4 stages deep, **6.25%** is unacceptable — design must add explicit **reset stitching**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sign-Off Simulation Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[RTL Design and UVM Testbench] --> B1[Synthesis Engine]

    D2[Sign Off Cell Library .lib] --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D3[SDF Delay File .sdf]
    B2 --> E1[Gate Level Simulator GLS]
    D3 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    A0 --> E1
    E1 --> F1{PVT Corner Sweep}
    F1 --> F2[FF Corner Min V Max T]
    F1 --> F3[SS Corner Max V Min T]
    F1 --> F4[TT Corner Nominal]
    F1 --> F5[FS and SF Mixed Corners]
    F2 --> G1[Coverage and X Prop Analysis]
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    F5 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{All Slacks Non Negative and Coverage Above 95 Percent}
    H1 -->|Yes| I1[SIGN OFF APPROVED]
    H1 -->|No| J1[ECO Engineering Change Order]

```

