<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

### **Q1. Define sign-off simulation. Why is it considered the "final gate" before tapeout?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sign-off simulation is the conclusive verification phase that uses silicon-accurate cell libraries, parasitic-extracted delays via SDF back-annotation, and exhaustive PVT corner sweeps to mathematically guarantee the design's correctness, timing, power, and reliability. It is the "final gate" because the design is frozen after this stage — any further modification requires re-running the entire corner matrix, and the sign-off engineer assumes technical responsibility for silicon success. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

### **Q2. List any four differences between RTL simulation and sign-off (gate-level) simulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| # | RTL Simulation | Sign-Off Simulation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zero / ideal delay | SDF-annotated real delays with parasitics |
| 2 | One corner (typical) | 20+ MMMC corners |
| 3 | X-propagation ignored | X-prop, reset, races actively checked |
| 4 | No OCV/SI effects | OCV, AOCV, crosstalk delta-delay modeled |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Module 1, Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat timing diagram, derive the **setup-time slack** expression for a single-clock-domain flip-flop-to-flip-flop path. Include clock jitter, clock skew, and a sign-off safety margin in your derivation. Clearly state the sign-off condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A register-to-register path in a 28 nm SoC has the following parameters: $T_{clk} = 1.0$ ns, $T_{cq} = 120$ ps, $T_{comb} = 580$ ps, $T_{su} = 90$ ps, $T_{skew} = +30$ ps, $T_{jitter} = 50$ ps, $T_{margin} = 50$ ps. Compute the **setup slack** and determine whether the design **passes sign-off** for this corner. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Path identification:** Launch FF1 captures FF2; same clock domain.

**Step 2 — Arrival/required times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{arrival} &= n \cdot T_{clk} + T_{cq} + T_{comb} \\
t_{required} &= (n+1) \cdot T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} - T_{jitter} - T_{margin}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply inequality $t_{arrival} \leq t_{required}$ and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cq} + T_{comb} &\leq T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} - T_{jitter} - T_{margin}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Slack definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup Slack} = \big[T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} - T_{jitter} - T_{margin}\big] - \big[T_{cq} + T_{comb}\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sign-off condition:** Setup Slack $\geq 0$ (ideally $\geq +50$ ps margin). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:** $T_{clk} = 1000$ ps, $T_{cq} = 120$ ps, $T_{comb} = 580$ ps, $T_{su} = 90$ ps, $T_{skew} = +30$ ps, $T_{jitter} = 50$ ps, $T_{margin} = 50$ ps.

**Substitute into the slack formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup Slack} &= \big[1000 + 30 - 90 - 50 - 50\big] - \big[120 + 580\big] \\
&= \big[840\big] - \big[700\big] \\
&= +140~\text{ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** Setup Slack $= +140$ ps, which is **non-negative** and **above the +50 ps sign-off safety margin**. **The design PASSES sign-off** for this corner. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain **SDF back-annotation** in detail. Discuss its role in gate-level sign-off simulation and the three types of delays it conveys. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8-bit counter has a known **reset-stuck-at-X** fault in bit[3]. If the X-propagation probability per stage is 0.4, calculate the probability that an unresolved X reaches the MSB output. If the sign-off X-tolerance is 0.5%, comment on whether a **reset-stitching** ECO is required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**SDF (Standard Delay Format) back-annotation** is the mechanism by which timing information computed by a static timing analyzer (post-layout, with parasitics) is fed back into a gate-level simulator.

**Three types of delays conveyed by SDF:**

1. **IOPATH delays** — combinational propagation delay from input pin to output pin of a cell. (2 marks)
2. **Interconnect delays** — net delays from the SPEF (Standard Parasitic Exchange Format) file, representing RC wire delays. (2 marks)
3. **Setup/Hold/Recovery/Removal checks** — sequential timing checks for flip-flops and latches. (3 marks)

**Role in sign-off:** SDF back-annotation is what transforms a **purely logical** gate-level simulation into a **timing-accurate** one, enabling the detection of races, glitches, hold violations, and X-propagation that RTL simulation cannot see. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the number of stages from bit[3] to MSB (bit[7]).** Number of stages $N = 7 - 3 = 4$ (bits 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7).

**Step 2 — Compute X-propagation probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X &= (0.4)^4 \\
&= 0.0256 \\
&= 2.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to sign-off tolerance of 0.5%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_X = 2.56\% \;&>\; 0.5\% \quad (\text{tolerance})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** Since $2.56\% > 0.5\%$, the X-propagation **violates** the sign-off tolerance. A **reset-stitching ECO is REQUIRED** to insert explicit reset buffering into the ripple chain so that each bit is independently and deterministically cleared before the counter enters functional mode. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting $T_{jitter}$ and $T_{margin}$** in setup slack: 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Adding $T_{clk}$ to hold equations**: 3-mark loss — hold is same-edge.
> 3. **Confusing OCV direction** (data late vs clock early): 1-mark loss per mistake.
> 4. **Writing absolute value $\vert x \vert$ in tables**: breaks presentation, -0.5 mark.
> 5. **Stating "RTL simulation is sufficient"**: this is *the* cardinal sign-off sin — 2-mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Sign-Off Simulation**

- **Definition:** Final verification with silicon-accurate models, parasitics, and PVT corners; last gate before tapeout.
- **Three Pillars:** PVT corners + OCV/AOCV + Signal Integrity / Crosstalk.
- **Sign-Off Equation (Setup):** $T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} - T_{jitter} - T_{margin} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb}$
- **Sign-Off Equation (Hold):** $T_{cq} + T_{comb} \geq T_{hold} + T_{skew}$ (same edge, no period).
- **OCV Direction:** Derate **up** the data path for both setup and hold; derate **down** the clock path for hold.
- **SDF Back-Annotation:** IOPATH + interconnect + sequential checks. Requires SPEF parasitic data.
- **GLS Workflow:** Same UVM testbench, re-targeted to gate-level netlist, with SDF applied.
- **X-Propagation:** $P_X = \prod P_{X,i}$; tolerance ≤ 0.01%; require reset-stitching ECO if violated.
- **Coverage Targets:** Functional ≥ 95%, Code ≥ 98%.
- **MMMC Corners:** 20+ scenarios; SS/FF/TT process × min/typ/max voltage × -40/25/125 °C.
- **Sign-Off Decision:** All slacks ≥ 0 with margin + coverage met + X-clean + reset clean ⇒ **APPROVED**.
- **Failure Path:** ECO → Re-synthesis → Re-place-and-route → Re-extraction → **Re-run full sign-off**.
- **KTU Exam Mantra:** Always mention *corner*, *margin*, *SDF*, and *silicon-accuracy* when writing about sign-off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate Level Simulation with Unit-Delay Timing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gate Level Simulation with Unit-Delay Timing**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gate-Level Simulation (GLS)** is a digital simulation methodology in which a circuit is modeled as an interconnection of primitive logic gates (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR, DFF, etc.) derived from a synthesized **structural netlist**. Each gate is evaluated individually based on the logical values at its inputs, and the resulting output is propagated through the circuit under a specific **delay model**.

**Unit-Delay Timing Model** is the simplest non-trivial timing abstraction in which every gate in the circuit is assigned an identical propagation delay of **exactly one (1) time unit** (typically denoted as **1 Δ**, **1 ns**, or **1 tᵤ**), irrespective of gate type, fan-out, capacitive loading, or physical routing. The output of any gate at simulation time *t* is computed based on the inputs that were stable at time *(t − 1)*.

Mathematically, for a generic gate *G* with inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$:

$$Y_G(t) = f\big(x_1(t - 1),\; x_2(t - 1),\; \dots,\; x_n(t - 1)\big)$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the boolean function of the gate and the lag of **1 time unit** is the *unit delay*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unit-delay simulation is positioned between **zero-delay** (purely functional, no timing) and **real-delay** (back-annotated SDF-based) simulation. It is the canonical teaching model for understanding event-driven simulation, hazard/glitch detection, and the foundations of fault simulation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine a **relay race in a stadium**:

- Each runner is a **logic gate**.
- Every runner, no matter how tall or fast, is mandated to take **exactly one second** to complete their leg of the race.
- The baton is the **logic signal** (a `0` or `1`).
- The moment the baton arrives at a runner, they hold it for one second, then pass it forward.

In this analogy:
- A **zero-delay model** would be teleportation — the baton moves instantly.
- A **real-delay model** would let each runner take a different time based on stamina.
- **Unit-delay** is the disciplined, fair system: **everyone gets exactly 1 second**. Even if runner A could finish faster, they wait so that the *temporal ordering* of events becomes observable.

This simple rule lets us catch subtle timing bugs (glitches, hazards, race conditions) that zero-delay simulation would silently miss.

---

## 1.3 Why Unit-Delay? The Engineering Rationale

| Need in VLSI Design | How Unit-Delay Helps |
|---|---|
| Early RTL sign-off before layout | Provides a **first-order timing estimate** without parasitic extraction |
| Detection of logic hazards/glitches | The 1-unit lag creates a time window during which transient pulses can be observed |
| Functional verification of synthesized netlist | Confirms **gate-level equivalence** with the RTL behavior |
| Foundation for event-driven simulation | Forces a **scheduled, time-ordered evaluation** instead of combinational flattening |
| Educational clarity | Students can **trace waveforms tick-by-tick** without complex SDF annotation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Time Unit (Δ):** In academic and KTU-board contexts, the smallest simulation tick is denoted **Δ** (delta). In unit-delay mode, every gate consumes **1 Δ** to evaluate. Hence a 4-gate combinational path consumes **4 Δ** end-to-end.

---

## 1.4 Position in the Verification Flow

The VLSI verification pyramid places Gate-Level Simulation **after RTL simulation** and **before post-layout gate-level simulation with SDF back-annotation**:

```
       ┌─────────────────────────┐
       │   System-Level / TLM    │   ← highest abstraction
       ├─────────────────────────┤
       │   RTL Simulation        │   ← cycle-accurate, no gate delays
       ├─────────────────────────┤
       │ ► Gate-Level Simulation │   ← unit-delay / zero-delay / SDF
       ├─────────────────────────┤
       │   Transistor-Level (SPICE) │ ← lowest abstraction
       └─────────────────────────┘
```

In **Module 1**, the focus is the gate-level layer, and **unit-delay** is the simplest gate-level timing assumption to analyse mathematically and algorithmically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Delay Models Compared

| Property | Zero-Delay | **Unit-Delay** | Real-Delay (SDF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delay per gate | **0 Δ** | **1 Δ (uniform)** | Variable (per cell, per net) |
| Timing awareness | ✗ None | ✓ First-order | ✓ Accurate |
| Hazard/glitch detection | ✗ Cannot detect | ✓ **Can detect static & dynamic hazards** | ✓ Can detect |
| Computational cost | Lowest | Low | Highest |
| SDF back-annotation | Not required | Not required | Required |
| Primary use | Combinational equivalence | **Educational, early functional check, hazard analysis** | Sign-off, timing closure |
| Output value at time *t* | $Y_G(t) = f(x(t))$ | $Y_G(t) = f(x(t-1))$ | $Y_G(t) = f(x(t-d_G))$ where $d_G \in \mathbb{R}^+$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick Question:** *"In unit-delay simulation, can a combinational circuit's output change in the same time step as its input?"* — **Answer: No.** By definition there is a 1-Δ lag, so the earliest observable output change is at **t+1**.

---

## 2.2 Underlying Algorithmic Principles

### 2.2.1 Event-Driven Simulation Paradigm

Gate-level simulation of any non-trivial VLSI circuit cannot afford to re-evaluate every gate at every time step (this is the naive **levelised compiled simulation** approach). Instead, the modern and KTU-recommended approach is **event-driven simulation**:

1. **Event** = any change in logic value at a net or gate output.
2. Maintain an **Event Queue (EQ)** keyed by simulation time.
3. At each time tick *t*, dequeue all events scheduled for *t* and update the corresponding nets.
4. For every gate whose input changed, **schedule an evaluation event** at time *t + delay_of_gate*.
5. Repeat until the EQ is empty (steady state).

In the unit-delay case, `delay_of_gate = 1` for **every** gate, so the algorithm simplifies beautifully.

### 2.2.2 Formal Algorithm (Pseudocode Outline)

```text
INITIALISE  :  set t = 0; load PI values; schedule gate fanouts at t+1
LOOP until t > T_END and EQ empty:
    EQ ← dequeue all events with timestamp == t
    FOR each event e in EQ:
        update net value at e.node
        FOR each gate g fed by e.node:
            recompute g.output based on g.inputs (snapshot at t)
            IF g.output != previous_value:
                enqueue (g.fanout_nodes, t + 1)  // unit delay
```

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase *"snapshot at t"* is critical — gate inputs are read as they were at the **start** of time *t*, before any updates scheduled at *t* are applied (this avoids re-evaluating with partially-updated inputs within the same delta).

---

## 2.3 Hazard and Glitch Detection — The Real Power of Unit-Delay

A **hazard** is an unwanted transient pulse in a combinational circuit caused by unequal path delays. Unit-delay makes path delays *visible* (each path = *k* gates = *k* Δ), so different paths produce different arrival times at the gate output.

### 2.3.1 Static-1 Hazard (Classical Example)

Consider $F = AB + \overline{A}C$ with $B = C = 1$. As *A* falls `1 → 0`:

- Path 1 (AB): A falls → AB falls after **1 Δ**.
- Path 2 ($\overline{A}C$): A falls → $\overline{A}$ rises → $\overline{A}C$ rises after **2 Δ**.

Between t=1 and t=2, the OR-gate sees *both* terms as 0, producing a **glitch of width 1 Δ**.

Under **zero-delay**, this glitch is invisible — the output remains constantly 1.
Under **unit-delay**, the glitch is correctly observed and can be flagged as a design concern.

### 2.3.2 Formal Hazard Condition

A static hazard exists at output *Y* if, when only one input $x_i$ changes (with all others held at values that should keep *Y* constant), the Boolean difference is non-zero:

$$\frac{\partial Y}{\partial x_i}\bigg|_{x_j = \text{const}} \neq 0$$

In unit-delay terms, this manifests as a temporary value of $\overline{Y_{expected}}$ for one or more delta cycles.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generic gate output at time *t* | $Y_G(t) = f\!\left(x_1(t - d_G),\; \dots,\; x_n(t - d_G)\right)$ | $d_G$ = gate delay |
| 2 | **Unit-delay output** | $Y_G(t) = f\!\left(x_1(t - 1),\; \dots,\; x_n(t - 1)\right)$ | $d_G = 1\;\Delta$ for **every** gate |
| 3 | Zero-delay output | $Y_G(t) = f\!\left(x_1(t),\; \dots,\; x_n(t)\right)$ | No lag, purely combinational |
| 4 | Real-delay output | $Y_G(t) = f\!\left(x_1(t - d_G),\;\dots\right)$, $d_G \in \mathbb{R}^+$ | Per-cell SDF value |
| 5 | Boolean difference (hazard detector) | $\dfrac{\partial Y}{\partial x_i} = Y(x_i=1)\oplus Y(x_i=0)$ | $= 1$ ⇒ path-dependent behaviour |
| 6 | Glitch width (in Δ) | $w_g = \vert d_{\text{long}} - d_{\text{short}} \vert$ | Difference of path delays |
| 7 | Path delay along *k* gates | $d_{\text{path}} = k \cdot 1\Delta = k\Delta$ | In unit-delay model |
| 8 | Critical-path delay | $d_{\text{crit}} = \max_{\text{all paths}} (k \cdot \Delta)$ | Determines worst-case latency |
| 9 | Event-queue size (worst-case) | $\vert EQ \vert_{\max} \leq N_{\text{gates}} \cdot N_{\text{cycles}}$ | For *N* gates, *T* cycles |
| 10 | Fanout-weighted work (event-driven) | $W = \sum_{t=0}^{T} (\text{number of events at } t)$ | Used for complexity analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the formula table above, absolute value is written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ (rendered with `\vert`) **inside** the LaTeX math mode. When the same notation appears in plain-text markdown cells, write it as `abs(...)` or as vertical bars surrounded by spaces to avoid breaking the table layout.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Industry

| Industry Use Case | Role of Unit-Delay GLS |
|---|---|
| **Pre-layout RTL-to-gate equivalence check** | Verifies synthesis preserved RTL behaviour with a quick timing-aware pass |
| **DFT / Stuck-At fault simulation** | Each test pattern can be evaluated with a known, deterministic timing model |
| **Static-timing approximation** | A coarse upper bound on path delays when SDF is unavailable |
| **Power-estimation seed** | Toggle counts from unit-delay simulation feed early power models |
| **Pedagogical simulator kernels** | Foundation of every university-level digital simulator (e.g., the classic *N2T* simulator family) |

---

## 2.6 Advantages & Limitations of Unit-Delay Model

### Advantages
- **Conceptually simple** — one rule, one parameter.
- **Deterministic** — same input pattern always produces the same waveform.
- **Detects static & dynamic hazards** — major advantage over zero-delay.
- **Cheap to compute** — no SDF parsing, no per-cell delay libraries.
- **Excellent didactic tool** — students can hand-trace small circuits tick-by-tick.

### Limitations
- **Ignores real loading effects** — a NAND gate and a 16-input mux are both "1 Δ".
- **Cannot detect critical-path violations** — a 30-gate path and a 5-gate path appear proportionally different from reality.
- **Glitch widths are quantised** — all glitches are integer multiples of Δ; sub-Δ pulses are invisible.
- **Misleading for high-speed designs** — at GHz frequencies, lumping all delays as 1 Δ loses essential margins.
- **Not adequate for sign-off** — real SDF-based timing simulation is mandatory for tape-out.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Assuming unit-delay = "approximately correct for the real circuit." It is **not**. It is a *modelling abstraction* with strict, uniform semantics. Always declare the delay model explicitly in any simulation report.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Formal Output Equation Under Unit-Delay

We start from the generic discrete-time digital model. Let $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ be the input signals to a gate *G* with boolean function $f(\cdot)$ and propagation delay $d_G \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ (measured in delta units).

### Step 1 — Generic Discrete-Time Gate Equation

The output $Y_G$ at simulation time *t* depends on the **delayed** versions of the inputs:

$$
Y_G(t) \;=\; f\!\big(x_1(t - d_G),\; x_2(t - d_G),\; \dots,\; x_n(t - d_G)\big)
$$

This is the **fundamental discrete-time gate equation**, applicable to zero-, unit-, and real-delay models alike. The only thing that changes between models is the value of $d_G$.

### Step 2 — Specialisation to Zero-Delay

Setting $d_G = 0$:

$$
Y_G(t) \;=\; f\!\big(x_1(t),\; x_2(t),\; \dots,\; x_n(t)\big)
$$

The output reacts **instantaneously** to any input change at the *same* time step.

### Step 3 — Specialisation to Unit-Delay

Setting $d_G = 1$:

$$
Y_G(t) \;=\; f\!\big(x_1(t - 1),\; x_2(t - 1),\; \dots,\; x_n(t - 1)\big)
$$

The output at *t* depends on the input snapshot taken at *t − 1*.

### Step 4 — Specialisation to Real (Per-Cell) Delay

For each gate *i*, $d_{G_i} = d_i$ where $d_i$ comes from the SDF file:

$$
Y_{G_i}(t) \;=\; f\!\big(x_1(t - d_i),\; x_2(t - d_i),\; \dots,\; x_n(t - d_i)\big)
$$

The integers $d_i$ are replaced by real numbers read from the standard delay format file.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Tracing a 2-Gate Circuit Under Unit-Delay

### Circuit Definition
A two-stage circuit: $\text{NOT}(A) \to \text{AND}(B, \cdot)$ producing $Y$. Inputs: $A, B$.

### Logical Function
$$
\overline{A}(t-1) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AND}\;\longrightarrow\; Y(t)
$$

$$
Y(t) \;=\; B(t-1)\;\wedge\;\overline{A(t-1)}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
Y(t) \;=\; B(t-1)\;\wedge\;\overline{A(t-1)}
$$

### Step-by-Step Trace

Let the input pattern be:
- $A: 0 \to 1$ at $t = 2$
- $B = 1$ (constant)

| Time *t* | $A(t)$ | $B(t)$ | $\overline{A}(t)$ (after 1 Δ) | $Y(t)$ (after 1 more Δ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | — (init) | — (init) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **1** | — |
| 2 | **1** | 1 | 1 | **1** (was —) |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | **0** | 1 |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **0** |

**Reading the table:**
- At *t = 2*, $A$ flips to 1.
- At *t = 3*, the NOT output reflects the *t = 2* input → $\overline{A} = 0$.
- At *t = 4*, the AND reflects its *t = 3* inputs ($B=1, \overline{A}=0$) → $Y = 0$.

So the input change at *t = 2* propagates to the output at *t = 4*, i.e., a total latency of **2 Δ** (one Δ per gate). This confirms the rule: **path delay = number of gates in the path × 1 Δ**.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Static-1 Hazard in $F = AB + \overline{A}C$

### Step 1 — Identify the Static Condition
Set $B = C = 1$. Then:

$$
F = A \cdot 1 + \overline{A}\cdot 1 = A + \overline{A} = 1
$$

So *F* should remain statically at **1**.

### Step 2 — Realise as a 2-Level Gate Netlist
- **AND1** computes $AB$.
- **NOT** computes $\overline{A}$.
- **AND2** computes $\overline{A}C$.
- **OR** computes $AB + \overline{A}C$.

Each gate has unit delay.

### Step 3 — Apply Input Change
At $t = 0$: $A = 1, B = 1, C = 1$.

Stable values:
- $\text{AND1}(0) = 1\cdot 1 = 1$
- $\text{NOT}(0) = 0$
- $\text{AND2}(0) = 0 \cdot 1 = 0$
- $F(0) = 1 + 0 = 1$

At $t = 1$: $A$ falls to 0. Gates evaluate based on **inputs at $t = 0$** (which were still the old values), so $F(1) = 1$ (no change yet).

At $t = 2$: Now gates fed by *A* see the new value.
- $\text{AND1}(2) = A(1)\cdot B(1) = 0\cdot 1 = 0$
- $\text{NOT}(2) = \overline{A(1)} = \overline{0} = 1$
- $\text{AND2}(2) = \overline{A(1)}\cdot C(1) = 1\cdot 1 = 1$  ← one Δ later
- $F(2) = \text{AND1}(1) + \text{AND2}(1) = 1 + 0 = 1$ (using the *t = 1* values of AND1, AND2)

At $t = 3$:
- AND1(3) = $A(2)\cdot B(2) = 0\cdot 1 = 0$
- AND2(3) = $\overline{A(2)}\cdot C(2) = 1\cdot 1 = 1$
- $F(3) = \text{AND1}(2) + \text{AND2}(2) = 0 + 1 = 1$

**Hmm** — the trace above is not quite right because the OR gate sees inputs at different times. Let me redo this more carefully using event scheduling.

### Step 3 (Corrected) — Event-Driven Trace with Explicit EQ

Initial state at $t = 0$: $A=1, B=1, C=1$.

| Δ | Event Source | New Value | Downstream Schedule |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | (initial) | AND1=1, NOT=0, AND2=0, F=1 | — |
| 1 | PI: A→0 | AND1 scheduled at t+1, NOT scheduled at t+1, AND2 scheduled at t+1 | @ t=1 |
| 1 | (eval) | AND1(1)=1, NOT(1)=0, AND2(1)=0, F(1)=1 | OR scheduled @ t=2 |
| 2 | (eval) | AND1(2)=0, NOT(2)=1, AND2(2)=1, F(2)=1 | (no change at OR inputs) |
| 2 | (eval OR) | OR sees AND1=1 (from t=1), AND2=0 (from t=1) → F=1 | stable |

The hazard becomes visible only if we look at the **AND2 output trace carefully**: AND2 first reflects A=1 (giving 0), then one Δ later reflects A=0 (giving 1). The OR-gate sees:
- AND1(t=1)=1, AND2(t=1)=0 → F(t=2)=1 (stable, no glitch here because AND1 was still 1).

The glitch becomes visible if the **paths are unbalanced** so that AND1 goes to 0 *before* AND2 goes to 1. Let us retune the example with a deliberately long path on $\overline{A}C$.

### Step 4 — Asymmetric Path (Insert an Extra Buffer)

Suppose AND2 is preceded by a buffer on $\overline{A}$ — so the $\overline{A}C$ path is 2 gates long (NOT + AND2), while $AB$ path is 1 gate long. Now:

| Δ | AND1 (= $AB$) | NOT ($\overline{A}$) | AND2 (= $\overline{A}C$) | OR (= F) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | **0** | 1 | **1** | **1** |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **0 ← GLITCH!** |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **1** |

At Δ=3, OR sees AND1(t=2)=0 and AND2(t=2)=1 — wait, that's still 1. Let me redo more carefully:

The OR at time *t* uses inputs from time *t−1*. So:
- $F(2) = \text{AND1}(1) + \text{AND2}(1) = 1 + 0 = 1$ ✓
- $F(3) = \text{AND1}(2) + \text{AND2}(2) = 0 + 1 = 1$ ✓ (no glitch here either, in this balanced case)

To get a **glitch**, the OR must see *both* terms as 0 simultaneously. That requires AND1's falling edge and AND2's rising edge to coincide at the OR's input slot. This happens when the **paths are imbalanced by exactly the right amount** — which is exactly what unit-delay analysis reveals.

### Step 5 — Glitch-Width Formula Derivation

Let $d_1$ = delay of path producing term $T_1$, $d_2$ = delay of path producing term $T_2$, and the OR-gate output is sampled at time $t_{\text{obs}}$.

If $T_1$ falls at $t_1 = t_{\text{PI-change}} + d_1$ and $T_2$ rises at $t_2 = t_{\text{PI-change}} + d_2$, then a static-1 hazard (a momentary 0 at the OR output) occurs **iff**:

$$
t_1 < t_{\text{obs}} < t_2
$$

The **glitch width** observed at the OR output is:

$$
w_g \;=\; t_2 - t_1 \;=\; \vert d_2 - d_1 \vert
$$

In unit-delay with *m* gates on path 1 and *n* gates on path 2 (including the final OR):

$$
w_g \;=\; \vert n - m \vert \cdot \Delta
$$

This matches the formula in the cheat sheet (row 6).

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for Step 5:** If path 1 has 2 gates and path 2 has 4 gates (imbalance of 2), then $w_g = 2\Delta$. If both paths have the same number of gates, $w_g = 0$ — no glitch. This is precisely why balanced logic (e.g., Karnaugh-map minimisation that adds redundant terms) is used to *eliminate* hazards.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Three Delay Models, Three Sample Circuits

| Circuit | Zero-Delay Output | **Unit-Delay Output** | Real-Delay Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inverter, A: 0→1 at t=2 | Y: 0→1 at **t=2** | Y: 0→1 at **t=3** | Y: 0→1 at $t = 2 + d_{\text{inv}}$ |
| 2-stage AND, A changes | Output changes at **t=2** | Output changes at **t=3** (one Δ per gate) | Output changes at $t = 2 + d_{G_1} + d_{G_2}$ |
| $F = AB + \overline{A}C$ with $B=C=1$, $A$ falls | F stays at **1 always** (glitch invisible) | F may show **1 Δ glitch** if paths imbalanced | F shows glitch of width $\vert d_2 - d_1 \vert$ |
| Feedback oscillator (odd inverter chain) | Behaves as combinational loop (undefined) | Behaves as **clocked oscillator** with period $2n\Delta$ | Behaves with real period $2\sum d_i$ |

This matrix makes it absolutely clear why **unit-delay is the minimal model that captures *temporal behaviour* while remaining tractable**.

---

## 3.5 Complexity Analysis of Event-Driven Unit-Delay Simulation

Let $N$ = number of gates, $T$ = number of simulation cycles, $E_t$ = number of events at time *t*.

**Total work** $W$:

$$
W \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{T} E_t
$$

**Average events per cycle** (for a well-distributed workload):

$$
\overline{E} \;=\; \frac{W}{T+1}
$$

**Worst-case work** (every gate toggles every cycle):

$$
W_{\max} \;=\; N \cdot (T+1)
$$

**Space complexity** for the event queue:

$$
S_{EQ} \;=\; O\!\left(\max_t E_t\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Event-driven simulation is **asymptotically optimal** for sparse activity (which is typical in real VLSI designs where only ~1–5% of nets toggle per cycle). This is why every commercial simulator (VCS, ModelSim, Questa) uses event-driven kernels at their core.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Event-Driven Gate-Level Simulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Netlist Parser] --> A1[Internal Data Structures: Gates, Nets, Fanout Lists]
    A1 --> A2[Stimulus Loader: PI Patterns]
    A2 --> A3[Initial Evaluator: t = 0]
    A3 --> A4[Event Queue Manager]
    A4 --> A5{Event at current time t?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Fetch Next Event from EQ]
    A6 --> A7[Update Net Value]
    A7 --> A8[Identify Fanout Gates]
    A8 --> A9[Evaluate Gate Boolean Function]
    A9 --> A10{Output Changed?}
    A10 -- Yes --> A11[Schedule New Event at t + DELAY]
    A11 --> A4
    A10 -- No --> A4
    A5 -- No --> A12{More Time Steps?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Advance t = t + 1]
    A13 --> A4
    A12 -- No --> A14[Generate Waveform / Report]

    classDef defBox fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A56DB,color:#0B2545
    classDef critBox fill:#FFE8E8,stroke:#D7263D,color:#5C0A0A
    classDef evalBox fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#1B7F3A,color:#0F3D1A
    class A4,A11,A12 critBox
    class A9,A10 evalBox
    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A5,A6,A7,A8,A13,A14 defBox
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The red blocks are the *control* elements (event queue and the scheduler decision), the green blocks are the *evaluation* core (boolean function and change-detection), and the blue blocks are the *support infrastructure* (parser, stimulus, waveform output). This is the canonical architecture taught in KTU Module 1.

---

## 4.2 Waveform Diagram — Unit-Delay Trace of a 2-Gate Circuit

```mermaid
gantt
    title Unit-Delay Trace: A → NOT → AND(B) → Y,  with B = 1
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  Δ

    section A (Primary Input)
    A = 0       :a1, 0, 2
    A = 1       :a2, 2, 5

    section NOT output (gate 1)
    NOT = 1     :n1, 1, 3
    NOT = 0     :n2, 3, 5

    section AND output Y (gate 2)
    Y = 1       :y1, 2, 4
    Y = 0       :y2, 4, 5
```

**Interpretation:**
- $A$ transitions at $\Delta = 2$.
- $\overline{A}$ responds one Δ later, at $\Delta = 3$.
- $Y$ responds one further Δ later, at $\Delta = 4$.
- Total propagation latency = **2 Δ** (one per gate), confirming the unit-delay rule.

---

## 4.3 Static Hazard Detection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[Identify output node F] --> H1[Find all PI pairs producing same F statically]
    H1 --> H2[For each pair, change one PI]
    H2 --> H3[Trace F with unit-delay simulation]
    H3 --> H4{F shows transient opposite value?}
    H4 -- Yes --> H5[FLAG: Static Hazard detected]
    H4 -- No --> H6[No hazard on this transition]
    H5 --> H7[Suggest hazard cover term]
    H6 --> H8[Move to next PI pair]

    classDef def fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B85C00,color:#3D1F00
    classDef flag fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#B80000,color:#400000
    class H0,H1,H2,H3,H8 def
    class H4,H5,H7 flag
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Zero-Delay vs Unit-Delay vs Real-Delay Waveform

```mermaid
gantt
    title Waveform Comparison for Single Inverter with A 0→1 at Δ=2
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  Δ

    section Input A
    A = 0        :a0, 0, 2
    A = 1        :a1, 2, 5

    section Zero-Delay Output
    Y0 = 1       :z0, 2, 5

    section Unit-Delay Output
    Y1 = 0       :u0, 0, 3
    Y1 = 1       :u1, 3, 5

    section Real-Delay Output (d = 1.7 Δ)
    YR = 0       :r0, 0, 4
    YR = 1       :r1, 4, 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note how the **zero-delay output** changes at the **same Δ** as the input, the **unit-delay output** changes at the **next integer Δ**, and the **real-delay output** changes at a **fractional Δ** (here, 1.7 Δ → rounds to Δ=4 in this discrete display). This visual asymmetry is exactly what makes unit-delay a useful *coarse-grained* approximation.

---

## 4.5 Data-Structure View of the Event Queue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0[Time Wheel / Sorted List] --> Q1[Slot t=0]
    Q0 --> Q2[Slot t=1]
    Q0 --> Q3[Slot t=2]
    Q0 --> Q4[Slot t=k]

    Q1 --> Q1A[Event: netA changes]
    Q2 --> Q2A[Event: netB changes]
    Q2 --> Q2B[Event: netC changes]
    Q4 --> Q4A[Event: netD changes]

    classDef qslot fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#1E1B4B
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 qslot
```

The event queue is **time-indexed**: events are bucketed by their scheduled simulation tick. A pointer (`current_t`) advances monotonically, dequeueing all events in the current bucket before moving forward. This is *O(1)* amortised per event.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define unit-delay timing model in gate-level simulation. How does it differ from zero-delay simulation?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~80–100 words):**

The **unit-delay timing model** assigns a propagation delay of **exactly one time unit (1 Δ)** to every gate in the circuit, regardless of gate type, loading, or physical implementation. The output of any gate at simulation time *t* is computed from the input values that were stable at time *t − 1*, i.e.:

$$Y_G(t) \;=\; f\!\big(x_1(t-1),\dots,x_n(t-1)\big)$$

In contrast, the **zero-delay model** sets $d_G = 0$, so $Y_G(t) = f(x_1(t), \dots, x_n(t))$ — the output changes in the same time step as the input. Zero-delay cannot detect timing hazards, whereas unit-delay exposes static and dynamic glitches by introducing a deterministic 1-Δ lag across every gate.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *What is event-driven simulation? Why is it preferred over compiled simulation for gate-level netlists?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~80–100 words):**

**Event-driven simulation** is a technique in which only those gates whose inputs have changed (i.e., have an "event") are re-evaluated. Each event is scheduled in a **time-indexed queue** at the current time + gate delay, and the simulator processes the queue chronologically. It is preferred over **compiled (levelised) simulation** because (i) only a small fraction of gates toggle per cycle (typically 1–5%), so event-driven avoids the exponential work of re-evaluating the entire circuit every cycle, (ii) it naturally supports multiple delay models (zero, unit, real), and (iii) it provides precise timing information for hazard and race-condition analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 1]** *Explain gate-level simulation with unit-delay timing model. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and how it detects hazards in combinational circuits.*

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)
**Explain the unit-delay timing model and the event-driven simulation algorithm in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** The unit-delay timing model assigns a uniform propagation delay of 1 Δ to every gate. The output of gate *G* at time *t* is:

$$Y_G(t) = f\!\big(x_1(t-1), x_2(t-1), \dots, x_n(t-1)\big)$$

This means the gate's response to an input change at *t* appears at *t+1*.

2. **Event-driven algorithm (3 marks):** The simulator maintains a time-ordered event queue. At each time step:
   - Dequeue all events at current time *t*.
   - Update the corresponding net values.
   - For each downstream gate, recompute its output.
   - If the output changed, schedule a new event at *t + 1* (the unit delay).
   - Advance time and repeat until the queue is empty.

3. **Example trace (2 marks):** Demonstrate on a small circuit (e.g., NOT feeding an AND). Show that an input change at Δ=2 propagates to the AND output at Δ=4, confirming 2-Δ latency = number of gates.

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)
**With a suitable example, explain how unit-delay simulation detects static hazards. Discuss advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Static hazard example (3 marks):** Use $F = AB + \overline{A}C$ with $B = C = 1$. Draw the gate-level netlist (AND1 = AB, NOT = $\overline{A}$, AND2 = $\overline{A}C$, OR = F). Show that when $A$ falls `1→0`, the path lengths are:
   - $AB$ path: 1 gate → reaches 0 at Δ+1.
   - $\overline{A}C$ path: 2 gates → reaches 1 at Δ+2.

   Construct a time-table showing AND1=0 at Δ+1, AND2 still 0 at Δ+1 but 1 at Δ+2. Demonstrate that the OR-gate momentarily sees (0, 0) → F = 0 for one Δ, producing a **1-Δ glitch**.

2. **Glitch-width formula (2 marks):** $w_g = \vert d_2 - d_1 \vert = \vert n - m \vert \cdot \Delta$ where *m*, *n* are gate counts along the two paths.

3. **Advantages (1 mark):** Detects hazards, deterministic, simple, low cost.
4. **Limitations (1 mark):** Uniform delay is unrealistic, glitches are quantised to integer Δ, no loading/fanout awareness, not sign-off quality.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1]** *Differentiate between zero-delay, unit-delay and real-delay simulation. Explain how the event-driven algorithm processes a given gate-level netlist under unit-delay timing, with a worked example.*

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)
**Differentiate the three delay models in a comparative table and discuss when each is appropriate in the VLSI design flow.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Zero-Delay | **Unit-Delay** | Real-Delay |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delay per gate | 0 Δ | **1 Δ (uniform)** | Per-cell SDF value |
| Timing awareness | None | First-order | Accurate |
| Hazard detection | No | Yes | Yes |
| Computational cost | Lowest | Low | High |
| Stage in flow | RTL/netlist equivalence | **Pre-layout functional + hazard check** | Sign-off / post-layout |

2. **Discussion (3 marks):**
   - **Zero-delay** is appropriate for pure functional verification of combinational equivalence between RTL and gate-level netlist (does the synthesis preserve behaviour?).
   - **Unit-delay** is appropriate in the **pre-layout stage** when SDF back-annotation is unavailable; it provides a *first-order timing view* sufficient to expose hazards and basic race conditions.
   - **Real-delay** is mandatory at **sign-off**; it uses SDF files with per-cell, per-net, per-edge timing arcs.

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)
**Trace the following circuit under unit-delay simulation. Input pattern: $A: 0 \to 1 \to 0$ at Δ = 2 and 5, $B = 0$ constant.**

Circuit: $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ (i.e., NAND of A and B).

**Model Solution:**

1. **Function identification (1 mark):** $Y(t) = \overline{A(t-1) \cdot B(t-1)}$.

2. **Initialisation (1 mark):** At Δ = 0: $A = 0, B = 0$. NAND output = $\overline{0 \cdot 0} = 1$.

3. **Event-driven trace table (4 marks):**

| Δ | $A$ | $B$ | NAND input snapshot | $Y$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | — | (init) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | $A(0)B(0) = 0\cdot 0 = 0$ | **1** |
| 2 | **1** | 0 | $A(1)B(1) = 0\cdot 0 = 0$ | 1 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | $A(2)B(2) = 1\cdot 0 = 0$ | **1** (no change yet) |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | $A(3)B(3) = 1\cdot 0 = 0$ | **1** (still) |
| 5 | **0** | 0 | $A(4)B(4) = 1\cdot 0 = 0$ | 1 |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | $A(5)B(5) = 0\cdot 0 = 0$ | **1** (stable) |

4. **Observation (1 mark):** Because $B = 0$ throughout, the AND-gate output is always 0 regardless of $A$, so the NAND output stays at 1. The change in $A$ produces **no observable glitch** at $Y$ — the unit-delay trace correctly shows *no spurious toggling*. This validates the simulator's correctness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1-Δ lag**: Writing the gate equation as $Y(t) = f(x(t))$ instead of $Y(t) = f(x(t-1))$ — **deduct 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing evaluation times**: Using *current* time *t* for some gates and *t−1* for others within the same trace — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the event-queue reasoning**: Just writing a final waveform without showing the scheduling steps loses the *process* marks (typically 2–3 marks).
> 4. **Confusing unit-delay with real-delay**: Stating the output changes at $t + d_G$ where $d_G$ varies per gate — **deduct 2 marks** (it must always be $t + 1$).
> 5. **Ignoring hazard detection**: In Q.A(b), failing to mention the glitch-width formula or path-delay analysis — **deduct 1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (must-revise before exam):**

- **Gate-Level Simulation (GLS)** operates on a *structural netlist* of primitive gates.
- The **unit-delay timing model** assigns **1 Δ** to *every* gate uniformly.
- The fundamental equation: $Y_G(t) = f(x_1(t-1), \dots, x_n(t-1))$.
- **Zero-delay** ⇒ $Y(t) = f(x(t))$ — no timing, no hazard detection.
- **Real-delay** ⇒ $Y(t) = f(x(t-d_G))$, $d_G$ from SDF.
- **Event-driven simulation** processes only changed signals via a time-ordered queue.
- Unit-delay **can** detect **static-1, static-0, and dynamic hazards**; zero-delay cannot.
- **Glitch width** $w_g = \vert d_2 - d_1 \vert = \vert n - m \vert \cdot \Delta$ where *n, m* are path gate counts.
- The **path delay** under unit-delay = **number of gates in the path × 1 Δ**.
- The **critical path delay** = $\max_{\text{all paths}}(k\Delta)$ = maximum gate count.
- Event-driven complexity: **O(W)** work where $W = \sum_t E_t$; queue space **O(max $E_t$)**.
- **Advantages**: simple, deterministic, hazard-aware, cheap, didactic.
- **Limitations**: ignores loading, glitches quantised, not sign-off grade, misleading at GHz frequencies.
- **Real-world use**: pre-layout equivalence check, DFT fault-simulation seed, hazard analysis, first-order timing estimate.
- Always **declare the delay model** explicitly in simulation reports.
- The simulator's correctness can be verified on trivial circuits (e.g., NAND with one input stuck at 0 ⇒ output is constant 1, regardless of unit-delay).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate Level Simulation with Full Timing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gate Level Simulation with Full Timing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gate Level Simulation (GLS) with Full Timing** is a highly accurate, event-driven digital logic simulation methodology in which every Boolean primitive (AND, OR, NAND, NOR, XOR, DFF, etc.) from a synthesized netlist is evaluated using **annotated propagation delays** — including **rise delay ($t_{LH}$)**, **fall delay ($t_{HL}$)**, **turn-off delay ($t_{LZ}$, $t_{ZL}$)**, and **min/typ/max timing triplets** — such that the simulator tracks the exact instant (in simulation time units) at which every logic transition must occur.

Unlike behavioral (RTL) simulation, where each statement executes in zero simulated time, **Full Timing GLS** loads an industry-standard **SDF (Standard Delay Format, IEEE 1497)** file and back-annotates per-pin delays onto the gate netlist. The result is a cycle-accurate, waveform-faithful representation of how signals will behave on silicon after fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** "Gate Level Simulation with Full Timing" is a direct mapping to the *delay annotation* and *timing verification* part of the syllabus. It connects logically with **fault simulation** (because full-timing GLS is required to activate and propagate delay-dependent faults such as transition faults and path-delay faults).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an orchestra conductor:

* **Zero-delay simulation** is like reading a score and instantly knowing what every instrument plays — it tells you *what* the music sounds like, but not *when* each note begins or ends.
* **Unit-delay simulation** says "every note starts exactly one beat after the previous one" — a coarse but useful approximation.
* **Full Timing Simulation**, however, is like a **professional symphony rehearsal**: each instrument (gate) has a precise moment to start, a duration to sustain, a known latency, and personal quirks (some start fast, others slow). The conductor (simulator) schedules every event on a wall-clock timeline and listens for glitches (where two flutes accidentally play a dissonant note for 50 ms).

The same logic applies to silicon: full timing GLS tells us not just *whether* a signal transitions from 0 to 1, but *exactly when* it transitions, *how long* it stays there, and *whether transient glitches* occur on intermediate nets.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Full Timing Simulation

* **Simulation Time Unit (STU):** usually **1 ps** in modern tools, **1 ns** in older flows.
* **Event Queue:** the priority queue used to schedule future transitions.
* **X-Propagated:** unknowns are propagated strictly per `xprop` switch (Verilog: `+vlxprop`).
* **Speculative Event:** an event scheduled in the future whose cause might be invalidated by an earlier event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Constants / Assumptions** — full timing simulation assumes:
> 1. The netlist is **glitch-clean at the gate boundary** if all min-delays exceed the inertial threshold.
> 2. SDF back-annotation follows **IEEE 1497** syntax.
> 3. Inertial delay model **rejects pulses shorter than the gate delay** (the pulse-rejection criterion).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Timing Models

Simulation accuracy scales with the complexity of the delay model applied:

| # | Delay Model | Propagation Rule | Use-Case | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Zero Delay** | All transitions occur at $t_{sim}+0$ | RTL functional check | Lowest |
| 2 | **Unit Delay** | Each gate contributes exactly **1 STU** | Quick design sanity | Low |
| 3 | **Nominal Delay** | Single fixed delay per gate (typ value) | Architectural estimate | Medium |
| 4 | **Full Timing** | Rise / Fall / Turn-off delays per pin from SDF | Sign-off, **post-layout verification** | Highest |

### 2.2 Components of a Full Timing Annotation

Each gate in the netlist, when back-annotated, receives the following timing tuples:

* **Rise delay** $t_{LH}$ : transition `0 → 1` propagation time.
* **Fall delay** $t_{HL}$ : transition `1 → 0` propagation time.
* **Turn-off delays** $t_{ZL}$ (Z → L), $t_{LZ}$ (L → Z) — primarily for tri-state buffers.
* **Min/Typ/Max triplet** : captures process-corner envelope (fast / typical / slow).
* **Path delays** : `(IOPATH a y (1.2)(1.5)(2.0))` — specifies min, typ, max per transition.

### 2.3 Transport vs. Inertial Delay Models

The two principal pulse-handling philosophies are:

* **Transport Delay** — every input transition, however brief, propagates faithfully to the output after the specified delay. *Used for wires and continuous assignments.*
* **Inertial Delay** — the gate acts like a low-pass filter; **input pulses shorter than the propagation delay are absorbed (rejected)**. *This is the physical reality of a CMOS gate because the output capacitance cannot respond faster than the drive strength allows.*

The pulse-rejection criterion can be expressed as:

$$
\boxed{\;
P_{in} < t_{pd} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Pulse rejected (inertial model)}
\;}
$$

where $P_{in}$ is the input pulse width and $t_{pd}$ is the propagation delay of the gate.

### 2.4 Event-Driven Simulation Algorithm

Full timing GLS uses an **event-driven, compiled-code or interpreted event-driven algorithm**. The control flow is:

1. Initialise all nets; place `0 → X` or `0 → 1` events at $t = 0$ for primary inputs driven by testbench.
2. Pop the earliest event from the **event wheel / priority queue**.
3. Re-evaluate the gate output. If the new logic value differs from the scheduled value, schedule a new event at $t_{now} + t_{LH}/t_{HL}$.
4. If a previously scheduled event becomes obsolete (because an earlier event overwrites it), cancel or invalidate it (this is the **race-condition check**).
5. Repeat until the event queue is empty.

### 2.5 Hazards Detectable Only in Full Timing GLS

The following real silicon behaviors are *invisible* to zero-delay simulation but are caught by full timing GLS:

* **Static-1 Hazard** : output should remain `1`, but briefly glitches to `0`.
* **Static-0 Hazard** : output should remain `0`, but briefly glitches to `1`.
* **Dynamic Hazard** : output should transition once, but glitches multiple times.
* **Setup / Hold Violation** : DFF data changes too close to the active clock edge.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following table — these formulas are repeat KTU favorites.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Notation | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output transition time | $t_{out} = t_{in} + t_{pd}$ | $t_{pd} = t_{LH}$ or $t_{HL}$ | ns / ps |
| Min-path slack | $T_{slack}^{min} = T_{ck} - (t_{cq} + t_{comb}^{max})$ | setup constraint | ns |
| Max-path slack (hold) | $T_{slack}^{hold} = (t_{cq} + t_{comb}^{min}) - T_{hold}$ | hold constraint | ns |
| Pulse rejection (inertial) | $P_{in} \ge t_{pd}$ | $P_{in}$ = input pulse width | ns |
| Glitch width on net | $W_g = \vert t_{LH}^{branch1} - t_{LH}^{branch2} \vert$ | arrival skew | ns |
| Event-scheduling time | $t_{sched} = t_{now} + t_{delay}$ | per-port SDF delay | ps |
| Setup time check | $t_{data} \ge t_{clk} - T_{setup}$ | DFF timing arc | ns |
| Hold time check | $t_{data} \ge t_{clk} + T_{hold}$ | DFF timing arc | ns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility** : Full timing GLS is the **gate-level sign-off (GLS-SO)** step before tape-out. Major EDA vendors — Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium, Mentor Questa — all run full-timing GLS as part of the "RTL → GLS II equivalence + timing" closure flow. Failing this step is one of the top reasons for silicon re-spins.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Glitch Width on a Reconvergent Net

Consider two paths from input $A$ to output $Y$ of an XOR gate as shown below logically:

$$
A \rightarrow \text{path-1 (delay } t_1 \text{)} \rightarrow X
$$
$$
A \rightarrow \text{path-2 (delay } t_2 \text{)} \rightarrow X
$$
$$
Y = X \oplus A
$$

Assume $A$ transitions from $0 \rightarrow 1$ at simulation time $t = 0$. Path-1 has $t_{LH} = 1.5$ ns, Path-2 has $t_{LH} = 2.0$ ns. We compute the glitch.

**Step 1:** Schedule the transition at $X$ via Path-1.

$$
t_{X}^{(1)} = 0 + 1.5 = 1.5 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Schedule the transition at $X$ via Path-2.

$$
t_{X}^{(2)} = 0 + 2.0 = 2.0 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Step 3:** At $X = 1$ first (from path-1), output $Y = X \oplus A = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$. So $Y$ glitches low.

**Step 4:** At $X = 1$ later (from path-2), no change in $X$, so $Y$ remains driven by the *late* evaluation. The simulator must invalidate the stale $X = 1$ from path-2 and re-evaluate: $Y = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$. Stable.

**Step 5:** Glitch window on $Y$:

$$
W_{glitch}^{Y} = t_{X}^{(2)} - t_{X}^{(1)} = 2.0 - 1.5 = 0.5 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; W_{glitch}^{Y} = 0.5 \;\text{ns}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Setup-Time Margin Calculation (KTU Hot Topic)

Given:
* Clock period $T_{ck} = 10$ ns
* Clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq} = 0.8$ ns
* Combinational max delay $t_{comb}^{max} = 6.5$ ns
* DFF setup time $T_{setup} = 0.4$ ns

**Step 1:** Apply the setup inequality.

$$
t_{cq} + t_{comb}^{max} + T_{setup} \le T_{ck}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numeric values.

$$
0.8 + 6.5 + 0.4 \le 10.0
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the LHS.

$$
0.8 + 6.5 + 0.4 = 7.7 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare.

$$
7.7 \le 10.0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5:** Compute slack.

$$
T_{slack} = T_{ck} - (t_{cq} + t_{comb}^{max} + T_{setup}) = 10.0 - 7.7 = 2.3 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; T_{slack}^{setup} = +2.3 \;\text{ns (timing met)}\;}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Hold-Time Margin Calculation

Given:
* $t_{cq}^{min} = 0.5$ ns
* $t_{comb}^{min} = 1.2$ ns
* $T_{hold} = 0.3$ ns

**Step 1:** Apply hold inequality.

$$
t_{cq}^{min} + t_{comb}^{min} \ge T_{hold}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
0.5 + 1.2 \ge 0.3
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate LHS.

$$
1.7 \ge 0.3 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4:** Hold slack.

$$
T_{slack}^{hold} = (t_{cq}^{min} + t_{comb}^{min}) - T_{hold} = 1.7 - 0.3 = 1.4 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; T_{slack}^{hold} = +1.4 \;\text{ns (no hold violation)}\;}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Event-Wheel Operations

| Event Order at $t_{sim}$ | Net Old Value | Net New Value | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scheduled earlier, fires now | 0 | 1 | Re-evaluate fanout gates, schedule new events |
| Cancelled by newer event | 0 | 0 | Drop from queue (optimisation) |
| Race: two events same time | X | 1 | Apply "last-write-wins" or "winner-stays" rule |
| SDF min-delay triggers | 0 | 1 | Schedule at $t + t_{delay}^{min}$ |
| SDF max-delay triggers | 0 | 1 | Schedule at $t + t_{delay}^{max}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Full-Timing GLS Architectural Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *full* architectural flow from netlist to annotated simulation, including SDF back-annotation, event-driven evaluation, and SDF/SDC sign-off.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Synthesized Gate-Level Netlist .v] --> B0[Static Timing Analysis STA]
    A0 --> C0[SDF Generation from Layout]
    B0 --> D0[SDF File .sdf]
    C0 --> D0
    D0 --> E0[Full Timing GLS Engine]
    A0 --> E0
    F0[Testbench .sv with timing checks] --> E0
    E0 --> G0{Event-driven Evaluator}
    G0 --> H0[Event Wheel Priority Queue]
    H0 --> I0[Re-evaluate Primitive]
    I0 --> J0{New output value?}
    J0 -- Yes --> K0[Schedule New Event at t_now + t_pd]
    J0 -- No --> L0[Drop Event]
    K0 --> H0
    L0 --> H0
    E0 --> M0[VCD or FSDB Waveform Dump]
    M0 --> N0[Coverage + Glitch Analysis]
    N0 --> O0{GLS Sign-off PASS?}
    O0 -- Yes --> P0[Tape-out Release]
    O0 -- No --> Q0[Fix RTL or Constraints - Loop Back]
```

### 4.2 SDF Annotation Flow per Gate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[GATE INV_X1] --> S1[IOPATH A Y 1.5 1.8 2.1]
    G1 --> S2[Cell-rise / fall tables]
    G2[GATE NAND2_X2] --> S3[IOPATH A1 Y 1.2 1.5 1.9]
    G2 --> S4[IOPATH A2 Y 1.3 1.6 2.0]
    S1 --> A1[Annotate Y with rise = 1.5 ns typ]
    S2 --> A2[Annotate Y with fall = 1.8 ns typ]
    S3 --> A3[Annotate Y per input pin]
    S4 --> A4[Annotate Y per input pin]
```

### 4.3 Pulse-Handling Decision Tree (Transport vs Inertial)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Input Pulse of Width P arrives] --> Q0{P compared to t_pd of gate?}
    Q0 -- P greater than t_pd --> R0[Inertial model: PASS through]
    Q0 -- P smaller than t_pd --> S0[Inertial model: REJECT pulse]
    R0 --> T0[Schedule output transition at t_now + t_pd]
    S0 --> U0[Output remains unchanged]
    P0 --> V0[Transport model path]
    V0 --> W0[Regardless of width: schedule transition]
```

### 4.4 Simulation Algorithm Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Initialise: read netlist + SDF + testbench] --> B1[Build event queue EQ]
    B1 --> C1[Pop earliest time t from EQ]
    C1 --> D1[Process all events at time t]
    D1 --> E1[For each event: update net value]
    E1 --> F1[Re-evaluate fanout gate primitives]
    F1 --> G1{Output transition?}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Schedule new event at t + t_pd]
    G1 -- No --> I1[Skip]
    H1 --> J1{EQ empty?}
    I1 --> J1
    J1 -- No --> C1
    J1 -- Yes --> K1[End simulation, dump VCD]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]*
Define **Gate-Level Simulation with Full Timing**. How does it differ from zero-delay simulation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Gate-Level Simulation with Full Timing is a digital logic simulation technique in which every gate of a post-synthesis netlist is evaluated with its **actual back-annotated propagation delays** (rise $t_{LH}$, fall $t_{HL}$, min/typ/max) loaded from an **SDF file**. It produces cycle-accurate waveforms replicating real silicon timing behaviour.

In **zero-delay simulation**, all transitions occur at the same simulation instant, so timing hazards, glitches, and setup/hold violations cannot be observed. Full-timing GLS preserves event ordering, detects hazards, and is mandatory for **gate-level sign-off (GLS-SO)** before tape-out.

*(Award full 3 marks for stating: definition, role of SDF, and timing hazards.)*

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]*
Explain the **inertial delay model** and state the **pulse-rejection criterion** with formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The inertial delay model treats a logic gate as a physical low-pass element: an **input pulse whose width is less than the gate propagation delay $t_{pd}$** is absorbed and does not appear at the output. This models the fact that CMOS output capacitance cannot follow arbitrarily narrow input transitions.

Pulse-rejection criterion:

$$
P_{in} < t_{pd} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Pulse rejected}
$$

*(Award 1.5 marks for the conceptual explanation, 1 mark for the formula, 0.5 mark for the physical CMOS justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper / Dec 2024 — Full Timing GLS]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **event-driven simulation algorithm** used in full-timing gate-level simulation. Discuss how race conditions are handled. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a netlist with two paths from input $A$ to net $X$ (reconverging at an XOR gate whose output is $Y$). Path-1 has rise delay $t_{LH} = 1.5$ ns and Path-2 has $t_{LH} = 2.2$ ns. Input $A$ toggles $0 \rightarrow 1$ at $t = 0$. Calculate the **glitch width on $Y$** and state whether the inertial model rejects it if the XOR delay is $0.6$ ns. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Event-driven simulation algorithm (7 marks):**

1. **Initialisation (1 mark):** Read netlist (.v), SDF (.sdf), testbench. Build event queue EQ as a priority queue keyed on simulation time. Place PI events at $t = 0$.
2. **Main loop (3 marks):** Pop the earliest-time event from EQ. Update the net value. Re-evaluate each fanout gate primitive. If output value changes, schedule a new event at $t_{now} + t_{pd}$ (where $t_{pd}$ comes from SDF: rise/fall tuple).
3. **Race handling (2 marks):** If two events targeting the same net at the same simulation time are scheduled, apply **last-write-wins** (latest event in EQ prevails). Stale events are cancelled to avoid spurious glitches. Modern tools use **accelerated race detection** with X-propagation flags.
4. **Termination:** When EQ is empty and testbench completes, dump VCD/FSDB waveform.

**(b) Glitch calculation (7 marks):**

Given:
* Path-1 delay $t_1 = 1.5$ ns
* Path-2 delay $t_2 = 2.2$ ns
* XOR propagation delay $t_{XOR} = 0.6$ ns
* Input transition $A: 0 \rightarrow 1$ at $t = 0$

**Step 1:** Schedule $X$ transitions.

$$
X \text{ via path-1 at } t = 0 + 1.5 = 1.5 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
X \text{ via path-2 at } t = 0 + 2.2 = 2.2 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $Y$ at $t = 1.5$ ns. $X = 1, A = 1 \Rightarrow Y = 0$.

$$
t_{Y}^{(1)} = 1.5 + 0.6 = 2.1 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Step 3:** At $t = 2.2$ ns, $X$ arrives again but value is still $1$, so $Y$ remains $0$. (XOR re-evaluation: $Y = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$, no transition.) The glitch on $Y$ exists from when $A$ propagates first to $Y$ at $t = 0 + 0.6 = 0.6$ ns (since $Y = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$, transitioning from initial $0$ to $1$) until $X = 1$ arrives and forces $Y = 0$ at $t = 2.1$ ns.

**Step 4:** Glitch width calculation:

$$
W_{glitch}^{Y} = 2.1 - 0.6 = 1.5 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Step 5:** Inertial check on the XOR gate. The glitch pulse on the *input* of XOR (at $A$) is effectively the period between $A$'s arrival and $X$'s arrival causing $Y$ to change. We compare glitch width to $t_{XOR}$:

$$
W_{glitch}^{Y} = 1.5 \;\text{ns} \;\; > \;\; t_{XOR} = 0.6 \;\text{ns}
$$

Since $1.5 > 0.6$, the **inertial model PASSES the glitch through** to output $Y$. The glitch is **propagated** and could cause downstream failure.

$$
\boxed{\; W_{glitch}^{Y} = 1.5 \;\text{ns, propagated by inertial model (no rejection)}\;}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define the **Standard Delay Format (SDF)**. Write a short SDF snippet annotating a 2-input NAND gate with the cell name `NAND2_X1`, input pins `A` and `B`, output pin `Y`, and propagation delays: rise = 1.5 ns, fall = 1.8 ns (typ only). Explain each line. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the timing arc shown — a flip-flop $FF_1$ launches data through combinational logic into $FF_2$ — given clock period $T_{ck} = 8$ ns, $t_{cq} = 0.6$ ns, $t_{comb}^{max} = 5.8$ ns, $T_{setup} = 0.5$ ns, $T_{hold} = 0.3$ ns, $t_{cq}^{min} = 0.4$ ns, $t_{comb}^{min} = 1.0$ ns, calculate the **setup slack** and **hold slack**. Comment on whether the design meets timing. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SDF Definition and Snippet (7 marks):**

SDF (IEEE 1497) is a standard ASCII format used to back-annotate timing data (delays, timing checks, constraints) onto a gate-level netlist from layout-extracted parasitic data.

```sdf
(DELAYFILE
  (CELL
    (CELLTYPE "NAND2_X1")
    (INSTANCE my_block.nand_inst)
    (DELAY
      (ABSOLUTE
        (IOPATH A Y (1.5) (1.5))   ; rise delay
        (IOPATH B Y (1.5) (1.5))   ; rise delay (B path)
        (IOPATH A Y (1.8) (1.8))   ; fall delay
        (IOPATH B Y (1.8) (1.8))   ; fall delay (B path)
      )
    )
  )
)
```

**Explanation of lines:**

* `DELAYFILE` — root SDF construct.
* `CELL` — declares the standard cell.
* `CELLTYPE` — matches the cell name in the library.
* `INSTANCE` — hierarchical path to the instance.
* `DELAY (ABSOLUTE` — uses pin-absolute delay mode.
* `IOPATH A Y (1.5) (1.5)` — min/typ rise delay from $A$ to $Y$ is **1.5 ns**.
* `IOPATH A Y (1.8) (1.8)` — min/typ fall delay from $A$ to $Y$ is **1.8 ns**.

*(Award 2 marks for the snippet, 3 marks for line-by-line explanation, 2 marks for the IEEE 1497 reference and conceptual statement.)*

**(b) Timing Slack Computation (7 marks):**

**Setup slack:**

Inequality: $t_{cq} + t_{comb}^{max} + T_{setup} \le T_{ck}$

$$
0.6 + 5.8 + 0.5 = 6.9 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{slack}^{setup} = T_{ck} - 6.9 = 8.0 - 6.9 = +1.1 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Hold slack:**

Inequality: $t_{cq}^{min} + t_{comb}^{min} \ge T_{hold}$

$$
0.4 + 1.0 = 1.4 \;\text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{slack}^{hold} = 1.4 - 0.3 = +1.1 \;\text{ns}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\; T_{slack}^{setup} = +1.1 \;\text{ns (met)},\quad T_{slack}^{hold} = +1.1 \;\text{ns (met)}\;}
$$

Both setup and hold constraints are satisfied with positive slack — the design **passes timing** under full-timing gate-level simulation. If either slack were negative, it would manifest as a setup or hold violation detectable only by full-timing GLS or STA.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing rise/fall directions.** Rise = `0 → 1`, fall = `1 → 0`. Mark deduction of 0.5–1 per wrong direction.
> 2. **Forgetting the SDF `IOPATH` triplet** — examiners expect ` (min)(typ)(max) ` triplet even if only one value is given (use duplicate). Missing the typ value costs 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Mixing transport and inertial** — always state which model is being applied before evaluating the glitch. Wrong model loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping race-condition discussion** in event-driven algorithm — examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for this; omitting it caps you at 5/7 on part (a).
> 5. **Sign convention on hold slack** — hold slack is computed *after* subtracting $T_{hold}$, not before.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **GLS with Full Timing** = event-driven simulation + SDF-annotated delays + per-pin rise/fall/turn-off timing.
* **SDF format** follows IEEE 1497; uses `IOPATH`, `ABSOLUTE`, `INCREMENT` constructs.
* **Inertial model rejects pulses narrower than $t_{pd}$; transport model passes everything.**
* **Pulse-rejection criterion:** $P_{in} < t_{pd} \Rightarrow$ rejected.
* **Hazards detected only in full-timing GLS:** static-0, static-1, dynamic hazards, glitches, setup/hold violations.
* **Event-driven algorithm:** initialise → pop earliest event → re-evaluate fanout → schedule new events at $t_{now} + t_{pd}$.
* **Race-condition handling:** last-write-wins, speculative-event cancellation, X-propagation flags.
* **Setup slack formula:** $T_{slack}^{setup} = T_{ck} - (t_{cq} + t_{comb}^{max} + T_{setup})$.
* **Hold slack formula:** $T_{slack}^{hold} = (t_{cq}^{min} + t_{comb}^{min}) - T_{hold}$.
* **Glitch width on output:** $W_g = \vert \Delta t_{arrival} \vert$ between reconverging paths.
* **GLS sign-off** is mandatory before tape-out and is the last line of defence against silicon re-spins due to timing failures.
* **KTU-favoured keywords to use in answers:** "back-annotation," "SDF," "event wheel," "race condition," "pulse rejection," "inertial/transport," "IOPATH."
* **Always state the delay model (zero / unit / nominal / full)** explicitly before any computation — examiners award marks for model identification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formal Verification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Formal Verification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Formal Verification — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Formal Verification (FV)** is a systematic, mathematically rigorous methodology used in VLSI design to prove or disprove the correctness of a hardware design with respect to a formally specified set of properties, *without* the need for test vectors or simulation stimuli. It operates by constructing an abstract mathematical model of the design (typically a Finite State Machine, FSM, or a transition system) and exhaustively reasoning about its behavior using formal logic, automata theory, and decision procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Alignment):** *"Formal verification is the process of establishing the functional correctness of a digital design using rigorous mathematical techniques, encompassing model checking, theorem proving, and equivalence checking, by exploring the entire reachable state space of the design."*

The three principal pillars of Formal Verification taught under **PEEVT635** are:
1. **Model Checking** — Property verification on a state-transition model.
2. **Theorem Proving** — Deductive proof using axioms and inference rules.
3. **Equivalence Checking** — Proving two designs are functionally identical.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blueprint Inspector"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you have just built a complex bridge. Instead of driving **10,000 trucks** across it and hoping none collapses (this is **simulation**), you hire a team of mathematicians who, *before a single truck ever crosses*, mathematically **prove** using physics equations that the bridge will NEVER collapse under *any possible* load configuration. **Formal verification is exactly that "mathematical proof" applied to chip designs.**

| Approach | Verification Method | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **Simulation** | Drives specific test vectors | Partial (sample-based) |
| **Formal Verification** | Mathematically explores *all* states | **Exhaustive (100%)** |
| **Emulation/FPGA Prototyping** | Runs at near-real-time speed | Partial / pseudo-exhaustive |

## 1.3 Why Formal Verification? — The Industrial Necessity

Modern SoCs contain **billions of transistors**. As per the **2024 ITRS roadmap**, verification now consumes **70–80%** of total design effort. Traditional dynamic simulation covers only a tiny fraction of corner-case behavior. Formal Verification is the *only* known technique that offers **mathematical completeness** for property verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metrics Used:**
> - **State Space:** 2^n where *n* = number of memory elements (flip-flops/latches).
> - **Coverage:** 100% reachable state coverage for a given property.
> - **Proof Depth (*k*):** Number of clock cycles explored (in Bounded Model Checking).
> - **Cone-of-Influence (COI):** Subset of logic affecting the target property — used to **reduce state-space explosion**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Formal Verification — Structured Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Model Checking

**Why?** Because engineers need to ask *"Will this design EVER violate property P?"* — and simulation can never answer "never" definitively.

**How?** Model checking operates by:
1. Building a **Kripke Structure** M = (S, S₀, R, L) representing the design FSM.
2. Expressing the desired property φ in **Temporal Logic** (CTL or LTL).
3. Exhaustively traversing **all reachable states** to verify M ⊨ φ (M satisfies φ).
4. If violated, returning a **counter-example trace** showing the failure path.

### Pillar 2: Theorem Proving

**Why?** Because some designs (e.g., processors, arithmetic units) are too large for state-space enumeration.

**How?** Uses higher-order logic (HOL), inference rules, and human-guided proofs:
- **Interactive theorem provers:** Coq, Isabelle/HOL, ACL2.
- The engineer provides *tactics/lemmas*; the prover attempts to discharge proof obligations.
- Proof obligations are decomposed using techniques like **High-Order Logic (HOL)** and **Decision Procedures**.

### Pillar 3: Equivalence Checking

**Why?** Because RTL changes (bug-fixes, optimizations) may inadvertently break functionality.

**How?** Proves two designs (typically gate-level vs. RTL, or pre- vs. post-synthesis) implement identical Boolean functions:
- **Combinational Equivalence Checking (CEC):** Uses canonical representations like **OBDD (Ordered Binary Decision Diagrams)**.
- **Sequential Equivalence Checking (SEC):** Uses **inductive reasoning** with cut-points and register correspondence.

## 2.2 Temporal Logic — The Language of Properties

| Logic | Full Name | Key Use Case | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CTL** | Computation Tree Logic | Branching time — "all possible futures" | **Very High** |
| **LTL** | Linear Temporal Logic | Linear time — "this specific execution path" | **High** |
| **PSL** | Property Specification Language | Industry-standard property language | Medium |
| **SVA** | SystemVerilog Assertions | Hardware-oriented assertions | High |

### CTL Operators (Mandatory Memorization)

| Operator | Meaning | English Translation |
|---|---|---|
| **AG p** | For *All* paths, *Globally* p holds | "p is true in every state of every path" |
| **EG p** | There *Exists* a path where *Globally* p holds | "Some path stays true forever" |
| **AF p** | For *All* paths, *Finally* p holds | "p is inevitable on every path" |
| **EF p** | There *Exists* a path where *Finally* p holds | "p is reachable from some path" |
| **AX p** | For *All* paths, *neXt* state satisfies p | "In every next state, p holds" |
| **EX p** | There *Exists* a path, *neXt* p | "Some next state satisfies p" |
| **A[p U q]** | For *All* paths, p *Until* q | "p holds until q becomes true" |
| **E[p U q]** | There *Exists* a path, p *Until* q | "Some path where p holds until q" |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA SHEET** — Memorize these for KTU Board Exams.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total State Space | $\vert S \vert = 2^{n}$ | *n* = # flip-flops |
| 2 | Reachable States | $R \subseteq S$, $\vert R \vert \le 2^{n}$ | Subset of all states |
| 3 | OBDD Size (worst-case) | $O(2^{n})$ | Decision Diagram nodes |
| 4 | Bounded Model Check Depth | $k$ clock cycles | Typically $k \le 60$ for BMC |
| 5 | Unfolding Complexity (BMC) | $O(k \cdot \vert S \vert)$ | Linear in bound, exponential in variables |
| 6 | CTL Model Checking | $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert \phi \vert)$ | Polynomial in model & formula |
| 7 | LTL Model Checking | $O(\vert S \vert \cdot 2^{\vert \phi \vert})$ | Exponential in formula size |
| 8 | Cone-of-Influence Reduction | $COI(\phi) \subseteq V$ | V = all variables |
| 9 | Equivalence Check (CEC) | $f_{1} \equiv f_{2} \iff f_{1} \oplus f_{2} = 0$ | XOR-based proof |
| 10 | Kripke Structure | $M = (S, S_{0}, R, L)$ | S=states, R=transition, L=labeling |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Note:** All vertical bars `|x|` in this sheet are written as `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity. Do NOT confuse reachability analysis complexity with state-space cardinality.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Formal Verification is **industry-standard** in:
- **Intel** — Processor control logic verification (Pentium, Core i-series).
- **IBM** — PowerPC and Z-series mainframe verification.
- **Cadence JasperGold**, **Synopsys VC Formal**, **Mentor Questa Formal** — Commercial EDA tools.
- **Safety-critical domains:** Automotive ISO 26262, Avionics DO-254, Medical IEC 62304 — all mandate formal methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: State-Space Cardinality in Sequential Designs

We want to derive the maximum number of distinct states an FSM can occupy given *n* binary memory elements.

**Step 1 — Each Flip-Flop Definition:**
A single D-flip-flop stores one bit. Its stored value $q_{i} \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step 2 — Independent State Assumption:**
For a design with $n$ flip-flops, each stores an *independent* binary value.

**Step 3 — Counting All Possible Combinations:**
The total number of unique combinations is the Cartesian product:

$$S_{total} = \underbrace{\{0,1\} \times \{0,1\} \times \cdots \times \{0,1\}}_{n \text{ times}}$$

**Step 4 — Cardinality Evaluation:**

$$\vert S_{total} \vert = 2^{n}$$

**Step 5 — Reachability Constraint:**
Not all states may be reachable from the initial state $S_{0}$. Therefore the *reachable* state set satisfies:

$$\vert S_{reachable} \vert \le 2^{n}$$

**Step 6 — Implication for Formal Verification:**
For $n = 64$ flip-flops, $\vert S \vert = 2^{64} \approx 1.844 \times 10^{19}$. This is the **State Space Explosion Problem** — the root motivation for OBDD-based symbolic methods and Cone-of-Influence reduction.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: CTL Model Checking Complexity

We derive the complexity of verifying a CTL formula φ on a Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$.

**Step 1 — Base Case (Atomic Propositions):**
For an atomic proposition $p$, the set of states satisfying it is directly obtained from the labeling function:

$$Sat(p) = \{s \in S \mid p \in L(s)\}$$

**Step 2 — Boolean Operators:**
For $\phi = \phi_{1} \wedge \phi_{2}$:

$$Sat(\phi_{1} \wedge \phi_{2}) = Sat(\phi_{1}) \cap Sat(\phi_{2})$$

This requires $O(\vert S \vert)$ intersection time.

**Step 3 — Temporal Operator EX (Exists Next):**

$$Sat(EX\,\phi) = \{s \in S \mid \exists s' \text{ such that } (s, s') \in R \text{ and } s' \in Sat(\phi)\}$$

Requires traversal of all transitions $R$, taking $O(\vert R \vert)$ time.

**Step 4 — Temporal Operator EG (Exists Globally):**
Computed via a *greatest fixed-point* iteration:

$$\begin{aligned}
X^{0} &= S \\
X^{i+1} &= X^{i} \cap Sat(EX\, \phi) \cap \{s \mid \forall s', (s,s') \in R \Rightarrow s' \in X^{i}\} \\
X^{k+1} &= X^{k} \implies \text{convergence}
\end{aligned}$$

Converges in at most $\vert S \vert$ iterations.

**Step 5 — Overall Complexity:**
Since φ has $\vert \phi \vert$ sub-formulas, each handled in $O(\vert S \vert + \vert R \vert)$:

$$T_{CTL}(M, \phi) = O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert \phi \vert)$$

This is **polynomial** — a key result from Clarke & Emerson (1981).

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalence Checking via OBDD Canonical Form

**Step 1 — Define Two Functions:**
Let $f_{1}(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n})$ be the reference (golden) and $f_{2}(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n})$ be the implementation under test.

**Step 2 — Construct XOR (Miter):**

$$f_{miter}(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}) = f_{1}(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}) \oplus f_{2}(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n})$$

**Step 3 — Canonical Reduction:**
Apply the Shannon decomposition recursively with a *fixed variable ordering* to build the OBDD:

$$f = x_{i} \cdot f_{x_{i}=1} + \overline{x_{i}} \cdot f_{x_{i}=0}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalence Test:**
If $f_{1} \equiv f_{2}$, then:

$$f_{miter} = 0 \text{ (the constant-zero OBDD)}$$

Otherwise, any satisfying assignment $(x_{1}^{*}, \ldots, x_{n}^{*})$ is a **counter-example vector**.

## 3.4 Complexity Comparison Matrix — KTU Exam Favorite

| Method | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Completeness | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simulation** | $O(\text{\#vectors})$ | $O(1)$ | Incomplete | Vector-driven |
| **CTL Model Checking** | $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert \phi \vert)$ | $O(\vert S \vert)$ | Complete | Clarke-Emerson |
| **LTL Model Checking** | $O(\vert S \vert \cdot 2^{\vert \phi \vert})$ | $O(\vert S \vert)$ | Complete | Uses Büchi automata |
| **Bounded Model Check (BMC)** | $O(k \cdot \vert S \vert)$ | $O(k)$ | *Bounded* complete | Up to depth k |
| **OBDD Equivalence** | Polynomial (avg) | $O(2^{n})$ worst | Complete | Canonical forms |
| **Theorem Proving** | Undecidable (HOL) | Variable | Semidecidable | Human-guided |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Formal Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RTL Design Source] --> B[Parse and Build FSM Model]
    B --> C[Kripke Structure M = S, S0, R, L]
    D[Property Specification in CTL or LTL or SVA] --> E[Formal Property phi]
    C --> F[Formal Verification Engine]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Property phi Holds on M}
    G -- Yes --> H[Verified - Proof Certificate Generated]
    G -- No --> I[Counter-example Trace Returned]
    I --> J[Debug and Fix RTL]
    J --> A
    H --> K[Sign-off Report]
```

## 4.2 Model Checking Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputA [Design Input]
        A1[Verilog or VHDL RTL]
    end
    subgraph inputB [Property Input]
        B1[SVA or PSL Property]
    end
    A1 --> C1[Frontend Parser]
    B1 --> C2[Temporal Logic Parser]
    C1 --> D1[Symbolic FSM Builder]
    D1 --> E1[Kripke Structure M]
    C2 --> D2[Property AST]
    D2 --> E2[Normalized CTL or LTL]
    E1 --> F1[Model Checker Core]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1{Decision Procedure}
    G1 -- SAT --> H1[SAT Solver Backend]
    G1 -- OBDD --> H2[OBDD Package]
    G1 -- BMC --> H3[Bounded Model Check]
    H1 --> I1[Result]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    I1 --> J1{Proof or Counter-example}
```

## 4.3 Equivalence Checking Flow (Combinational)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Golden Design f1] --> B1[Synthesize to Boolean Netlist]
    A2[Revised Design f2] --> B2[Synthesize to Boolean Netlist]
    B1 --> C1[Build OBDD for f1]
    B2 --> C2[Build OBDD for f2]
    C1 --> D1[Compute XOR Miter f1 XOR f2]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1{Miter equals Constant 0}
    E1 -- Yes --> F1[Equivalent - Proven]
    E1 -- No --> G1[Generate Counter-example Vector]
```
