The **car** is your **DUT**. The **crash track engineers** decide the test conditions (speed, angle, surface) — these are your **stimulus generators**. The **high-speed cameras and sensors** attached to the dummy record every deformation and acceleration — this is your **monitor**. Finally, the **safety inspector** compares the recorded data against the regulatory safety thresholds — this is the **checker**. Just as you would never ship a car without these tests, you would never tape-out a chip without integrating it into a testbench that exercises, observes, and validates its behavior. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchor | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **DUT** | Device Under Test — the hardware design being verified | | **Testbench** | Top-level verification module that wraps the DUT and provides stimulus/checking | | **Stimulus** | Time-varying input patterns applied to the DUT's primary inputs | | **Golden Model** | A reference (often behavioral) model used to compute expected outputs | | **Self-Checking Testbench** | A testbench that automatically compares DUT outputs vs. expected values | | **Test Harness** | The structural wrapper around the DUT inside the testbench | ### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics The following **industry-standard metrics** are universally applied during simple testbench integration (will be used in later modules as well): - **Functional Coverage Target**: **100%** (ideal) of specification features exercised - **Code Coverage Baseline**: Statement $\geq$ **95%**, Branch $\geq$ **90%**, Toggle $\geq$ **85%** - **Simulation Time Unit**: Default is **1 ns / 1 ps** precision depending on technology node - **Clock Jitter Tolerance**: Typically **±5%** of nominal $T_{clk}$ in pre-silicon dynamic verification ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Simple Testbench Architecture A simple testbench, as mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 syllabus, is structurally decomposed into three pillars: **Pillar 1 — Stimulus Generation (Driver Block)** - Produces time-aligned input vectors for every primary input of the DUT. - May use `initial` blocks, `always` blocks, or task-based procedures. - Clock and reset are part of this layer (they drive the synchronous infrastructure). **Pillar 2 — DUT Instantiation (Design Hookup)** - The DUT is instantiated with **port-mapped** connections to the testbench's local `wire`/`reg` signals. - All port directions must be respected: DUT `input` ← testbench `reg`, DUT `output` → testbench `wire`. **Pillar 3 — Response Checking (Self-Checking Layer)** - Monitors DUT outputs at key simulation events (typically `@(posedge clk)`). - Compares with golden/expected values using `if`, `case`, or SystemVerilog `assert`. ### 2.2 Operational Flow — The "Why" and "How" - **Why instantiate DUT inside testbench?** Because the testbench is the *root of the simulation tree*. Without a containing module, no external environment can drive the design or observe its reactions. The DUT is **passive**; the testbench is **active**. - **Why use `initial` blocks for stimulus?** Because `initial` blocks execute **once at simulation time 0** — perfect for one-shot sequence generation. For repetitive patterns, an `always` block is preferred. - **Why no ports on the testbench module?** Because the testbench sits at the apex of the simulation hierarchy. There is nothing above it to drive it, so ports would be meaningless and disallowed by most simulators. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains all critical formulas, structural rules, and decision metrics for this topic. Master these for the ESE. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Verification Coverage (VC) | $VC = \dfrac{N_{exercised}}{N_{specified}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**; target = **100%** | | 2 | Code Coverage — Statement | $C_s = \dfrac{S_{hit}}{S_{total}}$ | Hit-statements over total executable lines | | 3 | Code Coverage — Branch | $C_b = \dfrac{B_{hit}}{B_{total}}$ | Each `if/else`, `case` arm counted separately | | 4 | Toggle Coverage | $C_t = \dfrac{T_{0\to 1} + T_{1\to 0}}{2N}$ | $N$ = number of signals | | 5 | Simulation Throughput | $T_h = \dfrac{N_{vectors}}{t_{sim}}$ | Vectors per second | | 6 | Fault Coverage (post-test) | $FC = \dfrac{D_{detected}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Used after ATPG | | 7 | Testbench Time Advance | `@(#delay)` or `#(t)` | Time in **ns / ps** | | 8 | Clock Period | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | $f_{clk}$ in **MHz / GHz** | | 9 | Setup Margin Check | $t_{setup} < T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{comb}$ | All times in **ns** | | 10 | Stimulus Vector Count | $N_v = \prod_{i=1}^{n} k_i$ | $k_i$ = number of distinct values on input $i$ | | 11 | Display Format | `$display("time=%0t, sig=%b", $time, sig);` | `$time` returns current sim time | | 12 | Finish Directive | `$finish;` | Halts simulation cleanly | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are the **language of verification sign-off**. Whether you work at Intel, Qualcomm, NVIDIA, or any ASIC design services company, your manager will judge you on coverage closure (Items 1–4), throughput (Item 5), and sign-off readiness. The formulas above are the foundation of the Universal Verification Methodology (UVM) reporting dashboards, which are built upon these primitive metrics. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In commercial ASIC design flows: - A **simple testbench** is the *first* step in any IP block bring-up. Even large design houses start each new IP with a hand-written simple testbench before transitioning to UVM. - For **FPGA prototyping**, simple testbenches are often the *final* verification step due to in-system visibility constraints. - In **academic research**, simple testbenches dominate IoT and embedded system verification due to low design complexity. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping KTU Syllabus Outcomes to Testbench Layers The KTU 2024 Course Outcome mapping for this topic is summarized below. Each testbench layer addresses one or more Course Outcomes (COs): | Testbench Layer | Engineering Action | CO Mapped | Bloom's Level | |---|---|---|---| | Stimulus Generation | Apply input vectors | CO2 | Apply | | DUT Instantiation | Port-map DUT | CO2 | Understand | | Clock/Reset Driver | Build synchronous environment | CO2 | Apply | | Monitor | Sample outputs | CO3 | Analyze | | Checker | Compare with golden | CO3 | Evaluate | | Self-Checking Logic | Automate PASS/FAIL | CO3 | Create | ### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Building a Simple Testbench (Worked Example) **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Design and verify a simple **4-bit synchronous counter** using a simple testbench. The counter increments on every positive edge of `clk` when `enable` is high; it asynchronously resets to `0000` when `rst` is asserted. **Step 1 — Identify DUT Ports** From the specification: - Inputs: `clk` (1-bit), `rst` (1-bit, async, active-high), `enable` (1-bit) - Outputs: `count` (4-bit) **Step 2 — Identify Stimulus Requirements** A minimal complete verification requires: - Power-on reset assertion - Functional check with `enable = 0` (no counting) - Functional check with `enable = 1` (counting) - Mid-count reset behavior (asynchronous reset verification) Thus, the minimum vector set count (using Item 10 from the formula sheet): $$N_v = k_{clk} \times k_{rst} \times k_{enable} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8 \text{ distinct combinatorial vectors}$$ However, sequential verification demands a *time-ordered* sequence of $\geq$ **16 clock cycles** to exercise all 16 states of the 4-bit counter. **Step 3 — Code the DUT (Reference)** ```verilog // 4-bit Synchronous Counter — DUT module counter_4bit ( input wire clk, input wire rst, input wire enable, output reg [3:0] count ); always @(posedge clk or posedge rst) begin if (rst) count <= 4'b0000; else if (enable) count <= count + 1'b1; else count <= count; end endmodule ``` **Step 4 — Code the Simple Testbench** ```verilog // Simple Testbench for 4-bit Counter `timescale 1ns/1ps module tb_counter_4bit; // ---- Step 4a: Declare testbench signals (mirror DUT ports) ---- reg clk; reg rst; reg enable; wire [3:0] count; // ---- Step 4b: Instantiate the DUT ---- counter_4bit DUT ( .clk (clk), .rst (rst), .enable (enable), .count (count) ); // ---- Step 4c: Clock Generator (always block, period = 10 ns) ---- initial clk = 1'b0; always #5 clk = ~clk; // T_clk = 10 ns => f_clk = 100 MHz // ---- Step 4d: Stimulus + Self-Checking ---- integer errors; initial begin errors = 0; // Phase 1: Apply reset rst = 1'b1; enable = 1'b0; #12; // hold reset past one clock edge if (count !== 4'b0000) begin $display("FAIL @%0t: Reset did not clear counter. count=%b", $time, count); errors = errors + 1; end // Phase 2: Release reset, enable = 0 (counter should hold) @(negedge clk); rst = 1'b0; enable = 1'b0; @(posedge clk); #1; if (count !== 4'b0000) begin $display("FAIL @%0t: Counter incremented with enable=0. count=%b", $time, count); errors = errors + 1; end // Phase 3: Enable counting, sample after each clock enable = 1'b1; repeat (16) begin @(posedge clk); #1; end if (count !== 4'b1111) begin $display("FAIL @%0t: After 16 clocks, expected 1111, got %b", $time, count); errors = errors + 1; end // Phase 4: Async reset during operation #5 rst = 1'b1; #2; if (count !== 4'b0000) begin $display("FAIL @%0t: Async reset did not clear counter. count=%b", $time, count); errors = errors + 1; end // Final Report if (errors == 0) $display("=============== TEST PASSED ==============="); else $display("=============== TEST FAILED: %0d errors ===============", errors); $finish; end // ---- Step 4e: Optional waveform dump ---- initial begin $dumpfile("counter.vcd"); $dumpvars(0, tb_counter_4bit); end endmodule ``` **Step 5 — Analytical Walkthrough (Mathematical Trace)** Let $Q_n$ denote the counter value at the $n$-th positive clock edge after reset release. The DUT's behavior is governed by: $$Q_n = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } rst = 1 \\ Q_{n-1} + 1 \pmod{16} & \text{if } enable = 1 \\ Q_{n-1} & \text{if } enable = 0 \end{cases}$$ Tracing Phase 3: - After $Q_0 = 0$ (post-reset), and $enable = 1$, we apply 16 positive edges. - $Q_1 = 1$, $Q_2 = 2$, $\ldots$, $Q_{16} = 16 \pmod{16} = 0$. Wait — but our testbench checks **after** the 16th edge, so the snapshot is $Q_{16} = 0$. This reveals a **classic student bug**: the testbench's expected value should account for **what the counter value is at the sampling instant**, which is **post-edge**. After exactly 16 increments from 0, the counter reads `0000` again. The correct intermediate check after 15 cycles should yield `1111`. The above code, as written, has a logical flaw that we should fix: **Step 6 — Corrected Self-Checking Logic (Critical Insight)** To prove all 16 distinct states, sample after each of the 16 cycles and assert $Q_n = n \pmod{16}$: ```verilog // Corrected Phase 3: sample after each clock enable = 1'b1; for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++) begin @(posedge clk); #1; if (count !== i[3:0]) begin $display("FAIL @%0t: Expected %0d, got %b", $time, i, count); errors = errors + 1; end end ``` **Step 7 — Compute Achieved Coverage** Using Item 2 from the formula sheet: $$C_s = \frac{S_{hit}}{S_{total}} = \frac{5}{5} = 100\%$$ Statement coverage hits: reset branch, enable-high branch, enable-low branch, async-reset assignment, synchronous addition — **all 5 statements executed**. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Each Verification Construct | Verification Need | Construct | Why | |---|---|---| | One-time sequence | `initial begin ... end` | Executes exactly once at $t=0$ | | Periodic signal (clock) | `always #Δ sig = ~sig;` | Generates infinite periodic waveform | | Synchronized sampling | `@(posedge clk);` | Aligns observation to active edge | | Expected value comparison | `if (actual !== expected)` | Catches X/Z unknowns (use `!==`) | | Severity-graded reporting | `$fatal`, `$error`, `$warning`, `$info` | Industry-standard severity ladder | | Waveform capture | `$dumpfile` / `$dumpvars` | Generates `.vcd` for GTKWave/Verdi | | Test termination | `$finish;` or `$stop;` | `$finish` exits; `$stop` pauses | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight (Equivalence):** Every `initial` block that does not contain `#delay` or `@event` is logically **combinatorial with respect to time zero**. Always include at least one time-advance statement (e.g., `#1`) after a clock edge before sampling outputs to **avoid race conditions** between the testbench's blocking reads and the DUT's non-blocking `<=` updates. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Testbench Architecture (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram shows the canonical **Simple Testbench Integration** architecture as mandated in Module 2 of the KTU 2024 syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TESTBENCH_ROOT["TESTBENCH MODULE TB TOP"] direction TB subgraph STIM["STIMULUS LAYER"] CLK_GEN["CLOCK GENERATOR
always #5 clk = ~clk"] RST_DRV["RESET DRIVER
initial rst = 1, then deassert"] VEC_APP["VECTOR APPLICATOR
initial begin ... drive enable ... end"] end subgraph DUT_BOX["DUT INSTANTIATION"] DUT["COUNTER 4 BIT
Module counter 4bit"] end subgraph CHECK["CHECKING LAYER"] MON["MONITOR
Sample count on posedge clk"] GOLDEN["EXPECTED VALUE TRACKER
Compute expected count"] COMP["COMPARATOR
if actual vs expected"] REP["REPORTER
display PASS or FAIL"] end subgraph CTRL["CONTROL"] FIN["FINISH DIRECTIVE
finish after test complete"] DUMP["WAVEFORM DUMP
dumpfile / dumpvars"] end CLK_GEN -- clk --> DUT RST_DRV -- rst --> DUT VEC_APP -- enable --> DUT DUT -- count --> MON MON --> COMP GOLDEN --> COMP COMP --> REP REP --> FIN MON --> DUMP end style TESTBENCH_ROOT fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:3px style STIM fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#FBC02D,stroke-width:2px style DUT_BOX fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px style CHECK fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px style CTRL fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Data Flow Diagram — Stimulus → DUT → Check ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SIMULATION TIME t = 0"] -->|"#12 ns"| B["RESET APPLIED"] B -->|"rst = 0
enable = 0"| C["HOLD STATE"] C -->|"enable = 1"| D["COUNTING PHASE
16 Clock Edges"] D -->|"@posedge clk
sample count"| E["SELF CHECK
count vs expected"] E -->|"Match"| F["PASS COUNTER"] E -->|"Mismatch"| G["FAIL COUNTER + Display"] F --> H["FINAL REPORT"] G --> H H -->|"finish"| I["SIMULATION ENDS"] style A fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1976D2 style B fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#D32F2F style C fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#FBC02D style D fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C style E fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#F57C00 style F fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32 style G fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#C62828 style H fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#7B1FA2 style I fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0 ``` ### 4.3 Testbench–DUT Interface Mapping (Port-Level Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TB["TESTBENCH"] R_CLK["reg clk"] R_RST["reg rst"] R_EN["reg enable"] W_CNT["wire 3:0 count"] R_EXP["reg 3:0 expected"] end subgraph DUT["DUT counter 4bit"] I_CLK["input clk"] I_RST["input rst"] I_EN["input enable"] O_CNT["output reg 3:0 count"] end R_CLK -- "wire connection" --> I_CLK R_RST -- "wire connection" --> I_RST R_EN -- "wire connection" --> I_EN O_CNT -- "wire connection" --> W_CNT W_CNT -- "sampled at posedge clk" --> R_EXP style TB fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px style DUT fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram of Verification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["Step 1: SPECIFICATION
Read DUT contract"] --> P2["Step 2: TEST PLAN
Enumerate features to verify"] P2 --> P3["Step 3: STIMULUS DESIGN
Build vector sequences"] P3 --> P4["Step 4: DUT HOOKUP
Port map in testbench"] P4 --> P5["Step 5: SELF CHECKER
Expected vs actual logic"] P5 --> P6["Step 6: SIMULATE
Run with waveform dump"] P6 --> P7{"Step 7: ALL CHECKS PASS?"} P7 -- "Yes" --> P8["REPORT PASS / SIGN OFF"] P7 -- "No" --> P9["DEBUG
Inspect waveforms and logs"] P9 --> P3 style P1 fill:#BBDEFB style P2 fill:#C5CAE9 style P3 fill:#FFE082 style P4 fill:#FFAB91 style P5 fill:#A5D6A7 style P6 fill:#80CBC4 style P7 fill:#CE93D8 style P8 fill:#81C784,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px style P9 fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define a Simple Testbench. List its essential components.** **Model Answer (3 marks — full marks distribution):** A Simple Testbench is a top-level verification module (no ports) used to instantiate and verify a Design Under Test (DUT) by applying stimulus and observing responses. **Essential components (1 mark each):** 1. **Stimulus Generator / Driver** — applies input vectors to the DUT. 2. **DUT Instantiation** — the design being verified, port-mapped into the testbench. 3. **Response Checker / Monitor** — samples DUT outputs and compares with expected values; reports PASS/FAIL. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Why does a testbench module have no port list in SystemVerilog/Verilog?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A testbench module has no port list **(1 mark)** because it sits at the **root of the simulation hierarchy** **(1 mark)** — there is no parent module above it to drive inputs or sample outputs. All signals required for verification are declared *internally* as `reg`/`wire`, and the DUT is instantiated *within* the testbench. The simulator treats the testbench as the top-level instantiation **(1 mark)**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU 2024 ESE, Module 2 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. The structure below follows the official pattern: Part (a) for 7 marks and Part (b) for 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **simple testbench**. List the components and describe their functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **simple testbench** in Verilog to verify a **2-to-1 multiplexer** (select line `sel`, inputs `a`, `b`, output `y`). Show the complete DUT and testbench code, and explain how you perform self-checking. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A **Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):** The architecture of a simple testbench is described in three layers: 1. **Stimulus Layer (Driver Block) — 2 marks** - Generates clock, reset, and primary input vectors. - Implemented using `initial` blocks (one-shot) or `always` blocks (repetitive). - The clock generator: `always #5 clk = ~clk;` produces a 100 MHz clock. 2. **DUT Instantiation Layer (Design Hookup) — 2 marks** - DUT instantiated with `.port(signal)` connections. - DUT inputs are tied to testbench `reg`; DUT outputs connect to testbench `wire`. 3. **Checking Layer (Monitor + Comparator) — 3 marks** - **Monitor**: samples DUT outputs at clock edges using `@(posedge clk)`. - **Comparator**: compares sampled value with expected using `if (actual !== expected)`. - **Reporter**: prints PASS/FAIL using `$display`, ends with `$finish`. (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the architectural schematic.) **Part (b) — 2-to-1 MUX Testbench (7 marks):** **DUT Code (2 marks):** ```verilog module mux2x1 ( input wire a, input wire b, input wire sel, output wire y ); assign y = sel ? b : a; endmodule ``` **Testbench Code with Self-Checking (4 marks):** ```verilog `timescale 1ns/1ps module tb_mux2x1; reg a, b, sel; wire y; integer errors; // DUT instantiation mux2x1 DUT (.a(a), .b(b), .sel(sel), .y(y)); // Exhaustive stimulus with inline self-checking initial begin errors = 0; {a, b, sel} = 3'b000; #10; if (y !== 1'b0) begin $display("FAIL a=%b b=%b sel=%b y=%b", a,b,sel,y); errors=errors+1; end {a, b, sel} = 3'b001; #10; if (y !== 1'b1) begin $display("FAIL a=%b b=%b sel=%b y=%b", a,b,sel,y); errors=errors+1; end {a, b, sel} = 3'b010; #10; if (y !== 1'b0) begin $display("FAIL a=%b b=%b sel=%b y=%b", a,b,sel,y); errors=errors+1; end {a, b, sel} = 3'b011; #10; if (y !== 1'b1) begin $display("FAIL a=%b b=%b sel=%b y=%b", a,b,sel,y); errors=errors+1; end // Add remaining 4 combinations (100, 101, 110, 111) {a, b, sel} = 3'b100; #10; if (y !== a) begin $display("FAIL"); errors=errors+1; end {a, b, sel} = 3'b101; #10; if (y !== b) begin $display("FAIL"); errors=errors+1; end {a, b, sel} = 3'b110; #10; if (y !== a) begin $display("FAIL"); errors=errors+1; end {a, b, sel} = 3'b111; #10; if (y !== b) begin $display("FAIL"); errors=errors+1; end if (errors == 0) $display("===== TEST PASSED ====="); else $display("===== TEST FAILED: %0d errors =====", errors); $finish; end endmodule ``` **Self-Checking Explanation (1 mark):** The testbench applies **all 8 input combinations** ($2^3 = 8$) exhaustively. After each `#10` delay, the comparator checks `y` against the expected value using the MUX truth table. The `errors` counter accumulates failures and the final `$display` reports PASS/FAIL. The use of `!==` (rather than `!=`) is critical to catch X/Z unknowns. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the role of **clock generation** and **reset sequencing** in a simple testbench. Why is the `#1` delay often added after `@(posedge clk)` before sampling DUT outputs? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Verilog testbench for a **4-bit binary counter with synchronous active-low reset**. Show how you verify all 16 states. Mention the formula for computing the number of test vectors required. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B **Part (a) — Clock and Reset (7 marks):** **Clock Generation — 2 marks:** - Implemented using an `always` block: `always #5 clk = ~clk;` generates a 10 ns period clock. - Clock is the **heartbeat** of the synchronous DUT; without it, sequential elements (flip-flops) never update, and verification cannot proceed. - The clock signal drives the `clk` input of the DUT via the testbench's internal `reg clk`. **Reset Sequencing — 2 marks:** - Reset must be asserted **before** the first clock edge and held **past at least one rising edge** to ensure all flip-flops initialize correctly. - Sequence: `rst = 1; #12; rst = 0;` ensures stable reset at the first rising edge. - For asynchronous reset, the DUT's `always @(posedge clk or posedge rst)` block triggers on `rst` independently of the clock. **The `#1` Delay (Race-Free Sampling) — 3 marks:** - After `@(posedge clk)`, the DUT's flip-flops update their outputs using **non-blocking** assignments (`<=`) in Verilog. The NBA region of the simulation cycle executes **after** the active event region. - If the testbench samples the output **without** a delay, it reads the **old** value, leading to a **race condition**. - The `#1` delay shifts the testbench's sampling event into the next time step, after NBAs have settled. This guarantees the testbench sees the **post-edge** value, matching what real hardware would output. - Industry convention: `#1` is sufficient; larger delays are unnecessary and slow simulation. **Part (b) — 4-bit Synchronous Counter Testbench (7 marks):** **Vector Count Formula (2 marks):** For sequential verification, the testbench must apply a sequence long enough to traverse every state. For a 4-bit counter, **all 16 states** must be visited. The minimum vector count is: $$N_v = 2^n = 2^4 = 16 \text{ clock cycles}$$ where $n$ is the DUT state width. **Counter DUT (1 mark):** ```verilog module counter4_sync ( input wire clk, input wire rst_n, // active-low synchronous reset input wire enable, output reg [3:0] count ); always @(posedge clk) begin if (!rst_n) count <= 4'b0000; else if (enable) count <= count + 1'b1; end endmodule ``` **Testbench (3 marks):** ```verilog `timescale 1ns/1ps module tb_counter4_sync; reg clk, rst_n, enable; wire [3:0] count; integer errors; counter4_sync DUT (.clk(clk), .rst_n(rst_n), .enable(enable), .count(count)); initial clk = 0; always #5 clk = ~clk; initial begin errors = 0; // Reset rst_n = 1'b0; enable = 1'b0; #15; if (count !== 4'b0000) begin $display("FAIL: reset did not clear counter"); errors = errors + 1; end // Release reset, enable counting @(negedge clk); rst_n = 1'b1; enable = 1'b1; // Verify all 16 states for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++) begin @(posedge clk); #1; if (count !== i[3:0]) begin $display("FAIL @%0t: expected %0d, got %0d", $time, i, count); errors = errors + 1; end end if (errors == 0) $display("===== ALL 16 STATES VERIFIED — PASS ====="); else $display("===== FAILED: %0d mismatches =====", errors); $finish; end endmodule ``` **Final Reporting (1 mark):** The testbench reports PASS only when **all 16 states** are observed in sequence and the synchronous reset clears the counter to `0000`. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Simple Testbench Questions:** > > 1. **Missing `#1` delay after `@(posedge clk)`** → -2 marks. Examiners specifically look for race-free sampling. If absent, you'll lose marks for "incorrect verification methodology." > > 2. **Using `!=` instead of `!==`** → -1 mark. The `!=` operator treats X/Z as equal; `!==` correctly flags them as mismatches, which is mandatory for proper verification. > > 3. **Forgetting `$finish;` at the end of `initial` block** → -1 mark. Without `$finish`, the simulator enters an infinite loop or hangs. Examiners penalize "improper test termination." > > 4. **Confusing blocking (`=`) vs non-blocking (`<=`) in the DUT** → -2 marks. Sequential logic **must** use `<=`. Mixing them is a fatal synthesis-vs-simulation mismatch error. > > 5. **Omitting the `timescale directive** → -1 mark. Without `\`timescale 1ns/1ps`, simulation timing is undefined. > > 6. **Not using a separate `integer errors;` counter** → -1 mark. Examiners expect a quantitative PASS/FAIL summary, not just `$display("passed")`. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Simple Testbench Integration** - ✅ A **testbench** is a port-less top-level module that wraps the DUT for verification. - ✅ The DUT is **instantiated** inside the testbench, not vice versa. - ✅ DUT inputs ← testbench `reg` signals; DUT outputs → testbench `wire` signals. - ✅ Use `initial` blocks for **one-shot** sequences, `always` blocks for **periodic** signals like clocks. - ✅ Always add `#1` delay after `@(posedge clk)` to avoid **NBA race conditions**. - ✅ Use `!==` (4-state inequality) for self-checking, never `!=`. - ✅ Always include **`\`timescale`** directive at the top of the testbench. - ✅ Always terminate simulation with **`$finish;`** to avoid simulator hang. - ✅ For **self-checking**, declare an `integer errors;` counter and report a quantitative PASS/FAIL summary. - ✅ The minimum vector count for an exhaustive $n$-input DUT is $2^n$ combinatorial vectors. - ✅ For sequential verification, apply **enough clock cycles** to traverse the entire state space ($2^n$ states for an $n$-bit state element). - ✅ Use **`$dumpfile` / `$dumpvars`** for waveform generation (`.vcd` file). - ✅ Use the **severity ladder**: `$fatal > $error > $warning > $info` for graded diagnostics. - ✅ The verification tuple is $\mathcal{T} = \{D, S, M, C\}$: Design, Stimulus, Monitor, Checker. - ✅ Functional coverage target = **100%**; Statement coverage baseline = **95%**. - ✅ For clock period: $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$; example: 100 MHz → $T_{clk} = 10$ ns.

topics.title: System UVCs and Testbench Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System UVCs and Testbench Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a UVC (Universal Verification Component)?

A **Universal Verification Component (UVC)** is a reusable, configurable, and self-contained SystemVerilog verification IP block built using the **Universal Verification Methodology (UVM)** that encapsulates the complete verification intent for a specific interface, protocol, or subsystem. It is designed to be plugged into any UVM testbench with minimal effort, promoting **horizontal** (across projects) and **vertical** (from block-level to system-level) reuse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A UVC is a pre-verified, encapsulated verification unit containing its own sequence library, driver, monitor, coverage, and configuration logic, exposing a standardized API for higher-level testbench integration.

### 1.1.1 Real-World Analogy: The "Smart Power Tool" Analogy

Think of a UVC like a **modular kitchen appliance set** in a modern smart home:
- A **microwave** (UVC) has its own buttons, sensors, timers, and safety interlocks. You don't care *how* the magnetron works — you just press "Start."
- Similarly, a UVC hides the low-level protocol signaling (e.g., AXI handshakes, PCIe TLP formatting) inside a black box.
- The "smart home hub" (top-level testbench) just **plugs** each appliance into the network (testbench) and **speaks** to it through standard voice commands (TLM interfaces, virtual sequencers).
- If you swap one microwave brand for another, the hub doesn't change — only the **plug interface** must be compatible. This is the UVC **standardization** principle.

---

## 1.2 Interface UVC vs. System UVC

| Aspect | Interface UVC | System UVC |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| **Scope** | Single bus/protocol (AXI, AHB, APB, USB) | Subsystem or full SoC behavior (Memory subsystem, Bus Fabric) |
| **Abstraction** | Signal/pin-level transactions | Transaction-level behavior across multiple protocols |
| **Building Blocks** | Atomic — does not contain other UVCs | Composed — instantiates and orchestrates multiple Interface UVCs |
| **Typical Use** | Stimulus generation for a single DUT port | End-to-end scenarios (e.g., CPU writes to DDR via AXI fabric) |
| **Example** | AXI4 Master/Slave UVC | DDR Memory Controller System UVC |

> [!NOTE]
> A **System UVC** is essentially a higher-level UVC that **aggregates** multiple Interface UVCs, adds an internal virtual sequencer, and exposes high-level operational scenarios (e.g., "stream a video frame from USB to DDR via DMA").

---

## 1.3 What is Testbench Integration?

**Testbench Integration** is the process of assembling, configuring, and connecting multiple UVCs, the DUT (Design Under Test), scoreboards, and the test sequencer into a **single, executable UVM testbench hierarchy**. It involves:
1. Instantiating UVC environments inside a top-level `uvm_env`.
2. Wiring TLM (Transaction-Level Modeling) connections between components.
3. Setting up the **configuration database** (`uvm_config_db`) for parameters and virtual interfaces.
4. Establishing a **virtual sequencer** to coordinate stimulus across multiple UVCs.
5. Synchronizing clocks and resets across UVCs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** Testbench integration is governed by the **uvm_root**, the global top-level manager that orchestrates the `build_phase` → `connect_phase` → `run_phase` lifecycle of every UVC in the hierarchy.

---

## 1.4 Why System UVCs and Integration Matter

Modern SoCs integrate **dozens of IP blocks** with multiple protocols. Without a structured UVC-based methodology:
- Verification code becomes **unmaintainable**.
- Reuse across projects is **near zero**.
- Coverage closure is **chaotic**.

System UVCs enforce a **layered, hierarchical verification architecture** that scales to billion-gate designs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Generic UVC

A UVC has a **layered, modular structure**. From inside-out, the layers are:

### Layer 1: Transaction Layer (Sequence Item)
- Defines the **data structure** (packet, beat, transaction) passed through the UVC.
- Pure data class extending `uvm_sequence_item`.
- Example: `axi_transaction` with fields `addr`, `data`, `burst_len`, `resp`.

### Layer 2: Sequence Layer
- Library of pre-written `uvm_sequence` classes generating realistic stimulus patterns.
- Example: `axi_random_read_seq`, `axi_burst_write_seq`, `axi_stress_seq`.

### Layer 3: Sequencer + Driver Layer
- **Sequencer**: Schedules sequence items; arbitrates between multiple sequences.
- **Driver**: Converts abstract transaction items into **pin-level** (signal) activity using a **virtual interface** and a **clocking block**.

### Layer 4: Monitor Layer
- Passive — observes DUT signals and reconstructs transactions.
- Pushes observed transactions via **TLM analysis ports** to scoreboards and coverage subscribers.

### Layer 5: Agent Layer
- Container that bundles the **sequencer + driver + monitor**.
- Has a configuration flag: `is_active = UVM_ACTIVE` (drives) or `UVM_PASSIVE` (monitors only).

### Layer 6: Configuration Layer
- `uvm_config_db` entries: virtual interface handles, mode flags, timing parameters, address maps.

### Layer 7: Coverage & Scoreboard Layer
- Subscribers collect functional coverage points.
- Scoreboards perform **reference model comparison**.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a System UVC (Higher Level)

A System UVC differs from an Interface UVC because it **contains other UVCs as children**. The typical layers are:

1. **Sub-UVC Instances** (e.g., AXI Master UVC, APB Config UVC)
2. **Virtual Sequencer** that owns pointers to all sub-sequencers
3. **System-Level Reference Model** (often a SystemVerilog functional model, not a real DUT)
4. **Cross-Bar Scoreboard** correlating transactions across buses
5. **System Configuration Object** controlling the overall operating mode (e.g., "boot mode," "video streaming mode")

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A System UVC's *primary value* is **scenario generation** — it produces coherent, multi-protocol traffic that mimics real SoC use-cases.

---

## 2.3 Testbench Integration — The 5-Pillar Model

Testbench integration of UVCs is built on **five mechanical pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Configuration Database (`uvm_config_db`)
- A **global name-value store** scoped by hierarchy path.
- Used to pass **virtual interface handles**, mode flags, and configuration objects *down* the hierarchy.
- Anti-pattern: passing via global variables or constructor arguments.

### Pillar 2: Virtual Interfaces
- A SystemVerilog construct that **references a physical interface instance** (a bundle of signals).
- Decouples the UVC from the static module hierarchy.

### Pillar 3: TLM Connections
- **Ports (request)**, **Exports (request)**, **Analysis Ports (broadcast)**.
- TLM 1.0 (blocking/non-blocking) and TLM 2.0 (sockets with phases).
- UVCs connect via `monitor.analysis_port.connect(scoreboard.analysis_export)`.

### Pillar 4: Virtual Sequencer
- A sequencer whose body contains `uvm_sequencer` handles pointing to sub-sequencers of each UVC.
- A top-level sequence starts **parallel/serial sub-sequences** on each sub-sequencer.

### Pillar 5: Reset & Clock Management
- A single **clock/reset block** at the testbench top drives all interfaces.
- Resets must be sequenced with `uvm_event` or phase objections.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Key Parameters

Although this topic is largely architectural, the following **metrics, parameters, and standard terminologies** are repeatedly tested in KTU exams:

| Parameter / Term | Definition / Standard | KTU Typical Use |
|------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| `is_active` | UVM_ACTIVE or UVM_PASSIVE flag in `uvm_agent` | Determines if UVC drives DUT |
| `cfg_db_set` | `uvm_config_db#(T)::set()` API to push config | Used in `build_phase` |
| `cfg_db_get` | `uvm_config_db#(T)::get()` API to retrieve config | Used in component's `build_phase` |
| Virtual Interface | `virtual <interface_name> vif` | Required for signal access in class |
| TLM Port | `uvm_analysis_port#(T)` | Broadcast from monitor |
| TLM Export | `uvm_analysis_imp#(T, IMP)` | Receives from analysis port |
| Virtual Sequencer | Sequencer holding sub-sequencer handles | Coordinates multiple UVCs |
| Sequence Item | `uvm_sequence_item` subclass | Transaction abstraction |
| Reference Model | Golden model of DUT for self-checking | Built in C/C++/SV |
| `uvm_test_top` | Top-level test class | Starts via `+UVM_TESTNAME` |
| `build_phase` | UVM phase 0 — constructs hierarchy | Top-down construction |
| `connect_phase` | UVM phase 1 — wires TLM ports | Bottom-up connection |
| `end_of_elaboration` | UVM phase 2 — final adjustments | Used for last-mile config |
| `start_of_simulation` | UVM phase 3 — prints topology | Used for debug prints |
| `run_phase` | UVM phase 4 — time-consuming activity | Main stimulus + checks |
| `uvm_phase phase` | Phase handle argument | Used in all phase methods |
| Coverage Goal | Target % of functional coverage | Typically **95% – 100%** |
| Sequence Arbitration | FIFO, WEIGHT, RANDOM, etc. | Default = FIFO |
| Virtual Sequence | `uvm_sequence` running on virtual sequencer | Multi-UVC coordination |
| Reset Recovery Time | Time after reset deassertion before clocking | DUT-specific |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the *order* of UVM phases and the *direction* of construction vs. connection (top-down vs. bottom-up). This is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

System UVCs and structured integration are the **industry standard** at companies like Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, and NXP. The UVM class library provides the `uvm_pkg`, and a single well-designed UVC can be reused across:
- Block-level verification
- Subsystem verification
- Full-chip verification
- Hardware-software co-simulation (e.g., with SystemC TLM 2.0)
- Emulation (via Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium, Mentor Questa)

The **testbench integration topology** is what enables the same UVC to drive a **simulation model** in one phase and a **real silicon prototype** in another phase — the reusability is architecture-driven, not just code-driven.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Building a System UVC from Scratch

Below is an **exhaustive step-by-step analytical procedure** for constructing a System UVC and integrating it into a top-level testbench. Each step is broken down with full logic, so a student can reproduce it.

### Step 1: Identify the Verification Boundary
**Logic:** Before writing a single line of SystemVerilog, you must determine:
- What is the **DUT** (Design Under Test)?
- What is the **abstraction level** (signal, transaction, scenario)?
- What are the **external interfaces** of the DUT (e.g., AXI, APB, UART)?
- What is the **end-to-end behavior** to verify (e.g., "DMA can move a 1KB buffer from DDR to SRAM")?

**Output:** A verification specification listing:
- List of interface protocols
- List of scenarios
- Coverage goals (target = **100%** for safety-critical, **95%** for typical).

### Step 2: Acquire / Develop Interface UVCs
**Logic:** For each protocol in Step 1, you need an Interface UVC. These come from:
- **In-house legacy UVCs** (vertical reuse)
- **Third-party VIP** (Synopsys, Cadence) (commercial, but require customization)
- **Open-source UVCs** (e.g., OVM-World, GitHub OVM kits)

For each Interface UVC, ensure it exposes:
- A **sequence library** (e.g., `axi_master_base_seq`, `axi_master_random_seq`)
- A **virtual sequencer handle point** in its `uvm_sequencer` (so the System UVC can reach in)

### Step 3: Design the System UVC Skeleton
**Logic:** Create a new class `class my_system_uvc_env extends uvm_env;`. Inside it, declare:
- Sub-UVC environment handles: `axi_master_uvc_env axi_master;`
- A virtual sequencer: `my_system_vsequencer vsequencer;`
- A scoreboard: `my_system_scoreboard sb;`
- A coverage collector: `my_system_coverage cov;`

The class must register with the UVM factory via `\`uvm_component_utils(my_system_uvc_env)`.

### Step 4: Implement `build_phase`
**Logic:** The `build_phase` is executed **top-down**. It is responsible for:
1. Getting configuration from the `uvm_config_db` (passed in from the test).
2. Constructing sub-UVCs.
3. Setting their configuration via `cfg_db_set()` (passing it down to children).

The pseudocode is:

```systemverilog
function void build_phase(uvm_phase phase);
  super.build_phase(phase);
  // Step 4a: Get the top-level config
  if (!uvm_config_db#(my_system_cfg)::get(this, "", "sys_cfg", sys_cfg))
    \`uvm_fatal("NOCFG", "System config not found in DB");
  // Step 4b: Construct sub-UVCs
  axi_master = axi_master_uvc_env::type_id::create("axi_master", this);
  ahb_slave  = ahb_slave_uvc_env::type_id::create("ahb_slave", this);
  // Step 4c: Push config down to children
  uvm_config_db#(axi_master_cfg)::set(this, "axi_master*", "axi_cfg", sys_cfg.axi_cfg);
  uvm_config_db#(ahb_slave_cfg)::set(this,  "ahb_slave*",  "ahb_cfg",  sys_cfg.ahb_cfg);
  // Step 4d: Construct the virtual sequencer
  vsequencer = my_system_vsequencer::type_id::create("vsequencer", this);
endfunction
```

### Step 5: Implement `connect_phase`
**Logic:** The `connect_phase` is executed **bottom-up**. It is responsible for:
1. Wiring TLM analysis ports from sub-UVC monitors to the system scoreboard.
2. Pointing the virtual sequencer's sub-sequencer handles to the actual sub-sequencers.

The pseudocode is:

```systemverilog
function void connect_phase(uvm_phase phase);
  super.connect_phase(phase);
  // Step 5a: TLM connection
  axi_master.monitor.item_collected_port.connect(sb.axi_export);
  ahb_slave.monitor.item_collected_port.connect(sb.ahb_export);
  // Step 5b: Virtual sequencer handles
  vsequencer.axi_seqr = axi_master.sequencer;
  vsequencer.ahb_seqr  = ahb_slave.sequencer;
endfunction
```

### Step 6: Implement `end_of_elaboration_phase`
**Logic:** Optional, used for:
- Displaying the UVC topology.
- Final sanity checks (e.g., virtual interface handles are non-null).

### Step 7: Design the Virtual Sequencer
**Logic:** The virtual sequencer is a container with **non-virtual** sub-sequencer handles. It does **not** own a sequence item; it only **delegates** the sequence body to children.

```systemverilog
class my_system_vsequencer extends uvm_sequencer;
  \`uvm_component_utils(my_system_vsequencer)
  axi_master_sequencer axi_seqr;
  ahb_slave_sequencer  ahb_seqr;
endclass
```

### Step 8: Write the System Virtual Sequence
**Logic:** This is the most important class — it encodes the **scenario**.

```systemverilog
class my_system_vseq extends uvm_sequence;
  \`uvm_object_utils(my_system_vseq)
  \`uvm_declare_p_sequencer(my_system_vsequencer)
  task body();
    // Step 8a: Start a parallel AXI write
    fork
      axi_write_seq.start(p_sequencer.axi_seqr);
      ahb_poll_seq.start(p_sequencer.ahb_seqr);
    join
    // Step 8b: Wait and check
    #1000ns;
  endtask
endclass
```

### Step 9: Top-Level Testbench Integration in `uvm_test_top`
**Logic:** The test class is the entry point. It:
1. Sets the top-level `uvm_config_db` entries (virtual interface handles).
2. Constructs the System UVC environment.
3. Starts the virtual sequence on the `vsequencer`.

```systemverilog
class my_test extends uvm_test;
  \`uvm_component_utils(my_test)
  my_system_uvc_env sys_env;
  virtual my_if vif;
  function void build_phase(uvm_phase phase);
    super.build_phase(phase);
    if (!uvm_config_db#(virtual my_if)::get(null, "uvm_test_top", "vif", vif))
      \`uvm_fatal("NOVIF", "Virtual interface not set");
    sys_env = my_system_uvc_env::type_id::create("sys_env", this);
  endfunction
  task run_phase(uvm_phase phase);
    phase.raise_objection(this);
    my_system_vseq vseq = my_system_vseq::type_id::create("vseq");
    vseq.start(sys_env.vsequencer);
    phase.drop_objection(this);
  endtask
endclass
```

### Step 10: Static Top Testbench Module
**Logic:** The `module top_tb` instantiates:
- The DUT.
- The clock/reset generator.
- The physical interfaces (one per protocol).
- The `+UVM_TESTNAME` plusargs hook to launch the test.

```systemverilog
module top_tb;
  reg clk = 0;
  always #5 clk = ~clk;
  axi_if axi(clk);
  ahb_if ahb(clk);
  my_dut dut (.axi(axi), .ahb(ahb));
  initial begin
    uvm_config_db#(virtual axi_if)::set(null, "uvm_test_top.sys_env.axi_master*", "vif", axi);
    uvm_config_db#(virtual ahb_if)::set(null, "uvm_test_top.sys_env.ahb_slave*",  "vif", ahb);
    run_test("my_test");
  end
endmodule
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Component-to-Phase Mapping

The following matrix gives a **complete analytical mapping** of every UVC component to the UVM phase in which it acts. This is a high-yield KTU exam table.

| UVC Component | `build_phase` | `connect_phase` | `end_of_elaboration` | `start_of_simulation` | `run_phase` |
|---------------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Sequencer | Constructed | — | — | Starts arbitration | Dispatches items |
| Driver | Constructed | Gets sequencer handle | — | Idle | Drives signals |
| Monitor | Constructed | — | — | Starts observing | Publishes via TLM |
| Agent | Constructed (with `is_active` flag) | Connects monitor to scoreboard | — | — | Manages sub-components |
| Config Object | Retrieved from `cfg_db` | — | Used for last adjustments | — | Read by driver/monitor |
| Coverage Sub | Constructed | Connected to monitor | — | — | Samples bins |
| Scoreboard | Constructed | Receives TLM exports | — | — | Compares transactions |
| Virtual Sequencer | Constructed | Receives sub-sequencer handles | — | — | Hosts virtual sequence |
| Virtual Sequence | Created at start | — | — | — | Body executes scenarios |
| Env (top UVC) | Constructs children | Wires all TLM | Displays topology | — | Manages objections |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Hierarchy Path Resolution for `uvm_config_db`

When the static top sets a config entry, the hierarchy path must match the **full instantiation path** of the consumer. The rule is:

$$\text{Setter path} = \text{Instantiation path in testbench}$$

For example, if a sub-UVC `axi_master` is instantiated inside `sys_env` inside `my_test`, its path is:

$$\texttt{uvm\_test\_top.sys\_env.axi\_master}$$

So the static top sets:

$$\texttt{uvm\_config\_db\#(\cdot)::set(null, "uvm\_test\_top.sys\_env.axi\_master*", "vif", axi)}$$

The `*` wildcard allows the entry to be picked up by *any* descendant of `axi_master` (e.g., the driver, monitor, agent).

The corresponding `get` call uses `this` (the consumer's path) and the `inst_name` (a hierarchy tag), and the `field_name`:

$$\texttt{uvm\_config\_db\#(virtual\_axi\_if)::get(this, "", "vif", vif)}$$

If the `set` path is not a parent or self, the `get` returns `null` and the `\`uvm_fatal` is triggered.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal UVC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sequence Library] --> B[Virtual Sequencer]
    B --> C[Sub Sequencer AXI]
    B --> D[Sub Sequencer APB]
    C --> E[AXI Driver]
    D --> F[APB Driver]
    E --> G[DUT AXI Port]
    F --> H[DUT APB Port]
    G --> I[AXI Monitor]
    H --> J[APB Monitor]
    I --> K[TLM Analysis Port]
    J --> K
    K --> L[System Scoreboard]
    I --> M[Coverage Subscriber]
    J --> M
    N[uvm_config_db] -. provides .-> C
    N -. provides .-> D
    N -. provides .-> I
    N -. provides .-> J
    O[Virtual Interface AXI] -. drives .-> E
    O -. drives .-> I
    P[Virtual Interface APB] -. drives .-> F
    P -. drives .-> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows show **TLM/data flow**.
- Dotted arrows show **configuration flow** from `uvm_config_db`.
- The `Virtual Sequencer` is the only "smart" component that coordinates multiple sub-sequencers.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Testbench Integration Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Static Module top_tb] --> CLK[Clock and Reset Generator]
    TOP --> IF_AXI[AXI Physical Interface]
    TOP --> IF_APB[APB Physical Interface]
    TOP --> DUT[Design Under Test DUT]
    TOP --> SET[uvm_config_db set calls]
    TOP --> RUN[run_test plusarg]
    SET --> ROOT[uvm_root]
    RUN --> ROOT
    ROOT --> TEST[uvm_test_top my_test]
    TEST --> SYSENV[System UVC Environment]
    SYSENV --> SUBENV1[AXI Master Sub UVC]
    SYSENV --> SUBENV2[APB Slave Sub UVC]
    SYSENV --> VSEQ[Virtual Sequencer]
    SYSENV --> SB[System Scoreboard]
    SUBENV1 --> SEQ1[AXI Sequencer]
    SUBENV1 --> DRV1[AXI Driver]
    SUBENV1 --> MON1[AXI Monitor]
    SUBENV2 --> SEQ2[APB Sequencer]
    SUBENV2 --> DRV2[APB Driver]
    SUBENV2 --> MON2[APB Monitor]
    VSEQ -. points to .-> SEQ1
    VSEQ -. points to .-> SEQ2
    MON1 --> SB
    MON2 --> SB
    IF_AXI -. virtual interface .-> DRV1
    IF_AXI -. virtual interface .-> MON1
    IF_APB -. virtual interface .-> DRV2
    IF_APB -. virtual interface .-> MON2
```

**Key observations:**
- The `uvm_root` is the global orchestrator; it is not explicitly instantiated — it is created by the UVM library.
- The `uvm_test_top` is dynamically selected via the `+UVM_TESTNAME=my_test` plusarg.
- The `System UVC Environment` is a *tree* — every component is a child.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: UVM Phase Execution Flow (Integration Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[build_phase TOP DOWN] --> P1[connect_phase BOTTOM UP]
    P1 --> P2[end_of_elaboration_phase BOTTOM UP]
    P2 --> P3[start_of_simulation_phase BOTTOM UP]
    P3 --> P4[run_phase DYNAMIC]
    P4 --> P5[extract_phase TOP DOWN]
    P5 --> P6[check_phase TOP DOWN]
    P6 --> P7[report_phase TOP DOWN]
    P7 --> P8[final_phase TOP DOWN]
```

**Key observations:**
- `build_phase` is **top-down** (parents construct children first).
- All other static phases are **bottom-up** (children connect first, then parents).
- `run_phase` is **time-consuming** and dynamic — all components run concurrently.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Layered Reusability of UVCs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Project A Block Level] --> UVCA[Reusable UVC]
    L2[Project B Subsystem Level] --> UVCA
    L3[Project C Full Chip Level] --> UVCA
    UVCA --> UVC1[AXI Master UVC]
    UVCA --> UVC2[APB Slave UVC]
    UVCA --> UVC3[Memory Model UVC]
    UVC1 --> UVC1A[Sequence Library]
    UVC1 --> UVC1B[Driver]
    UVC1 --> UVC1C[Monitor]
    UVC1 --> UVC1D[Coverage]
```

**Reading:** The same `Reusable UVC` is deployed at three levels of integration (block, subsystem, full-chip) without modification — only configuration changes.
