# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define a Universal Verification Component (UVC). List its four essential building blocks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A UVC is a reusable, encapsulated verification IP block that follows the UVM methodology and provides a standardized API to verify a specific interface, protocol, or subsystem. **(1 mark)**

The four essential building blocks are:
1. **Sequence Library** — generates stimulus. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Sequencer + Driver** — converts transactions to signals. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Monitor** — observes DUT signals and reconstructs transactions. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Coverage Collector + Configuration** — measures verification completeness and allows reusability. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a System UVC? How does it differ from an Interface UVC?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A System UVC is a higher-level UVC that verifies a complete subsystem or chip-level behavior, rather than a single protocol. **(1 mark)**

| Aspect | Interface UVC | System UVC |
|--------|---------------|------------|
| Building block | Atomic | Composed of other UVCs |
| Abstraction | Protocol-level | Scenario-level |
| Example | AXI4 Master | DDR Memory Subsystem |

**(2 marks)** for the differentiation with a valid example.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In testbench integration questions, students often **forget the `\`uvm_component_utils()` macro**, omit the **`super.build_phase()`** call, or **swap the `set` and `get` paths**. These three mistakes alone account for **3–4 marks lost** in a 14-mark question. Always remember: **config flows down (top-down), connection happens bottom-up**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the layered architecture of a SystemVerilog UVC with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the testbench integration topology for an SoC that has one **AXI Master UVC**, one **APB Slave UVC**, and a **virtual sequencer** to coordinate them. Show the `build_phase`, `connect_phase`, and the virtual sequence skeleton. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Layered UVC Architecture (7 Marks):**

The UVC is organized into seven layers. Each layer has a clear responsibility, enabling modularity and reuse.

1. **Transaction Layer — Sequence Item (1 mark):** Defines the data class. E.g., `axi_transaction` with fields `addr`, `data`, `burst_len`. Extends `uvm_sequence_item`.
2. **Sequence Layer (1 mark):** Library of `uvm_sequence` classes that produce meaningful patterns (e.g., `axi_random_read_seq`, `axi_stress_seq`).
3. **Driver Layer (1 mark):** The `uvm_driver` retrieves items from the sequencer and drives them onto a `virtual interface` using a `clocking block`. Handles timing, handshakes, and bus protocol.
4. **Monitor Layer (1 mark):** The `uvm_monitor` passively samples the bus and reconstructs transactions. It publishes to subscribers via TLM `analysis_port`.
5. **Agent Layer (1 mark):** The `uvm_agent` groups sequencer + driver + monitor. It has an `is_active` flag — `UVM_ACTIVE` (driving) or `UVM_PASSIVE` (monitoring only).
6. **Coverage Layer (1 mark):** The `uvm_subscriber` extends a monitor's output with `covergroup` definitions to track functional coverage.
7. **Configuration Layer (1 mark):** The `uvm_config_db` provides the agent with the virtual interface handle and mode flags.

> **KTU Marker Note:** The candidate must include the **block diagram** in the answer. Without it, 2 marks are deducted.

---

**(b) Testbench Integration with Virtual Sequencer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Class Skeleton (1 mark):**
```systemverilog
class soc_env extends uvm_env;
  \`uvm_component_utils(soc_env)
  axi_master_uvc_env    axi_m;
  apb_slave_uvc_env     apb_s;
  soc_vsequencer        vseqr;
  soc_scoreboard        sb;
  function new(string name, uvm_component parent);
    super.new(name, parent);
  endfunction
endclass
```

**Step 2: `build_phase` (2 marks):**
```systemverilog
function void build_phase(uvm_phase phase);
  super.build_phase(phase);
  axi_m  = axi_master_uvc_env::type_id::create("axi_m", this);
  apb_s  = apb_slave_uvc_env ::type_id::create("apb_s", this);
  vseqr  = soc_vsequencer   ::type_id::create("vseqr", this);
  sb     = soc_scoreboard   ::type_id::create("sb", this);
endfunction
```

**Step 3: `connect_phase` (2 marks):**
```systemverilog
function void connect_phase(uvm_phase phase);
  super.connect_phase(phase);
  axi_m.monitor.item_collected_port.connect(sb.axi_export);
  apb_s.monitor.item_collected_port.connect(sb.apb_export);
  vseqr.axi_seqr = axi_m.sequencer;
  vseqr.apb_seqr = apb_s.sequencer;
endfunction
```

**Step 4: Virtual Sequence (2 marks):**
```systemverilog
class soc_vseq extends uvm_sequence;
  \`uvm_object_utils(soc_vseq)
  \`uvm_declare_p_sequencer(soc_vsequencer)
  task body();
    fork
      axi_master_write_seq::type_id::create("wr") .start(p_sequencer.axi_seqr);
      apb_slave_poll_seq  ::type_id::create("pl") .start(p_sequencer.apb_seqr);
    join
  endtask
endclass
```

**Verdict:** The student must demonstrate (i) the `\`uvm_component_utils` registration, (ii) the `\`uvm_declare_p_sequencer` macro for virtual sequencing, and (iii) the `fork…join` parallelism. Any miss = 2 marks lost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the role of the **virtual sequencer** and the **virtual sequence** in a System UVC. How do they coordinate multiple Interface UVCs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **five pillars of testbench integration** with suitable code snippets. Why is the `uvm_config_db` preferred over global variables? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Virtual Sequencer and Virtual Sequence (7 Marks):**

A **virtual sequencer** is a sequencer that does not own a sequence item. Instead, it holds **typed handles** to the sequencers of multiple sub-UVCs. **(2 marks)**

A **virtual sequence** is a sequence that runs on the virtual sequencer. Its `body()` task starts sub-sequences on each sub-sequencer handle, allowing **synchronized, multi-protocol stimulus**. **(2 marks)**

Coordination mechanism:
- Step 1: Virtual sequencer is built in the System UVC's `build_phase`.
- Step 2: In `connect_phase`, the virtual sequencer's handles are pointed to each sub-UVC's sequencer.
- Step 3: The virtual sequence's `body()` calls `sub_seq.start(p_sequencer.<sub_seqr>)` for each protocol.
- Step 4: `fork…join` allows **concurrent** stimulus; `join` allows **serial** stimulus. **(3 marks)**

**Example:**
```systemverilog
class mem_sys_vsequencer extends uvm_sequencer;
  \`uvm_component_utils(mem_sys_vsequencer)
  axi_master_sequencer   axi_seqr;
  apb_config_sequencer   apb_seqr;
endclass
```

---

**(b) Five Pillars of Testbench Integration (7 Marks):**

| Pillar | Description | Code Snippet |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| 1. `uvm_config_db` | Global name-value store | `uvm_config_db#(virtual axi_if)::set(null, "uvm_test_top.env*", "vif", axi);` |
| 2. Virtual Interface | Decouples SV class from static module | `virtual axi_if vif;` inside driver class |
| 3. TLM Connections | Pipes transactions between components | `mon.ap.connect(sb.exp);` |
| 4. Virtual Sequencer | Coordinates multiple UVCs | `vseqr.axi_seqr = axi_env.sequencer;` |
| 5. Reset/Clock Mgmt | Common clock and reset | `always #5 clk = ~clk;` block in `top_tb` |

**(5 marks)** for the table.

**Why `uvm_config_db` over global variables (2 marks):**
1. **Hierarchy-scoped** — only the right component gets the config, avoiding name collisions.
2. **Configurable at runtime** — different tests can pass different configs without recompiling.
3. **Type-safe** — the template parameter `#(T)` ensures the right type is passed.
4. **Debuggable** — UVM has built-in hooks to print the config database state.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [ ] A **UVC** = reusable verification IP built on UVM.
- [ ] A **System UVC** is composed of multiple Interface UVCs and a virtual sequencer.
- [ ] An **Interface UVC** has: sequence, sequencer, driver, monitor, agent, config, coverage.
- [ ] **Five pillars of testbench integration:** `uvm_config_db`, virtual interface, TLM, virtual sequencer, reset/clock management.
- [ ] UVM phase order: `build` (top-down) → `connect` (bottom-up) → `end_of_elaboration` → `start_of_simulation` → `run` → `extract` → `check` → `report` → `final`.
- [ ] **`build_phase`** constructs the hierarchy; **`connect_phase`** wires TLM.
- [ ] **`uvm_config_db::set()`** is called by the parent, **`get()`** is called by the child.
- [ ] **Virtual sequencer** holds handles to sub-sequencers; it does **not** carry sequence items.
- [ ] **Virtual sequence** runs on a virtual sequencer to coordinate multi-UVC scenarios.
- [ ] **TLM `analysis_port`** is a broadcast (1 → many); `analysis_imp` is a sink (1 → 1).
- [ ] **`is_active = UVM_ACTIVE`** means the agent drives; **`UVM_PASSIVE`** means monitor-only.
- [ ] **Static top** (`module top_tb`) sets the `uvm_config_db` and calls `run_test()`.
- [ ] The `+UVM_TESTNAME=<name>` plusarg selects the test class at runtime.
- [ ] The `\`uvm_component_utils()` macro registers a class with the UVM factory.
- [ ] The `\`uvm_declare_p_sequencer()` macro provides the `p_sequencer` handle.
- [ ] **Coverage goal** is typically **95%** in industry, **100%** in safety-critical.
- [ ] Reusability of UVCs is the **primary reason** UVM is the industry standard for SoC verification.
- [ ] Testbench integration **must** be done in the UVM phase order; bypassing phases causes `null` handle errors.
- [ ] A System UVC's **reference model** is often written in C/C++/SystemC and wrapped via DPI.
- [ ] In KTU exams, always draw the **block diagram** — it carries **2–3 marks** of free score.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Circuits Design using SystemVerilog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Circuits Design using SystemVerilog # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Basic Circuits Design using SystemVerilog** refers to the hardware description and synthesis-oriented modeling of fundamental digital building blocks—**combinational circuits** (multiplexers, demultiplexers, encoders, decoders, adders) and **sequential circuits** (latches, flip-flops, counters, shift registers, Finite State Machines)—using the SystemVerilog IEEE 1800 standard HDL. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEVT635 – Module 3):** > "Design of basic combinational and sequential digital circuits using SystemVerilog, including dataflow, behavioral, and structural modeling styles, and synthesis-aware coding practices suitable for ASIC/FPGA implementation." In KTU's outcome-based framework, this topic maps directly to: - **CO3**: *Design digital subsystems using SystemVerilog HDL with appropriate modeling abstraction.* - **CO4**: *Apply verification methodologies to validate synthesized RTL designs.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Architect's Blueprint"** > Imagine constructing a house. A **schematic diagram** is like the *visual blueprint* showing where every wire and gate connects. **SystemVerilog code** is the *written, machine-readable architectural specification* that an automated construction crew (the **synthesis tool**, e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler, Xilinx Vivado) reads to actually *build* the chip. > > Just as a blueprint must be unambiguous (else the builder misplaces a wall), SystemVerilog code must obey strict **synthesis semantics**—the rules a tool uses to translate text into transistors. The **Three Modeling Abstractions** in SystemVerilog are: | Abstraction Level | Analogy | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Gate-Level** | Pre-fabricated LEGO bricks snapped together | Post-synthesis netlist, technology mapping | | **Dataflow (RTL)** | Recipe using ingredients and equations | Continuous logic, arithmetic operations | | **Behavioral** | "Cook the dish until golden" — describes *what*, not *how* | Algorithms, FSMs, complex control logic | ## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics to Remember - **Clock Period (T_clk)** — measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)** - **Propagation Delay (t_pd)** — time for a signal to travel from input to output of a gate - **Setup Time (t_su)** and **Hold Time (t_h)** — timing constraints for flip-flops, both in **ns** - **Maximum Operating Frequency (f_max)** — measured in **MHz** or **GHz** - **Standard Logic Values:** `0`, `1`, `x` (unknown), `z` (high-impedance) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SystemVerilog Design Flow (The "Why" Behind Each Step) > [!NOTE] > **Why is the design flow important for KTU?** > Board questions frequently ask students to *explain the steps* or *differentiate between simulation and synthesis*. Memorize this flow. 1. **Specification** — Define the circuit's behavior (truth table, state diagram, timing). 2. **Modeling** — Write SystemVerilog using one of three styles: - **Structural:** Instantiate primitive gates (`and`, `or`, `not`). - **Dataflow:** Use `assign` with Boolean/logic expressions. - **Behavioral:** Use `always` procedural blocks. 3. **Simulation (Functional Verification)** — Run testbenches to confirm logic correctness *pre-synthesis*. 4. **Synthesis** — The tool maps RTL to a technology-specific gate-level netlist. 5. **Place & Route** — Physical layout on silicon. 6. **Post-Synthesis Timing Simulation** — Verify timing constraints are met. ## 2.2 Core SystemVerilog Constructs — "The How" ### A. Continuous Assignment (Dataflow) Used for **combinational** logic. Drives outputs whenever an input changes. ```systemverilog assign Y = (S) ? D1 : D0; // 2:1 MUX ``` ### B. Procedural `always` Blocks Two critical flavors: | Construct | Sensitivity List | Synthesis Inference | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | `always @(*)` | All RHS signals (implicit) | Combinational logic | MUX, decoder, adder | | `always @(posedge clk)` | Clock edge | Sequential element (Flip-Flop) | Registers, counters, FSMs | | `always @(negedge clk or posedge rst)` | Async reset | FF with async reset | Reset-dominated designs | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting items in the sensitivity list of a combinational `always` block causes **simulation vs. synthesis mismatch**. Always use `always @(*)` for combinational logic in modern SV (post-IEEE 1800-2005). ### C. Blocking (`=`) vs. Non-Blocking (`<=`) Assignments - **Blocking `=`** — Executes immediately, sequentially. Used in **combinational** and **task** code. - **Non-Blocking `<=`** — Schedules update at the end of the time step. Used for **sequential** elements (FFs) to model parallel hardware behavior. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Golden Rule:** *In `always @(posedge clk)` blocks, ALWAYS use non-blocking assignments (`<=`). Mixing blocking and non-blocking for the same signal causes race conditions.* ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Key equations and synthesis-mapped constructs every KTU student must memorize.** | Concept | SystemVerilog Construct | Synthesis Output | Unit / Standard | |---|---|---|---| | **2:1 MUX** | `assign Y = S ? D1 : D0;` | 3-input AND/OR/NOT | Logic gates | | **Full Adder Sum** | $S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$ | XOR + AND/OR | Boolean algebra | | **Full Adder Carry** | $C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B))$ | Majority gate | Boolean algebra | | **D Flip-Flop** | `always @(posedge clk) Q <= D;` | 1-bit edge-triggered FF | Storage cell | | **N-bit Counter (freq)** | $f_{out} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{2^{N}}$ | N-bit ripple/synchronous counter | Hz | | **Setup Time Constraint** | $t_{clk} \geq t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{su}$ | Timing path constraint | seconds (s) | | **Max Frequency** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk,\,min}}$ | Critical path inverse | Hz | | **Binary Encoding** | Gray code: $G_{i} = B_{i+1} \oplus B_{i}$ | Power-efficient FSM encoding | Bit-wise | | **Moore Output** | Depends on current state only | Output decoder block | State register | | **Mealy Output** | Depends on state and input | Combinational output block | State register + input | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constraint — Never use the pipe `|` character inside table cells.** Use `\vert` (e.g., $G \vert B$) or `\mid` (e.g., $G \mid B$) to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ASIC/FPGA Design:** Every digital chip you use (your smartphone's SoC, a Xilinx FPGA in a Tesla's ECU) is designed via RTL code similar to the constructs above. - **Verification & Testing:** Module 3 of PEEVT635 bridges to Module 4 (Testbench architecture) — the same SV modules you design here become the **DUT (Design Under Test)** in the testbench. - **DFT Integration:** Sequential elements designed here (FFs) are later augmented with scan chains for manufacturing test (covered in later modules). - **Industry Tools:** Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium, Mentor Questa all consume SystemVerilog IEEE 1800-2017. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern:** The 14-mark question in Module 3 typically asks the student to *(a)* draw the truth table / state diagram, and *(b)* write the synthesizable SystemVerilog code for a given basic circuit. We walk through **four canonical designs** that are board favorites. ## 3.1 Design Walkthrough #1 — 4:1 Multiplexer (Dataflow + Behavioral) ### Step 1 — Specification (Truth Table) | S1 | S0 | Y | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0 | I0 | | 0 | 1 | I1 | | 1 | 0 | I2 | | 1 | 1 | I3 | ### Step 2 — Boolean Equation Derivation We sum the minterms where Y = 1: $$ Y = \overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0}\,I_0 \;+\; \overline{S_1}\,S_0\,I_1 \;+\; S_1\,\overline{S_0}\,I_2 \;+\; S_1\,S_0\,I_3 $$ **Logic:** The output is selected by the binary-encoded select lines. Each AND term acts as a "gate" that opens only for one input combination. ### Step 3 — SystemVerilog Implementation (Three Styles) **Style 1 — Dataflow (using `assign`):** ```systemverilog module mux4x1 ( input logic [3:0] I, input logic [1:0] S, output logic Y ); assign Y = I[S]; // Indexed selection — synthesizable endmodule ``` **Style 2 — Behavioral with `case`:** ```systemverilog module mux4x1_behav ( input logic [3:0] I, input logic [1:0] S, output logic Y ); always @(*) begin case (S) 2'd0 : Y = I[0]; 2'd1 : Y = I[1]; 2'd2 : Y = I[2]; 2'd3 : Y = I[3]; default : Y = 1'bx; endcase end endmodule ``` > [!NOTE] > The `default` arm is **mandatory** in `case` statements inside combinational `always` blocks. Omitting it causes inferred latches (a major KTU valuation pitfall). **Style 3 — Structural (using primitives):** ```systemverilog module mux4x1_struct ( input logic I0, I1, I2, I3, input logic S0, S1, output logic Y ); wire nS0, nS1, t0, t1, t2, t3; not (nS0, S0); not (nS1, S1); and (t0, nS1, nS0, I0); and (t1, nS1, S0, I1); and (t2, S1, nS0, I2); and (t3, S1, S0, I3); or (Y, t0, t1, t2, t3); endmodule ``` --- ## 3.2 Design Walkthrough #2 — 4-Bit Synchronous Up-Counter ### Step 1 — State Transition Table | Present State (Q3 Q2 Q1 Q0) | Next State (Q3+ Q2+ Q1+ Q0+) | |:---:|:---:| | 0000 | 0001 | | 0001 | 0010 | | ... | ... | | 1110 | 1111 | | 1111 | 0000 (wrap) | ### Step 2 — Excitation Equations (D-FF based) For each bit, the next-state equals the input to its D flip-flop: $$ D_0 = \overline{Q_0} $$ $$ D_1 = Q_1 \oplus Q_0 $$ $$ D_2 = Q_2 \oplus (Q_1 \cdot Q_0) $$ $$ D_3 = Q_3 \oplus (Q_2 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_0) $$ **Logic:** A bit toggles when all less-significant bits are 1 — this is the ripple-carry condition realized in parallel. ### Step 3 — Frequency Derivation For an **N-bit counter**, the output bit $Q_0$ toggles every clock cycle, $Q_1$ every 2 cycles, $Q_2$ every 4 cycles, etc. $$ f_{Q_k} = \frac{f_{clk}}{2^{(k+1)}} $$ For a 4-bit counter fully wrapped (carry-out from MSB): $$ f_{wrap} = \frac{f_{clk}}{2^{4}} = \frac{f_{clk}}{16} $$ ### Step 4 — SystemVerilog Implementation ```systemverilog module counter_4bit ( input logic clk, input logic rst_n, // Active-low asynchronous reset output logic [3:0] Q ); always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin if (!rst_n) Q <= 4'b0000; else Q <= Q + 4'b0001; end endmodule ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synthesis Inference:** The synthesis tool recognizes `Q <= Q + 1` and infers a **synchronous up-counter** with a carry-chain adder — no need to manually code the toggle equations. --- ## 3.3 Design Walkthrough #3 — D Flip-Flop with Asynchronous Reset ### Step 1 — Truth Table | clk | rst_n | D | Q_next | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | ↑ | 0 | x | 0 | | ↑ | 1 | 0 | 0 | | ↑ | 1 | 1 | 1 | | otherwise | x | x | Q (no change) | ### Step 2 — SystemVerilog Code ```systemverilog module dff_async_reset ( input logic clk, input logic rst_n, input logic D, output logic Q ); always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin if (!rst_n) Q <= 1'b0; else Q <= D; end endmodule ``` > [!NOTE] > The presence of `negedge rst_n` in the sensitivity list (alongside `posedge clk`) tells the synthesis tool to use the reset pin **asynchronously**. Without it, the reset becomes synchronous. --- ## 3.4 Design Walkthrough #4 — Mealy FSM: "Sequence Detector for '101'" ### Step 1 — State Diagram (ASCII Representation) ``` 1/0 1/0 1/0 (S0) ───────► (S1) ───────► (S2) ───────► (S2) │ 0/0 │ 0/0 │ 0/0 │ 0/0 └─────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────┘ (and Z=1 at S2→S2 on input 1) ``` - **S0:** Initial state (no bits matched). - **S1:** Last bit was '1' (matched first bit of "101"). - **S2:** Last two bits were "10" (matched first two bits of "101"). - **Z = 1** when in S2 and input is '1' (the third bit completes the pattern). ### Step 2 — State Transition Table (Mealy) | Present State | Input (X) | Next State | Output (Z) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | S0 | 0 | S0 | 0 | | S0 | 1 | S1 | 0 | | S1 | 0 | S0 | 0 | | S1 | 1 | S2 | 0 | | S2 | 0 | S0 | 0 | | S2 | 1 | S2 | 1 | ### Step 3 — State Encoding (Binary) $$ S_0 = 2'b00, \quad S_1 = 2'b01, \quad S_2 = 2'b10 $$ ### Step 4 — SystemVerilog Implementation ```systemverilog module seq_det_101_mealy ( input logic clk, input logic rst_n, input logic X, output logic Z ); typedef enum logic [1:0] { S0, S1, S2 } state_t; state_t ps, ns; // State register always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin if (!rst_n) ps <= S0; else ps <= ns; end // Next-state and output logic always @(*) begin ns = S0; Z = 1'b0; case (ps) S0 : begin ns = (X) ? S1 : S0; Z = 1'b0; end S1 : begin ns = (X) ? S2 : S0; Z = 1'b0; end S2 : begin ns = (X) ? S2 : S0; Z = (X) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0; // Mealy: output depends on input end default : begin ns = S0; Z = 1'b0; end endcase end endmodule ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Differentiation:** > - **Mealy:** Output depends on present state **AND** current input (faster response, 1-cycle earlier output). > - **Moore:** Output depends **only** on present state (more stable, glitch-free output). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SystemVerilog Design & Verification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Problem Specification
Truth Table / State Diagram] --> B[SystemVerilog Modeling
Dataflow / Behavioral / Structural] B --> C[Functional Simulation
using Testbench] C --> D{Logic Correct?} D -- No --> E[Debug and Fix RTL] E --> B D -- Yes --> F[Logic Synthesis
Technology Mapping] F --> G[Gate-Level Netlist] G --> H[Place and Route
Physical Layout] H --> I[Post-Silicon Validation] I --> J[Fabrication and Testing] ``` ## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a 4:1 Multiplexer ```mermaid flowchart LR I0[Input I0] --> A0 I1[Input I1] --> A1 I2[Input I2] --> A2 I3[Input I3] --> A3 S0[Select S0] --> A0 S0 --> A1 S1[Select S1] --> A0 S1 --> A1 S1 --> A2 S1 --> A3 S0 --> A2 S0 --> A3 A0[AND Gate] --> OR[OR Gate] A1[AND Gate] --> OR A2[AND Gate] --> OR A3[AND Gate] --> OR OR --> Y[Output Y] ``` ## 4.3 Mealy FSM Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Input X] --> NS_LOGIC[Next State Logic
Combinational] PS_REG[Present State
Register FFs] --> NS_LOGIC PS_REG --> OUT_LOGIC[Output Logic
Combinational] NS_LOGIC --> PS_REG OUT_LOGIC --> OUT[Output Z] IN --> OUT_LOGIC ``` ## 4.4 Synchronous Counter Architecture (N-bit) ```mermaid flowchart LR CLK[Clock] --> FF0[FF Q0] CLK --> FF1[FF Q1] CLK --> FF2[FF Q2] CLK --> FF3[FF Q3] FF0 --> INC[Increment Logic] INC --> FF1 FF1 --> INC FF1 --> FF2 FF2 --> FF3 FF0 --> Q_OUT[Q3 Q2 Q1 Q0] FF1 --> Q_OUT FF2 --> Q_OUT FF3 --> Q_OUT RST[Reset rst_n] --> FF0 RST --> FF1 RST --> FF2 RST --> FF3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEVT635):** > - **Part A (2 marks each, no choice):** Conceptual definitions. > - **Part B (14 marks each, internal choice):** Design + coding + analysis. > - Module 3 typically carries **15–20% weightage** in the End Semester Exam. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the three modeling styles in SystemVerilog. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** **Model Answer:** 1. **Structural Modeling:** Describes a circuit as a netlist of interconnected primitive gates and modules. Mirrors a gate-level schematic. 2. **Dataflow Modeling:** Describes data flow through the circuit using continuous assignments (`assign`) and Boolean expressions. Focuses on *how data transforms*. 3. **Behavioral Modeling:** Describes the *functional behavior* of the circuit using procedural blocks (`always`, `initial`), without specifying internal structure. Highest level of abstraction. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between blocking (`=`) and non-blocking (`<=`) assignments in SystemVerilog. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Blocking (`=`) | Non-Blocking (`<=`) | |---|---|---| | **Execution** | Sequential, immediate | Deferred to end of time step | | **Use Case** | Combinational logic, initial blocks, tasks | Sequential logic (flip-flops, FSMs) | | **Hardware Model** | Software-like execution | True parallel hardware behavior | | **Race Conditions** | Susceptible | Immune (within same always block) | | **Synthesis Inference** | Combinational gates / latches | Edge-triggered flip-flops | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Tip:** Most students write only "blocking is for combinational, non-blocking is for sequential." For full marks, mention the **simulation semantics** (NBA region vs. active region) and the **race-condition immunity** aspect. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Internal Choice Format:** "Answer any ONE full question from the following. Each carries 14 marks, split into part (a) for 7 marks and part (b) for 7 marks." --- ### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1): Design a 4-Bit Synchronous Counter **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Draw the block diagram and state transition table of a **4-bit synchronous up-counter** with **active-low asynchronous reset**. Explain its operation in detail. **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** ``` ┌──────────────┐ clk ────►│ Counter │──── Q[3:0] rst_n ──►│ (4-bit) │ └──────────────┘ ``` **State Transition Table (excerpt):** | Present Q | Next Q (Q+1) | |:---:|:---:| | 0000 | 0001 | | 0001 | 0010 | | 0010 | 0011 | | ... | ... | | 1110 | 1111 | | 1111 | 0000 | **Explanation:** - The counter increments by 1 on every rising edge of `clk`. - When `rst_n = 0` (asynchronously, regardless of clock), the counter resets to `0000`. - The counter **wraps around** from `1111` to `0000` automatically due to 4-bit overflow. - Output frequency from MSB: $f_{MSB} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{16}$. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks Write the **synthesizable SystemVerilog RTL code** for the above counter. Explain why non-blocking assignments are used. **Model SystemVerilog Code:** ```systemverilog module sync_counter_4bit ( input logic clk, input logic rst_n, output logic [3:0] Q ); always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin if (!rst_n) Q <= 4'b0000; // Non-blocking else Q <= Q + 1'b1; // Non-blocking, automatic wrap end endmodule ``` **Why Non-Blocking (`<=`)?** 1. Models true **parallel hardware** — all FFs update simultaneously at the clock edge. 2. Prevents **race conditions** between read and write to `Q` within the same time step. 3. Synthesis tools explicitly expect non-blocking assignments in `posedge clk` blocks to infer edge-triggered flip-flops. --- ### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2): Design a Mealy Sequence Detector **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 (Alternative)]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Design a **Mealy FSM** that detects the overlapping sequence "**1011**" from a serial input bit stream. Draw the state diagram and derive the state transition table. **Model Solution:** **State Definitions:** - **S0:** Reset / No prefix matched. - **S1:** "1" matched. - **S2:** "10" matched. - **S3:** "101" matched. - Output **Z = 1** when input makes it "1011" → triggers in S3 on input `1` and moves to S1 (overlap). **State Transition Table:** | Present State | X = 0 | X = 1 | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | S0 | S0 / 0 | S1 / 0 | | S1 | S0 / 0 | S1 / 0 | | S2 | S0 / 0 | S3 / 0 | | S3 | S0 / 0 | S1 / 1 | #### Part (b) — 7 Marks Write the **SystemVerilog RTL code** for this Mealy FSM using `enum` and `always` blocks. Provide a brief testbench. **Model SystemVerilog Code:** ```systemverilog module seq_det_1011_mealy ( input logic clk, input logic rst_n, input logic X, output logic Z ); typedef enum logic [1:0] { S0, S1, S2, S3 } state_t; state_t ps, ns; // State register always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin if (!rst_n) ps <= S0; else ps <= ns; end // Next state and output (Mealy) always @(*) begin ns = S0; Z = 1'b0; case (ps) S0 : ns = (X) ? S1 : S0; S1 : ns = (X) ? S1 : S0; S2 : ns = (X) ? S3 : S0; S3 : begin ns = (X) ? S1 : S0; Z = (X) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0; end default : begin ns = S0; Z = 1'b0; end endcase end endmodule ``` **Simple Testbench:** ```systemverilog module tb_seq_det; logic clk = 0; logic rst_n, X, Z; seq_det_1011_mealy uut (.clk(clk), .rst_n(rst_n), .X(X), .Z(Z)); always #5 clk = ~clk; // 100 MHz clock initial begin rst_n = 0; X = 0; #12 rst_n = 1; // Apply: 1 0 1 1 0 1 1 1 (expect Z=1 at last '1' of first 1011) X = 1; #10; X = 0; #10; X = 1; #10; X = 1; #10; X = 0; #10; X = 1; #10; X = 1; #10; X = 1; #10; $finish; end endmodule ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Mistakes (4-mark deductions typical):** > 1. ❌ Forgetting the `default` arm in `case` — causes inferred latches. > 2. ❌ Using `posedge clk` in the combinational `always @(*)` block. > 3. ❌ Mixing blocking `=` for state register (must be non-blocking `<=`). > 4. ❌ Omitting `else` branch in `if (!rst_n)`, causing latches on the counter. > 5. ❌ Confusing Moore with Mealy: writing the output to depend on the input when asked for Moore (and vice versa). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Basic Circuits Design using SystemVerilog** ### ✅ Core Constructs - [ ] **Three modeling styles:** Structural, Dataflow, Behavioral — know definitions and code examples. - [ ] **`assign`** → combinational dataflow; **`always @(*)`** → combinational behavioral; **`always @(posedge clk)`** → sequential. - [ ] **Blocking `=`** for combinational / tasks; **Non-blocking `<=`** for sequential registers. ### ✅ Combinational Circuits You Must Know - [ ] **MUX (2:1, 4:1, 8:1)** — both `?:` ternary and `case` styles. - [ ] **DEMUX** — `case` block with `default` arm. - [ ] **Encoder / Decoder** — `case` and `assign` with minterms. - [ ] **Full Adder / Ripple Carry Adder** — from dataflow Boolean to structural gate-level. ### ✅ Sequential Circuits You Must Know - [ ] **D Latch** vs. **D Flip-Flop** — sensitivity list difference (`@(*)` vs. `@(posedge clk)`). - [ ] **Asynchronous vs. Synchronous Reset** — sensitivity list tells the synthesis tool. - [ ] **Synchronous Up/Down Counter** — `Q <= Q + 1` and `Q <= Q - 1`; MSB frequency = $f_{clk}/2^N$. - [ ] **Shift Register** — `Q <= {Q[N-2:0], serial_in}` for left shift. ### ✅ FSM Design - [ ] **Moore:** output depends on state only — output block reads from state register. - [ ] **Mealy:** output depends on state + input — output block reads from both. - [ ] Always use `typedef enum` for state declarations in modern SV. - [ ] Three-process style (state register, next-state logic, output logic) is preferred for clarity. ### ✅ Synthesis Pitfalls to Avoid - [ ] Incomplete `if`/`case` → inferred latches (KTU's #1 deduction cause). - [ ] Mixing blocking and non-blocking for the same register. - [ ] Hard-coded delays (`#5`) in synthesizable code — synthesizers **ignore** them. - [ ] Initial values in `always_ff` (use reset instead, for portability across FPGA/ASIC libraries). ### ✅ KTU Board Buzzwords to Use - "Synthesizable RTL" / "Inference" / "Sensitivity list" / "Edge-triggered" / "Latch inference" / "Race condition" / "NBA region" / "Mealy vs. Moore" / "Asynchronous reset" / "Synchronous logic"

topics.title: Procedural Statements and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Procedural Statements and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **IEEE 1800-2017 SystemVerilog** standard (the verification language adopted under the KTU 2024 PEEVT635 syllabus), **Procedural Statements** are the executable behavioral constructs that drive simulation time. They are statements that execute sequentially within a procedural block, allowing the designer or verification engineer to model complex algorithmic behavior, decision-making, and iterative logic.

**Procedural Statements** in SystemVerilog include:
- **Timing-controlled statements** (`@event`, `wait`, `#delay`)
- **Conditional statements** (`if`, `if-else`, `case`, `casex`, `casez`, `unique`, `priority`)
- **Loop statements** (`for`, `while`, `do-while`, `repeat`, `forever`, `foreach`)
- **Procedural assignment statements** (blocking `=` and non-blocking `<=`)

A **Function** in SystemVerilog is a *subroutine* that encapsulates a piece of procedural logic, executes in **zero simulation time**, returns a single value (or is declared `void`), and has **no output or inout ports**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Board-Examiner Favourite):**
> *"A SystemVerilog function is a behavioral encapsulation that executes atomically in zero simulation time, returns exactly one value through its return mechanism, and is forbidden from containing any timing-control statements or invoking tasks."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a restaurant kitchen (the procedural block)**:

- **Procedural Statements** are the **individual cooking instructions** inside a recipe card: *"stir for 2 minutes, if it thickens then reduce heat, while boiling add salt, repeat the mixing 3 times."*
- A **Function** is like a **pre-prepared spice mix on your shelf** — you grab it (call it), it instantly returns a combined flavor (a return value), and you can sprinkle it into many different recipes (reuse) without re-creating it. The spice mix never takes time to "cook itself" — it is always ready (zero simulation time).
- A **Task** (the alternative subroutine) is like **cooking a side dish** — it can take time, involves actual cooking (timing controls), and produces a finished plate of food (can have multiple outputs).

This analogy is critical for KTU exams: examiners often ask students to explain *why* a function cannot contain `#delay` — the answer is that an already-pre-made spice mix does not need to cook!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO Alignment):**
> - This topic directly maps to **CO3**: *Apply procedural and functional constructs in SystemVerilog testbench development.*
> - It is also a foundational prerequisite for **CO4** (Randomization, coverage-driven verification).
> - Cognitive level targeted: **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** in Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### 1.3 Procedural Block Types — The Four Variants

SystemVerilog refined the legacy `always` block into **four specialized variants** to enable synthesis-friendly and verification-friendly coding. KTU 2024 specifically tests these:

| Block Type | Triggering Sensitivity | Synthesis/Verification Use |
|---|---|---|
| `always` | Explicit (must list) or implicit (inferred) | **Discouraged** — legacy style |
| `always_comb` | Combinational logic — **auto-sensitive** to RHS | Combinational RTL & verification |
| `always_ff` | Triggered on **clock edges** | Sequential / flip-flop RTL |
| `always_latch` | Triggered on level changes | Latch-inferred logic (rare) |

> [!TIP]
> The `always_comb` and `always_ff` blocks were introduced in SystemVerilog to **eliminate the common bug** of missing sensitivity lists in legacy Verilog `always` blocks. In KTU exams, writing `always @*` in an answer may be considered outdated — prefer `always_comb`.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Blocking vs Non-Blocking Assignments — The "Why"

In SystemVerilog, two assignment operators carry **profoundly different semantics** for both simulation ordering and synthesis race conditions. This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU ESE papers.

#### 2.1.1 Blocking Assignment `=`
- Executes **immediately** and **sequentially**.
- The LHS is updated **before** the next statement in the same procedural block begins.
- In **zero simulation time**, all blocking assignments in a block complete in textual order.
- Used for **combinational logic** modeling.

#### 2.1.2 Non-Blocking Assignment `<=`
- The RHS is **evaluated immediately**, but the **LHS update is deferred** until all events at the current simulation time have been processed.
- At the end of the time slot, all NBA regions update together.
- Used for **sequential logic / pipelined / register-transfer** modeling.
- Prevents **race conditions** in testbenches driving the DUT.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick**: *Blocking = "Block everything else until I finish"*. *Non-Blocking = "Note it down, do it at the end of the time step"*.

---

### 2.2 Conditional Statements — The "Priority" Family

SystemVerilog adds the `unique` and `priority` modifiers to the standard `if` and `case` constructs to give the simulator *extra information* about mutually-exclusive or one-hot conditions. KTU 2024 examiners love this distinction.

| Modifier | Applicable To | Run-Time Check | KTU Mark Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| `unique if` | `if-else if` chain | Reports error if **no** condition is true (X more than one path matches) | **High** — very frequently asked |
| `priority if` | `if-else if` chain | Reports error if **no** condition is true (X guaranteed 1-hot) | **High** |
| `unique case` | `case` | No two case expressions match; no default needed | **High** |
| `priority case` | `case` | Exactly one case must match | **High** |

The case variants `casex` and `casez` treat `'x` and `'z` (or only `'z`) as **don't-cares** during comparison. These are **discouraged in synthesis** but remain useful in verification for handling unknown bit patterns.

---

### 2.3 Loop Constructs — The Iteration Family

SystemVerilog provides **six loop constructs**, each with specific niche uses:

| Loop | Iteration Style | Verification Use Case | Synthesis Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `for` | Bounded counter | Memory initialization, bit-bashing | Yes |
| `while` | Pre-test condition | Wait for an event to clear | Rarely |
| `do-while` | Post-test condition | At least one iteration guaranteed | Rarely |
| `repeat` | Count-based | "Run N times" — N can be an expression | Limited |
| `forever` | Infinite | Clock generation, monitor loops | Never |
| `foreach` | Multi-dimensional array traversal | Bit-bashing, packet field checking | Yes (synthesis-2017+) |

The `foreach` loop is the **newest SystemVerilog addition** and is a KTU favourite because it elegantly traverses dynamic and unpacked arrays.

---

### 2.4 Functions — The Zero-Time Subroutine

A **function** in SystemVerilog:
1. **Executes in zero simulation time** — no `#`, `@`, or `wait` allowed.
2. **Returns exactly one value** — either via an explicit `return` statement, or by assigning the function's implicit return variable (same name as the function).
3. **Has no output/inout ports** — only input ports.
4. **Can be `automatic` (stack-allocated, re-entrant) or `static` (shared, default)**.
5. **Can be `void`** — SystemVerilog extension; a `void` function acts like a task in zero time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In SystemVerilog (unlike old Verilog), a function **can** call other functions and **can** be recursive if declared `automatic`. This is a very common 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Time Domain | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blocking assign | `a = b + c;` | Immediate, sequential | Use for combinational |
| Non-blocking assign | `a <= b + c;` | End-of-time-step, parallel | Use for sequential |
| `always_comb` | Auto-sensitive combinational | Zero-time re-eval on RHS change | Preferred over `always@*` |
| `always_ff @(posedge clk)` | Edge-sensitive | Edge-triggered | Use for flip-flops |
| `unique case` | Mutually-exclusive guard | Tool checks exclusivity | "If you know the patterns never overlap" |
| `priority case` | One-hot guard | Tool checks at-least-one match | "If one MUST match" |
| `foreach` | `foreach(arr[i,j])` | Compact array traversal | "Loop over a 2-D array" |
| Function return | `return value;` | Zero-time exit with value | "Modern SV style" |
| Automatic function | `function automatic int add(...);` | Stack-allocated, re-entrant | "Required for recursion" |
| `void` function | `function void display(...);` | Zero-time side-effect only | "Like a task in zero time" |
| Recursive depth | `automatic` keyword | Default 8 levels | "Increase via `depth = N`" |

> **Note on Table Symbols:** All absolute-value and conditional expressions in this note are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to remain markdown-table safe. For example, $\vert x \vert$ is the magnitude and $a \mid b$ is the divisibility / guard condition.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in VLSI Verification

In a real **Universal Verification Methodology (UVM)** testbench, procedural statements and functions form the **spine of every component**:
- **Transaction randomization** uses `foreach` over dynamic arrays.
- **Reference models** are built as a hierarchy of `automatic` functions for math operations.
- **Scoreboards** use `forever` loops with `@event` triggers.
- **Functional coverage** uses `case` with `priority` to ensure all FSM states are hit.
- **Clock generators** use `initial begin ... forever #5 clk = ~clk; end`.

Mastery of this topic is therefore a **mandatory pre-requisite** for the UVM module that follows in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Simulation Scheduling of Blocking vs Non-Blocking

SystemVerilog follows the **IEEE 1800 stratified event scheduler**. This section derives the *effective ordering* of blocking (`=`) versus non-blocking (`<=`) assignments at a single simulation time slot.

#### 3.1.1 The Five Scheduling Regions

The Verilog/SystemVerilog reference simulator maintains the following event regions per simulation time step:

1. **Active Region** — Blocking assignments, continuous assignments, evaluation of primitives.
2. **Inactive Region** — `#0` delayed statements.
3. **NBA (Non-Blocking Assignment Update) Region** — LHS updates of non-blocking assignments.
4. **Postponed Region** — `$strobe` and `$monitor` events.
5. **Time advances** to the next pending event.

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Ordering Derivation

Let us analyze a classic 2-FF swap race scenario:

```systemverilog
always @(posedge clk) begin
    a = b;        // Statement 1 — Blocking
    b = a;        // Statement 2 — Blocking
end
```

Step 1: At simulation time $t$, the **Active region** fires on the rising clock edge.

Step 2: Statement 1 executes. The RHS `b` is read, producing $a_{\text{new}} = b_{\text{old}}$.

Step 3: Statement 2 executes. The RHS `a` is read, but since Statement 1 has **already updated** `a` in the Active region, $b_{\text{new}} = a_{\text{new}} = b_{\text{old}}$.

Step 4: The net effect at time $t$ is $a = b = b_{\text{old}}$ — **the value of `a` is lost**. This is a race.

Now consider the non-blocking version:

```systemverilog
always @(posedge clk) begin
    a <= b;
    b <= a;
end
```

Step 1: At simulation time $t$, the **Active region** fires.

Step 2: Both RHS expressions are **evaluated immediately** and stored in the NBA queue:
- NBA_1: $a \leftarrow b_{\text{old}}$
- NBA_2: $b \leftarrow a_{\text{old}}$

Step 3: Active region completes; the **NBA region** then performs the deferred LHS updates atomically. The queue is processed in scheduling order (FIFO per block):
- $a_{\text{new}} = b_{\text{old}}$
- $b_{\text{new}} = a_{\text{old}}$

Step 4: Net effect: `a` and `b` are correctly **swapped** with no race.

#### 3.1.3 Mathematical Summary

The two semantics can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Blocking: } \quad LHS_{t} \;=\; RHS_{t}^{(\text{updated previous line})}
$$

$$
\text{Non-Blocking: } \quad LHS_{t^{+}} \;=\; RHS_{t}^{(\text{original snapshot})}
$$

where $t^{+}$ denotes the NBA region at the end of time $t$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Function Recursion Depth

A `function automatic` may call itself. The IEEE 1800 standard specifies a **default recursion depth of 8** levels for verification efficiency. To override, the syntax is:

```systemverilog
function automatic int factorial(int n);
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    return n * factorial(n - 1);
endfunction
```

The compiler enforces:

$$
\text{Stack frames} \;\le\; \text{default depth} \;=\; 8
$$

For factorial of 5, the call graph is:

$$
f(5) \rightarrow f(4) \rightarrow f(3) \rightarrow f(2) \rightarrow f(1)
$$

which requires **5 stack frames**, safely within the default 8. For factorial of 12, the requirement becomes:

$$
12 - 1 = 11 \;\text{frames needed}, \quad \text{exceeds default}
$$

The `depth` keyword allows explicit override:

```systemverilog
function automatic int factorial(int n);
    // body
endfunction : factorial
```

The `automatic` qualifier makes the function **stack-allocated** (each invocation has its own local memory), which is the formal requirement for safe recursion. A `static` function shares one memory region, so recursion on a `static` function **corrupts variables** and is a KTU common pitfall.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Function vs Task (KTU Favourite)

| Property | Function | Task |
|---|---|---|
| Simulation time consumption | **Zero** | Can consume time |
| Timing controls (`#`, `@`, `wait`) | **Forbidden** | Allowed |
| Return value | Exactly one (or `void`) | Zero or more (via output/inout) |
| Can call other functions | **Yes** | Yes |
| Can call tasks | **No** | Yes |
| Output / inout ports | **Not allowed** | Allowed |
| `automatic` qualifier | Required for recursion | Required for parallel launch |
| `static` qualifier | Default | Default |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Coverage Case Statement

A common 14-mark KTU question asks to model an FSM decoder with `unique case`. The analytical solution is:

```systemverilog
typedef enum logic [1:0] {IDLE, RUN, DONE, ERR} state_t;

always_comb begin
    unique case (current_state)
        IDLE: next_state = (start) ? RUN : IDLE;
        RUN : next_state = (done)  ? DONE : RUN;
        DONE: next_state = (ack)   ? IDLE : DONE;
        ERR : next_state = (reset) ? IDLE : ERR;
    endcase
end
```

The `unique` keyword instructs the simulator: *"if no case matches AND no default is present, raise a runtime error"*. For an FSM with $N$ states, the exhaustive check guarantees no implicit latch inference.

$$
\text{Total transitions validated} \;=\; 2^{N} \;-\; 1 \quad \text{(excluding all-X)}
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Simulation Event Scheduling — Block Diagram

The IEEE 1800 event scheduling flow is the single most important structural concept to visualize.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Time t Event Fires"] --> nodeB["Active Region Eval"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Blocking Assignments ="]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Continuous Assign Eval"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Primitive Outputs"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Inactive Region #0 Delays"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["NBA Region Deferred Updates <="]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Postponed Region strobe and monitor"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Any Pending Events?"}
    nodeI -- "Yes" --> nodeJ["Advance Time to Next Event"]
    nodeJ --> nodeA
    nodeI -- "No" --> nodeK["End Simulation"]
```

> **Reading guide:** Notice the **NBA region is downstream** of the Active region. This is the geometric reason non-blocking assignments are race-free — RHS is *snapshotted* in Active, and LHS is *updated* in NBA after all Active logic has settled.

---

### 4.2 Function vs Task Architectural Flow

A **functional architecture flow** comparing what each subroutine can and cannot do:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeCaller["Caller Block always or initial"] --> nodeQ{"Subroutine Type?"}
    nodeQ -- "Function" --> nodeF1["Inputs Only"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2["Zero Simulation Time"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3["Single Return Value"]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4["No # or @ Inside"]
    nodeQ -- "Task" --> nodeT1["Inputs Outputs Inouts"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2["Time Can Pass"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["Multiple Side Effects"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4["# and @ Allowed"]
```

> **Reading guide:** This is a quick-reference architecture map. The **left branch (Function)** is the constrained, fast path; the **right branch (Task)** is the powerful, time-consuming path. KTU examiners often ask students to draw a similar two-column comparison.

---

### 4.3 `foreach` Loop Iteration Topology

The `foreach` loop over a 2-D unpacked array visits elements in **row-major order**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeArr["Unpacked Array arr 2x3"] --> nodeR0["Row 0"]
    nodeR0 --> nodeA00["arr 0 0"]
    nodeR0 --> nodeA01["arr 0 1"]
    nodeR0 --> nodeA02["arr 0 2"]
    nodeArr --> nodeR1["Row 1"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeA10["arr 1 0"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeA11["arr 1 1"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeA12["arr 1 2"]
```

The corresponding SystemVerilog statement is:

```systemverilog
foreach (arr[i,j]) begin
    $display("arr[%0d][%0d] = %h", i, j, arr[i][j]);
end
```

Iteration order is fixed by the language: outermost index is the **first `foreach` index**, inner indices follow left-to-right in the loop signature. This predictability is what makes `foreach` ideal for verification reference models.

---

### 4.4 Unique vs Priority Case Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Evaluate case expression"] --> nodeCheck{"How many case items match?"}
    nodeCheck -- "Exactly 0" --> nodeUniqueA["unique case RAISES ERROR"]
    nodeCheck -- "Exactly 1" --> nodeExec["Execute that arm"]
    nodeCheck -- "More than 1" --> nodeUniqueB["unique case RAISES ERROR"]
    nodeCheck -- "Exactly 0" --> nodePrioA["priority case RAISES ERROR"]
    nodeCheck -- "Exactly 1" --> nodeExecP["Execute that arm"]
    nodeCheck -- "More than 1" --> nodeExecFirst["priority case EXECUTES FIRST MATCH"]
```

> **Reading guide:** `unique` = *exclusive-OR*; `priority` = *first-one-wins*. Use `unique` for one-hot encoders, `priority` for interrupt controllers.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the difference between blocking and non-blocking procedural assignments in SystemVerilog. Why is non-blocking preferred for sequential logic modeling?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Blocking `=`**: Executes immediately; the LHS is updated before the next statement runs. Used for combinational and verification model logic. *(1 mark)*
2. **Non-blocking `<=`**: RHS is evaluated immediately but the LHS update is deferred to the NBA region of the current time slot, eliminating simulator race conditions. *(1 mark)*
3. **Why preferred for sequential logic:** In flip-flop modeling, all RHS values must be read from the *old* state. Non-blocking guarantees that all registers sample the *same* pre-edge state, preventing the swap-race error seen with blocking assignments. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State any three rules that a SystemVerilog function must follow.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. A function **executes in zero simulation time**; it cannot contain any delay (`#`), event control (`@`), or `wait` statements.
2. A function **returns exactly one value** (or is declared `void`); it cannot have `output` or `inout` arguments.
3. A function **cannot invoke a task**; it may only call other functions, and if recursive calls are made, it must be declared `automatic`.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice 1)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Module 3, 7 marks]**
*With suitable code examples, explain the SystemVerilog conditional modifiers `unique` and `priority` as applied to `case` statements. How do they help in verification?*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition and Syntax (2 marks):**
- `unique case` asserts at run-time that *exactly one* case item matches the case expression and that the case is exhaustive.
- `priority case` asserts that *at least one* case item matches; if multiple match, the first in source order is executed.

```systemverilog
unique case (opcode)
    2'b00: alu_op = ADD;
    2'b01: alu_op = SUB;
    2'b10: alu_op = AND;
    2'b11: alu_op = OR;
endcase
```

**How They Help Verification (3 marks):**
1. **Exhaustiveness check:** If a corner-case opcode is missed in test enumeration, `unique case` immediately flags a simulation error, shortening the debug loop.
2. **Encoding discipline:** Forces the verification engineer to enumerate all states of an FSM, preventing silent default-path execution.
3. **Coverage closure:** Combined with functional coverage points, `unique case` ensures the testbench reaches every valid state at least once, accelerating coverage-driven verification methodology sign-off.

**Contrast with `casex` and `casez` (2 marks):**
- `casex` treats both `'x` and `'z` as don't-care.
- `casez` treats only `'z` as don't-care.
- These are useful in *pattern-matching verification* (e.g., matching a bus with unknown upper bits) but are **forbidden in synthesizable RTL** because they imply X-optimism.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Module 3, 7 marks]**
*Write a SystemVerilog `automatic` recursive function to compute the Fibonacci number $F(n)$, where $F(0)=0$, $F(1)=1$, and $F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2)$ for $n \ge 2$. Explain why the `automatic` qualifier is essential for correct recursion. What is the default recursion depth in SystemVerilog?*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Code (3 marks):**

```systemverilog
function automatic int fib(int n);
    if (n < 0) begin
        $error("Invalid input to fib: %0d", n);
        return -1;
    end
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    if (n == 1) return 1;
    return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2);
endfunction
```

**Why `automatic` is essential (2 marks):**
- A `static` function uses a single shared memory region for its local variables across all calls. If `fib` were `static`, the local argument `n` and intermediate return values would be **shared** between the recursive invocations, causing values to be overwritten and producing wrong results.
- An `automatic` function allocates a **new stack frame** for each invocation. Each recursive call has its own private copy of `n` and its own return address, so the recursion is well-defined.

**Default recursion depth (1 mark):**
- The IEEE 1800 standard specifies a **default recursion depth of 8** for functions. For `fib`, the depth grows as the input grows (e.g., `fib(7)` requires 8 levels). For larger inputs, the system task `$stack_size` and the `depth` qualifier can be used to raise the limit.

**Trace of `fib(4)` (1 mark):**

$$
fib(4) = fib(3) + fib(2) = (fib(2)+fib(1)) + (fib(1)+fib(0))
$$

$$
= ((fib(1)+fib(0))+1) + (1+0) = (1+0+1) + 1 = 3
$$

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Module 3, 7 marks]**
*Explain the four procedural block types in SystemVerilog: `always`, `always_comb`, `always_ff`, and `always_latch`. Discuss which is the most appropriate for combinational verification logic and why.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**The Four Block Types (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **`always`**: Legacy Verilog block. The user *must* list the sensitivity list explicitly (e.g., `@(a, b, sel)`). Missing a signal causes simulation-synthesis mismatch. **Discouraged** in SystemVerilog.

2. **`always_comb`**: SystemVerilog block that *automatically* builds the sensitivity list from the RHS expressions. It triggers once at simulation time 0 and on every subsequent change of any RHS signal. Designed for combinational logic.

3. **`always_ff @(posedge clk [, posedge rst])`**: SystemVerilog block restricted to edge-sensitive statements. Designed to model flip-flops and registered sequential logic. Compiler checks that the block is purely sequential (no asynchronous latches, no multiple clock edges).

4. **`always_latch`**: SystemVerilog block intended to model level-sensitive latches. Warns the user if the inferred logic is not latch-like.

**Best for Verification Combinational Logic (2 marks):**
- `always_comb` is the **only correct choice** for combinational verification models (e.g., reference models, scoreboard combiners).
- It auto-detects missing sensitivity bugs at compile time, eliminating the famous *Verilog `always @*` mistake* of forgetting a signal.
- In UVM, all combinational reference models are written using `always_comb` blocks, which integrate seamlessly with assertions (`assert property`).

**Code Example (1 mark):**

```systemverilog
always_comb begin
    case (sel)
        2'b00: y = a;
        2'b01: y = b;
        2'b10: y = c;
        2'b11: y = d;
    endcase
end
```

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Module 3, 7 marks]**
*Compare SystemVerilog functions and tasks in a tabular form with at least six points. State one scenario where a `void` function is preferred over a task.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (6 marks — 0.5 mark each for 12 entries, take 6 salient ones):**

| Criterion | Function | Task |
|---|---|---|
| Return value | Exactly one (or `void`) | None required |
| Time consumption | Zero | May consume time |
| Timing controls | Forbidden | Allowed |
| I/O ports | Inputs only | Inputs, outputs, inouts |
| Calling other functions | Yes | Yes |
| Calling tasks | No | Yes |
| Recursion | Allowed (with `automatic`) | Allowed (with `automatic`) |
| `static` vs `automatic` | Both | Both |
| Use in assertions | Preferred (zero-time) | Not preferred |
| Synthesis | Limited | Generally not synthesized |
| Calling context | Expressions | Statements |
| Multiple returns | Single (via `return` or name) | Through `output` ports |

**Scenario Where `void` Function is Preferred Over a Task (1 mark):**
- A `void` function is preferred when a verification component needs to **perform a side-effect action (e.g., printing, logging, queue update, coverage sampling) without consuming simulation time**.
- Example: A `void` function `log_transaction()` that is called inside an `always_comb` block to record a transaction in a mailbox. Because `always_comb` cannot contain time-consuming statements, a task would not be legal; a `void` function works.

```systemverilog
function void log_transaction(bit [31:0] data);
    $display("[%0t] TX logged: %h", $time, data);
    cov_sample.sample(data);
endfunction
```

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PEEVT635 Module 3 Answers:**
> 1. **Mixing blocking and non-blocking in the same `always_ff` block** — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this. Either all blocking (combinational) or all non-blocking (sequential).
> 2. **Forgetting `automatic` while writing a recursive function** — the code compiles but produces wrong results, and the examiner will mark it as 0/7 for the recursion question.
> 3. **Calling a task from inside a function** — illegal in SystemVerilog. Many students attempt this in transaction generator questions and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing `always @(*)` in the answer** — outdated Verilog-2001 syntax. KTU 2024 expects `always_comb` for full marks.
> 5. **Confusing `priority case` and `unique case`** — these are *opposite* in semantics. Priority = at least one matches, may match more. Unique = exactly one matches, no more, no less.
> 6. **Treating `void` function as equivalent to a task** — a `void` function is still zero-time. A task with no outputs is *not* a `void` function.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Procedural Statements and Functions**

- [ ] **Blocking `=`** is sequential and immediate; **Non-blocking `<=`** is deferred to the NBA region.
- [ ] Use `always_comb` for combinational, `always_ff` for sequential — never mix in synthesis code.
- [ ] `unique case` = exactly one match allowed; `priority case` = at least one match required.
- [ ] `casex` treats both `x` and `z` as don't-care; `casez` treats only `z` as don't-care.
- [ ] A **function** executes in zero simulation time, returns one value, has no `output` ports, and cannot contain timing controls.
- [ ] A **task** can consume time, has full port directions, and is invoked as a statement.
- [ ] A **function cannot call a task** — but a **task can call a function**.
- [ ] Use `function automatic` for **recursion** — `static` functions share state and corrupt recursion.
- [ ] The IEEE 1800 **default recursion depth is 8**.
- [ ] A **`void` function** is a zero-time side-effect routine — a hybrid between a function and a task.
- [ ] The `foreach` loop iterates **row-major** over unpacked arrays.
- [ ] NBA region sits *after* the Active region in the event scheduler — the structural reason for race-free non-blocking semantics.
- [ ] In KTU 2024 ESE, expect at least one 7-mark question on `unique`/`priority` and one 7-mark question on function recursion per paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of OOPs Concepts in SystemVerilog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementation of OOPs Concepts in SystemVerilog

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for **PEEVT635 – VLSI Verification & Testing**, **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts in SystemVerilog** refer to the set of class-based programming constructs introduced by the IEEE 1800-2017 (and 2012) SystemVerilog standard that allow verification engineers to model complex, reusable, and modular verification environments. The four foundational OOP pillars implemented in SystemVerilog are:

1. **Encapsulation** — Bundling of data (class properties) and the methods (functions/tasks) that operate on that data into a single unit called a *class*, while restricting direct external access via access qualifiers such as `local` and `protected`.
2. **Abstraction** — Hiding internal implementation details through **virtual methods** and **pure virtual methods**, exposing only essential interfaces.
3. **Inheritance** — A mechanism by which a *derived/child* class acquires the properties and methods of a *base/parent* class, enabling code reuse and hierarchical extension.
4. **Polymorphism** — The ability of a base class handle to invoke the appropriate *overridden* method of a derived class at **run-time** through SystemVerilog's **virtual method dispatch table** mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SystemVerilog OOP is a **core Module 3** topic because it forms the backbone of the **Universal Verification Methodology (UVM)**. Without mastery of `class`, `extends`, `virtual`, and `super`, a student cannot write a single UVM component (driver, monitor, sequencer, agent, env, test).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real World Intuition

Imagine a **smartphone factory**. Each phone is an **object** (an *instance*) built from a single **blueprint** (the *class*). The blueprint defines what data the phone holds (battery %, IMEI number, contact list) and what actions it can perform (call, browse, click photo).

- **Encapsulation** is like the phone's sealed back cover — you can press buttons (call the methods), but you cannot directly tamper with the internal circuit board (the `local` properties).
- **Inheritance** is like a *Pro Max* model that inherits all features of a standard phone, then adds a telephoto lens and a bigger battery — the *derived* class extends the *base*.
- **Polymorphism** is the *single "shutter button"* — when an entry-level phone and a Pro Max both receive the click, each fires its own *different* camera pipeline, even though the same button (base class handle) was pressed.
- **Abstraction** is the *user manual* — it tells you "press this to take a photo" but hides the lens-control, sensor-read, and JPEG-encode pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Class):** A *class* in SystemVerilog is a user-defined data type that encapsulates data (properties) and subroutines (methods), and serves as a template from which *objects* (class instances) are dynamically created at simulation time using the `new()` constructor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Handle vs Object):** A *handle* is a reference (pointer) to an object; the object itself is the dynamically allocated memory block. This distinction is **critical** in SystemVerilog because assigning one handle to another does **not** copy the object — it only copies the reference.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Bold

- **Static class property lifetime:** Entire duration of the **simulation** (i.e., from time **0** to `$finish`).
- **Automatic (non-static) class property lifetime:** Tied to the **lifetime of the object**; released when no handle references the object.
- **Default access qualifier in SystemVerilog classes:** `public` (no keyword required) — accessible from anywhere in the scope.
- **Maximum inheritance depth:** SystemVerilog allows **single inheritance only** for classes; multiple inheritance is achieved through the use of **interfaces** (interfaces can extend multiple interfaces).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of OOP in SystemVerilog — Structured Analysis

### 2.1.1 Encapsulation (Data Hiding)

Encapsulation in SystemVerilog is enforced through **three access qualifiers**:

| Qualifier | Visibility | Use Case |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| `public` (default) | Accessible from any scope | Public API methods (`get_full_name()`) |
| `protected` | Accessible within the class and its **derived** classes | Helper methods, internal state for inheritance |
| `local` | Accessible **only** within the class itself | Sensitive data (e.g., password, internal counter) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `local` is **NOT** inherited. A `local` member in a base class is **completely inaccessible** to derived classes. Use `protected` when you want derived classes to see the member.

### 2.1.2 Inheritance

SystemVerilog supports **single class inheritance** via the `extends` keyword. A derived class automatically inherits all `public` and `protected` members. The constructor of the base class is invoked **first** (automatically, before the derived class body runs) — unless explicitly overridden using `super.new()`.

**Polymorphism requires the `virtual` keyword** in the base class. Without `virtual`, the method call is resolved **statically** (at elaboration time) based on the *handle type*, not the *object type*.

### 2.1.3 Polymorphism (Dynamic Dispatch)

When a method is declared `virtual` in a base class and overridden in a derived class with the **same signature**, the call is resolved at **run time** based on the actual object the handle points to. This is implemented internally via a **virtual method table (v-table)** — a per-class lookup table populated at elaboration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Virtual Method):** A method whose call-site resolution is **deferred until run time**, allowing a base class handle to invoke a derived class override. In SystemVerilog, virtuality is an explicit property declared with the `virtual` keyword in the base class.

### 2.1.4 Abstract Classes (Pure Virtual Methods)

A class containing at least one **pure virtual method** is called an **abstract class**. A pure virtual method is declared with the `pure virtual` keyword and has **no implementation body**. Abstract classes **cannot be instantiated** directly — they serve as base classes for derived classes that provide the concrete implementation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | SystemVerilog Syntax | Memory / Lifetime | KTU Significance |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Class Declaration | `class ClassName; … endclass` | None until `new()` | Defines blueprint |
| Object Creation | `ClassName h = new();` | Dynamic (heap) | Creates instance |
| Handle Assignment | `h2 = h1;` | Copies reference only | Both point to same object |
| Inheritance | `class Child extends Parent;` | Single inheritance only | Code reuse |
| Virtual Method | `virtual function void display();` | v-table entry | Enables polymorphism |
| Pure Virtual | `pure virtual function void run();` | No body | Abstract class marker |
| Encapsulation | `local`, `protected` | Compile-time check | Data hiding |
| Static Property | `static int count;` | Global, simulation-lifetime | Shared across instances |
| `this` keyword | `this.var = x;` | Self-reference | Disambiguates name collision |
| `super` keyword | `super.run();` | Base class call | Calls parent's method |
| Shallow Copy | `h2 = new h1;` | Copies handle refs | Built-in |
| Deep Copy | Custom `copy()` method | Recursive duplication | Manual implementation |
| Parameterized Class | `class Mem #(type T = bit);` | Templated | Generic data structures |
| Null Handle | `h = null;` | Pointer is invalid | Causes run-time error on use |

**Units / Boundaries:**
- **Automatic (default) class properties:** simulation-time allocated, deallocated when last handle drops reference.
- **Static class properties:** allocated at simulation start (time **0**), persist until `$finish`.
- **Virtual method dispatch latency:** **0 simulation time units** — resolution happens in the same event region as the call.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **UVM-based verification environment** (which is the industry standard taught in KTU's follow-up modules):

- **Encapsulation** is used to hide the internal state of UVM components (e.g., the sequencer's internal queue of pending sequences is `local`).
- **Inheritance** is the foundation of UVM class hierarchy: `uvm_test` extends `uvm_component` extends `uvm_object`.
- **Polymorphism** enables the UVM factory pattern: a base `uvm_component` handle can be reassigned at run time to any derived component (`uvm_driver`, `uvm_monitor`, etc.) without changing the testbench topology.
- **Abstract classes** are used to enforce that all derived drivers implement a standardized interface (e.g., `uvm_driver::run_phase()` is pure virtual).

> [!NOTE]
> In industry (Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, NXP), every modern ASIC/SoC verification environment is built using these four OOP pillars in SystemVerilog. Mastering them is **non-negotiable** for a verification engineer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **conceptual derivations and analytical reasoning** behind SystemVerilog OOP constructs. Since OOP is a design discipline (not a numerical computation), the "derivations" here take the form of **logical construction steps**, **code-construction walkthroughs**, and **memory-model derivations** of how the SystemVerilog simulator handles these constructs.

## 3.1 Derivation: How a Class Object Is Allocated in Memory

### Step 1 — Class Declaration is Just a Blueprint

When the SystemVerilog elaborator encounters a `class` declaration, it does **not** allocate any memory. The class body is stored in the simulator's type table as a *type descriptor*.

$$
\text{Elaborator} : \texttt{class Packet;} \longrightarrow \text{TypeDescriptor}_{\text{Packet}} \;\; (\text{no memory yet})
$$

### Step 2 — Handle Declaration Creates a Pointer

Declaring a class variable creates a **handle** (a reference / pointer) which is initially `null`.

$$
\text{ClassName } h; \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; h = \text{null}_{\text{handle}}
$$

The handle itself occupies a fixed size in memory (typically 64 bits on a 64-bit simulator) but points to nothing.

### Step 3 — `new()` Allocates the Object

The `new()` built-in method (a.k.a. the **constructor**) dynamically allocates memory on the simulator's **automatic storage** and returns a handle to it.

$$
h = \text{new}(); \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{heap} \leftarrow \text{allocate}(\text{sizeof(properties)}), \;\; h \leftarrow \text{address}_{\text{new}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Every property declared in the class is allocated as a member of the object; the constructor's return value is a handle pointing to the start of this block.

### Step 4 — Handle Assignment Copies Only the Reference

$$
h_2 = h_1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; h_2.\text{addr} = h_1.\text{addr}
$$

Now both `h1` and `h2` point to the **same** underlying object. Modifying the object through `h1` is visible through `h2`.

### Step 5 — Object Lifetime Ends When No Handle Refers

The SystemVerilog simulator uses **reference counting**. When the last handle is overwritten (e.g., `h1 = null;` or `h1 = new_obj;`) the object is deallocated. This is conceptually equivalent to:

$$
\text{refCount}(obj) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{deallocate}(obj)
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Method Dispatch Resolution

| Handle Type (Compile-time view) | Object Type (Run-time reality) | `virtual` keyword in base? | Method actually called |
|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| Base | Base | N/A (no override) | Base method |
| Base | Derived (override exists) | **Yes** | **Derived method** (polymorphism) |
| Base | Derived (override exists) | **No** | **Base method** (static binding) |
| Derived | Derived | Yes | Derived method |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reasoning for the third row:** Without `virtual`, the simulator resolves the call at **elaboration** time using the *handle's declared type*. This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction: A Polymorphic Class Hierarchy

**Goal:** Build a base class `Transaction` and a derived class `ReadTransaction` that overrides a `display()` method.

**Step 1 — Declare the base class with a `virtual` method:**

The base class is defined first. The method is marked `virtual` to enable run-time dispatch.

**Step 2 — Declare the derived class using `extends`:**

The derived class uses the `extends` keyword and redeclares the method with the **same signature** to *override* the base.

**Step 3 — Construct handles and objects:**

A base-class handle `t` is created and assigned a derived-class object. The call `t.display()` now resolves via the v-table to the **derived** version.

**Step 4 — Verify dynamic dispatch:**

If the base method were **not** marked `virtual`, `t.display()` would call the base version regardless of the object type. With `virtual`, the derived version runs.

> **Conceptual result:**
>
> $$
> \text{v-table}_{\text{Base}} = \{ \text{display} \to \text{Base::display} \}
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{v-table}_{\text{Derived}} = \{ \text{display} \to \text{Derived::display} \}
> $$
>
> Since `t` (base handle) points to a `Derived` object, the call `t.display()` consults `v-table_Derived` and dispatches to `Derived::display`.

## 3.4 Deep Copy vs Shallow Copy — Derivation of Behaviour

### Shallow Copy: `new h1` Syntax

$$
h_2 = \text{new } h_1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \forall \text{property } p : h_2.p = h_1.p
$$

- For **value-type** properties (e.g., `bit`, `int`): the value is duplicated.
- For **handle-type** properties (e.g., nested class objects): only the **handle** is copied; both objects share the inner reference.

### Deep Copy: Custom `copy()` Method

$$
\text{For each handle property } p : h_2.p = \text{new } h_1.p
$$

This recurses through the entire object graph, allocating fresh objects at each level.

| Property Kind | Shallow Copy Behaviour | Deep Copy Behaviour |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| `bit [31:0] addr` | Value duplicated | Value duplicated |
| `Packet payload` (class) | Handle shared | New payload object created |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering relevance:** In UVM, transactions are deep-copied from the sequencer to the driver to prevent data corruption when the same sequence item is reused across multiple drivers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — OOPs Pillar Interaction Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **four OOP pillars** in SystemVerilog and shows how they interlink to form a UVM-style reusable class hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Base Class] -->|extends| nodeB[Derived Class]
    nodeA -->|declares virtual method| nodeC[Virtual Method Table v-table]
    nodeC -->|resolves at run time| nodeD[Polymorphic Dispatch]
    nodeA -->|contains local and protected members| nodeE[Encapsulation Layer]
    nodeE -->|access qualifier public| nodeF[Public API]
    nodeE -->|access qualifier protected| nodeG[Derived Class Access]
    nodeE -->|access qualifier local| nodeH[Class Private Access]
    nodeA -->|has pure virtual method| nodeI[Abstract Class]
    nodeI -->|cannot instantiate| nodeJ[Forces Concrete Derived]
    nodeB -->|uses super dot new| nodeK[Constructor Chain]
    nodeB -->|uses this dot property| nodeL[Self Reference]
    nodeB -->|static property count| nodeM[Shared Across Instances]
    nodeD -->|foundation of| nodeN[UVM Factory Pattern]
    nodeJ -->|foundation of| nodeN
    nodeF -->|foundation of| nodeN
    nodeN -->|used in| nodeO[Industry Verification Environments]
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The four pillars (`Encapsulation`, `Inheritance`, `Polymorphism`, `Abstraction`) are the left half of the diagram; the right half shows how SystemVerilog-specific keywords (`virtual`, `pure virtual`, `local`, `protected`, `super`, `this`, `static`) attach to those pillars; the far right shows the **engineering outcome** — the UVM factory pattern that drives modern ASIC verification.
