3. **Backtracking inefficiency:** When the algorithm backtracks from a deep internal-line decision, it must undo many downstream implications, wasting computation.

4. **No exploitation of PI constraints:** All decisions are treated uniformly whether at a PI or deep inside the circuit, ignoring the natural structural advantage of PIs.

5. **Impractical for large circuits:** Published results show D-Algorithm becomes infeasible beyond ~100 gates.

**Why PODEM (1981) was proposed:**
PODEM restricted *all decisions* to Primary Inputs, eliminating internal-line decisions entirely. By using **forward implication only**, PODEM avoids undoing complex internal implications on backtrack. The **objective-backtrace-decision** cycle is simpler, and the algorithm is provably complete despite being simpler than D-Algorithm. Empirically, PODEM runs 10×–100× faster than D-Algorithm on typical circuits.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **PODEM algorithm** with the help of an illustrative example. Demonstrate detection of the $s\text{-}a\text{-}0$ fault at line $C$ in the circuit $F = (A \land B) \lor C$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** $F = A \cdot B + C$, **Fault:** $C \equiv s\text{-}a\text{-}0$

**Step 1 — Good vs Faulty:**

$$F_{good} = AB + C,\quad F_{faulty} = AB + 0 = AB$$

**Step 2 — Detection condition:**
Need $F_{good} \neq F_{faulty}$. Specifically need $F_{good} = 1$ (so $C = 1$) and $F_{faulty} = 0$ (so $AB = 0$).

But $F_{good} = 1$ requires $C = 1$. So this fault is **untestable** for this simple form! Let us re-verify:

- If $C = 0$: $F_{good} = AB$, $F_{faulty} = AB$ → same
- If $C = 1$: $F_{good} = 1$, $F_{faulty} = AB$ → different only if $AB = 0$
  - $A=0, B=0, C=1$: $F_{good} = 1, F_{faulty} = 0$ → detected! ✓

**Step 3 — PODEM trace:**

Initialize: $A = X, B = X, C = X$

**Objective 1:** Activate fault at $C$ — set $C = 1$ in good circuit.
Backtrace: $C$ is itself a PI. Try $C = 0$ first.
Forward imply: $F = AB$. No fault effect.
Try $C = 1$: $F = 1$. Fault not yet propagated through AND gate.

**Objective 2:** Make $AB = 0$ in faulty circuit so output shows discrepancy.
Backtrace to PI: try $A = 0$.
Forward imply: $F_{good} = 0 + 1 = 1$, $F_{faulty} = 0 \cdot B = 0$.
Output: $F = D$ (good=1, faulty=0) → **Test found: (A=0, B=X, C=1)** ✓

**Step 4 — Final PODEM output:**

$$\boxed{A = 0,\quad B = X,\quad C = 1,\quad F = D}$$

**(b)** Compare **PODEM** and **FAN** algorithms. Why is FAN preferred for VLSI circuits with high fanout? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Dimension | PODEM | FAN |
|----------------------|-------|-----|
| Decision granularity | Single PI per objective | Multiple objectives per decision |
| Backtrace direction | From internal line → nearest PI | From PO cone → head line |
| Fanout treatment | Implicit (treated as ordinary lines) | Explicit (head lines + multiple backtrace) |
| Sensitization logic | Generic frontier propagation | Unique sensitization points prioritized |
| Computational complexity | $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ with multiple backtrace |

**Why FAN excels for high-fanout VLSI circuits:**

1. **Head-line abstraction:** FAN recognizes that fanout stems are the natural decision bottlenecks. By limiting decisions to head lines, FAN eliminates redundant backtraces down reconvergent fanout branches.

2. **Multiple backtrace via D-intersection:** When several objectives demand different values at a shared PI, the D-intersection immediately detects conflict and triggers backtrack *before* committing to a decision — saving exponential exploration.

3. **Unique sensitization points:** In a circuit with $N$ gates having output $G_i$, FAN identifies the single gate whose output uniquely controls fault propagation at any moment — this dramatically reduces the propagation search.

4. **Empirical results from Fujiwara-Shimono (1983):** On benchmark circuits like ISCAS c432, c499, c880, FAN achieved 3×–10× speedup over PODEM and detected all testable faults where PODEM often timed out.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse **fanout branches** with **fanout stems**. A fanout **stem** is the *single line* before the branch point; the **branches** are the multiple destination lines *after* the branch. FAN treats branches as head lines, but decisions on the stem are typically not made directly — this distinction is a common marking trap in 14-mark questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Combinational ATPG** generates test vectors for purely combinational circuits using the 5-valued logic $\{0, 1, D, \bar{D}, X\}$.
- **D-Algorithm (1966):** First complete algorithm; uses PDCF (Primitive D-Cube of Fault), PDC (Propagation D-Cube), and Singular Cover for line justification; decisions allowed on internal lines.
- **PODEM (1981):** Decisions restricted to **Primary Inputs only**; uses objective-backtrace-decision cycle; forward implication only; far more efficient than D-Algorithm.
- **FAN (1983):** Adds **head lines**, **multiple backtrace**, and **unique sensitization points**; best suited for high-fanout industrial circuits; reduced complexity from $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
- **D-cube algebra:** $D \cdot 1 = D$, $D \cdot 0 = 0$, $D + 0 = D$, $D + 1 = 1$ — these rules govern how $D/\bar{D}$ flows through AND/OR gates.
- **Fault coverage formula:** $FC = \vert D_{detected} \vert / \vert F_{total} \vert \times 100\%$ — a critical KTU exam formula.
- **Complete vs heuristic:** All three algorithms are **complete** (guaranteed to find a test if one exists), but differ in practical efficiency.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the *detection condition* $F_{good} \oplus F_{faulty} = 1$ explicitly when solving worked examples — losing marks for omitting this equation is the #1 student mistake.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Generation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Test Generation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Generation Systems** are automated computational frameworks (hardware or software) that systematically produce a set of input stimulus vectors (test patterns) capable of detecting a specified class of faults in a digital circuit under test (CUT). The goal is to achieve maximum **Fault Coverage (FC)** with the minimum number of test vectors, measured against a target fault model (typically the **stuck-at fault model** in KTU syllabus contexts).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Test Generation System is the bridge between the **Fault Model** (abstract defect representation) and the **Test Application Mechanism** (the ATE – Automatic Test Equipment). It encapsulates the algorithms (D-Algorithm, PODEM, FAN, SAT-based), the fault simulator, and the test compaction logic.

### Key Performance Metrics

- **Fault Coverage (FC):** The percentage of detected faults out of the total modeled faults.
- **Test Vector Length (N):** Total number of input stimulus words applied.
- **Test Generation Time (T_gen):** Computational complexity in CPU seconds.
- **Diagnostic Resolution:** Ability to pinpoint the exact fault location.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **deterministic ATPG** (Automatic Test Pattern Generation) over random pattern testing, and explicitly includes **Path-Oriented Decision Making (PODEM)**, **FAN algorithm**, and **SAT-based ATPG** as high-yield topics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor examining 1,000 patients** in a hospital. Each patient may or may not have a specific disease (fault). You cannot give every patient every possible medical test — that would be too expensive and time-consuming.

- **Faults** = Possible diseases
- **Test Patterns** = Specific medical tests (blood test, X-ray, ECG)
- **Test Generation System** = The diagnostic protocol — a smart decision tree that says: "If symptom A is present, run Test B; otherwise, skip to Test C."
- **Fault Coverage** = Percentage of diseases correctly identified.
- **Test Length** = Total tests administered.

The system does **not** try every possible test on every patient (that would be exhaustive testing = $2^n$ patterns for $n$ inputs). Instead, it intelligently **prunes** the test space using fault simulation, sensitization, and justification logic.

> [!NOTE]
> For a circuit with 50 primary inputs, exhaustive testing would require $2^{50} \approx 1.125 \times 10^{15}$ patterns — impossible! Hence, **Test Generation Systems** are engineering compromises that balance coverage, cost, and time.

## 1.3 Classification of Test Generation Systems

| Category | Description | KTU Example |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Exhaustive** | All $2^n$ input combinations applied | Trivially complete, infeasible |
| **Pseudo-Exhaustive** | Each cone of logic gets all $2^k$ patterns ($k$ = cone width) | Segmented testing |
| **Random** | Pseudo-random patterns with weighted bias | LFSR-based BIST |
| **Deterministic (ATPG)** | Algorithmically targets each specific fault | D-Algorithm, PODEM, FAN |
| **Hybrid** | Combines random + deterministic + compaction | Industrial flows (Synopsys TetraMAX) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ATPG Problem — Formal Statement

Given:
- A **combinational** or **sequential** circuit $C$ with $n$ primary inputs (PIs) and $m$ primary outputs (POs).
- A **fault list** $F = \{f_1, f_2, ..., f_k\}$ derived from a target fault model.

Find:
- A set of test patterns $T = \{t_1, t_2, ..., t_p\}$ such that each fault $f_i \in F$ is **detected** by at least one $t_j \in T$.

Optimization goals:
- Minimize $p = \vert T \vert$ (test length).
- Maximize FC.
- Minimize computational time.

## 2.2 Foundational Concepts (Five-Valued Logic)

Most KTU-relevant ATPG algorithms (D-Algorithm, PODEM) use **five-valued logic** or **nine-valued logic** to capture signal behavior under fault presence.

### Five-Valued Logic (Roth's Original D-Calculus)

| Symbol | Meaning | Both Fault-Free & Faulty Values |
|--------|---------|--------------------------------|
| **D** | $\bar{D}$ in faulty circuit | 1 / 0 |
| **$\bar{D}$** | $D$ in faulty circuit | 0 / 1 |
| **0** | 0 / 0 | (Same in both) |
| **1** | 1 / 1 | (Same in both) |
| **X** | Unknown / Unassigned | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D-frontier:** The set of gates whose outputs are currently **D** or **$\bar{D}$** while all inputs are justified. The algorithm must **propagate** D through this frontier to a primary output (PO).

## 2.3 Core Algorithmic Steps (Universal ATPG Template)

Every classical deterministic ATPG follows this skeleton:

1. **Fault Activation (Sensitization):** Set the target fault site to its activating value (e.g., $s-a-1$ requires the line to be driven to **0** in the fault-free circuit).
2. **Fault Propagation (D-drive):** Propagate the fault effect (D or $\bar{D}$) along a sensitized path to a primary output.
3. **Line Justification (Consistency):** Set remaining PIs to values that internally satisfy all gate output requirements (backtracking step).
4. **Implication:** Apply Boolean logic implications to determine the consequences of each decision. If a conflict arises → **backtrack**.
5. **Test Compaction:** Merge compatible test vectors to reduce the test cube size.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Fault Coverage** | $FC = \dfrac{F_{detected}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Primary ATPG metric |
| **Defect Level** | $DL \approx 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ | Williams-Brown Model; $Y$ = yield |
| **Test Length Lower Bound** | $\lceil \log_2(F_{total}) \rceil$ | Information-theoretic minimum |
| **Exhaustive Patterns** | $N_{exh} = 2^n$ | $n$ = primary input count |
| **Pseudo-Random Coverage** | $FC(t) = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)^t$ | $k$ = input cone width, $t$ = patterns |
| **Transition Fault Probability** | $P_{tf} = \dfrac{N_{tf-detected}}{N_{tf-total}}$ | At-speed testing metric |
| **N-Detect Coverage** | $FC_N = \dfrac{\sum_i \min(N_i, N)}{N_{total}}$ | $N_i$ = times fault $i$ is detected |
| **Controllability (C0, C1)** | Approximate via SCOAP | Sandia Controllability/Observability Program |
| **Observability (O)** | Approximate via SCOAP | Measures ease of observing a node |
| **Implication cone** | Set of lines forced by a PI assignment | Used in PODEM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SCOAP Metrics (Sandia Controllability/Observability Analysis Program):** Six integer values (CC0, CC1, CO for combinational; SC0, SC1, SO for sequential). Lower values = easier to control/observe. KTU often asks: *"Calculate CC1 of a 2-input AND gate if both inputs have CC1 = 3."*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Test Generation Systems are the **backbone of design-for-test (DFT)** in modern ICs. Every commercial chip — from a smartphone SoC (Apple A17, Snapdragon 8 Gen 3) to an automotive microcontroller (NXP S32K3) — relies on ATPG-generated patterns stored in on-chip ROM or applied via scan chains. Without these systems, post-silicon debugging and field returns would be catastrophic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Defect Level (Williams-Brown Model)

The **Defect Level** is the fraction of defective chips shipped to customers — the single most important business metric of testing.

### Step 1: Define Yield

Let $Y$ be the probability that a randomly manufactured chip is fault-free.

$$Y = e^{-\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is the average number of defects per chip (Poisson distribution assumption).

### Step 2: Define Fault Coverage

Let $FC$ be the probability that the test detects a fault, given that one exists.

### Step 3: Probability of Escape

A defective chip **escapes** testing if:
1. The chip has a defect (probability $= 1 - Y$).
2. The test fails to detect that defect (probability $= 1 - FC$).

The two events are **assumed independent** in the Williams-Brown model.

$$P_{escape} = (1 - Y) \cdot (1 - FC)$$

### Step 4: Conditional Defect Level

The Defect Level is the conditional probability that a chip **is defective, given that it passed testing**:

$$DL = \dfrac{P(\text{defective} \cap \text{passed})}{P(\text{passed})}$$

$$DL = \dfrac{(1 - Y)(1 - FC)}{(1 - Y)(1 - FC) + Y}$$

### Step 5: Taylor Expansion Approximation

For $Y \approx 1$ (high-yield mature process) and small $1 - FC$:

$$DL \approx 1 - Y^{1 - FC}$$

### Step 6: Numerical Example

Suppose $Y = 0.95$ (95% yield) and $FC = 0.98$ (98% coverage):

$$DL \approx 1 - 0.95^{1 - 0.98}$$

$$DL \approx 1 - 0.95^{0.02}$$

$$DL \approx 1 - e^{0.02 \cdot \ln(0.95)}$$

$$DL \approx 1 - e^{0.02 \cdot (-0.0513)}$$

$$DL \approx 1 - e^{-0.001026}$$

$$DL \approx 1 - 0.998975$$

$$DL \approx 0.001025 = 1025 \text{ defective parts per million (PPM)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry rule of thumb:** To achieve $DL < 100 \text{ PPM}$ with $Y = 0.9$, you need $FC > 99.5\%$. This is why automotive and medical ICs target $FC > 99.9\%$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pseudo-Random Pattern Coverage Curve

### Step 1: Define the Probability of Non-Detection

A fault is detected by a **single** pseudo-random pattern with probability $p$, where for an $s-a-V$ fault requiring $k$ specific input assignments:

$$p = \dfrac{1}{2^k}$$

### Step 2: Probability of Non-Detection in $t$ Independent Trials

For $t$ independent pseudo-random patterns:

$$P_{miss}(t) = (1 - p)^t = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)^t$$

### Step 3: Probability of Detection (Coverage)

$$FC(t) = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)^t$$

### Step 4: Solve for $t$ Given Target Coverage

Setting $FC(t) = FC^*$:

$$1 - FC^* = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)^t$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln(1 - FC^*) = t \cdot \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t = \dfrac{\ln(1 - FC^*)}{\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^k}\right)}$$

Using the approximation $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$t \approx \dfrac{FC^* \cdot 2^k}{1} \cdot \text{(sign adjustment)}$$

More precisely:

$$t \approx \dfrac{-\ln(1 - FC^*)}{\dfrac{1}{2^k} - \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot 2^{2k}}}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example

Target: $FC^* = 99\% = 0.99$, fault depth $k = 10$ (requires 10 specific PIs).

$$t = \dfrac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(1 - 2^{-10})} = \dfrac{-4.6052}{\ln(1 - 0.000977)}$$

$$\ln(1 - 0.000977) \approx -0.000977 - \dfrac{0.000977^2}{2} \approx -0.000978$$

$$t \approx \dfrac{-4.6052}{-0.000978} \approx 4709 \text{ patterns}$$

This confirms why **deeply embedded faults** are **random-pattern resistant** — they require impractically long test sequences.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of ATPG Algorithms

| Algorithm | Year | Search Space | Decision Variables | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|-----------|------|--------------|--------------------|-----------|------------|
| **D-Algorithm** (Roth) | 1966 | Internal lines | Gate outputs & inputs | First complete ATPG | Heavy backtracking, slow |
| **PODEM** (Goel) | 1981 | Primary Inputs only | PIs only | Massive search-space reduction | Still exponential worst case |
| **FAN** (Fujiwara) | 1983 | PIs + fanout stems | Head lines + free PIs | Multiple backtrace, unique sensitization | Limited for complex sequential |
| **SOCRATES** | 1988 | + Learning | + 9-valued logic | Constraint learning from conflicts | Higher memory overhead |
| **EST (Equivalence)** | 1992 | + Static learning | + implication graphs | Reduces search via equivalence | Setup time |
| **SAT-based ATPG** | 2000s | CNF formula | Boolean satisfiability | Polynomial on many hard faults | Encoding complexity |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: PODEM Algorithm Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PODEM = Path-Oriented Decision Making.** KTU frequently asks: *"Explain PODEM with a flow chart"* and *"Differentiate D-Algorithm and PODEM."*

**Input:** Combinational circuit, target fault $f$.

**Output:** Test vector $t$ (or "no test possible").

**Procedure:**

```
PODEM(Circuit C, Fault f):
    1. Initialize all PIs and gates to X (unknown)
    2. Set objective: Activate fault f
       - If s-a-1: set target line to 0
       - If s-a-0: set target line to 1
    3. BACKTRACE(objective):
       - Propagate objective backwards toward PIs
       - Choose a PI that can satisfy the objective
       - Try PI = 1 first, then PI = 0 (heuristic)
    4. SIMULATE(PI = value):
       - Forward implication (event-driven simulation)
       - Update all reachable lines
    5. CHECK:
       - If fault activated AND D reaches a PO → SUCCESS
       - If conflict detected → undo last PI assignment (backtrack)
       - If all PIs tried without success → FAULT UNTESTABLE
    6. Repeat until test found or PIs exhausted
```

**Key Insight of PODEM over D-Algorithm:**
- D-Algorithm makes decisions on **internal gate outputs** (millions of options).
- PODEM restricts decisions to **primary inputs only** (typically 50–500 PIs).
- Search space reduces from **exponential in total lines** to **exponential in PIs only**.

## 3.5 SCOAP Computation Example

Consider the circuit: $F = (A \cdot B) + C$ with $A, B, C$ as primary inputs.

**Combinational Controllability-0 (CC0):**
- For each input: $CC0(A) = CC0(B) = CC0(C) = 1$ (just assign 0).
- For AND gate output $G_1 = A \cdot B$: $CC0(G_1) = CC0(A) + CC0(B) + 1 = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$.
- For OR gate output $F$: $CC0(F) = \min(CC0(G_1), CC0(C)) + 1 = \min(3, 1) + 1 = 2$.

**Combinational Controllability-1 (CC1):**
- For each input: $CC1(A) = CC1(B) = CC1(C) = 1$.
- For AND gate: $CC1(G_1) = CC1(A) + CC1(B) + 1 = 3$.
- For OR gate: $CC1(F) = \min(CC1(G_1), CC1(C)) + 1 = \min(3, 1) + 1 = 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> Both CC0 and CC1 of $F$ equal 2, meaning it takes 2 levels of justification to control $F$ from primary inputs. This metric guides ATPG to choose easy-to-control decision points first.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic ATPG System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Netlist VHDL or Verilog] --> nodeB[Logic Synthesis Extractor]
    nodeC[Fault Model Library StuckAt Transition Bridge] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[Collapsed Fault List]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Fault Simulator Engine]
    nodeF[ATPG Core D Algo PODEM FAN SAT] --> nodeE
    nodeG[Random Pattern Generator LFSR] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH[Test Pattern Database TDB]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Test Compactor]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[STIL or WGL Output for ATE]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Fault Coverage Report]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Coverage Target Met}
    nodeL -- No --> nodeF
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM[Final Test Set]
```

## 4.2 PODEM Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start PODEM]) --> init[Initialize all PIs to X]
    init --> selectFault[Select target fault from list]
    selectFault --> activate[Set Activation Objective]
    activate --> backtrace[BACKTRACE toward PIs]
    backtrace --> choosePI[Choose PI and assign trial value]
    choosePI --> simulate[Forward Simulate implication]
    simulate --> checkD{Does D reach PO}
    checkD -- Yes --> success([Return test vector])
    checkD -- No --> checkConflict{Conflict detected}
    checkConflict -- Yes --> flip[Flip PI assignment]
    flip --> simulate
    checkConflict -- No --> newObj[New propagation objective]
    newObj --> backtrace
    simulate --> exhausted{All PIs tried}
    exhausted -- Yes --> untestable[Fault untestable]
    exhausted -- No --> backtrace
    untestable --> nextFault[Next fault in list]
    success --> nextFault
    nextFault --> moreFaults{More faults}
    moreFaults -- Yes --> selectFault
    moreFaults -- No --> endNode([ATPG Complete])
```

## 4.3 Test Generation System Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TGS[Test Generation Systems] --> EXH[Exhaustive]
    TGS --> PE[Pseudo Exhaustive]
    TGS --> RAN[Random]
    TGS --> DET[Deterministic ATPG]
    TGS --> HYB[Hybrid Industrial Flow]

    EXH --> EXH1[All 2 to N input combinations]
    PE --> PE1[Cone based logic segmentation]
    RAN --> RAN1[LFSR or CA or weighted random]
    RAN --> RAN2[Coverage driven stop criteria]

    DET --> D1[D Algorithm 1966 Roth]
    DET --> D2[PODEM 1981 Goel]
    DET --> D3[FAN 1983 Fujiwara]
    DET --> D4[SOCRATES 1988 Schulz]
    DET --> D5[SAT based 2000s]

    HYB --> H1[Random seed patterns]
    HYB --> H2[Deterministic top up]
    HYB --> H3[Dynamic compaction]
    HYB --> H4[Static compaction]

    style TGS fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style DET fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style HYB fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Fault Simulation Engine Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePat[Test Pattern t] --> nodeSim[Fault Simulator]
    nodeNet[Netlist] --> nodeSim
    nodeFL[Fault List] --> nodeSim
    nodeSim --> nodeFF[Fault Free Simulation]
    nodeSim --> nodeFS[Faulty Simulation per fault]
    nodeFF --> nodeCMP[Comparator]
    nodeFS --> nodeCMP
    nodeCMP --> nodeDEC{Difference at PO}
    nodeDEC -- Yes --> nodeDET[Mark fault as DETECTED]
    nodeDEC -- No --> nodeUND[Mark as POSSIBLY DETECTED or UNDETECTED]
    nodeDET --> nodeOUT[Output Coverage Report]
    nodeUND --> nodeOUT
```

## 4.5 Scan-Based Test Generation Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeATPG[ATPG Tool TetraMAX or FastScan] --> nodeSC[Test Patterns plus Scan Chains]
    nodeSC --> nodeCOM[Compression Logic]
    nodeCOM --> nodeEDT[Embedded Deterministic Test]
    nodeEDT --> nodeCHIP[Chip Under Test]
    nodeCHIP --> nodeRESP[Response Capture]
    nodeRESP --> nodeMISR[MISR Signature]
    nodeMISR --> nodeEXP[Expected Signature Compare]
    nodeEXP --> nodePASS{Pass or Fail}
    nodePASS -- Fail --> nodeDIAG[Failure Diagnosis]
    nodePASS -- Pass --> nodeSHIP[Ship to Customer]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Test Generation System and state its primary objective.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Test Generation System** is a software/hardware framework that automatically generates input stimulus patterns to detect faults in a digital circuit under test (CUT). Its **primary objective** is to achieve **maximum Fault Coverage (FC)** with the **minimum test length** (number of vectors) while minimizing **test generation time**.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition — 1 mark
- Primary objective (max FC, min length, min time) — 2 marks

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Combinational ATPG and Sequential ATPG.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Combinational ATPG | Sequential ATPG |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Feedback loops | None (acyclic) | Present (cyclic) |
| Memory elements | Ignored | Must be initialized/controlled |
| Time-frame expansion | Not required | Required (iterative logic array) |
| Algorithms | D-Algorithm, PODEM, FAN | Extended PODEM, Genetic ATPG |
| Complexity | Polynomial average | NP-hard worst case |
| Test length | Small | Large (often 10×–100× combinational) |

**Mark Distribution:**
- 3 key differences with technical accuracy — 3 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B: Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks): ATPG Algorithms and PODEM** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — Explain the D-Algorithm for stuck-at fault test generation with the concept of D-cube and D-frontier. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of D-cube:**  
A **primitive D-cube of a fault (PDCF)** specifies the minimum input assignment to a gate that activates the fault and creates a **D** or **$\bar{D}$** at the gate output.

**Step 2 — D-Frontier Definition:**  
The **D-frontier** is the set of all gates whose inputs contain at least one **D** or **$\bar{D}$** value AND whose output is currently **X** (unassigned).

**Step 3 — Algorithm Flow:**

```
D-ALGORITHM:
1. Select target fault f (e.g., line L s-a-0)
2. Select a PDCF for the fault
3. IMPLY: propagate values forward
4. Select D-drive step: choose a gate from D-frontier
5. Choose a propagation D-cube
6. IMPLY again
7. Repeat until D reaches a primary output
8. If conflict → backtrack and choose different D-drive
9. Once D at PO → perform line justification back to PIs
10. If all PIs justified without conflict → SUCCESS
11. Else → backtrack further
```

**Step 4 — Example for NAND gate, line A s-a-1:**

The NAND gate has inputs A, B and output F = $\overline{A \cdot B}$. For line A s-a-1, the activation requires setting A = 0 (since fault-free expects A = 0, faulty has A = 1). The propagation D-cube is:

$$A = 0/D, \quad B = 1, \quad F = \bar{D}$$

If B is forced to 1 (to make NAND behave as inverter on A), the fault effect propagates as $\bar{D}$ to output F.

**Mark Distribution:**
- D-cube and D-frontier definitions — 2 marks
- Algorithm flow — 3 marks
- Example with NAND — 2 marks

---

#### **Part (b) — Explain PODEM algorithm. Compare PODEM with D-Algorithm. Why is PODEM preferred for large circuits? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**PODEM Working (4 Marks):**

1. PODEM restricts all decision-making to **primary inputs (PIs)** only.
2. Starts with all PIs = X.
3. **Objective setting:** Either *activation* (drive target line to activating value) or *propagation* (drive D to a PO).
4. **Backtrace:** Walks from objective backward to PIs, choosing the **closest controllable PI** with lowest CC1/CC0 value.
5. **Simulation:** Performs forward logic simulation upon each PI assignment.
6. **Conflict detection:** If implication creates inconsistency (e.g., line must be both 0 and 1), PODEM **flips** the last PI decision or backtracks.
7. **Success criterion:** Fault activated AND D/$\bar{D}$ observable at some PO.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | D-Algorithm | PODEM |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Decision variables | Internal lines + PIs | PIs only |
| Search space | Exponential in lines | Exponential in PIs |
| Backtracking cost | Very high | Much lower |
| Typical circuits | Small | Large (10K–1M gates) |
| Speed (relative) | 1× | 10×–100× faster |

**Why PODEM Preferred (1 Mark):**

For a circuit with $L$ total lines and $n$ PIs (where $n \ll L$), PODEM's search space is bounded by $2^n$ instead of $2^L$. Since modern ICs have $L \approx 10^6$ but $n \approx 100$–$1000$, this reduction is **the difference between infeasible and feasible** ATPG.

**Mark Distribution:**
- PODEM steps — 4 marks
- Comparison — 2 marks  
- Justification — 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Fault Simulation and Random Testing** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Explain Parallel Fault Simulation and Deductive Fault Simulation with neat diagrams. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Parallel Fault Simulation (4 Marks):**

In **Parallel Fault Simulation**, multiple faults are simulated **simultaneously** using bit-parallel operations on a host computer's word (32 or 64 bits).

- Each bit position in the word represents the logic value of the circuit under one specific fault.
- Bit 0 = fault-free circuit.
- Bits 1 to (W-1) = up to (W-1) faults simulated in parallel.

**Procedure:**

1. Load fault-free values in bit 0.
2. Load faulty values for each of (W-1) faults into respective bits.
3. For each gate, perform bitwise AND, OR, NOT operations.
4. After simulation, compare each bit with bit 0 at each PO.
5. If difference → corresponding fault detected by current pattern.

**Deductive Fault Simulation (3 Marks):**

In **Deductive Fault Simulation**, only the **fault-free** circuit is simulated explicitly. The set of faults detectable at each line is **deduced** (propagated) using set operations.

**Fault List Propagation Rules:**

- For an AND gate $F = A \cdot B$, the set of faults detectable at $F$ given sets $L_A$ and $L_B$:
$$L_F = (L_A \cap L_B) \cup (L_B \cap \text{SA0 of A}) \cup (L_A \cap \text{SA1 of B}) \cup (\text{SA0 of B if } A=1)$$

- Simplified rule:
$$L_F = L_A \cup L_B$$
only when both A and B are sensitized (both = 1 for AND).

**Advantage:** Only one simulation per pattern regardless of fault count.

**Disadvantage:** Set operations become expensive for large fault lists.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Parallel simulation — 4 marks
- Deductive simulation with formula — 3 marks

---

#### **Part (b) — A digital circuit has 25 primary inputs and 200 gates. Compute: (i) the number of patterns for exhaustive testing, (ii) the test length to detect a fault requiring 8 specific input assignments using pseudo-random testing at 99% coverage, (iii) the fault coverage achieved after applying 500 random patterns for a fault with depth k = 12. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Exhaustive test patterns (2 Marks):**

$$N_{exh} = 2^{25} = 33{,}554{,}432 \approx 3.36 \times 10^7 \text{ patterns}$$

This is **infeasible** in practice (test time would exceed hours even at 100 MHz ATE).

**(ii) Test length for 99% coverage, $k = 8$ (3 Marks):**

Using the formula:

$$t = \dfrac{\ln(1 - FC^*)}{\ln(1 - 2^{-k})}$$

$$t = \dfrac{\ln(1 - 0.99)}{\ln(1 - 2^{-8})} = \dfrac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(1 - 0.003906)}$$

$$\ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

$$\ln(1 - 0.003906) \approx -0.003914$$

$$t = \dfrac{-4.6052}{-0.003914} \approx 1176 \text{ patterns}$$

**(iii) Fault coverage after 500 patterns, $k = 12$ (2 Marks):**

$$FC(500) = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2^{12}}\right)^{500}$$

$$FC(500) = 1 - \left(1 - 0.000244\right)^{500}$$

$$FC(500) = 1 - (0.999756)^{500}$$

$$\ln(0.999756) \approx -0.000244$$

$$(0.999756)^{500} = e^{-0.000244 \times 500} = e^{-0.1221} \approx 0.8851$$

$$FC(500) = 1 - 0.8851 = 0.1149 = 11.49\%$$

This shows that faults with **large depth ($k$)** are **random-pattern resistant** and need **deterministic ATPG top-up**.

**Mark Distribution:**
- (i) Exhaustive calculation — 2 marks
- (ii) Random length derivation with formula — 3 marks
- (iii) Coverage computation — 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Test Generation Systems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing D and $\bar{D}$:** D = 1/0 (good/faulty), $\bar{D}$ = 0/1. Students often invert this and lose 1 mark instantly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting fault collapsing:** When asked to compute the *number of faults*, KTU expects the **collapsed fault count** (using equivalence and dominance), which is roughly **40–60%** of the uncollapsed count. Stating the uncollapsed count loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **PODEM backtrace direction:** Backtrace moves **from objective toward PIs**, NOT the reverse. This direction error is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Five-valued logic vs. nine-valued:** KTU-2024 syllabus expects **five-valued** for combinational ATPG but **nine-valued** for sequential ATPG (to handle unknown initialization). Mixing them loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Williams-Brown formula:** The approximation $DL \approx 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ is only valid for $Y \approx 1$. For low-yield processes, the full formula must be used. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you use the approximation without stating the assumption.
>
> 6. **Skipping the SCOAP table:** Even if you compute CC0/CC1 correctly, presenting them in a **neat table** earns an extra 1 mark for presentation in KTU answer scripts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Test Generation Systems":**

- **Definition:** Automated framework producing test patterns to maximize fault coverage.
- **Primary Metrics:** Fault Coverage (FC), Test Length, Test Generation Time, Defect Level.
- **Five-Valued Logic:** Symbols D, $\bar{D}$, 0, 1, X — memorize meaning of each.
- **D-Frontier:** Gates with D/$\bar{D}$ input and X output — drives propagation.
- **D-Algorithm (Roth 1966):** First complete algorithm; decisions on internal lines.
- **PODEM (Goel 1981):** Decisions restricted to PIs; **faster** than D-Algorithm.
- **FAN (Fujiwara 1983):** Multiple backtrace + unique sensitization; even faster.
- **SAT-based ATPG:** Modern; uses Boolean satisfiability solvers; handles large designs.
- **Parallel Fault Simulation:** Bit-parallel — simulates W faults per word simultaneously.
- **Deductive Fault Simulation:** Propagates fault sets; one simulation per pattern.
- **Random Pattern Resistance:** Deeply embedded faults ($k > 15$) need ATPG.
- **Williams-Brown:** $DL \approx 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ — critical industry formula.
- **Pseudo-random coverage:** $FC(t) = 1 - (1 - 2^{-k})^t$ — derived and tested.
- **SCOAP:** CC0, CC1, CO metrics — guide ATPG decision-making.
- **Test Compaction:** Dynamic (during ATPG) + Static (post-process) — reduces test cost.
- **Industrial Tools:** Synopsys TetraMAX, Mentor FastScan, Cadence Encounter Test.
- **DFT Linkage:** ATPG generates patterns for scan chains; compression via EDT/MISR.

> **Final Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always combine a **flow chart**, a **comparison table**, and a **numerical example** — KTU examiners award bonus marks for structured answers with diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Compaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Test Compaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **TEST COMPACTION (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> Test Compaction is the process of **reducing the length of a pre-computed test set** $T$ for a digital circuit, such that the compacted test set $T_c \subseteq T$ detects the same set of faults (or an equivalent set) as $T$, while using **fewer test vectors**. The goal is to minimize the test application time $T_{app}$ and test data volume $V_{TDV}$ without sacrificing the **Fault Coverage (FC)** metric.

Formally, if $T = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_n\}$ is the original test set and $F$ is the target fault list, then compacted test set $T_c$ satisfies:

$$\text{FaultCoverage}(T_c) \geq \text{FaultCoverage}(T) - \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon \to 0$ is an acceptable tolerance (typically **0%** in industrial practice, meaning **100% fault coverage preservation**).

### Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Length** | $L$ | Number of test patterns | Minimize |
| **Test Application Time** | $T_{app}$ | $L \times f_{clk}^{-1}$ | $< 1$ sec |
| **Test Data Volume** | $V_{TDV}$ | $L \times N_{chain}$ bits | $< 100$ MB |
| **Compaction Ratio** | $CR$ | $\frac{L - L_c}{L} \times 100\%$ | $30\% - 70\%$ |
| **Fault Coverage** | $FC$ | $\frac{F_{det}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 99\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT**
> Test Compaction is a **test economics** technique. It is applied **after** test generation (ATPG) and **before** test compression. It operates on the **symbolic test cube** level, not the bit-stream level.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **REAL-WORLD ANALOGY: "The Packing Problem"**
> Imagine you have a checklist of **100 items** to verify in a factory quality-control process. Each item requires a separate inspection. However, after analysis, you discover that **checking 25 specific items automatically validates 75 others** (because they are correlated). Additionally, you find that the order of checking matters — some items checked early make later items redundant.
>
> **Test Compaction** does exactly this: it is the "smart packing" of test vectors so that:
> 1. Redundant vectors (those detecting already-found faults) are **removed**.
> 2. The sequence is **reordered** to maximize early fault detection.
> 3. The total number of vectors is **shrunk** without losing diagnostic power.
>
> Just as a **traveling salesman finds the shortest route** through 100 cities, a compactor finds the **fewest essential test vectors** through the circuit-under-test.

### Classification of Test Compaction Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TWO BROAD CATEGORIES (KTU Board Favorite)**

1. **Static Compaction** — Operates on a **fixed, pre-generated** test set $T$. No new vectors are added.
2. **Dynamic Compaction** — Performed **during ATPG** itself. New vectors are added to the set as they are generated to detect remaining faults.

```
Test Compaction
├── 1. Static Compaction
│   ├── (a) Fault Simulation–Based
│   ├── (b) Random Vector Reordering
│   └── (c) Reverse-Order Simulation
└── 2. Dynamic Compaction
    ├── (a) Test Generation During ATPG
    └── (b) Heuristic Reordering
```

### Test Compaction vs. Test Compression

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON KTU EXAM CONFUSION: Do NOT confuse these!**

| Aspect | Test Compaction | Test Compression |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Reduce number of **test vectors** | Reduce number of **bits per vector** |
| **Operates on** | Test set $T$ (vector level) | Test cube $TC$ (bit level) |
| **Technique** | Removing redundant vectors | Coding (Golomb, Huffman, LFSR) |
| **Phase** | Post-ATPG | Post-ATPG |
| **Tool output** | Smaller $L$ | Smaller $V_{TDV}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static Compaction — Operational Logic

Static compaction takes an existing test set and **systematically eliminates redundant vectors**. A vector $t_i \in T$ is **redundant** if every fault it detects is **already detected** by other vectors in $T \setminus \{t_i\}$.

#### Algorithm 1: Fault-Simulation–Based Static Compaction

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Initialize**: Start with original test set $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$. Initialize detected fault set $F_d = \emptyset$.

2. **Essential Vector Identification**: For each vector $t_i$, perform **fault simulation** to find the set of faults it uniquely detects $U_i$ (faults detected ONLY by $t_i$).

3. **Mark Essential Vectors**: If $U_i \neq \emptyset$, then $t_i$ is **essential** and must be retained in $T_c$.

4. **Greedy Selection (Reverse Order)**:
   - Process vectors in **reverse order** $t_n, t_{n-1}, \ldots, t_1$.
   - For each non-essential $t_i$, simulate against $F_d$.
   - If $t_i$ detects any new fault (not in $F_d$), add it to $T_c$ and update $F_d$.

5. **Termination**: Stop when all faults in $F$ are detected or all vectors are processed.

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY REVERSE ORDER?**
> Reverse-order processing (proposed by **Pomeranz & Reddy, 1992**) gives near-optimal results because vectors generated later in ATPG tend to target **harder-to-detect faults**. Processing them first ensures they enter $T_c$ before they become "covered" by earlier vectors.

### 2.2 Dynamic Compaction — Operational Logic

In dynamic compaction, the ATPG engine itself attempts to **detect multiple faults with a single vector**. The flow is:

1. ATPG generates a vector $t_i$ to target a specific target fault $f_{target}$.
2. Before finalizing $t_i$, perform **fault simulation** with $t_i$ against the **entire remaining fault list**.
3. Any **incidentally detected faults** are marked as detected and removed from the remaining list.
4. This continues until either all faults are detected or the ATPG gives up.

**Advantage**: No separate compaction pass is needed.
**Disadvantage**: Increases ATPG runtime due to extra simulations.

### 2.3 Compaction Ratio — Quantitative Formulation

Let:
- $L$ = Original test set length
- $L_c$ = Compacted test set length
- $F_{total}$ = Total targeted faults
- $F_{det}$ = Faults detected by $T_c$

The **Compaction Ratio (CR)** is defined as:

$$CR = \left(1 - \frac{L_c}{L}\right) \times 100\%$$

The **Compacted Fault Coverage (CFC)** is:

$$CFC = \frac{F_{det}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%$$

The **compaction efficiency** can be measured as:

$$\eta_{compact} = \frac{CR}{1 - \frac{F_{det}}{F_{total}}}$$

A high $\eta_{compact}$ means the compactor achieved good length reduction without sacrificing coverage.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{app} = L \times T_{clk}$ | Test application time | seconds |
| 2 | $V_{TDV} = L \times N_{chain}$ | Test data volume | bits |
| 3 | $CR = \left(1 - \frac{L_c}{L}\right) \times 100\%$ | Compaction ratio | percentage |
| 4 | $FC = \frac{F_{det}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Fault coverage | percentage |
| 5 | $\eta_{compact} = \frac{CR}{1 - FC_{preserved}}$ | Compaction efficiency | ratio |
| 6 | $T_{total} = T_{gen} + T_{compact} + T_{app}$ | Total test cost | seconds |
| 7 | $C_{test} = C_{ATPG} + C_{compactor} + C_{compressor}$ | Test economics | dollars |
| 8 | $F_{ess} = \{t_i \mid U_i \neq \emptyset\}$ | Essential vector set | set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU EXAM NOTE**: When asked for "test cost," always include **ATPG cost + Compaction cost + Test Application cost**. Test compaction REDUCES application cost but ADDS compaction cost — the net effect is what matters.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **INDUSTRY APPLICATION**
> 1. **Synopsys DFT Compiler** — Uses static compaction as a post-ATPG pass.
> 2. **Mentor Tessent** — Implements both static and dynamic compaction.
> 3. **Cadence Genus** — Combines ATPG with built-in dynamic compaction.
> 4. **Production ATE (Automatic Test Equipment)** — Compaction reduces ATE memory requirements by **30–60%**, directly cutting testing cost per die.

**Real-world example**: For a modern 7nm SoC with $N_{chain} = 16$ scan chains and $L = 10{,}000$ uncompacted vectors:
- $V_{TDV}^{orig} = 10{,}000 \times 16 \times 100 = 16$ Mbits
- After 50% compaction: $V_{TDV}^{comp} = 5{,}000 \times 16 \times 100 = 8$ Mbits
- **Savings = 8 Mbits of ATE memory** per device tested.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Compaction Ratio Bound

**Problem Statement**: Given a test set $T$ of length $L$ with $F$ target faults, derive the **theoretical lower bound** on the compacted length $L_c^{min}$.

**Given Information**:
- Each test vector $t_i$ can detect a maximum of $F$ faults (all of them, in the ideal case).
- Each fault must be detected by at least one vector in $T_c$.
- $\det(t_i)$ = set of faults detected by $t_i$.

**Step 1**: Establish the constraint that every fault must be covered.

$$\bigcup_{i=1}^{L_c} \det(t_i) = F_{total}$$

This means the **union of detection sets** must equal the full fault list.

**Step 2**: Apply the **Pigeonhole Principle**.

Since each vector can detect at most $F_{total}$ faults, the absolute lower bound is:

$$L_c \geq \left\lceil \frac{F_{total}}{F_{total}} \right\rceil = 1$$

This is the trivial bound. A tighter bound comes from considering the **average detection count per vector**.

**Step 3**: Define the average detection count.

Let $\bar{D}$ be the average number of faults detected per vector in the original set $T$:

$$\bar{D} = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{L} \vert \det(t_i) \vert$$

**Step 4**: Account for **overlap** in detections.

Let $\Omega$ be the total overlap (sum of detections counted multiple times):

$$\Omega = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \vert \det(t_i) \vert - F_{total}$$

**Step 5**: The theoretical lower bound on compacted length is:

$$L_c^{min} = \left\lceil \frac{F_{total}}{\bar{D}_{max}} \right\rceil$$

where $\bar{D}_{max}$ is the maximum achievable average detection rate.

**Step 6**: For practical circuits (ISCAS benchmarks), empirical results show:

$$L_c^{min} \approx 0.2 \cdot L \text{ to } 0.5 \cdot L$$

$$\boxed{CR_{typical} = 50\% \text{ to } 80\%}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Reverse-Order Static Compaction

**Problem**: Consider a circuit with **8 target faults** $F = \{f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4, f_5, f_6, f_7, f_8\}$ and a test set $T = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5\}$ with the following detection matrix:

| Vector | Detects |
|---|---|
| $t_1$ | $f_1, f_2$ |
| $t_2$ | $f_3, f_4$ |
| $t_3$ | $f_2, f_5$ |
| $t_4$ | $f_6, f_7$ |
| $t_5$ | $f_5, f_8$ |

**Step 1**: Identify essential vectors (vectors that detect a unique fault).

- $f_1$ detected only by $t_1$ → $t_1$ is **essential**
- $f_3$ detected only by $t_2$ → $t_2$ is **essential**
- $f_4$ detected only by $t_2$ → confirms $t_2$ is essential
- $f_6$ detected only by $t_4$ → $t_4$ is **essential**
- $f_7$ detected only by $t_4$ → confirms $t_4$ is essential
- $f_8$ detected only by $t_5$ → $t_5$ is **essential**
- $f_2$ detected by $t_1$ and $t_3$ → not unique to $t_3$
- $f_5$ detected by $t_3$ and $t_5$ → not unique to $t_3$

**Step 2**: Apply reverse-order processing starting from $t_5$ (already essential).

$T_c = \{t_5\}$ covers $\{f_5, f_8\}$.

**Step 3**: Process $t_4$ (essential). $T_c = \{t_5, t_4\}$ covers $\{f_4, f_5, f_6, f_7, f_8\}$.

**Step 4**: Process $t_3$. Already covered faults $f_2, f_5$ are in $F_d$. **No new faults detected** → Skip $t_3$.

**Step 5**: Process $t_2$ (essential). $T_c = \{t_5, t_4, t_2\}$ covers $\{f_2, f_3, f_4, f_5, f_6, f_7, f_8\}$.

**Step 6**: Process $t_1$ (essential). $T_c = \{t_5, t_4, t_2, t_1\}$ covers all 8 faults.

**Final Result**:

$$T_c = \{t_1, t_2, t_4, t_5\}, \quad L_c = 4, \quad L = 5$$

$$CR = \left(1 - \frac{4}{5}\right) \times 100\% = 20\%$$

$$\boxed{Fault\ Coverage\ Preserved = 100\%}$$

### 3.3 Cost-Benefit Derivation

**Test Cost Equation**:

$$C_{total} = C_{ATPG} + C_{compact} + \left(L_c \times C_{app}\right)$$

where:
- $C_{ATPG}$ = ATPG generation cost (fixed)
- $C_{compact}$ = Compaction cost (depends on $L$ and circuit size)
- $C_{app}$ = Cost per vector application (ATE time × cost rate)

**Differentiating** with respect to $L_c$:

$$\frac{d C_{total}}{d L_c} = C_{app} > 0$$

This shows $C_{total}$ is **monotonically increasing** in $L_c$. Therefore, **minimizing $L_c$ minimizes total test cost**, justifying the compaction goal.

The **break-even condition** for compaction to be worthwhile:

$$C_{compact} < (L - L_c) \times C_{app}$$

This translates to: compaction must save more in application cost than it adds in compaction cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Overall Test Compaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Circuit Netlist] --> B[ATPG Engine]
    B --> C[Test Set T]
    C --> D{Target Faults All Detected?}
    D -- No --> E[Generate More Vectors]
    E --> B
    D -- Yes --> F[Static Compaction]
    F --> F1[Step 1: Fault Simulation]
    F1 --> F2[Step 2: Identify Essential Vectors]
    F2 --> F3[Step 3: Reverse-Order Greedy Drop]
    F3 --> G[Compacted Test Set Tc]
    G --> H[Test Compression]
    H --> I[Compressed Bitstream]
    I --> J[ATE Storage and Application]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
    style G fill:#d4f1f4
    style I fill:#f4d4f4
    style J fill:#ffe1f4
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Static Compaction Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start with Test Set T] --> Init[Initialize: F_d = empty set, T_c = empty set]
    Init --> Loop1[For each vector t_i in T, in reverse order]
    Loop1 --> Sim[Run Fault Simulation t_i against F_d]
    Sim --> Q1{New faults detected?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Add[Add t_i to T_c, Update F_d]
    Q1 -- No --> Drop[Discard t_i]
    Add --> Check{All faults covered?}
    Drop --> Check
    Check -- No --> Loop1
    Check -- Yes --> Output[Output Compacted T_c]
    Output --> End[End]

    style Start fill:#b3e5fc
    style Init fill:#fff9c4
    style Sim fill:#c8e6c9
    style Add fill:#a5d6a7
    style Drop fill:#ef9a9a
    style Output fill:#ce93d8
    style End fill:#ffccbc
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Dynamic vs Static Compaction Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATIC[Static Compaction Pipeline]
        S1[ATPG Generates Full T] --> S2[Fault Sim All Vectors]
        S2 --> S3[Identify Redundant Vectors]
        S3 --> S4[Remove Redundants]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMIC[Dynamic Compaction Pipeline]
        D1[ATPG Generates One Vector] --> D2[Sim Against All Remaining Faults]
        D2 --> D3{Multiple Faults Detected?}
        D3 -- Yes --> D4[Mark All as Detected, Continue]
        D3 -- No --> D5[Generate Next Vector]
    end

    S4 --> OUT[Final Compacted T_c]
    D4 --> OUT
    D5 --> OUT

    style S1 fill:#ffe0b2
    style S2 fill:#ffe0b2
    style S3 fill:#ffe0b2
    style S4 fill:#ffe0b2
    style D1 fill:#c5e1a5
    style D2 fill:#c5e1a5
    style D3 fill:#c5e1a5
    style D4 fill:#c5e1a5
    style D5 fill:#c5e1a5
    style OUT fill:#f8bbd0
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Compaction Impact on Test Economics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Test Set T: 10000 vectors] --> Compactor[Test Compaction]
    Compactor --> Output1[Compacted T_c: 4000 vectors]
    
    Compactor --> C1[Cost: ATPG time increased by 5%]
    Compactor --> C2[Benefit: ATE time reduced by 60%]
    Compactor --> C3[Benefit: ATE memory reduced by 60%]
    Compactor --> C4[FC preserved at 99.5%]

    Output1 --> ATE[Automatic Test Equipment]
    ATE --> Wafer[Test Wafer: Cost per die reduced by 35%]

    style Input fill:#ffccbc

    style Output1 fill:#c5e1a5

    style C3 fill:#a5d6a7

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define Test Compaction. List its two main types.**

**Model Answer**:
Test Compaction is the process of reducing the number of test vectors in a pre-generated test set $T$ while preserving the original fault coverage. The compacted test set $T_c$ must detect the same faults as $T$ but with $L_c < L$.

The two main types are:
1. **Static Compaction** — Operates on a fixed test set post-ATPG.
2. **Dynamic Compaction** — Operates during the ATPG process itself by detecting multiple faults per vector.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Test Compaction and Test Compression.**

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Test Compaction | Test Compression |
|---|---|---|
| Level | Vector level | Bit level |
| Operation | Removes redundant vectors | Encodes bit patterns |
| Reduces | $L$ (test length) | $V_{TDV}$ (data volume) |
| Methods | Fault sim, reverse order | Golomb, Huffman, LFSR |
| Phase | Post-ATPG (pre-compression) | Post-ATPG (post-compaction) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Static Compaction** technique based on fault simulation. Mention the role of essential vector identification and reverse-order processing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A test set $T = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5, t_6\}$ detects the following faults on a circuit with target fault set $F = \{f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4, f_5, f_6, f_7, f_8, f_9, f_{10}\}$:

| Vector | Faults Detected |
|---|---|
| $t_1$ | $f_1, f_2, f_3$ |
| $t_2$ | $f_4, f_5$ |
| $t_3$ | $f_2, f_6$ |
| $t_4$ | $f_7, f_8$ |
| $t_5$ | $f_3, f_9$ |
| $t_6$ | $f_5, f_{10}$ |

Apply **reverse-order static compaction** and determine the compacted test set $T_c$, original length $L$, compacted length $L_c$, and compaction ratio $CR$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Static Compaction Explanation (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark)**: Static compaction is a **post-ATPG** optimization that takes a complete, fixed test set and removes redundant vectors.

**Algorithm Steps (4 Marks)**:

**Step 1 — Fault Simulation of All Vectors**:
Run parallel fault simulation on the full test set $T$ to record the detection set $\det(t_i)$ for each vector and the set of faults detected uniquely by each vector $U_i$.

**Step 2 — Essential Vector Identification**:
A vector $t_i$ is **essential** if $U_i \neq \emptyset$ (it detects at least one fault that no other vector detects). All essential vectors **must be retained** in $T_c$ because removing them would drop fault coverage.

**Step 3 — Reverse-Order Greedy Drop**:
Process vectors in **reverse order** $t_n, t_{n-1}, \ldots, t_1$. Maintain a running set of detected faults $F_d$. For each non-essential vector:
- If $t_i$ detects any fault in $F \setminus F_d$, add it to $T_c$ and update $F_d$.
- Otherwise, drop $t_i$.

**Rationale for Reverse Order (2 Marks)**:
Vectors generated later in ATPG target **harder-to-detect faults**. Processing them first ensures that these critical vectors are not dropped by mistake. This heuristic achieves compaction ratios of **50%–70%** on ISCAS benchmarks.

#### Part (b) — Worked Example (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify Uniquely Detected Faults (2 Marks)**:

| Fault | Detected By | Unique Vector? |
|---|---|---|
| $f_1$ | $t_1$ | Yes — $t_1$ essential |
| $f_2$ | $t_1, t_3$ | No |
| $f_3$ | $t_1, t_5$ | No |
| $f_4$ | $t_2$ | Yes — $t_2$ essential |
| $f_5$ | $t_2, t_6$ | No |
| $f_6$ | $t_3$ | Yes — $t_3$ essential |
| $f_7$ | $t_4$ | Yes — $t_4$ essential |
| $f_8$ | $t_4$ | (confirms $t_4$) |
| $f_9$ | $t_5$ | Yes — $t_5$ essential |
| $f_{10}$ | $t_6$ | Yes — $t_6$ essential |

**Essential vectors**: $T_{ess} = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5, t_6\}$.

All vectors are essential because every fault is uniquely detected by at least one vector.

**Step 2: Reverse-Order Compaction (3 Marks)**:

Starting with $F_d = \emptyset$, $T_c = \emptyset$:

| Step | Vector | New Faults Detected | Add to $T_c$? | Updated $F_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_6$ | $f_5, f_{10}$ | Yes | $\{f_5, f_{10}\}$ |
| 2 | $t_5$ | $f_3, f_9$ | Yes | $\{f_3, f_5, f_9, f_{10}\}$ |
| 3 | $t_4$ | $f_7, f_8$ | Yes | $\{f_3, f_5, f_7, f_8, f_9, f_{10}\}$ |
| 4 | $t_3$ | $f_2, f_6$ | Yes | $\{f_2, f_3, f_5, f_6, f_7, f_8, f_9, f_{10}\}$ |
| 5 | $t_2$ | $f_4$ (new) | Yes | all except $f_1$ |
| 6 | $t_1$ | $f_1$ (new) | Yes | all 10 faults |

**Step 3: Final Results (2 Marks)**:

- Original length: $L = 6$
- Compacted length: $L_c = 6$
- Compaction ratio: $CR = (1 - 6/6) \times 100\% = 0\%$

**Observation**: In this specific example, no compaction was possible because every vector was essential. This illustrates a key insight: compaction effectiveness depends on the **overlap** in detection sets between vectors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT**
> - **Mistake 1 (–2 marks)**: Students often forget to identify essential vectors FIRST. Without this step, the algorithm is incomplete. Always show the unique-fault analysis.
> - **Mistake 2 (–2 marks)**: Confusing "detected" with "uniquely detected." A vector is essential ONLY if it detects a fault no other vector detects.
> - **Mistake 3 (–1 mark)**: Writing $CR = 0\%$ without explaining WHY (high overlap in detection sets). Always provide interpretive analysis.
> - **Mistake 4 (–1 mark)**: Forgetting the formula: $CR = (1 - L_c/L) \times 100\%$, not $L_c/L \times 100\%$.
> - **Mistake 5 (–2 marks)**: In comparison questions, students write "compaction is better than compression" or vice versa — both are needed and complementary.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss **Dynamic Compaction** during ATPG. How does it differ from static compaction in terms of methodology, advantages, and limitations? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a circuit with $L = 5000$ test vectors, $L_c = 1800$ after compaction, and $F_{total} = 12{,}500$ faults with $F_{det} = 12{,}400$:
- Calculate the **Compaction Ratio (CR)**.
- Calculate the **Fault Coverage (FC)** before and after compaction.
- Calculate the **Test Application Time** saving if $T_{clk} = 100$ ns.
- Comment on whether the compaction is **worthwhile** if $C_{compact} = \$50$ and $C_{app} = \$0.01$/vector. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Dynamic Compaction Discussion (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark)**: Dynamic compaction is performed **during the ATPG process** itself, where the test generator actively tries to detect **multiple faults with a single test vector**.

**Methodology (2 Marks)**:
1. ATPG targets a specific fault $f_{target}$ and generates a vector $t_i$.
2. Before committing $t_i$, **fault simulation** is run against the **entire remaining fault list** $F_{rem}$.
3. Any fault in $F_{rem}$ incidentally detected by $t_i$ is **removed from $F_{rem}$**.
4. This process continues until $F_{rem} = \emptyset$ or ATPG gives up.

**Advantages (2 Marks)**:
- **No separate compaction pass** required — saves tool runtime.
- Achieves **higher compaction ratios** (60–80%) compared to static (40–60%).
- Better **fault simulation efficiency** by detecting multiple faults in one pass.

**Limitations (2 Marks)**:
- **Increases ATPG runtime** by 2–3× due to per-vector fault simulation.
- May **miss compaction opportunities** because the ATPG is locally focused on a single target fault.
- Requires **modifications to the ATPG core**, increasing tool complexity.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given**:
- $L = 5000$, $L_c = 1800$, $F_{total} = 12{,}500$, $F_{det} = 12{,}400$, $T_{clk} = 100$ ns
- $C_{compact} = \$50$, $C_{app} = \$0.01$/vector

**Step 1: Compaction Ratio (1 Mark)**

$$CR = \left(1 - \frac{L_c}{L}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{1800}{5000}\right) \times 100\% = 64\%$$

**Step 2: Fault Coverage (1 Mark)**

$$FC = \frac{F_{det}}{F_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{12{,}400}{12{,}500} \times 100\% = 99.2\%$$

(Fault coverage is **preserved** since compaction does not target the fault set.)

**Step 3: Test Application Time Saving (2 Marks)**

Original application time:
$$T_{app}^{orig} = L \times T_{clk} = 5000 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 500 \text{ μs}$$

Compacted application time:
$$T_{app}^{comp} = L_c \times T_{clk} = 1800 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 180 \text{ μs}$$

Time saved:
$$\Delta T_{app} = 500 - 180 = 320 \text{ μs per device}$$

**Step 4: Cost-Benefit Analysis (3 Marks)**

Compaction cost:
$$C_{compact} = \$50$$

Application cost saved (per device):
$$C_{saved} = (L - L_c) \times C_{app} = (5000 - 1800) \times 0.01 = 3200 \times 0.01 = \$32$$

**Analysis**:
- $C_{compact} = \$50$ > $C_{saved} = \$32$ per device
- **Per-device compaction is NOT cost-effective.**
- However, for **batch production** (e.g., 1 million devices):
$$\text{Total savings} = 32 \times 10^6 = \$32 \text{ million}$$
$$\text{Compaction cost} = \$50 \text{ (one-time)}$$
- **Verdict: Compaction is highly worthwhile for high-volume manufacturing.**

**Net savings for N devices**:
$$S_{net} = (L - L_c) \times C_{app} \times N - C_{compact}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Break-even: } N_{BE} = \frac{C_{compact}}{(L - L_c) \times C_{app}} = \frac{50}{32} \approx 1.56 \text{ devices}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL FOR PART (b)**:
> - Many students forget to multiply $C_{app}$ by $(L - L_c)$, not just $L$.
> - Always show the **break-even analysis** for cost-effectiveness questions.
> - The compaction cost is **one-time**, not per device. This is crucial.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — TEST COMPACTION**

- [ ] **Test Compaction reduces the NUMBER of test vectors**; Test Compression reduces the **BITS per vector**.
- [ ] **Static Compaction** = post-ATPG; **Dynamic Compaction** = during ATPG.
- [ ] **Essential vector**: detects at least one fault that NO other vector detects.
- [ ] **Reverse-order processing** is preferred because later ATPG vectors target hard-to-detect faults.
- [ ] **Compaction Ratio Formula**: $CR = (1 - L_c/L) \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Fault coverage MUST be preserved** — compaction never intentionally drops coverage.
- [ ] **Static compaction** typically achieves 40–60% CR; **Dynamic compaction** achieves 60–80% CR.
- [ ] **Test Application Time** $T_{app} = L \times T_{clk}$ — directly proportional to $L$.
- [ ] **Test Data Volume** $V_{TDV} = L \times N_{chain} \times N_{cycles}$ — compaction reduces $L$.
- [ ] **Industrial tools**: Synopsys DFT Compiler, Mentor Tessent, Cadence Genus all support both compaction modes.
- [ ] **Compaction is cost-effective only for high-volume production** — break-even is usually < 10 devices.
- [ ] **Pomeranz & Reddy (1992)** proposed the classical reverse-order fault-simulation-based static compactor.
- [ ] **Compaction ≠ ATPG optimization** — compaction does not generate new vectors, only removes redundant ones.
- [ ] **Two-stage flow**: ATPG → Compaction → Compression → ATE application.
- [ ] For exam, always draw the **flow diagram** with all four stages clearly labeled.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 SCHEME EMPHASIS**
> This topic carries **high weightage** in Module 4. Questions typically appear as 3-mark (definition/comparison) or 7-mark (algorithm explanation with example) or 14-mark (full question with sub-parts). Always:
> 1. Define formally with **equation**.
> 2. Provide **classification**.
> 3. Explain **at least one algorithm** in numbered steps.
> 4. Show a **worked numerical example** when asked.
> 5. End with a **comparison table or industrial relevance statement**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequential Circuit Test Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sequential Circuit Test Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Sequential Circuit Test Generation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Sequential Circuit Test Generation (SCTG)** is the process of deriving a finite sequence of input vectors (a *test sequence*) applied over multiple clock cycles, such that the response at primary outputs and/or at the state memory elements distinguishes the fault-free circuit from a faulty version of the circuit. Unlike combinational test generation, the test must account for the **state** of the memory elements (flip-flops/latches) at every clock tick.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Sequential circuit test generation involves determining a sequence of primary input vectors $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k$ that, when applied to a synchronous sequential circuit, sensitizes a target fault $f$, propagates its effect to an observable point, and distinguishes the faulty response from the fault-free response.

**Formal Model:** A synchronous sequential circuit $M$ is a 6-tuple:
$$M = \langle S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0 \rangle$$
where:
- $S$ = finite set of internal states
- $I$ = finite set of input alphabets
- $O$ = finite set of output alphabets
- $\delta: S \times I \to S$ = next-state (transition) function
- $\lambda: S \times I \to O$ = output function
- $s_0$ = initial state (often unknown $X$ in real circuits)

A **test sequence** for a fault $f$ in a sequential circuit is a finite input sequence $\langle V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_L \rangle$ that produces a difference in the output sequence between the good and faulty machines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Maze and Flashlight"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine testing a complex vending machine that remembers your previous coins inserted and selections made (sequential circuit). To verify a hidden fault, you cannot just push a single button (combinational test). You must press a *sequence* of buttons in a precise order — first to put the machine in a specific internal state, then to "turn on" the faulty logic, and finally to "light up" the result so the cashier can see the problem. This is exactly the role of **time-frame expansion** in sequential testing.

### 1.3 Why Sequential Testing is Harder than Combinational

| Aspect | Combinational Circuit | Sequential Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Memory elements | None | Flip-flops / latches |
| Test vector | Single PI vector | Sequence of vectors |
| Initial state | Not relevant | Must be considered / initialized |
| State observability | Direct via PO | Requires PO + state accessibility |
| Search space | $2^{n_{PI}}$ | $2^{n_{PI}} \times 2^{n_{FF}}$ |
| Algorithm | D-Algorithm, PODEM, FAN | Extensions (e.g., Extended D-Algorithm) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — State Space Cardinality:** A circuit with $n$ flip-flops has $\mathbf{2^n}$ possible internal states. For modern designs where $n \ge 10^6$, the state space is astronomically large — making exhaustive enumeration impossible.

### 1.4 The Iterative Logic Array (ILA) Model
To handle sequential circuits, engineers "unroll" the time dimension using the **Iterative Logic Array (ILA)** representation. The original sequential circuit is replicated as a cascade of combinational copies (one per clock cycle), where the state-output lines of frame $i$ feed the state-input lines of frame $i+1$. This reduces a sequential ATPG problem to a multi-frame combinational ATPG problem.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Fault coverage (FC):** $FC = \dfrac{\text{Number of detected faults}}{\text{Total number of modeled faults}} \times 100\%$
- **Test length (L):** Number of input vectors in a test sequence
- **Sequential depth (d):** Number of flip-flops on the longest path from any PI to any PO
- **State observability:** Ability to bring FF contents to a primary output
- **State controllability:** Ability to set FF contents from primary inputs

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time Frame Expansion — Operational Concept

The **time frame expansion** technique converts a sequential circuit into a series of combinational sub-circuits, each representing the circuit's behavior at a particular clock cycle.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Time frame 0:** The initial state $s_0$ (often all-X / unknown) is assumed.
2. **Time frame $i$ (for $i \ge 1$):** The combinational logic of the circuit is replicated. Primary inputs $V_i$ are applied; pseudo-inputs represent the present state $S_{i-1}$; pseudo-outputs represent the next state $S_i$.
3. **Fault effect propagation:** The fault is *activated* in one time frame, and its effect is *propagated* through subsequent frames until it reaches a primary output.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" time frame expansion works:** Because synchronous sequential circuits are deterministic and have periodic behavior, the test generation problem can be discretized into discrete clock cycles. Each cycle is purely combinational when state is fixed.

## 2.2 Three-Phase Sequential Test Strategy

A complete sequential test sequence consists of three phases:

| Phase | Purpose | Length |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1 — Initialization** | Bring the circuit to a known state (often the all-0 reset state) | $L_1$ vectors |
| **Phase 2 — Fault Activation & State Setup** | Drive the circuit to a state that activates the fault and prepares the propagation path | $L_2$ vectors |
| **Phase 3 — Fault Propagation / Observation** | Propagate the fault effect to a primary output | $L_3$ vectors |

Total test length: $L = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$

## 2.3 Five Key Difficulties in Sequential ATPG

1. **Unknown Initial State:** Real circuits may power up in an unknown state. ATPG must therefore handle $X$ (don't-care) values extensively.
2. **Sequential Depth:** A fault effect may have to traverse many flip-flops to reach a PO. For $d$ flip-flops, at least $d$ time frames are required.
3. **State Justification Problem:** Justifying a desired state may itself require an arbitrarily long sequence (unbounded).
4. **Self-Justification & Cycles:** A circuit may have feedback loops that make the state space hard to search.
5. **Redundancy Identification:** Many faults are sequentially redundant — undetectable by *any* input sequence. ATPG must avoid infinite loops trying to detect them.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why heuristics are mandatory:** Sequential ATPG is provably NP-hard (even harder than combinational ATPG). Exact algorithms like the 9-V D-Algorithm extension are impractical for circuits with $n_{FF} > 50$. Hence industry relies on **simulation-based heuristic methods** (e.g., GENTEST, HITEC, FAST).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State-space cardinality | $\vert S \vert = 2^{n_{FF}}$ | States, $n_{FF}$ = number of flip-flops |
| 2 | Fault coverage | $FC = \dfrac{F_d}{F_t} \times 100\%$ | %, $F_d$ = detected, $F_t$ = total |
| 3 | Test length bound | $L \ge d$ | Vectors, $d$ = sequential depth |
| 4 | Combined search space | $2^{n_{PI}} \times 2^{n_{FF}}$ | Vectors × states |
| 5 | Transition function | $s_{i+1} = \delta(s_i, V_{i+1})$ | Boolean/algebraic |
| 6 | Output function | $o_{i+1} = \lambda(s_i, V_{i+1})$ | Boolean/algebraic |
| 7 | Sequential depth | $d = \max_{p} \vert p \vert$ | $p$ = path from PI to PO through FFs |
| 8 | Test length with init | $L = L_{init} + L_{setup} + L_{prop}$ | Vectors |
| 9 | Sequential redundancy | $R_{seq} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{detectable faults}}{F_t}$ | Fraction |
| 10 | Scan-test length | $L_{scan} = \dfrac{n_{FF}}{1} + n_{comb} + \dfrac{n_{FF}}{1}$ | Shift-in + capture + shift-out |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Sequential test generation underpins **automotive ICs, aerospace SoCs, and safety-critical designs (ISO 26262, DO-254)**. Without rigorous sequential ATPG, latent defects in state elements would escape manufacturing test and cause field failures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: State-Space Explosion Bound

Let a sequential circuit contain $n_{FF}$ D flip-flops and $n_{PI}$ primary inputs.

**Step 1:** Each flip-flop stores one bit, so the total number of distinct internal states is:
$$N_{states} = 2^{n_{FF}}$$

**Step 2:** Each primary input vector is one of:
$$N_{vectors} = 2^{n_{PI}}$$

**Step 3:** The total combined search space (states × vectors) is the Cartesian product:
$$N_{search} = N_{states} \times N_{vectors} = 2^{n_{FF}} \times 2^{n_{PI}} = 2^{n_{FF} + n_{PI}}$$

**Step 4:** Converting to base-10 magnitude:
$$N_{search} = 10^{(n_{FF} + n_{PI}) \cdot \log_{10} 2} \approx 10^{0.301 \cdot (n_{FF} + n_{PI})}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $n_{FF} = 20$ and $n_{PI} = 20$:
$$N_{search} = 2^{40} = 1{,}099{,}511{,}627{,}776 \approx 1.1 \times 10^{12}$$

This huge number demonstrates why exhaustive simulation is infeasible for non-trivial designs.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Time Frames for Fault Propagation

**Given:** A fault $f$ located in the combinational part of the circuit.

**Let:**
- $d_{PI \to f}$ = number of flip-flops on the path from any PI to the fault site $f$
- $d_{f \to PO}$ = number of flip-flops on the path from fault $f$ to any PO

**Step 1:** To activate the fault, the controlling state values must propagate from primary inputs (or other FFs) to the fault site. Minimum activation time frames:
$$T_{activate} \ge d_{PI \to f}$$

**Step 2:** To observe the fault effect, it must propagate from the fault site to a primary output. Minimum propagation time frames:
$$T_{observe} \ge d_{f \to PO}$$

**Step 3:** Total minimum time frames for the test:
$$T_{min} = T_{activate} + T_{observe} = d_{PI \to f} + d_{f \to PO}$$

**Step 4:** Since both may overlap in the same frame, the tighter bound is:
$$T_{min} = \max(d_{PI \to f}, d_{f \to PO}) + \text{activation slack}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If a circuit has $n_{FF} = 10$ flip-flops on the longest scan path, then $T_{min}$ can be as low as **1** (for combinational faults) or as high as **10+** (for faults deeply embedded in sequential loops).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Test Generation Complexity

| Circuit Parameter | Small Circuit | Medium Circuit | Large SoC |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n_{FF}$ | $< 100$ | $100$–$10^4$ | $> 10^4$ |
| $n_{PI}$ | $< 50$ | $50$–$500$ | $> 500$ |
| State space $\vert S \vert$ | $\approx 2^{50}$ | $\approx 2^{10^4}$ | $\approx 2^{10^6}$ |
| Feasible method | Exact (9-V D-Alg.) | Heuristic (GENTEST) | Simulation + BIST |
| Typical test length | $10$–$10^3$ | $10^3$–$10^5$ | $10^5$–$10^7$ |
| Achievable FC w/o DFT | $\approx 95\%$ | $\approx 70\%$–$85\%$ | $< 50\%$ |
| Achievable FC **with scan DFT** | $> 99\%$ | $> 98\%$ | $> 95\%$ |

## 3.4 Derivation: Test Length with Full Scan DFT

With full scan, every flip-flop is replaced/controlled by a scan chain.

**Step 1:** Shifting in a test vector: requires $n_{FF}$ clock cycles.
$$L_{shift\_in} = n_{FF}$$

**Step 2:** Capturing the response: 1 clock cycle (in capture mode).
$$L_{capture} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Shifting out the captured response: requires $n_{FF}$ clock cycles.
$$L_{shift\_out} = n_{FF}$$

**Step 4:** For $N_{test}$ combinational test vectors, total test length:
$$L_{total\_scan} = N_{test} \times (n_{FF} + 1 + n_{FF}) = N_{test} \times (2 \cdot n_{FF} + 1)$$

**Example:** For $N_{test} = 1000$ vectors and $n_{FF} = 500$ flip-flops:
$$L_{total\_scan} = 1000 \times (2 \times 500 + 1) = 1{,}001{,}000 \text{ cycles}$$

This shows why **test compression** (e.g., EDT, MLBIST) is critical for modern designs.

## 3.5 Sequential Depth — Worked Example

Consider a 4-bit counter with the following structural data:
- $n_{FF} = 4$
- $n_{PI} = 2$ (clock, enable)

**Step 1:** The longest feedback path traverses all 4 flip-flops.
$$d = 4$$

**Step 2:** Without scan DFT, a fault at the LSB may need:
$$L \ge 4 \text{ time frames to propagate to MSB}$$

**Step 3:** With full scan, the same fault requires only:
$$L_{scan} = 1 \text{ (capture) + scan shift costs}$$

This **1000× reduction** in test length is the central motivation for scan-based DFT.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Time Frame Expansion — Unrolled Sequential Circuit

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how a synchronous sequential circuit is unrolled into an **Iterative Logic Array (ILA)** of combinational time frames for ATPG processing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TF0["TIME FRAME 0 - INITIALIZATION"]
        PI0["Primary Input V1"] --> CL0["Combinational Logic"]
        Y0["Present State S0 - usually X"] --> CL0
        CL0 --> FF0["Next State S1"]
        CL0 --> PO0["Primary Output O1"]
    end

    subgraph TF1["TIME FRAME 1 - ACTIVATION"]
        PI1["Primary Input V2"] --> CL1["Combinational Logic"]
        FF0 --> Y1["Present State S1"]
        Y1 --> CL1
        CL1 --> FF1["Next State S2"]
        CL1 --> PO1["Primary Output O2"]
    end

    subgraph TF2["TIME FRAME 2 - PROPAGATION"]
        PI2["Primary Input V3"] --> CL2["Combinational Logic"]
        FF1 --> Y2["Present State S2"]
        Y2 --> CL2
        CL2 --> FF2["Next State S3"]
        CL2 --> PO2["Primary Output O3 - OBSERVE FAULT EFFECT HERE"]
    end

    FF0 -.feedback path.-> Y1
    FF1 -.feedback path.-> Y2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each **Time Frame $i$** is a pure combinational copy of the original circuit.
- The state outputs of frame $i$ become the state inputs of frame $i+1$.
- The fault is *activated* in TF1 and *propagated* through to PO at TF2.
- This is conceptually how Extended D-Algorithm, PODEM, and FAN operate on sequential circuits.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sequential ATPG System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Netlist Input - Verilog or Gate Level"] --> B["Circuit Preprocessor"]
    B --> C["Fault List Generator - Collapsed SSA Faults"]
    C --> D["Fault Selector - Pick Untested Fault"]
    D --> E["Time Frame Manager - Allocates K Frames"]
    E --> F["Combinational ATPG Engine - 9V D Alg or PODEM"]
    F --> G{"Test Found?"}
    G -- "YES" --> H["Fault Simulator - Drop Detected Faults"]
    G -- "NO" --> I["Backtrack or Abort"]
    I --> J["Mark Fault as Untestable or ATPG Untestable"]
    H --> K{"All Faults Processed?"}
    J --> K
    K -- "NO" --> D
    K -- "YES" --> L["Test Set Output - V1 V2 ... VL"]
    L --> M["Coverage Reporter - FC Percentage"]
```

**Functional Stages Explained:**

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| **Netlist Preprocessor** | Parses Verilog/gate-level netlist; identifies FFs, PIs, POs |
| **Fault List Generator** | Creates collapsed single stuck-at (SSA) fault list |
| **Time Frame Manager** | Allocates $K$ unrolled frames for each fault |
| **Combinational ATPG Engine** | Runs extended D-Algorithm / PODEM across frames |
| **Fault Simulator** | Concurrent simulation to drop faults detected by current test |
| **Coverage Reporter** | Computes FC, lists undetected faults |

## 4.3 Scan vs. Non-Scan Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Sequential Circuit with nFF FFs"] --> B{"DFT Strategy"}
    B -- "No Scan - Functional Test" --> C["Test Length L1 = large - up to 10 to power 7"]
    B -- "Full Scan" --> D["Replace FFs with Scan FFs"]
    D --> E["Chain all Scan FFs"]
    E --> F["Test Length L2 = nFF plus Ntest plus nFF"]
    B -- "Partial Scan" --> G["Scan only Critical FFs"]
    G --> H["Balance ATE Cost and FC"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Insight:** Full scan maximizes fault coverage (FC > 95%) but inflates test data volume ($L_2 \approx 2 \cdot n_{FF} \cdot N_{test}$). Partial scan reduces area overhead and shifts FFs but sacrifices some FC and requires more careful DFT planning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark Short Answer / 3-Mark Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions are short, definition/concept-based, and require crisp 2-3 sentence answers.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]
**Differentiate between combinational and sequential circuit test generation.**

**Model Answer:**
Combinational circuit test generation requires a *single* primary input vector to detect a fault because the circuit has no memory.
