In contrast, sequential circuit test generation requires a *sequence* of input vectors applied over multiple clock cycles because the circuit's response depends on its internal state stored in flip-flops. Sequential ATPG must also solve the *initialization* and *state justification* problems, in addition to activation and propagation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]
**What is meant by the Iterative Logic Array (ILA) model of a sequential circuit?**

**Model Answer:**
The ILA model unrolls a sequential circuit in the time dimension, replicating its combinational logic block multiple times. Each replica represents the circuit's behavior in one clock cycle (one "time frame"). The next-state outputs of frame $i$ become the present-state inputs of frame $i+1$. The ILA model reduces sequential ATPG to a multi-frame combinational ATPG problem.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks each, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The university provides an internal choice — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]
**Explain the difficulties encountered in sequential circuit test generation. Discuss why sequential ATPG is computationally harder than combinational ATPG.**

**Model Solution:**

Sequential ATPG is significantly harder than combinational ATPG due to the following difficulties:

**Difficulty 1 — Unknown Initial State:**
In real circuits, flip-flops power up in unknown state $X$. ATPG must therefore reason with three-valued or nine-valued logic, drastically increasing the search space.

**Difficulty 2 — State Justification:**
To activate a fault deep in the circuit, ATPG may need to drive FFs into a specific state. Justifying that state may itself require a long, unpredictable sequence of vectors. This is essentially a sub-problem of test generation nested inside the main problem.

**Difficulty 3 — Fault Effect Masking in Memory Elements:**
A fault effect generated in cycle $t$ may be overwritten by a fault-free transition in cycle $t+1$. ATPG must carefully time the propagation window.

**Difficulty 4 — Sequential Redundancy:**
Many faults are sequentially redundant — undetectable by *any* input sequence. ATPG algorithms can loop forever trying to find a test.

**Difficulty 5 — Search Space Explosion:**
The search space is $2^{n_{PI}} \times 2^{n_{FF}} = 2^{n_{PI} + n_{FF}}$. For a circuit with 50 FFs and 50 PIs, the space is $2^{100} \approx 10^{30}$, which is intractable.

**Why it's computationally harder:**
- Combinational ATPG is NP-complete but practical heuristics (FAN, SOCRATES) solve circuits of $10^4$–$10^5$ gates in minutes.
- Sequential ATPG is **provably harder** — it requires both *temporal* (multi-frame) reasoning and *spatial* (combinational) reasoning.
- The presence of feedback cycles (sequential loops) introduces additional complexity unconstrained by the topological depth of the circuit.

**Summary table:**

| Difficulty | Root Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Unknown initial state | No reset structure | Add reset; use full scan |
| State justification | Feedback loops | Scan chain; BIST |
| Fault masking | FF overwrites | Careful time-frame management |
| Sequential redundancy | Undetectable faults | Abort after $K_{\max}$ frames |
| Search explosion | $2^{n_{FF}}$ states | Heuristic search; partitioning |

**(7 marks: ~1 mark per difficulty, plus 1–2 marks for synthesis and summary.)**

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the time-frame expansion method of sequential circuit test generation. How many time frames are typically required for a fault with sequential depth $d$?**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Time Frame Expansion:**
In time frame expansion, a sequential circuit is replicated as a cascade of combinational time frames. The present-state (PS) lines of frame $i+1$ are the next-state (NS) lines of frame $i$.

**Procedure:**

1. **Frame 0 (Initialization Frame):** The state is set to the all-$X$ (unknown) vector unless a known reset state is available.

2. **Frame 1 to $K-1$ (State Setup + Fault Activation):** Apply vectors $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_{K-1}$ to drive the FFs to a state that activates the fault and prepares the propagation path.

3. **Frame $K$ (Fault Propagation + Observation):** Apply vector $V_K$ such that the fault effect reaches a primary output.

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.1):** A cascade of $K$ combinational time frames, with the state outputs of frame $i$ feeding the state inputs of frame $i+1$.

**Number of Time Frames:**
The minimum number of time frames $K_{\min}$ is bounded below by the sequential depth $d$:
$$K_{\min} \ge d$$
where $d$ is the number of flip-flops on the longest path from the fault site to a primary output (combined with activation depth). Empirically, ATPG tools use $K = 8$ to $K = 25$ frames as a practical upper bound; if no test is found within $K_{\max}$, the fault is declared **ATPG-untestable**.

**(7 marks: 3 for explanation, 2 for diagram, 2 for the $K \ge d$ relationship and the bound discussion.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]
**Explain the design for testability (DFT) approach of full scan and partial scan for sequential circuits. Compare them in terms of area overhead, test length, and fault coverage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Full Scan DFT:**
In full scan, every flip-flop in the design is replaced by a **scan flip-flop** (a multiplexer in front of a D-FF). All scan FFs are chained into one or more **scan chains** controlled by dedicated scan-in (SI) and scan-enable (SE) signals.

**Operation:**
- **Shift mode (SE = 1):** Scan chain shifts test data in/out.
- **Capture mode (SE = 0):** Circuit runs in normal functional mode for one cycle.

**Advantages:**
- Fault coverage > 95% achievable.
- Test generation reduces to combinational ATPG on the un-scanned logic.
- Mature EDA tool support (DFT Compiler, Tessent, Modus).

**Disadvantages:**
- Area overhead: ~5–15% (extra MUX per FF + routing).
- Performance penalty due to MUX insertion in FF data path.
- Test data volume can be large: $L = 2 \cdot n_{FF} \cdot N_{test} + N_{test}$.

**Partial Scan DFT:**
In partial scan, only a *subset* of flip-flops (typically chosen via testability analysis) is converted to scan FFs. The remaining FFs remain as ordinary flip-flops.

**Selection Criteria for Scanned FFs:**
- Scan FFs that break all **sequential feedback loops**.
- Scan FFs that lie on paths with low controllability/observability.
- Typical scan ratio: 30–70% of FFs.

**Advantages:**
- Lower area overhead (3–8%).
- Reduced performance impact.
- Shorter scan chains → faster shift operations.

**Disadvantages:**
- Fault coverage typically 80–95% (lower than full scan).
- Still requires a (limited) sequential ATPG for the non-scanned portion.
- Heuristic selection of FFs may miss critical loops.

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Full Scan | Partial Scan | No Scan |
|---|---|---|---|
| Area overhead | High (5–15%) | Medium (3–8%) | None |
| Test length | $2 n_{FF} N_{test} + N_{test}$ | $\approx 0.5 \times$ full scan | Very large |
| Fault coverage | $> 95\%$ | $80$–$95\%$ | $50$–$85\%$ |
| ATPG complexity | Combinational | Hybrid | Sequential |
| ATE memory | High | Medium | Low |
| Performance impact | 5–10% slower | 2–5% slower | None |

**(7 marks: 2 marks full scan, 2 marks partial scan, 3 marks comparison table.)**

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Describe the GENTEST algorithm for sequential circuit test generation. How does it differ from the Extended D-Algorithm?**

**Model Solution:**

**GENTEST Algorithm (Simulation-Based Heuristic):**
GENTEST, proposed by Seshu (1965) and later refined by Agrawal, is a **fault-simulation-driven** heuristic for sequential ATPG. It works as follows:

**Steps:**

1. **Initialize:** Set the circuit to a known state (e.g., all-0) using a reset sequence.

2. **Pick a target fault** from the undetected list.

3. **Random Input Generation:** Generate random input vectors; simulate both the good and faulty circuits.

4. **Fault Activation:** If the fault effect propagates to a PO, record the vector; otherwise, repeat random simulation $N_{\max}$ times.

5. **Deterministic Phase (if needed):** If random phase fails, invoke a deterministic ATPG (e.g., extended PODEM) for a bounded number of time frames $K_{\max}$.

6. **Fault Simulation Drop:** Use a concurrent fault simulator (e.g., PROOFS) to drop all other faults detected by the new test vector.

7. **Repeat** until all faults are processed or aborted.

**Difference from Extended D-Algorithm:**

| Aspect | Extended D-Algorithm | GENTEST |
|---|---|---|
| Paradigm | Deterministic, D-drive | Simulation-based, random + deterministic |
| Multiple faults | Single-fault at a time | Concurrent fault simulation |
| Backtracking | Yes (D-frontier management) | Limited (random vectors) |
| Test length | Often shorter | Often longer |
| Time per fault | Long | Short (fast random sim) |
| Scalability | Poor beyond 1k gates | Good for 10k+ gates |
| Initial state handling | Heuristic | Reset-driven |

**Key Insight:**
The Extended D-Algorithm is a **deterministic** algorithm that explicitly manages a D-frontier across multiple time frames. GENTEST is **simulation-based** and trades optimality for scalability — it works for much larger circuits.

**(7 marks: 3 marks GENTEST steps, 2 marks deterministic phase, 2 marks comparison table.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sequential ATPG Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing time frames with scan chain length.** Time frame $K$ is a *temporal* unroll, not a hardware chain. Drawing a scan chain when asked for time-frame expansion will lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the initialization phase.** Many students describe only the propagation phase. Always mention the three phases: *init → activation → propagation*.
> 3. **Omitting the state space bound.** When discussing difficulty, always quantify with $2^{n_{FF}}$ or the combined $2^{n_{PI}+n_{FF}}$ expression. Vague qualitative answers lose marks.
> 4. **Mishandling the sequential depth formula.** $L \ge d$ is a *lower bound*, not an equality. Always clarify $K_{\min} \le K \le K_{\max}$.
> 5. **Skipping the area vs. FC trade-off in scan DFT.** Examiners reward students who explicitly tabulate the trade-offs, not just narrate them.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sequential Circuit Test Generation**

- ✅ Sequential circuits need a **sequence** of input vectors, not a single vector.
- ✅ The **Iterative Logic Array (ILA)** unrolls a sequential circuit into combinational time frames.
- ✅ The **time frame expansion** technique has three phases: *initialize → activate → propagate*.
- ✅ **Five key difficulties:** unknown initial state, state justification, fault masking, sequential redundancy, search-space explosion.
- ✅ Sequential ATPG is **NP-hard**; exact algorithms don't scale beyond ~1k gates.
- ✅ **GENTEST** is a simulation-based heuristic; **Extended D-Algorithm** is deterministic.
- ✅ Search space cardinality: $2^{n_{PI} + n_{FF}}$.
- ✅ **Sequential depth** $d$ = number of FFs on the longest path from PI to PO; minimum test length $L \ge d$.
- ✅ **Full scan** = all FFs in scan chain → FC > 95%, area overhead 5–15%.
- ✅ **Partial scan** = selected FFs in scan chain → FC 80–95%, lower area overhead.
- ✅ Test length with full scan: $L_{scan} = N_{test} \times (2 n_{FF} + 1)$.
- ✅ **Boundary scan** (IEEE 1149.1) is a board-level extension of scan DFT.
- ✅ **BIST (Built-In Self-Test)** is used when external ATE cannot access the circuit — uses on-chip PRPG and MISR.
- ✅ **Test compression** (EDT, MLBIST) reduces test data volume by 10×–100×.
- ✅ Industry tools: Synopsys DFT Compiler, Mentor Tessent, Cadence Modus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ATPG for Single-Clock Synchronous Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ATPG for Single-Clock Synchronous Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ATPG (Automatic Test Pattern Generation)** is the algorithmic process of automatically generating input vectors (test patterns) to a digital circuit that distinguish the correct (fault-free) circuit behavior from a faulty circuit behavior caused by specific manufacturing defects (modeled as stuck-at faults).

For **Single-Clock Synchronous Circuits**, ATPG must additionally handle the fact that circuit outputs and next-state values are stored in flip-flops (typically D flip-flops) that are all clocked by a single global clock. Consequently:

> [!NOTE]
> A **single-clock synchronous circuit** is a sequential circuit in which all memory elements (flip-flops) are edge-triggered D-type and share exactly one common clock signal, with no asynchronous set/reset paths.

The standard KTU textbook treatment frames sequential ATPG as an extension of combinational ATPG using the **Time-Frame Expansion (TFE)** method, originally formalized by Roth, based on the **Iterative Logic Array (ILA)** model of a synchronous sequential machine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor trying to diagnose a patient** by asking a sequence of questions. In a combinational circuit, you only need *one* question (one input vector) to find the disease (fault). But in a sequential circuit, the patient only reveals information **after each heartbeat (clock pulse)**, and his memory (flip-flops) carries symptoms between heartbeats.

So you must:
1. **Inject** a known stimulus (initialize the flip-flops).
2. **Wait one clock** — observe state changes.
3. **Propagate** the fault effect through multiple clocks until it surfaces at the primary output.
4. **Observe** the primary output.

The "time frame expansion" is essentially *unrolling* this multi-clock diagnostic conversation into a long list of combinational questions — each clock tick becoming one *frame* (slice) of combinational logic to be solved.

### 1.3 Key Metrics (KTU Standard)

| Metric | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Fault Coverage** | Detected faults / Total possible faults | **\mathbf{95\% - 99\%}** is industry target |
| **Test Length (L)** | Total number of vectors in test set | **\mathbf{O(n)} to \mathbf{O(n^2)}** |
| **Sequential Depth** | Min. number of frames needed to detect a fault | **\mathbf{1} to \mathbf{50+}** |
| **FLT (Fault List)** | Set of target faults being processed | **\mathbf{2n}** for n lines |

### 1.4 Why Sequential ATPG is Fundamentally Harder

> [!WARNING]
> Sequential ATPG is **NP-complete** (even harder than combinational ATPG which is also NP-complete but tractable in practice). The state space grows **exponentially** with the number of flip-flops: a circuit with **k** flip-flops has **2^k** possible internal states.

The four compounding difficulties are:
1. **State Justification** — we must find an input sequence that drives the flip-flops into a desired state (reverse-time problem).
2. **Fault Propagation** — the fault effect must travel through flip-flops across many clock cycles.
3. **Initialization** — we must find a sequence that brings the circuit from power-on state (X) to a known state.
4. **Cycle-Free Constraint** — for ILA expansion to work, the unrolled circuit must be **acyclic**, requiring that the next-state logic never feed back into the same time frame.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Iterative Logic Array (ILA) Model

The single-clock synchronous circuit is modeled as a sequence of **combinational copies** of the next-state and output logic, separated by the flip-flops. By replicating the combinational block **T** times (where **T** = time-frame depth), we get a purely combinational circuit — and any combinational ATPG algorithm (D-algorithm, PODEM, FAN) can be applied directly.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Replace every flip-flop with two pins: a **pseudo-input** (the Q-output going into the combinational logic) and a **pseudo-output** (the D-input driven by the combinational logic).
2. Concatenate **T** time frames: pseudo-output of frame *i* → pseudo-input of frame *i+1*.
3. Apply a combinational ATPG algorithm across the entire unrolled ILA.
4. The resulting vector sequence is the test sequence.

### 2.2 Three-System Formulation (Roth's 9-Valued D-Calculus for Sequential)

To handle the unknown initial state **X** of the flip-flops at power-on, Roth generalized the D-cube calculus from 5 values to **9 values**:

| Symbol | Good Circuit | Faulty Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 0 |
| **1** | 1 | 1 |
| **D** | 1 | 0 |
| **D̄** | 0 | 1 |
| **X** | X | X |
| **D/U** | 1 | 0 or X |
| **D̄/U** | 0 | 1 or X |
| **U/D** | 1 or X | 0 |
| **U/D̄** | 0 or X | 1 |

The **"U" (Unknown)** symbols capture the fact that a flip-flop value depends on the unknown initial state and can be either 0 or 1 in subsequent frames.

### 2.3 The 9-Valued Calculus for Time-Frame *i*

For a flip-flop's pseudo-output at time frame *i*:
- If the fault effect has been activated and propagated into the FF by frame *i-1*, the pseudo-input to frame *i* takes values **D, D̄, D/U, D̄/U**.
- Otherwise it stays **X** (unknown).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every key equation, bound, and complexity result that KTU examiners expect for Module 4.

| Concept | Equation / Expression | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total possible stuck-at faults | $\mathbf{N_{faults} = 2n}$ | **n** = total signal lines |
| ILA unrolling length | $\mathbf{T \ge d_{seq}(f)}$ | **d_seq(f)** = sequential depth of fault **f** |
| State space size | $\mathbf{\vert S \vert = 2^k}$ | **k** = number of flip-flops |
| Test length (avg) | $\mathbf{L \approx 20 \text{ to } 100 \text{ vectors}}$ | Depends on design |
| Fault coverage | $\mathbf{FC = \frac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%}$ | Target ≥ **95%** |
| Time-frame size (gates) | $\mathbf{G = G_{comb} + k}$ | **G_comb** = combinational gates |
| PODEM backtrack limit | $\mathbf{B_{max} = 10 \text{ to } 100}$ | Configurable parameter |
| Random pattern detection prob. | $\mathbf{p_d \approx 1 - (1 - 0.5^{k_i})^{N}}$ | **k_i** = # controllable inputs of gate **i**, **N** = vectors |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sequential ATPG underpins **scan-based DFT (Design for Testability)**, which is mandatory in modern ASICs. Without sequential ATPG:
- We could not test state machines, microprocessors, or controllers.
- **Stuck-open** and **stuck-on** faults in CMOS logic would remain undetected.
- Field returns (RMA) would explode — every untested fault becomes a customer defect.

Industry tools (**TetraMAX, FastScan, Tessent, Encounter Test**) all implement variants of time-frame expansion under the hood, augmented with **scan-chain** insertion to convert the sequential problem back into a combinational one — covered in Module 5.

### 2.6 Why Scan Helps (Preview of Module 5)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Scan insertion reduces sequential ATPG to combinational ATPG by making every flip-flop controllable and observable. The penalty is **area + pin overhead** (one scan-in pin, one scan-out pin) and **performance degradation** due to the scan multiplexer in the data path.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Time-Frame Expansion: Formal Construction

Consider a single-clock synchronous circuit with:
- Primary inputs: $\mathbf{x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m)}$
- Primary outputs: $\mathbf{z = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_p)}$
- State variables (FF outputs): $\mathbf{y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_k)}$
- Next-state (FF inputs): $\mathbf{Y = (Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_k)}$

The combinational logic block **C** realizes:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= f_{ns}(x, y) \\
z &= f_o(x, y)
\end{aligned}
$$

To create **T** time frames, we replicate **C** into **T** copies indexed $C^{(1)}, C^{(2)}, \ldots, C^{(T)}$ with inputs and outputs at each frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame } i: \quad Y^{(i)} &= f_{ns}(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \\
z^{(i)} &= f_o(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})
\end{aligned}
$$

The flip-flop constraint binds pseudo-output of frame *i* to pseudo-input of frame *i+1*:
$$
y^{(i+1)} = Y^{(i)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, T-1
$$

The ILA is now a **pure combinational** network of **T·(m + p + 2k)** terminals and **T·G** gates, where **G** is the number of gates in the original combinational logic.

### 3.2 Derivation: Why Cycles Break ILA Expansion

The ILA model assumes the unrolled network is **acyclic**. Suppose at frame *i* we have:
$$
y_j^{(i)} \rightarrow \text{gate } g \rightarrow Y_j^{(i)} = y_j^{(i+1)}
$$

If the same gate also receives $y_j^{(i)}$ as input (creating a combinational loop in the *same* frame), then the unrolled network contains a cycle, and standard combinational ATPG algorithms cannot terminate.

**Resolution:** KTU Module 4 assumes the circuit has **no combinational feedback loops within a single time frame** — a property guaranteed by proper single-clock synchronous design with edge-triggered D flip-flops.

### 3.3 Derivation: 9-Valued D-Cube for Sequential D-Algorithm

The classical 9-value D-notation is defined as a pair $(g, f)$ where $g \in \{0, 1, X\}$ and $f \in \{0, 1, X\}$:
- $g$ = value in good circuit
- $f$ = value in faulty circuit

$$
\begin{aligned}
(g, f) \in \{& (0,0), (1,1), (1,0), (0,1), \\
& (X,X), (1,X), (0,X), (X,0), (X,1) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

These map to $\{0, 1, D, \overline{D}, X, D/U, \overline{D}/U, U/D, U/\overline{D}\}$.

The **propagation D-cube** for an AND gate with input sequence $(D, 1)$ in the good machine and $(0, 1)$ in the faulty machine propagates the fault to the output as $(1, 0) \equiv D$.

### 3.4 Derivation: Initialization Sequence Length Bound

Let $S_0$ be the unknown power-on state and $S_i$ be the state after $i$ initialization vectors. The initialization sequence must drive all flip-flops from $\{X\}^k$ to a fully specified state. The maximum length required is:

$$
L_{init} \le 2^k - 1
$$

In practice, for randomly initializable designs, expected length is approximately:
$$
L_{init} \approx \log_2(k) + c, \quad c \approx 3 \text{ to } 5
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (Sequential ATPG for a 2-bit counter with s-a-0 fault):**
>
> Circuit: 2-bit synchronous up-counter using D flip-flops. $D_1 = y_1 \oplus y_2$, $D_2 = \overline{y_2}$, with output $z = y_1$.
> Target fault: $y_2$ stuck-at 0.
>
> **Step 1:** Identify state for fault activation. Need $y_2 = 1$ (good), but faulty $y_2 = 0$.
>
> **Step 2:** Time frame 1. Apply input vector to set $y^{(1)} = (y_1=0, y_2=1)$. This requires previous state $y^{(0)} = (1, 1)$ — justification gives input vector (none, since counter has no PI). Result: $Y^{(1)} = (1, 0)$.
>
> **Step 3:** Time frame 2. Pseudo-input $y^{(2)} = Y^{(1)} = (1, 0)$. In good circuit, $D_2 = \overline{0} = 1$. In faulty circuit, $D_2 = 0$. So $Y^{(2)}_{good} = (0, 1)$, $Y^{(2)}_{faulty} = (0, 0)$. Output $z^{(2)}_{good} = 0$, $z^{(2)}_{faulty} = 0$ → fault NOT observable yet.
>
> **Step 4:** Time frame 3. Pseudo-input $y^{(3)} = (0, 1)$ vs $(0, 0)$. $D_2$ good = $\overline{1} = 0$, faulty = $0$ (because $y_2$ stuck-at 0). So $Y^{(3)}_{good} = (1, 0)$, $Y^{(3)}_{faulty} = (1, 0)$. No difference — fault masked.
>
> **Step 5:** Time frame 4. Pseudo-input $y^{(4)} = (1, 0)$ vs $(1, 0)$. $D_2$ good = $\overline{0} = 1$, faulty = $0$. So $Y^{(4)}_{good} = (0, 1)$, $Y^{(4)}_{faulty} = (0, 0)$. Output $z^{(4)}_{good} = 0$, $z^{(4)}_{faulty} = 0$ — still no output difference.
>
> **Conclusion:** This particular stuck-at-0 fault on $y_2$ is **redundant** in the presence of the counter — no test sequence exists without scan. This motivates scan insertion.

### 3.6 Analytical Complexity Matrix

| Method | Time Complexity | Memory | Detects All Detectable Faults? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brute-force simulation | $\mathbf{O(2^k \cdot 2^m)}$ | Low | Yes (in theory) |
| Extended D-Algorithm (Sequential) | Exponential in *k* | High | Yes, but backtrack-bound |
| PODEM-extended | Exponential in *k* | Medium | Yes, with good heuristics |
| FAN-extended | Exponential in *k* | Medium | Yes |
| Genetic / Simulation-based | Polynomial in *k* | Low | **No — heuristic only** |
| Scan-based ATPG | Polynomial in *n* | Medium | Yes (with scan chain) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sequential Circuit → Iterative Logic Array (Time Frame Expansion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Frame1["Time Frame i = 1"]
        PI1["Primary Inputs x1"]
        YPI1["Pseudo-Inputs y1 (from power-on X)"]
        COMB1["Combinational Logic Block C"]
        YPO1["Pseudo-Outputs Y1 (to FFs)"]
        PO1["Primary Outputs z1"]
    end
    subgraph Frame2["Time Frame i = 2"]
        PI2["Primary Inputs x2"]
        YPI2["Pseudo-Inputs y2"]
        COMB2["Combinational Logic Block C"]
        YPO2["Pseudo-Outputs Y2"]
        PO2["Primary Outputs z2"]
    end
    subgraph Frame3["Time Frame i = T"]
        PI3["Primary Inputs xT"]
        YPI3["Pseudo-Inputs yT"]
        COMB3["Combinational Logic Block C"]
        YPO3["Pseudo-Outputs YT"]
        PO3["Primary Outputs zT"]
    end
    YPO1 --> YPI2
    YPO2 --> YPI3
    YPI1 --> COMB1
    PI1 --> COMB1
    COMB1 --> YPO1
    COMB1 --> PO1
    YPI2 --> COMB2
    PI2 --> COMB2
    COMB2 --> YPO2
    COMB2 --> PO2
    YPI3 --> COMB3
    PI3 --> COMB3
    COMB3 --> YPO3
    COMB3 --> PO3
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Sequential ATPG Tool

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Netlist + Library + Fault List"]
    B["Circuit Preprocessor"]
    C["Time-Frame Expander (T frames)"]
    D["Combinational ATPG Engine (PODEM / FAN)"]
    E["Test Sequence Assembler"]
    F["Fault Simulator (Parallel / Deductive)"]
    G["Drop / Retain Decision"]
    H["Test Set Output"]
    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -->|Test found| E
    D -->|Abort frame| F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G -->|Detected| H
    G -->|Undetected| D
```

### 4.3 Sequential D-Algorithm State Machine (Detailed Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0["Start: Pick untested fault f"]
    S1["Choose frame i (begin with i = 1)"]
    S2["Activate f in frame i (D-drive)"]
    S3["Propagate D-frontier to PO of frame i"]
    S4{"D-frontier reaches PO?"}
    S5["Justify pseudo-inputs of frame i (backward)"]
    S6{"Justification OK?"}
    S7["Justify pseudo-inputs of frame i-1 (recursive)"]
    S8{"All frames justified?"}
    S9["TEST FOUND — record vector sequence"]
    S10["Increase frame count i ← i + 1"]
    S11{"i ≤ T_max?"}
    S12["ABORT — fault aborted"]
    S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S4 -->|Yes| S5
    S4 -->|No| S12
    S5 --> S6
    S6 -->|Yes| S7
    S6 -->|No| S12
    S7 --> S8
    S8 -->|Yes| S9
    S8 -->|No| S12
    S12 --> S10
    S10 --> S11
    S11 -->|Yes| S2
    S11 -->|No| S12
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Mapping: From Testability Problem to Scan Solution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Sequential Circuit with k flip-flops"]
    P2["State: 2 to the k possibilities"]
    P3["Test generation time: NP-complete in k"]
    P4["Scan Insertion (Module 5)"]
    P5["All FFs become a shift register in test mode"]
    P6["State controllable in k clock cycles"]
    P7["Combinational ATPG suffices (PODEM / FAN)"]
    P8["Test generation time: O of n"]
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P1 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Why is sequential ATPG considered fundamentally harder than combinational ATPG? List any four reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sequential ATPG is harder than combinational ATPG for the following reasons:

1. **State-space explosion (1 mark):** A circuit with *k* flip-flops has $2^k$ possible states, so the search space is exponential in *k*, whereas combinational ATPG is exponential only in the number of primary inputs.
2. **State justification is a reverse-time problem (0.5 mark):** To set a desired internal state, the algorithm must search backwards through time, which has no analog in combinational ATPG.
3. **Initialization problem (0.5 mark):** The power-on state of the flip-flops is unknown (**X**), requiring a separate initialization sequence to be found before fault testing begins.
4. **Fault propagation across time frames (0.5 mark):** A fault effect may need to be carried through several flip-flops over multiple clock cycles before it reaches a primary output.
5. **Possibility of redundant or undetectable faults (0.5 mark):** Sequential circuits can contain faults that are detectable only after an unbounded or unacceptably long sequence.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the Iterative Logic Array (ILA) model? How does it enable sequential ATPG?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Iterative Logic Array (ILA) model** (1 mark) is a structural representation of a single-clock synchronous sequential circuit obtained by **replicating the combinational logic block** *T* times in time, with each copy called a **time frame**. The flip-flops are replaced by **pseudo-inputs** and **pseudo-outputs**, and the pseudo-output of frame *i* is connected to the pseudo-input of frame *i+1* (1 mark).

This conversion makes the unrolled network a **pure combinational circuit**, which means any combinational ATPG algorithm such as the D-algorithm, PODEM, or FAN can be applied directly to generate a **test vector sequence** rather than a single vector (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Describe the **9-valued D-calculus** used for sequential ATPG. Show how the unknown initial state **X** of flip-flops is represented, and explain how the **D/U** and **U/D** symbols propagate across time frames. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **Time-Frame Expansion (TFE) method** for generating tests for single-clock synchronous circuits. Include the construction of the ILA and discuss the choice of the number of time frames **T**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — The 5-value D-calculus is insufficient (1 mark).**
Classical combinational D-calculus uses 5 values: $\{0, 1, D, \overline{D}, X\}$. In sequential ATPG, the **X** value cannot simply stay **X** indefinitely — once a flip-flop is clocked, the **X** may resolve to either 0 or 1 depending on the previous state.

**Step 2 — Roth's 9-value extension (2 marks).**
Roth defined each value as an ordered pair $(g, f)$ where $g$ is the good-circuit value and $f$ the faulty-circuit value. The 9 distinct pairs are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0,0) &= 0 \\
(1,1) &= 1 \\
(1,0) &= D \\
(0,1) &= \overline{D} \\
(X,X) &= X \\
(1,X) &= D/U \\
(0,X) &= \overline{D}/U \\
(X,0) &= U/D \\
(X,1) &= U/\overline{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial flip-flop values (1 mark).**
At time frame $i = 1$, all flip-flops are in the unknown power-on state. So every pseudo-input $y_j^{(1)}$ is set to **X**, which means both good and faulty machines start in state **X**.

**Step 4 — Propagation across time frames (3 marks).**
- A pseudo-output $Y_j^{(i)}$ equal to **D/U** in the good machine means the value is 1, but the faulty value is unknown (because the FF inputs depend on the unknown initial state).
- When this $Y_j^{(i)}$ becomes the pseudo-input $y_j^{(i+1)}$ of the next frame, the next-frame logic produces a value that may be **D, U/D, U/$\overline{D}$**, depending on how the next frame's combinational logic processes the partial information.
- A **U/D** at a pseudo-output means: "good machine value is either 1 or X, faulty value is 0" — this lets the algorithm **delay** the decision until enough information is available.

This 9-valued notation is the **backbone** of all KTU-level sequential ATPG problems.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Construction of the ILA (2 marks).**
Take the original sequential circuit. Identify every flip-flop. Replace each by:
- a **pseudo-input terminal** $y_j^{(i)}$ (where $j$ is the FF index and $i$ is the time frame), and
- a **pseudo-output terminal** $Y_j^{(i)}$.

Duplicate the combinational logic block *T* times. Wire the pseudo-outputs of frame $i$ to the pseudo-inputs of frame $i+1$ for $i = 1, \ldots, T-1$. The result is a purely combinational network of size $T \cdot G$ where $G$ is the number of gates in the original combinational logic.

**Step 2 — Apply combinational ATPG (2 marks).**
Invoke PODEM, FAN, or the D-algorithm on the entire ILA. The algorithm produces:
- A sequence of input vectors $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(T)}$,
- A sequence of pseudo-input assignments $y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}, \ldots, y^{(T)}$,
- A set of output vectors $z^{(1)}, z^{(2)}, \ldots, z^{(T)}$.

**Step 3 — Extract the test sequence (1 mark).**
The vectors $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(T)}$ form the test sequence applied at the primary inputs. The pseudo-input assignments must be **realizable** as the natural evolution of the flip-flops — this is the **state-justification constraint**.

**Step 4 — Choice of T (2 marks).**
The minimum $T$ is the **sequential depth** of the fault, defined as the smallest number of time frames after fault activation in which the fault effect reaches a primary output. In practice, $T$ is chosen heuristically (typically $T \le 20$ for combinational ATPG tools, but up to $T = 50$ for difficult sequential faults). If no test is found within $T$, the fault is **aborted**.

**Step 5 — Advantages and limitations (1 mark).**
*Advantages:* Reuse of mature combinational ATPG; theoretical completeness for detectable faults.
*Limitations:* NP-complete in $k$ (number of FFs); very large memory for deep time frames.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain how the **Time-Frame Expansion** converts a sequential circuit into an equivalent combinational structure suitable for ATPG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sequential circuit has **4 flip-flops** and is being targeted by sequential ATPG with a maximum time-frame depth **T = 5**. Estimate (i) the maximum number of states the algorithm must consider, and (ii) the maximum test length. Justify your answers with formulas. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw a 2-frame ILA. Show the original sequential circuit (left), the split into pseudo-inputs/ pseudo-outputs (middle), and the replicated 2-frame ILA (right). Reference the diagram in Section 4.1 above.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Label each terminal clearly:
- Pseudo-inputs $y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}$
- Pseudo-outputs $Y^{(1)}, Y^{(2)}$
- Primary inputs $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}$
- Primary outputs $z^{(1)}, z^{(2)}$
- Show the **arrow** from $Y^{(i)} \rightarrow y^{(i+1)}$ representing the flip-flop transfer.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Explain that for ATPG, the algorithm treats the entire ILA as a combinational network and uses a D-algorithm or PODEM to assign signal values.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Discuss the **acyclic** requirement: there must be no combinational feedback loop within a single time frame (single-clock synchronous circuits satisfy this).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude with how the algorithm extracts $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(T)}$ as the final test sequence.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $k = 4$ flip-flops, $T = 5$ time frames.

**(i) Maximum number of states considered (3 marks):**

The algorithm explores the state space implicitly. Each time frame introduces $2^k = 2^4 = 16$ possible present states. Across $T = 5$ frames, the theoretical state graph contains:

$$
N_{states} = 2^k \cdot T = 16 \times 5 = 80 \text{ nodes}
$$

If we consider the full state transition graph (without time-frame unrolling), the total reachable states are bounded by $2^k = 16$. However, the **search space** the ATPG explores is:

$$
\mathbf{S_{search} = O(2^k \cdot T)} = O(16 \cdot 5) = O(80)
$$

In practice, due to backtracking and pruning, the **worst-case** states visited can approach the full reachable set across all time frames:

$$
\mathbf{S_{worst} = O(2^k \cdot T + 2^k)} = O(80 + 16) = O(96)
$$

**(ii) Maximum test length (4 marks):**

The test length $L$ equals the number of input vectors needed to detect the fault. For sequential ATPG with $T$ time frames:

$$
L = T = 5 \text{ vectors (lower bound)}
$$

Including initialization sequence $L_{init}$:

$$
L_{total} = L_{init} + T \le 2^k - 1 + T
$$

$$
L_{total}^{max} = 2^4 - 1 + 5 = 15 + 5 = \mathbf{20 \text{ vectors}}
$$

**Justification:** Each flip-flop must be driven from unknown to a known value (worst case $2^k - 1$ steps for a counter), then the fault-detection sequence requires $T$ additional vectors. The upper bound is $L_{total} \le 2^k - 1 + T$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "states" with "vectors":** Students often write "max test length = $2^k$" without realizing the test length is bounded by $T$ plus initialization, not by state space alone. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting the X/U handling:** When asked about 9-valued D-calculus, many students list only the 5 classical values, missing the **D/U** and **U/D** symbols. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Missing the acyclic requirement:** ILA expansion assumes no within-frame combinational loops. Students forget to mention this. (Lose 0.5–1 mark.)
> 4. **Not drawing the ILA diagram:** For 7-mark questions on TFE, the diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself. A textual description alone is insufficient.
> 5. **Writing $|S| = 2k$ instead of $2^k$:** A frequent careless error in state-space calculations. (Lose 0.5 mark.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ATPG for Single-Clock Synchronous Circuits**

- [x] **Single-clock synchronous circuit** = all FFs are edge-triggered D-type, share one clock, no within-frame combinational feedback.
- [x] **ATPG goal** = generate input vector sequence to distinguish fault-free and faulty behavior at the POs.
- [x] **Time-Frame Expansion (TFE)** = replicate combinational logic *T* times; replace FFs with pseudo-inputs/pseudo-outputs; bind $Y^{(i)} \rightarrow y^{(i+1)}$.
- [x] **ILA (Iterative Logic Array)** = the unrolled, purely combinational equivalent of the sequential circuit.
- [x] **9-valued D-calculus** = Roth's extension of the 5-value D-algebra to handle unknown FF initial states via **D/U** and **U/D** symbols.
- [x] **Fault coverage** $FC = N_{detected}/N_{total} \times 100\%$; target **≥ 95%**.
- [x] **State space** grows as $\mathbf{2^k}$ where $k$ = number of FFs — the root cause of sequential ATPG's hardness.
- [x] **Initialization sequence** $L_{init} \le 2^k - 1$ in the worst case; typically $\log_2(k) + c$.
- [x] **Time-frame depth $T$** = sequential depth of the target fault; chosen heuristically, often $T \le 20$.
- [x] **Test length** $L = L_{init} + T \le 2^k - 1 + T$.
- [x] **Combinational ATPG algorithms** (D-algorithm, PODEM, FAN) are reused on the ILA — no new algorithm required.
- [x] **Complexity** = NP-complete in $k$ → motivates **scan-based DFT** (preview of Module 5).
- [x] **Cyclic dependency** within a single frame would break ILA; single-clock synchronous design guarantees acyclic ILA.
- [x] **Redundant sequential faults** exist; they cannot be detected without design modification.
- [x] **Standard fault model** = single stuck-at (SSA); bridging and delay faults require extended models beyond Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delay Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delay Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delay Test (Timing Test):** A non-trivial DFT (Design-for-Test) technique used to verify that a digital circuit operates correctly at its **specified clock frequency (rated speed)** by validating that signal transitions propagate through all logic paths within the **allocated timing budget**. Unlike stuck-at tests that check logical correctness, delay tests verify *temporal correctness*.

A delay test is a **two-pattern test** consisting of:
- **V1 (Initialization Vector):** Sets up the initial logic state of the target path.
- **V2 (Launch Vector / Transition Vector):** Launches a transition (0→1 or 1→0) that propagates through the path under test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Delay testing falls under the *At-Speed Testing* domain, and is essential because modern nanometer CMOS designs (≤ 65nm) suffer from process variation, IR drop, and crosstalk, which manifest as *timing violations* that are invisible to stuck-at ATPG.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Relay Race"

Imagine a relay race with **N runners** representing a logic path of N gates:

| Real-World Analogy | VLSI Delay Test Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Race track | Combinational logic path |
| Each runner's sprint time | Gate propagation delay ($t_{pd}$) |
| Track length between runners | Interconnect delay ($t_{wire}$) |
| Race deadline = 100 m finish line | Clock period ($T_{clk}$) |
| Stopwatch reading at finish | Measured path delay ($D_{path}$) |
| Baton (signal transition) | Signal edge 0→1 or 1→0 |

**The Test:** We start the stopwatch the moment the baton leaves the first runner, and stop it when the last runner crosses the finish line. If `Stopwatch Reading > 100 m sprint time allowed`, the team **fails the test** — even though each individual runner looks healthy. This is precisely a **delay fault**: the circuit is *logically* correct but *temporally* broken.

> [!TIP]
> **Why can't Stuck-At Tests catch this?** A stuck-at test only asks *"Is the answer right?"* — never *"Did the answer come on time?"* A delay-faulty chip produces correct outputs at slow clocks but **erroneous outputs at the rated speed**, causing system failure in the field.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard CMOS timing parameters govern delay testing:

- **$t_{pd}$ (Propagation Delay):** Time for a signal to travel from gate input to output — typically **10 ps – 500 ps** per gate in modern nodes.
- **$T_{clk}$ (Clock Period):** The rated operating period, e.g., **1 ns @ 1 GHz** or **10 ns @ 100 MHz**.
- **$t_{su}$ (Setup Time):** Minimum time data must be stable before clock edge — typically **50 – 200 ps**.
- **$t_h$ (Hold Time):** Minimum time data must remain stable after clock edge — typically **20 – 100 ps**.
- **$t_{r}$ / $t_{f}$ (Rise / Fall Time):** Signal transition duration — typically **0.1 – 0.3 × $t_{pd}$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Delay Fault Models

Delay faults are modelled at different abstraction levels. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the first two:

#### 2.1.1 Transition Delay Fault (TDF) — *Gross Delay Model*

A **single fault site** along a path exhibits a delay large enough to violate timing. It has two polarities per line:

- **Slow-to-Rise (STR):** Transition 0→1 is delayed.
- **Slow-to-Fall (STF):** Transition 1→0 is delayed.

$$\text{Number of TDFs} = 2 \times N_{\text{total lines}}$$

**Why it matters:** TDFs are *easy to generate* (ATPG complexity is similar to stuck-at) and *cover most manufacturing defects* (bridges, opens, resistive vias) that produce gross timing violations. They are the **industry-standard at-speed test model** in production scan.

#### 2.1.2 Path Delay Fault (PDF) — *Distributed Delay Model*

A delay fault distributed across an *entire path* such that the cumulative delay exceeds $T_{clk}$. The fault is detectable only if every gate along the path operates at the edge of its timing window — making PDFs excellent at catching **small delay defects** undetectable by TDFs.

$$\text{Number of PDFs} = \text{Exponentially large (often } 10^6 \text{ to } 10^9\text{ paths)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Limit:** Path Delay Faults are *combinatorially explosive*. Industrial ATPG tools therefore target **a critical subset** of long paths (statistical path selection / slack-based selection).

#### 2.1.3 Gate Delay Fault (GDF) & Line Delay Fault (LDF)

- **Gate Delay Fault:** Excess delay localized to one gate input pin.
- **Line Delay Fault:** Excess delay on a single interconnect segment.

---

### 2.2 Robust vs. Non-Robust Delay Tests

A critical KTU board-question concept:

| Test Type | Definition | Other Delays' Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Robust Test** | The test detects the target delay fault **regardless of delays on other (off-path) inputs** | Immune to hazards, races, and other-gate delays |
| **Non-Robust Test** | The test detects the fault **only if no other unexpected delays exist** | May produce false positives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robust tests are the gold standard** for delay testing. A robustly testable path is guaranteed to be delay-fault-free if the test passes. Non-robust tests require *timing simulation* to confirm validity.

---

### 2.3 At-Speed Test Application Schemes

Modern ATE (Automatic Test Equipment) supports two schemes for delivering two-pattern tests through scan chains:

#### 2.3.1 Launch-on-Shift (LOS) — *Functional Justification*

```
Capture_Clock = Shift_Clock (last shift cycle)
Test time = 2 × N (where N = scan length)
```

- The V1 pattern is the *last shifted-in* value; V2 is generated by the *one additional shift* in functional/capture mode.
- **Disadvantage:** High power dissipation; test pattern may not reflect functional operation.

#### 2.3.2 Launch-on-Capture (LOC) — *Functional Justification via PI*

```
V1 = Response captured from previous test
V2 = Response of combinational logic to V1
Test time = 2 × N + functional clocking overhead
```

- **Advantage:** Test patterns are *functionally justified* through primary inputs (PIs) — low power, high correlation to functional operation.
- **Industry-preferred scheme** for at-speed scan.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Equations** — Memorize the boxed quantities.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{\text{path}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{pd,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} t_{\text{wire},j}$ | Total path delay = sum of gate + wire delays | ns |
| 2 | $T_{clk} = D_{\text{path,max}} + t_{su} + \text{slack}$ | Clock period definition | ns |
| 3 | $\text{Slack} = T_{clk} - (D_{\text{path}} + t_{su})$ | Timing margin (positive = passes) | ns |
| 4 | $N_{\text{TDF}} = 2 \times L$ | TDF count (L = total lines) | faults |
| 5 | $f_{\text{test}} = f_{\text{operational}}$ | At-speed test frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $t_{\text{capture}} = t_{\text{launch}} + D_{\text{path}}$ | Capture edge timing | ns |
| 7 | $\text{Coverage}_{\text{delay}} = \dfrac{\text{Detected PDFs}}{\text{Targeted PDFs}} \times 100\%$ | Delay fault coverage | % |
| 8 | $t_{2\text{-pattern}} = N \cdot T_{\text{shift}} + T_{\text{capture}}$ | Two-pattern test application time | ns |
| 9 | $E_{\text{energy}} = C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f \cdot N_{\text{toggles}}$ | Dynamic switching energy (LOS penalty) | J |
| 10 | $\text{Hazard-free condition: } t_{pd,\text{on-path}} < t_{pd,\text{off-path}} + \Delta t$ | Robustness condition | ns |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Equation 3 (Slack) is the *most-tested formula* in KTU 2024 ESE. Always show the algebraic substitution of $D_{\text{path}}$ from Eq. 1 into Eq. 3.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Delay testing is **non-negotiable** in:

1. **Automotive ICs (ISO 26262 / ASIL-D):** A timing failure can cause airbag deployment failure — *life-critical*.
2. **Aerospace & Defense (DO-254):** Bit-error rates must remain below $10^{-12}$.
3. **5G/6G Baseband:** mmWave designs run at 28–60 GHz; even **1 ps** timing error is catastrophic.
4. **AI Accelerators (GPUs/TPUs):** Datapaths must complete matrix multiplies within microsecond budgets.
5. **Memory Interfaces (DDR5, HBM3):** Eye-diagram compliance requires sub-picosecond jitter control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Path Delay for a Multi-Stage CMOS Path

**Problem Statement:** Consider a 4-stage carry-propagation path consisting of 4 identical NAND gates. Each gate has $t_{pd} = 50$ ps and each interconnect contributes $t_{\text{wire}} = 20$ ps. Compute the total path delay.

**Given:**
- $N = 4$ gates
- $t_{pd,\text{NAND}} = 50$ ps (per gate)
- $t_{\text{wire}} = 20$ ps (per segment)
- $M = 3$ inter-stage wires (between 4 gates)

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
We invoke Equation 1 from the formula sheet:

$$D_{\text{path}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{pd,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} t_{\text{wire},j}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the gate-delay summation.**
Since all four gates are identical NANDs with $t_{pd} = 50$ ps:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} t_{pd,i} = t_{pd,1} + t_{pd,2} + t_{pd,3} + t_{pd,4}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} t_{pd,i} = 50 + 50 + 50 + 50 = 200 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the wire-delay summation.**
With 3 inter-stage interconnect segments of 20 ps each:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{3} t_{\text{wire},j} = 20 + 20 + 20 = 60 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain total path delay.**

$$D_{\text{path}} = 200 \text{ ps} + 60 \text{ ps} = 260 \text{ ps}$$

**Result:** $D_{\text{path}} = 260$ ps.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slack Computation Against Rated Clock

**Problem Statement:** The 4-stage path above must be operational at $T_{clk} = 400$ ps with a flip-flop setup time $t_{su} = 50$ ps. Compute the **timing slack** and determine pass/fail status.

**Given:**
- $D_{\text{path}} = 260$ ps (from Derivation 1)
- $T_{clk} = 400$ ps
- $t_{su} = 50$ ps

**Step 1 — Write the slack equation (Equation 3):**

$$\text{Slack} = T_{clk} - (D_{\text{path}} + t_{su})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\text{Slack} = 400 - (260 + 50)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inner parentheses first.**

$$260 + 50 = 310 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the slack.**

$$\text{Slack} = 400 - 310 = 90 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

Since Slack $= +90$ ps $> 0$, the path has **positive timing margin** of 90 ps and **passes** the delay test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *negative* slack would indicate a **timing violation** — the circuit is too slow for the rated clock and would fail at-speed functional verification.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fault Coverage Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 6-input LUT-based design has $L = 120$ signal lines. An ATPG tool generates delay tests that detect 95 slow-to-rise and 88 slow-to-fall faults. Compute the **delay fault coverage** (TDF-based).

**Given:**
- $L = 120$ total lines
- Detected STR $= 95$
- Detected STF $= 88$

**Step 1 — Compute total possible TDFs (Equation 4):**

$$N_{\text{TDF,total}} = 2 \times L = 2 \times 120 = 240$$

**Step 2 — Sum the detected faults.**

$$N_{\text{detected}} = 95 + 88 = 183$$

**Step 3 — Apply the coverage formula (Equation 7):**

$$\text{Coverage} = \frac{N_{\text{detected}}}{N_{\text{TDF,total}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$\text{Coverage} = \frac{183}{240} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Coverage} = 0.7625 \times 100\% = 76.25\%$$

**Result:** Delay fault coverage $= 76.25\%$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Production-quality VLSI designs target **≥ 95% TDF coverage** at-speed. Coverage below 90% is generally flagged as a DFT sign-off failure.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Two-Pattern Test Application Time (LOS Scheme)

**Problem Statement:** A scan chain of length $N = 512$ flip-flops runs at shift frequency $f_{\text{shift}} = 100$ MHz, with one additional functional capture cycle at $f_{\text{test}} = 500$ MHz. Compute the total two-pattern test time using **Launch-on-Shift**.

**Given:**
- $N = 512$
- $T_{\text{shift}} = 1 / f_{\text{shift}} = 1 / (100 \times 10^{6}) = 10$ ns
- $T_{\text{capture}} = 1 / f_{\text{test}} = 1 / (500 \times 10^{6}) = 2$ ns

**Step 1 — Apply Equation 8 (LOS variant):**

$$t_{2\text{-pattern,LOS}} = 2N \cdot T_{\text{shift}} + T_{\text{capture}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the shift-time component.**

$$2N \cdot T_{\text{shift}} = 2 \times 512 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 10240 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Add the capture-cycle overhead.**

$$t_{2\text{-pattern,LOS}} = 10240 + 2 = 10242 \text{ ns} \approx 10.242 \text{ μs}$$

**Result:** Two-pattern test time $\approx 10.24$ μs per test.

> [!TIP]
> **Test-Cost Insight:** For $10^6$ test patterns, the ATE time becomes $10.24$ s, which is significant. This is why **test compaction** and **compression architectures** (e.g., EDT, DFTMAX) are essential in industrial flows.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Derived Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Numerical Value | Pass/Fail Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total path delay | $D_{\text{path}}$ | 260 ps | < $T_{clk} - t_{su}$ |
| Slack | $\text{Slack}$ | +90 ps | > 0 |
| TDF coverage | $\text{Cov}_{\text{delay}}$ | 76.25% | ≥ 95% |
| Two-pattern test time | $t_{2\text{-pattern}}$ | 10.24 μs | < ATE budget |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow — Delay Test Application Process

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the end-to-end industrial delay test application process from pattern generation to fault diagnosis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Netlist + Timing Library] --> B[Static Timing Analysis STA]
    B --> C[Critical Path Identification]
    C --> D{Path Selection}
    D -->|Longest paths| E[Path Delay Fault List]
    D -->|All lines| F[Transition Fault List]
    E --> G[Delay ATPG Engine]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Two-Pattern Test V1 V2]
    H --> I[LOC or LOS Selection]
    I --> J[Scan Chain Loading V1]
    J --> K[Capture Cycle Launch V2]
    K --> L[At-Speed Response Capture]
    L --> M[ATE Signature Comparison]
    M --> N{Test Outcome}
    N -->|Match| O[PASS]
    N -->|Mismatch| P[FAIL]
    P --> Q[Diagnosis and Repair]
```

---

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Two-Pattern At-Speed Timing Waveform

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PI as Primary Inputs
    participant SC as Scan Chain
    participant FF1 as Launch FF
    participant CPL as Combinational Path
    participant FF2 as Capture FF
    participant ATE as Tester
    PI->>SC: Shift-in V1 (functional pattern)
    SC->>FF1: Apply V1
    Note over FF1,CPL: Wait for setup
    FF1->>CPL: Launch transition 0 to 1
    CPL->>FF2: Propagated transition
    Note over FF2: Must arrive BEFORE capture edge
    FF2->>ATE: Latch output response
    ATE->>ATE: Compare against golden signature
    ATE-->>ATE: PASS or FAIL
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — On-Chip Delay Test Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TEST_CTRL [On-Chip Delay Test Controller]
        TPG[Test Pattern Generator]
        OCC[On-Chip Clock Controller]
        CC[Capture Comparator]
        MISR[Multiple Input Signature Register]
    end
    subgraph CORE [Design Under Test]
        SCAN[Scan Chains]
        CUT[Combinational Logic + Flip-Flops]
    end
    TPG --> SCAN
    OCC --> CUT
    SCAN --> CUT
    CUT --> CC
    CC --> MISR
    MISR --> EXT[External ATE Output]
    OCC --> CC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Note:** The **On-Chip Clock Controller (OCC)** is the heart of at-speed testing. It generates the *launch* and *capture* edges with a precisely controlled time interval $T_{\text{launch-to-capture}}$ matching the rated $T_{clk}$ — usually using a frequency divider + PLL bypass path.

---

### 4.4 Comparative State Diagram — Robust vs. Non-Robust Test Activation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> V1_Applied
    V1_Applied --> Hazard_Check
    Hazard_Check --> OffPath_Stable: Off-path inputs constant
    Hazard_Check --> OffPath_Toggle: Off-path inputs toggle
    OffPath_Stable --> Robust_Detect
    OffPath_Toggle --> NonRobust_Detect
    Robust_Detect --> [*]: Valid under all timing
    NonRobust_Detect --> TimingSim_Required
    TimingSim_Required --> [*]: Pass or Fail
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> Define *Transition Delay Fault* and *Path Delay Fault*. State one key difference between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Transition Delay Fault (TDF):** A localized delay fault at a single signal line, modelled as either Slow-to-Rise (0→1 delayed) or Slow-to-Fall (1→0 delayed). Total TDF count = $2L$ where $L$ is the number of lines. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Path Delay Fault (PDF):** A distributed delay fault along an entire combinational path, where the cumulative delay exceeds the rated clock period. **(1 mark)**
- **Key Difference:** TDFs model *gross* delay defects at individual nodes, are *easy to generate* (ATPG similar to stuck-at), and have *linear complexity*; PDFs model *small distributed* delays, are *combinatorially explosive* (exponential paths), and require *path-oriented ATPG*. **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Differentiate between *Launch-on-Shift (LOS)* and *Launch-on-Capture (LOC)* schemes for at-speed delay testing. Which scheme is more commonly used in industry and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | LOS | LOC |
|---|---|---|
| V2 generation | One extra shift cycle | Functional response of CUT |
| Power dissipation | High (toggling shift) | Low (functional justification) |
| Functional correlation | Poor | High |
| Industrial preference | — | **Preferred (Synopsys DFTMAX, Tessent)** |

- **Why LOC is preferred:** It mimics *real functional operation* (low switching activity, no spurious toggles), thereby **reducing IR-drop-induced yield loss** and providing higher defect correlation. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **two-pattern delay test** application scheme. Clearly define the initialization vector and launch vector with an example of a Slow-to-Fall fault on a NAND gate output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **robust delay test**? Explain with a 2-input AND gate example how an off-path input transition can cause a non-robust test to fail to detect a real delay fault. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Two-Pattern Delay Test — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1 — Setup:** A two-pattern test consists of the pair $\langle V_1, V_2 \rangle$ applied to the combinational logic. **V1** initializes the target node, **V2** launches a transition.

**Step 2 — NAND Example:** Consider a 2-input NAND gate with inputs $A$, $B$ and output $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

**Step 3 — Slow-to-Fall (STF) on $F$:**

- $V_1 = \{A=1, B=1\} \Rightarrow F = 0$ (initialization to 0).
- $V_2 = \{A=1, B=0\} \Rightarrow F$ should transition to 1.

**Step 4 — Timing:** Apply V1 at clock cycle $n$, apply V2 at cycle $n+1$, capture $F$ at cycle $n+2$. The transition $F: 0 \rightarrow 1$ must complete within $T_{clk} - t_{su}$.

**Step 5 — Pass Criterion:** $F$ measured = 1 within one rated clock period. If $F = 0$ at capture, the gate has a **Slow-to-Fall fault** detected.

**Diagram (textual block):**

```
V1: A=1, B=1 ----> F=0  (Initialization)
V2: A=1, B=0 ----> F=1  (Launch + Capture)
If F is still 0 at rated clock edge  ==>  STF Fault Detected
```

**(b) Robust Delay Test**

**Definition:** A *robust* delay test detects the target delay fault *regardless* of any additional delays on off-path inputs. It guarantees no masking.

**AND Gate Example — Non-Robust Failure:**

Consider 2-input AND gate: $F = A \cdot B$. Suppose the **on-path input $A$** has a delay fault (slow-to-rise), and we want to test it.

- Choose $V_1 = \{A=0, B=1\}$, $V_2 = \{A=1, B=1\}$ — launching 0→1 on $A$.
- If $A$ is slow, $F$ may remain 0 at capture.

**However**, if the **off-path input $B$** has a *glitch* (transient 1→0→1) due to its own delay, then during the measurement window, $F = A \cdot B$ may incorrectly show 1 because $B$ momentarily dropped to 0. The test becomes *non-robust* and may give a *false pass*.

**Robust test condition:** Off-path input $B$ must remain *non-controlling* (here $B=1$ constant) throughout the transition window. Then the output transition depends *only* on the on-path delay — making the test **robust**.

**Why robustness matters (2 marks extra):**
- Robust tests are *hazard-free* and *race-free*.
- Non-robust tests require **timing simulation** to validate — costly in production.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** For a combinational circuit with critical path consisting of 5 identical NOR gates, where each gate has $t_{pd} = 60$ ps and inter-stage wire delay $t_{\text{wire}} = 25$ ps:
  - (i) Calculate the total path delay.
  - (ii) If $T_{clk} = 500$ ps and $t_{su} = 70$ ps, compute the slack and verify if the path passes delay test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a block diagram how an **On-Chip Clock Controller (OCC)** generates the launch and capture clocks for at-speed delay testing. Why is at-speed testing essential in nanometer designs? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Numerical Computation**

**(i) Total path delay:**

Apply Equation 1:
$$D_{\text{path}} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} t_{pd,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{4} t_{\text{wire},j}$$

Gate-delay sum (5 identical NOR gates):
$$\sum t_{pd} = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ ps}$$

Wire-delay sum (4 inter-stage wires between 5 gates):
$$\sum t_{\text{wire}} = 4 \times 25 = 100 \text{ ps}$$

$$D_{\text{path}} = 300 + 100 = 400 \text{ ps}$$

**(ii) Slack computation:**

Apply Equation 3:
$$\text{Slack} = T_{clk} - (D_{\text{path}} + t_{su})$$

$$\text{Slack} = 500 - (400 + 70) = 500 - 470 = +30 \text{ ps}$$

**Verification:** Slack $= +30$ ps $> 0$ ⟹ **The path passes the delay test** with a 30 ps timing margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Mark-Loss Callout (5 marks deducted historically):** Many students forget to use **$M = N-1$** wires between $N$ gates, computing $4 \times 25 = 100$ correctly but then writing $5 \times 25 = 125$ erroneously. **Always count wires between stages, not gates.**

**(b) OCC Block Diagram & At-Speed Necessity**

**OCC Block Diagram (textual):**

```
       +-------------------+
PLL -->|  Frequency Divider|----> Slow Test Clock (Shift)
       +-------------------+
                |
                v
       +-------------------+
       |  Pulse Generator  |----> Launch Clock (at-speed edge)
       +-------------------+
                |
                v
       +-------------------+
       |  Edge Combiner    |----> Capture Clock (at-speed edge)
       +-------------------+
                |
                v
         Scan Flip-Flops
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The ATE supplies a slow external clock (e.g., 100 MHz) for scan shift.
2. The OCC uses a PLL to multiply the frequency to the at-speed rate (e.g., 1 GHz).
3. A pulse-generation circuit creates **two precisely-spaced at-speed clock edges** separated by exactly $T_{clk}$ — the **launch** and **capture** edges.
4. The flip-flops capture the response under these two edges.

**Why at-speed testing is essential in nanometer designs (≥ 45 nm):**
- **Process variations** cause $V_{th}$ mismatch, leading to path delays unpredictable at the design stage.
- **IR-drop** and **voltage droop** slow down gates dynamically — only observable at rated clock.
- **Crosstalk-induced delays** between adjacent wires depend on switching activity — visible only at-speed.
- **Resistive opens and partial bridges** produce *small delay defects* (SDDs) that pass DC stuck-at tests but fail at-speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Note:** Below the **65 nm node**, stuck-at ATPG alone yields less than 30% defect coverage. **At-speed transition delay testing becomes mandatory** for shipping product.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Delay Test Questions (Compiled from KTU 2022–2024 ESE Trends):**
> 1. **Forgetting the two-pattern requirement** — writing V1 only, or describing a single-pattern stuck-at test. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing TDF with PDF** — using $2L$ for path count, or vice versa. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Wrong wire-count** — using $N$ wires instead of $N-1$ between $N$ gates. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Missing robustness condition** — describing a delay test without stating the off-path-input constraint. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
> 5. **Skipping the units in numerical problems** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark** per quantity without units.
> 6. **Not stating pass/fail interpretation** — computing slack without concluding "passes" or "fails". **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Delay Test (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Delay test verifies *temporal correctness* at the rated clock speed, complementing stuck-at tests that verify *logical correctness*.
- **Two-Pattern Requirement:** Every delay test is $\langle V_1, V_2 \rangle$ — initialization + launch.
- **TDF Count:** $N_{\text{TDF}} = 2L$ (linear in lines; ATPG-friendly).
- **PDF Count:** Exponential in paths; subset selection is mandatory.
- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  * $D_{\text{path}} = \sum t_{pd} + \sum t_{\text{wire}}$
  * $\text{Slack} = T_{clk} - (D_{\text{path}} + t_{su})$
  * Pass condition: Slack $> 0$
- **Robust Test:** Off-path inputs *non-controlling* throughout transition window — guaranteed detection.
- **Non-Robust Test:** Susceptible to off-path delays, glitches, races — needs timing simulation.
- **LOS vs. LOC:** LOC is the industrial favourite (functional justification, low power).
- **OCC Role:** Generates launch + capture edges at rated $T_{clk}$ using PLL + pulse generator.
- **At-Speed Necessity:** Mandatory below 65 nm to catch small delay defects (SDDs) from process variation, IR-drop, and crosstalk.
- **Coverage Target:** Industry benchmark ≥ 95% TDF coverage for sign-off.
- **Real-World Domains:** Automotive (ASIL-D), 5G/6G, AI accelerators, DDR/HBM interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDDQ Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IDDQ Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IDDQ Test (Quiescent Power Supply Current Test):** A CMOS-specific **defect-based testing methodology** that detects physical and parametric faults in a digital CMOS circuit by measuring the **quiescent (steady-state) current (I_DDQ)** drawn from the power supply (V_DD) pin when the circuit is in a stable, non-switching logic state.

In a defect-free CMOS complementary circuit, the quiescent current is theoretically zero (excluding subthreshold leakage and reverse-biased junction leakage). The IDDQ test therefore relies on a fundamental CMOS design invariant: **I_DDQ ≈ I_leakage** (very small) in a fault-free state, and **I_DDQ ≫ I_leakage** in the presence of bridging, gate-oxide-short, or punch-through defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IDDQ testing is classified under *defect-oriented testing* in the broader **Module 4 – Testing** syllabus. It is taught alongside fault simulation and ATPG, but operates at a lower level of abstraction (parametric/analog current measurement) rather than logical fault coverage.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **sealed water pipe** in your home. When all taps are closed (steady state), the flow of water should be **practically zero**. Now suppose there is a small crack hidden inside one of the pipe joints. Even when all taps are closed, water will **slowly leak out** through the crack, producing a measurable flow that the meter can detect.

IDDQ testing is the **electrical equivalent** of this idea:
- **Closed taps** → CMOS gates in a stable 0/1 state (no switching).
- **Expected flow** ≈ 0 (only microscopic leakage).
- **Leak (defect)** → A bridging short, gate-oxide pinhole, or punch-through path between V_DD and GND, which causes a continuous abnormal current that the tester can sense.

Just as a plumber uses a flow meter to find a hidden leak, the Automatic Test Equipment (ATE) uses a **precision ammeter** to identify defective ICs.

### 1.3 Core Physical Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **I_DDQ (Quiescent Current):** Measured in **µA** (typical) or **nA** (deep-submicron).
- **I_DD (Dynamic Supply Current):** In the **mA** range during switching — this is NOT measured in IDDQ testing.
- **Test Threshold (I_th):** A pass/fail current bound, typically set at **10 µA – 100 µA** for older nodes, dropping to **1 µA – 10 µA** for sub-100 nm technologies.
- **Test Vector Count:** Usually **10 – 100 vectors** (very small compared to stuck-at ATPG).
- **Defect Coverage:** Excellent for **bridging faults** (~70–80% of real silicon defects).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the letter "Q"?** The "Q" subscript stands for **Quiescent**, distinguishing the steady-state measurement from the active switching current. This notation was standardized by **Maly and Nigh** in late-1980s VLSI test literature and is now an IEEE-recognized convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CMOS Quiescent Current — Theoretical Foundation

In a properly fabricated CMOS inverter (or any CMOS gate), the steady-state current from V_DD to GND is governed by three leakage mechanisms:

1. **Reverse-biased pn-junction leakage** (I_rev): between source/drain diffusions and the substrate/well.
2. **Subthreshold leakage** (I_sub): weak inversion current of "OFF" transistors.
3. **Gate-oxide tunneling leakage** (I_gate): Fowler-Nordheim tunneling through the thin gate dielectric.

For a fault-free chip, the total quiescent current is:

$$I_{DDQ,\text{fault-free}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( I_{sub,i} + I_{rev,i} + I_{gate,i} \right) \approx \text{10 nA – 10 µA}$$

The **critical design invariant** is that this sum stays in a tight band across all valid input vectors. A defect breaks this invariant.

### 2.2 Why IDDQ Detects "Unmodeled" Faults

Classical stuck-at fault models cover only logical behavior. However, **real silicon defects** that IDDQ uniquely catches include:

| Defect Type | Stuck-At Coverage? | IDDQ Coverage? |
|---|---|---|
| Bridging fault (V_DD–GND short) | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Gate-oxide short (GOS) | Variable | **Excellent** |
| Punch-through (drain-substrate) | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Open via / partial open | Moderate | **Good (delta-IDDQ)** |
| Transistor stuck-on | Variable | **Excellent** |
| Resistive shorts | Poor | **Excellent** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is why IDDQ is a **defect-oriented test**, not a fault-oriented test. It catches manufacturing defects that may or may not behave as a logical stuck-at fault.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step IDDQ Test Procedure

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Power up the CUT (Circuit Under Test) to nominal **V_DD = V_DD(nom)**.
- **Step 2 — Vector Application:** Apply a test vector that sets internal nodes to a known stable state (no transient switching).
- **Step 3 — Settling Delay:** Wait for a "settling window" (typically **1 ms – 100 ms**) so all dynamic currents decay.
- **Step 4 — Current Measurement:** Use a high-precision ammeter (e.g., **Keithley 2400**, **HP 4142B**, or on-die BICS) to measure the current from V_DD.
- **Step 5 — Comparison:** Compare I_measured with the threshold **I_th**.
  - If **I_measured < I_th** → **PASS**
  - If **I_measured > I_th** → **FAIL (defect suspected)**
- **Step 6 — Repeat:** Apply next test vector, repeat until all vectors in the IDDQ set are applied.
- **Step 7 — Final Bin:** Bin the device as "Pass" only if **all** measurements are below I_th.

### 2.4 Key KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{DDQ}$ | Quiescent supply current | µA |
| $I_{th}$ | Pass/fail current threshold | 10 µA – 100 µA |
| $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{gs} - V_t}{nV_T}}\left(1 - e^{\frac{-V_{ds}}{V_T}}\right)$ | Subthreshold leakage | nA – µA |
| $I_{gate} \approx A \cdot E_{ox}^2 \cdot e^{-B/E_{ox}}$ | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | nA – µA |
| $I_{DDQ,\text{fault}} \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{R_{\text{short}}}$ | Current through resistive short | µA – mA |
| $\Delta I_{DDQ} = I_{DDQ,j} - \bar{I}_{DDQ}$ | Delta-IDDQ deviation from mean | µA |
| $N_{\text{vec}}$ | Number of IDDQ test vectors | 10 – 100 |
| $T_{\text{test}} = N_{\text{vec}} \cdot (T_{\text{settle}} + T_{\text{meas}})$ | Total IDDQ test time | ms – s |
| $\eta = \frac{N_{\text{defects caught}}}{N_{\text{total defects}}}$ | Defect coverage | 70 – 80 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Markdown Tables:** All absolute value notations use $\vert$ or $\mid$, **never** the bare pipe $\vert$ symbol, to prevent breaking table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production IC Test:** Used by Intel, AMD, TSMC, and Qualcomm for **quality-binning** of microprocessors and SoCs.
- **Reliability Screening:** Rejects infant-mortality parts before they reach customers.
- **Failure Analysis Trigger:** A high IDDQ sample is flagged for physical failure analysis (PFA) to find the root cause.
- **DFM/DFT Co-design:** IDDQ results feed back into the design rules to harden layouts against bridging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of IDDQ Test Threshold (I_th)

A robust IDDQ threshold must sit **above the worst-case fault-free current** and **below the smallest detectable defect current**. Let us derive this formally.

**Step 1 — Define the worst-case fault-free current distribution.**

In a population of $M$ fault-free chips, the measured IDDQ for a given vector $j$ is $I_{DDQ,j}^{(i)}$, where $i = 1, 2, \dots, M$.

The mean and standard deviation of the fault-free distribution are:

$$\mu_{IDDQ} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} I_{DDQ,j}^{(i)}$$

$$\sigma_{IDDQ} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{M-1} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \left( I_{DDQ,j}^{(i)} - \mu_{IDDQ} \right)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Set the threshold to limit yield loss (false rejects).**

To limit false rejects to a fraction $\alpha$ (e.g., $\alpha = 0.001$, i.e., 0.1%), we use a $k$-sigma bound:

$$I_{th} = \mu_{IDDQ} + k \cdot \sigma_{IDDQ}$$

For a Gaussian distribution, $k = 3.1$ corresponds to $\alpha \approx 10^{-3}$ and $k = 4.0$ corresponds to $\alpha \approx 10^{-4}$.

**Step 3 — Verify that the smallest defect current is detectable.**

The minimum defect current must exceed $I_{th}$:

$$I_{defect,\min} = \frac{V_{DD}}{R_{\text{short,max}}} \gg I_{th}$$

Solving for the largest tolerable defect resistance:

$$R_{\text{short,max}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{I_{th}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{\mu_{IDDQ} + k\sigma_{IDDQ}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a worked numerical example.**

Let us take $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$, $\mu_{IDDQ} = 5 \text{ µA}$, $\sigma_{IDDQ} = 1 \text{ µA}$, and $k = 4$.

First, compute the threshold:

$$I_{th} = 5 \text{ µA} + 4 \cdot 1 \text{ µA} = 9 \text{ µA}$$

Next, the largest detectable defect resistance:

$$R_{\text{short,max}} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{9 \text{ µA}} = 366.7 \text{ k}\Omega$$

So a resistive short of $R \le 366.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ between V_DD and GND will be detected by IDDQ.

**Step 5 — Defect coverage estimation.**

If we know the defect density per unit area $D_0$ (defects/cm²) and the percentage $p$ of defects that produce a low-resistance short, the IDDQ defect coverage is:

$$\eta_{IDDQ} = p \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-D_0 \cdot A \cdot P_{\text{escape}}} \right)$$

where $A$ is the chip area and $P_{\text{escape}}$ is the escape probability per defect.

For typical values $D_0 = 1$ def/cm², $A = 1$ cm², $p = 0.7$, $P_{\text{escape}} = 0.1$:

$$\eta_{IDDQ} = 0.7 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.07}) = 0.7 \cdot 0.0677 = 0.0474$$

Multiplied over many test vectors, the cumulative coverage approaches:

$$\eta_{\text{cumulative}} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{N_{\text{vec}}} (1 - \eta_j) \approx 0.75 \text{ (typical for 20–50 vectors)}$$

### 3.2 Delta-IDDQ Methodology — Step-by-Step Derivation

When manufacturing variation inflates $\sigma_{IDDQ}$ (a problem in deep submicron), absolute thresholds become unreliable. **Delta-IDDQ** is a more robust alternative.

**Step 1 — Collect $I_{DDQ}$ values across all $N_{\text{vec}}$ vectors for one chip.**

$$\vec{I}_{DDQ} = [I_{DDQ,1}, I_{DDQ,2}, \dots, I_{DDQ,N_{\text{vec}}}]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the chip's own mean and standard deviation.**

$$\bar{I}_{DDQ} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{vec}}} \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{vec}}} I_{DDQ,j}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{chip}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_{\text{vec}}-1} \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{vec}}} \left( I_{DDQ,j} - \bar{I}_{DDQ} \right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Delta-IDDQ statistic.**

$$\Delta I_{DDQ,j} = I_{DDQ,j} - \bar{I}_{DDQ}$$

A defect typically causes **one or a few vectors** to have a large $\Delta I_{DDQ,j}$ while the others remain normal.

**Step 4 — Apply a per-chip threshold.**

$$\Delta I_{th} = c \cdot \sigma_{\text{chip}}$$

where $c$ is a constant (typically 5 – 10) chosen empirically.

**Step 5 — Decision rule.**

If $\max_j \vert \Delta I_{DDQ,j} \vert > \Delta I_{th}$ for any $j$, the chip **fails**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Delta-IDDQ helps:** It cancels out the chip-to-chip variation in baseline leakage, focusing on the *signature* of a defect — a single vector (or small group) with elevated current.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Test Approaches

| Metric | Stuck-At ATPG | IDDQ Test | Delta-IDDQ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test type | Logical | Parametric (current) | Parametric (signature) |
| Vector count | 100 – 10,000 | 10 – 100 | 10 – 100 |
| Coverage of bridging | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Robust to leakage | Yes | Poor (sub-90 nm) | Good |
| ATE cost | Medium | High (precision ammeter) | High |
| Test time per vector | µs | ms (settling) | ms |
| Industry adoption | Universal | Strong (>180 nm) | Niche |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IDDQ Test Conceptual Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On CUT at VDD] --> B[Apply Test Vector Vj]
    B --> C[Wait Settling Window t_settle]
    C --> D[Measure IDDQ via Ammeter]
    D --> E{IDDQ less than Ith}
    E -- Yes --> F[Mark Vector PASS]
    E -- No --> G[Mark Vector FAIL]
    F --> H{All Vectors Done}
    G --> H
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[Final Bin: Pass or Fail]
```

### 4.2 IDDQ Current Signatures — Fault-Free vs Defective

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
