```mermaid
    subgraph FAULT_FREE[Fault-Free Chip]
    FF1[Vector 1: 0.5 uA] --> FF2[Vector 2: 0.6 uA]
    FF2 --> FF3[Vector 3: 0.55 uA]
    FF3 --> FF4[Vector 4: 0.5 uA]
    end
    subgraph DEFECTIVE[Defective Chip - Bridging Fault]
    DF1[Vector 1: 0.6 uA] --> DF2[Vector 2: 0.7 uA]
    DF2 --> DF3[Vector 3: 150 uA - SPIKE]
    DF3 --> DF4[Vector 4: 0.65 uA]
    end
    style FAULT_FREE fill:#e0ffe0
    style DEFECTIVE fill:#ffe0e0
```

### 4.3 Built-In Current Sensor (BICS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VDD[VDD Pin] --> S1[PMOS Switch M1]
    S1 --> CORE[Core Circuit CUT]
    CORE --> GND[VSS Pin]
    CORE -.-> MS[Mirror Transistor M2]
    MS --> CMP[Comparator]
    REF[Reference Current Iref] --> CMP
    CMP --> OUT[Pass or Fail Output]
    style CORE fill:#fff4cc
    style CMP fill:#cce0ff
```

### 4.4 IDDQ Test Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start IDDQ Test] --> INIT[Initialize VDD and VSS]
    INIT --> VEC[Apply N test vectors sequentially]
    VEC --> MEAS[Measure I at each vector]
    MEAS --> DELTA[Compute Delta-IDDQ statistics]
    DELTA --> COMPARE{Any vector above threshold}
    COMPARE -- Yes --> FAIL[Bin as FAIL]
    COMPARE -- No --> PASS[Bin as PASS]
    style START fill:#e0f0ff
    style PASS fill:#d0ffd0
    style FAIL fill:#ffd0d0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define IDDQ testing. Why is it particularly effective for CMOS circuits?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
IDDQ testing is a defect-oriented testing technique in CMOS circuits that measures the **quiescent (steady-state) power supply current** when the circuit is in a stable, non-switching state. (1 mark)
In a fault-free CMOS circuit, this current is theoretically near zero, limited only to leakage currents. (1 mark)
It is highly effective because most CMOS defects such as bridging, gate-oxide shorts, and punch-through cause a significant increase in quiescent current, which can be easily detected by a simple current measurement. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List any **three defects** that are efficiently detected by IDDQ testing but are poorly covered by stuck-at fault testing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Bridging faults between power and ground rails (1 mark)
2. Gate-oxide shorts (GOS) in MOS transistors (1 mark)
3. Punch-through defects between drain and substrate (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, every Part B question offers an internal choice. Question A and Question B below are independent alternatives — students answer **either** A **or** B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the theoretical basis of IDDQ testing. Derive the relationship between the test threshold $I_{th}$, mean quiescent current $\mu_{IDDQ}$, and the standard deviation $\sigma_{IDDQ}$ for a Gaussian fault-free population. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS ASIC operating at $V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$ shows a fault-free IDDQ distribution with $\mu_{IDDQ} = 2 \text{ µA}$ and $\sigma_{IDDQ} = 0.5 \text{ µA}$. The design team selects a $k$-sigma threshold with $k = 4$ to limit false rejects.
   (i) Compute $I_{th}$. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) Find the maximum short resistance $R_{\text{short,max}}$ that the IDDQ test can detect. **(3 marks)**
   (iii) Comment on the suitability of this threshold for a 65 nm CMOS process. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Basis of IDDQ Testing (7 marks):**
- In a CMOS circuit, the quiescent current is the small current drawn from V_DD when no transistors are switching. (1 mark)
- This current arises from subthreshold leakage, reverse-biased junction leakage, and gate-oxide tunneling. (2 marks)
- For a fault-free chip, this current is small and statistically well-bounded. (1 mark)
- Defects like bridging, gate-oxide shorts, and punch-through create abnormal low-resistance paths, raising IDDQ above the normal band. (1 mark)
- The test exploits this by applying a stable input vector, waiting for transients to settle, and then measuring the current with a precision ammeter. (1 mark)
- Decision is made by comparing the measured current with a threshold $I_{th}$. (1 mark)

**Derivation of Threshold:**
The fault-free current distribution is approximated as Gaussian with mean $\mu_{IDDQ}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{IDDQ}$.

$$P(I_{DDQ} \le I_{th}) = \int_{-\infty}^{I_{th}} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} dx$$

To obtain a yield-loss fraction of $\alpha$, we set:

$$I_{th} = \mu_{IDDQ} + k \cdot \sigma_{IDDQ}$$

For a Gaussian distribution, choosing $k = 3.1$ gives $\alpha \approx 10^{-3}$ and $k = 4.0$ gives $\alpha \approx 10^{-4}$. (1 mark for derivation)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Compute $I_{th}$ (2 marks):**

$$I_{th} = \mu_{IDDQ} + k \cdot \sigma_{IDDQ} = 2 \text{ µA} + 4 \cdot 0.5 \text{ µA} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ µA}$$

**(ii) Find $R_{\text{short,max}}$ (3 marks):**

The smallest detectable defect current is $I_{defect,\min} = I_{th} = 4 \text{ µA}$.

$$R_{\text{short,max}} = \frac{V_{DD}}{I_{th}} = \frac{1.8 \text{ V}}{4 \text{ µA}} = 450 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**(iii) Comment on 65 nm Suitability (2 marks):**
In 65 nm CMOS, leakage currents (subthreshold and gate tunneling) are extremely high and vary significantly from die to die. A 4 µA threshold is likely to cause large yield loss (false rejects). Delta-IDDQ or alternative techniques such as **VDD ramping** or **adaptive thresholding** are recommended. (2 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the operation of a **Built-In Current Sensor (BICS)** for on-chip IDDQ measurement. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **standard IDDQ testing** and **Delta-IDDQ testing**. Under what manufacturing conditions is Delta-IDDQ preferred? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Built-In Current Sensor (7 marks):**

A BICS integrates the current-sensing function directly onto the die, eliminating the need for an external precision ammeter. (1 mark)

**Operation:**
- The core CUT is supplied through a PMOS pass transistor M1 acting as a current-sensing element. (1 mark)
- A mirror transistor M2 samples the current flowing through M1 at a reduced ratio (e.g., 1:1000). (1 mark)
- This mirrored current is compared against a reference $I_{ref}$ using an on-chip comparator. (1 mark)
- The comparator output goes HIGH (or LOW) if the mirrored current exceeds $I_{ref}$, indicating a defect. (1 mark)
- The pass/fail decision is latched for each test vector. (1 mark)

**Advantages:**
- Removes the need for slow, expensive ATE current measurements. (1 mark)
- Test time is reduced since current measurement is parallel. (0.5 mark)
**Limitations:**
- Adds silicon area overhead and design complexity. (0.5 mark)

**(b) Standard IDDQ vs Delta-IDDQ (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Standard IDDQ | Delta-IDDQ |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold basis | Absolute current | Deviation from chip's own mean |
| Sensitivity to leakage variation | High | Low |
| Effective in deep submicron | Poor | Good |
| Vector requirement | Few | Moderate |
| Computational complexity | Low | Medium |

**When Delta-IDDQ is Preferred (3 marks):**
- When process variation is high, leading to large $\sigma_{IDDQ}$ across chips. (1 mark)
- In deep submicron nodes (≤ 90 nm) where leakage dominates. (1 mark)
- When a single absolute threshold causes too many false rejects. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing IDDQ with IDD:** Many students write "supply current test" without specifying *quiescent* (no switching). This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** $I_{th}$ is in µA, $V_{DD}$ in V, $R$ in kΩ. Missing units → 0.5 mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong sigma formula:** Students often write $I_{th} = \mu - k\sigma$ instead of $\mu + k\sigma$, which gives incorrect threshold.
> 4. **Ignoring settling time:** In test methodology steps, omitting the *settling window* loses 1 mark.
> 5. **No mention of leakage in submicron:** In modern-era questions, ignoring leakage as a *limitation* of standard IDDQ is a frequent mark-loss.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **IDDQ = Quiescent current in a stable CMOS state**, not switching current.
- **CMOS invariant:** Fault-free I_DDQ ≈ leakage only (very small).
- **Defects caught:** Bridging, gate-oxide short, punch-through, transistor stuck-on.
- **Threshold formula:** $I_{th} = \mu_{IDDQ} + k \cdot \sigma_{IDDQ}$ (k-sigma rule).
- **Short resistance detectability:** $R_{\text{short,max}} = V_{DD} / I_{th}$.
- **Settling time** is essential before current measurement (ms scale).
- **Delta-IDDQ** uses per-chip signature, robust against leakage variation.
- **BICS** = on-chip current sensor; removes need for slow ATE ammeters.
- **Limitation in deep submicron:** Rising leakage shrinks the gap between fault-free and faulty currents.
- **Vector count is small** (10–100) compared to stuck-at ATPG (thousands).
- **IDDQ is defect-oriented, not fault-oriented.**
- **Industry usage:** Intel, AMD, Qualcomm for production test and reliability screening.
- **Delta-IDDQ is preferred** for ≤ 90 nm CMOS technologies.
- **Two key formulas to memorize:**
  - $I_{th} = \mu_{IDDQ} + k\sigma_{IDDQ}$
  - $R_{\text{short,max}} = V_{DD}/I_{th}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Testability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design for Testability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Design for Testability (DFT) — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Testability (DFT)** is a collection of VLSI design techniques, methodologies, and architectural modifications integrated during the **front-end design phase** to simplify, accelerate, and reduce the cost of testing digital integrated circuits after fabrication. It ensures that internal nodes of a complex IC are **controllable** (can be set to required logic values) and **observable** (their response can be propagated to primary outputs) during manufacturing test.

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically evaluates DFT under the premise that **testability is a design attribute** that must be planned *before* silicon is produced, not retrofitted. The primary measurable goals of DFT are:

| Metric | Target Value (Industry Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Fault Coverage (FC)** | $\geq 95\%$ (commercial), $\geq 99\%$ (mission-critical ICs) |
| **Test Application Time (TAT)** | Minimal seconds, often $<1\text{ s}$ |
| **Area Overhead** | $5\% - 20\%$ of total die area |
| **Performance Penalty** | $\leq 5\%$ on critical path delay |
| **Power During Test** | Bounded to avoid over-test damage |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hospital CT-Scan Machine vs. The Locked Wooden Box"**

Imagine two medical diagnostic scenarios:

1. **The Locked Wooden Box (No DFT):** A patient swallows a small sealed device. A doctor must diagnose it from outside. With no access, no buttons, no sensors — diagnosis is nearly impossible. The doctor can only check whether it *works* (functional test) but cannot probe internals.

2. **The CT-Scan-Ready Patient (With DFT):** The patient is designed with **contrast agents** (controllability), **biomarkers** (observability), and **standard access ports** (scan chains). The doctor can now inject signals and read responses from specific internal points.

A VLSI chip without DFT is the locked wooden box. With DFT — by adding **multiplexers on flip-flops**, **dedicated test pins**, and **on-chip test pattern generators** — the chip becomes a CT-scan-ready patient, allowing automatic test equipment (ATE) to pinpoint manufacturing defects down to specific logic gates.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of Testability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Controllability** — The ability to drive any internal node of the circuit to either logic **0** or logic **1** from the primary inputs (PIs) within a bounded number of clock cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observability** — The ability to propagate the logic value present at any internal node to a primary output (PO) so that an external tester can capture and verify it.

Any DFT technique can be evaluated on how well it improves these two metrics *simultaneously* without violating timing closure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of DFT Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with three hierarchical levels of DFT:

### 2.1.1 Ad-Hoc DFT (Pre-Structural)
- Insert **test points** (control/observe points) manually after gate-level synthesis.
- Add **multiplexers** to break long feedback paths.
- Use **one-hot state encoding** in FSMs.
- **Drawback:** Non-scalable, designer-dependent, not automated. Largely obsolete in modern flows.

### 2.1.2 Structured DFT (Industry Standard)
- **Scan Design** (Full Scan, Partial Scan)
- **Boundary Scan** (IEEE Std 1149.1 / JTAG)
- **Built-In Self-Test (BIST)**

### 2.1.3 Built-In Repair and Self-Healing
- BISR (Built-In Self-Repair) for memories
- Redundancy-based fault tolerance

## 2.2 Scan Design — The Core of Structured DFT

### 2.2.1 Full Scan Architecture
Every sequential element (flip-flop/latch) in the design is replaced with a **scan flip-flop (SFF)** — a standard flip-flop augmented with a 2:1 multiplexer at its data input.

**Operational Modes:**
- **Normal (Functional) Mode:** $\text{SE} = 0$ → SFF behaves as a regular D-FF, capturing $D_{functional}$ on the rising clock edge.
- **Scan (Shift) Mode:** $\text{SE} = 1$ → SFF captures data from $Q_{prev}$ (the previous scan flip-flop in the chain), enabling serial shift-in of test patterns.

### 2.2.2 Partial Scan Architecture
Only a **subset** of flip-flops is made scannable — typically those that break cycles in the sequential graph or reside on critical timing paths. Trade-off: lower area overhead at the cost of lower fault coverage (typically 70%–95%).

### 2.2.3 Scan Chain Topology

A scan chain is a **serial shift register** formed by cascading all scan flip-flops. The scan-out of flip-flop $i$ feeds the scan-in of flip-flop $i+1$. For a design with $N$ scan flip-flops:

- **Scan-in time** = $N$ clock cycles
- **Scan-out time** = $N$ clock cycles
- **Capture cycle** = $1$ clock cycle
- **Total per-pattern test time** = $2N + 1$ cycles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two phases?** During scan-in, the combinational logic is *frozen* (mode = shift). During capture, the combinational logic *responds* to the loaded stimulus, and results are latched into the scan FFs (mode = capture).

## 2.3 Boundary Scan (IEEE 1149.1 — JTAG)

Boundary Scan adds a **dedicated test access port (TAP)** consisting of 4 (or 5) pins: **TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK, and optional TRST*.** Each primary I/O pin of the chip is wrapped with a **boundary scan cell (BSC)** — a transparent latch that can:
- **Capture** data flowing into/out of the pin.
- **Update** the pin's driven value during interconnect test.

**Primary Use Case:** Testing **interconnects between chips on a PCB** (board-level test) and **internal cluster logic** in modern SoCs.

## 2.4 Built-In Self-Test (BIST)

BIST moves the **Automatic Test Pattern Generator (ATPG) and the response compactor on-chip.** This eliminates the need for expensive external ATE.

**Architectural Components:**
| Block | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **PRPG** (Pseudo-Random Pattern Generator) | LFSR-based on-chip test stimulus generator |
| **MISR** (Multiple Input Signature Register) | Compacts test responses into a compact signature |
| **ROM / Channel** | Stores test patterns or seeds |
| **BIST Controller** | Orchestrates the BIST sequence |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 4. All absolute-value notations use `\vert` to remain markdown-table-safe.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Fault Coverage | $FC = \dfrac{D_{det}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of detected faults to total possible faults |
| 2 | Defect Level (Williams-Brown) | $DL = 1 - Y^{(1 - FC)}$ | $Y$ = process yield, $DL$ = fraction of bad parts shipped |
| 3 | Scan Chain Length | $L = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{FF}}{k} \right\rceil$ | $N_{FF}$ = flip-flops, $k$ = number of parallel scan chains |
| 4 | Total Test Patterns | $P_{tot} = P_{ATPG} + P_{scanN}$ | ATPG patterns + chain-filling patterns |
| 5 | Test Application Time | $T_{app} = (L + 1) \times P_{ATPG} \times T_{clk}$ | $L$ = chain length, $T_{clk}$ = clock period |
| 6 | Area Overhead (Scan) | $AO_{scan} = \dfrac{A_{MUX} \cdot N_{FF}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | MUX adds $\approx 3$ gates per FF |
| 7 | LFSR Maximal Length | $n = 2^{m} - 1$ | $m$ = number of flip-flops in LFSR, $n$ = period |
| 8 | BIST Aliasing Probability | $P_{alias} = \dfrac{1}{2^{m}}$ | $m$ = MISR length |
| 9 | Number of Test Vectors (BIST) | $N_{test} = L \times 2^{m}$ | For full BIST of an $L$-bit circuit |
| 10 | Effective Test Cost | $C_{eff} = C_{ATE} \cdot T_{app} + C_{design}$ | Sum of test-time $\times$ ATE rate + DFT design cost |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

DFT is **non-negotiable** in modern ASIC design. Without it:
- A 1-billion-gate SoC could not be tested within acceptable time/cost.
- Field defects in automotive or medical ICs could cause fatalities.
- Time-to-market windows collapse due to test development bottlenecks.

DFT engineers are some of the highest-paid specialists in the semiconductor industry, and the IEEE 1149.x family remains the most widely deployed test standard in history — used in essentially every CPU, GPU, microcontroller, and FPGA shipped today.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fault Coverage to Defect Level (Williams-Brown Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation connects the **testability metric** (FC) to a **business-critical metric** (Defect Level). It is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Define the yield $Y$ as the probability that a chip is fault-free.**

For a given manufacturing process, $Y$ is the fraction of fabricated dies that pass all functional tests, typically $0.90 \leq Y \leq 0.99$.

**Step 2 — Define the Defect Level $DL$ as the fraction of *shipped* chips that are actually defective.**

This is the most damaging metric — a defective chip in the field means a returned product (RMA), warranty cost, and brand damage.

**Step 3 — Apply probability reasoning.** A chip escapes detection if:
- It is fault-free (probability $Y$), **OR**
- It is faulty (probability $1 - Y$) but the test fails to detect the fault (probability $1 - FC$).

**Step 4 — Write the escape probability equation.**

$$
P_{escape} = Y \cdot 1 + (1 - Y)(1 - FC)
$$

The first term represents the correctly-working chips (always escape "correctly"). The second term represents faulty chips that slip through because the test missed the fault.

**Step 5 — Simplify algebraically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{escape} &= Y + (1 - Y)(1 - FC) \\
&= Y + 1 - FC - Y + Y \cdot FC \\
&= 1 - Y \cdot FC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — The Defect Level is the fraction of *faulty* chips among the shipped population.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL &= \frac{P_{escape} - Y}{1 - Y} \\
&= \frac{1 - Y \cdot FC - Y}{1 - Y} \\
&= \frac{1 - Y(1 + FC)}{1 - Y} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the standard Williams-Brown simplification** (assuming $Y$ is small loss when $1 - Y$ cancels):

$$
\boxed{DL = 1 - Y^{(1 - FC)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** To achieve $DL \leq 100$ ppm (parts per million) with $Y = 0.95$, we need $FC \geq 0.9994$ (i.e., $99.94\%$). This is why mission-critical ICs demand near-100% fault coverage.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Scan Chain Test Application Time

**Given:** A design with $N_{FF} = 10{,}000$ flip-flops, $k = 4$ parallel scan chains, and $P_{ATPG} = 500$ test patterns generated by an ATPG tool at a tester clock of $T_{clk} = 10\text{ ns}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the scan chain length $L$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \left\lceil \frac{N_{FF}}{k} \right\rceil \\
  &= \left\lceil \frac{10{,}000}{4} \right\rceil \\
  &= 2500 \text{ flip-flops per chain}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate cycles per test pattern.** Each pattern requires $L$ shift-in cycles, 1 capture cycle, and $L$ shift-out cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pattern} &= L + 1 + L \\
             &= 2L + 1 \\
             &= 2(2500) + 1 \\
             &= 5001 \text{ cycles per pattern}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total test application time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{app} &= T_{pattern} \times P_{ATPG} \times T_{clk} \\
        &= 5001 \times 500 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \\
        &= 25.005 \times 10^{-3}\text{ seconds} \\
        &= 25.005 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify reduction vs. single-chain approach.**

For $k = 1$ (single chain): $L = 10{,}000$, $T_{pattern} = 20{,}001$ cycles, $T_{app} = 100.005$ ms. Adding more parallel chains scales test time down by factor $k$ but increases routing congestion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Area Overhead of Full Scan

**Given:** A design with $N_{FF} = 50{,}000$ flip-flops, each scan FF adds a 2:1 multiplexer ($\approx 4$ NAND gates of area). Total die area (logic + memory) is $A_{total} = 4 \text{ mm}^2$ in 28 nm CMOS.

**Step 1 — Compute area added by multiplexers.**

$$
A_{MUX} = 4 \text{ gates} \times 4 \text{ transistors/gate} = 16 \text{ transistors per FF}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total transistor overhead.**

$$
N_{overhead} = 16 \times 50{,}000 = 800{,}000 \text{ additional transistors}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to die area.** In 28 nm, a 2-input NAND gate occupies approximately $\approx 0.5 \mu m^2$.

$$
A_{overhead} = 0.5 \mu m^2 \times 4 \text{ gates} \times 50{,}000 = 0.1 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate percentage overhead.**

$$
AO_{scan} = \frac{0.1 \text{ mm}^2}{4 \text{ mm}^2} \times 100\% = 2.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A $2.5\%$ area overhead is **highly acceptable** for a design yielding $99.9\%$ fault coverage. Industry standard targets $5\%$–$15\%$ area overhead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Scan Flip-Flop Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Data Input D] --> MUX1{Multiplexer 2 to 1}
    SI1[Scan Input SI] --> MUX1
    SE1[Scan Enable SE] --> MUX1
    MUX1 --> FF1[D Flip Flop]
    FF1 --> Q1[Q Output]
    FF1 --> Q2[Q to next scan FF]
    style D1 fill:#cce5ff
    style SI1 fill:#ffe5cc
    style SE1 fill:#ccffcc
    style FF1 fill:#ffccff
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **multiplexer-based scan flip-flop** (mux-DFF) architecture. In **normal mode**, $\text{SE} = 0$ and the functional $D$ input is selected. In **scan mode**, $\text{SE} = 1$ and the scan-in $SI$ (from the previous FF) is selected, creating a shift register.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Full Scan Chain Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SI2[Scan In TDI] --> SFF1[SFF 1]
    SFF1 --> SFF2[SFF 2]
    SFF2 --> SFF3[SFF 3]
    SFF3 --> SFFn[SFF N]
    SFFn --> SO2[Scan Out TDO]
    SE2[Scan Enable SE] --> SFF1
    SE2 --> SFF2
    SE2 --> SFF3
    SE2 --> SFFn
    COMBO[Combinational Logic Under Test] -.feedback.-> SFF1
    COMBO -.feedback.-> SFF2
    COMBO -.feedback.-> SFF3
    COMBO -.feedback.-> SFFn
    style SI2 fill:#cce5ff
    style SO2 fill:#cce5ff
    style SE2 fill:#ccffcc
    style COMBO fill:#fff4cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The scan chain is a **single serial path** (or multiple parallel paths). During shift, data flows $TDI \to SFF_1 \to SFF_2 \to \dots \to SFF_N \to TDO$. During capture, all SFFs load from the combinational logic simultaneously.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — IEEE 1149.1 Boundary Scan Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TAP[TAP Controller State Machine] --> CTRL{Control Signals}
    CTRL --> IR[Instruction Register]
    CTRL --> DR[Data Register Chain]
    IR --> DEC1[Instruction Decode]
    DEC1 --> BSR[Boundary Scan Register]
    DR --> BSR
    DR --> BYR[Bypass Register]
    BSR --> BSC1[BSC at Pin A]
    BSR --> BSC2[BSC at Pin B]
    BSR --> BSCn[BSC at Pin N]
    BSC1 --> PAD1[Chip Pad 1]
    BSC2 --> PAD2[Chip Pad 2]
    BSCn --> PADn[Chip Pad N]
    style TAP fill:#ffcccc
    style IR fill:#cce5ff
    style DR fill:#cce5ff
    style BSR fill:#ccffcc
    style BYR fill:#e5ccff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The TAP controller is a **16-state FSM** driven by TCK and TMS. It decodes JTAG instructions (BYPASS, EXTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, INTEST, IDCODE) and routes the active data register to the TDO pin.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — BIST Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LFSR[LFSR PRPG] --> CUT[Circuit Under Test CUT]
    CUT --> MISR1[MISR Signature Register]
    MISR1 --> CMP[Comparator with Golden Signature]
    CTRL1[BIST Controller FSM] --> LFSR
    CTRL1 --> MISR1
    CTRL1 --> CMP
    ROM1[ROM with Test Seeds] --> LFSR
    style LFSR fill:#cce5ff
    style CUT fill:#fff4cc
    style MISR1 fill:#ffe5cc
    style CMP fill:#ffcccc
    style CTRL1 fill:#ccffcc
```

> [!NOTE]
> In BIST, the **LFSR** generates pseudo-random test vectors without needing an external ATE. The **MISR** compacts the output response into a signature. If the signature matches the **golden (fault-free) signature**, the chip passes. The BIST controller sequences the entire operation autonomously.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Design for Testability (DFT). List and briefly explain the two fundamental properties of testability.**

**Model Answer:**

Design for Testability (DFT) refers to a set of design techniques incorporated into a VLSI circuit to make manufacturing testing easier, faster, and more cost-effective. The two fundamental properties are:

1. **Controllability:** The ease with which a tester can drive any internal signal/node of the circuit to a desired logic value (0 or 1) by applying stimuli at the primary inputs.
2. **Observability:** The ease with which the logic value at any internal node can be propagated to a primary output for monitoring by the test equipment.

A circuit with high controllability and high observability is considered easily testable. DFT techniques such as scan, boundary scan, and BIST are designed to enhance both properties.

*(3 marks — 1 for DFT definition, 1 for each property with brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a scan chain? Mention any two advantages of full scan design.**

**Model Answer:**

A scan chain is a serial shift register formed by connecting all the scan flip-flops (SFFs) in a design in a daisy-chain manner, with the scan-out of one SFF feeding the scan-in of the next. A single dedicated scan enable (SE) signal globally controls whether the chain operates in functional mode or shift mode.

**Two Advantages of Full Scan Design:**
1. **High Fault Coverage:** Full scan achieves $95\%$–$100\%$ fault coverage for stuck-at faults because every flip-flop is controllable and observable.
2. **Automated ATPG:** Commercial ATPG tools can automatically generate test patterns with minimal manual effort, reducing time-to-market.

*(3 marks — 1 for scan chain definition, 2 for advantages.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q.A (a) Explain the full scan design technique with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Full scan design is a structured DFT technique in which **every flip-flop** in the sequential circuit is replaced with a **scan flip-flop (SFF)**, which is a standard D-flip-flop augmented with a 2:1 multiplexer at its data input.

**Architecture:**
- A global **Scan Enable (SE)** signal controls all multiplexers.
- When $\text{SE} = 0$ (Normal Mode), each SFF behaves as a regular D-FF, capturing the functional data input.
- When $\text{SE} = 1$ (Scan Mode), each SFF captures the scan-in data from the previous SFF, forming a long shift register (the scan chain).

**Test Procedure:**
1. **Shift-In Phase:** Apply $\text{SE} = 1$ and clock $L$ times ($L$ = chain length) to serially load a test vector through the scan-in (SI) pin.
2. **Capture Phase:** Switch $\text{SE} = 0$ and apply one functional clock. The combinational logic's response is captured into all SFFs.
3. **Shift-Out Phase:** Switch $\text{SE} = 1$ and shift out the captured response through the scan-out (SO) pin while simultaneously shifting in the next vector.

**Advantages:**
- **High Fault Coverage:** Typically $95\%$–$100\%$ for single stuck-at faults.
- **Automation:** ATPG tool complexity drops drastically; combinational ATPG is sufficient.
- **Standardized:** Compatible with industry-standard EDA flows.

**Limitations:**
- **Area Overhead:** $\sim 5\%$–$15\%$ extra area due to MUX on every FF.
- **Performance Penalty:** MUX adds delay on the data path.
- **Sequential Depth:** Long scan chains mean high test application time.
- **Power During Test:** Shift activity can cause excessive switching, leading to voltage drop issues.

---

**Q.A (b) With a block diagram, explain the IEEE 1149.1 Boundary Scan standard. List its mandatory and optional pins. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The IEEE 1149.1 standard, popularly known as **JTAG (Joint Test Action Group)**, defines a serial scan architecture for testing interconnects on a printed circuit board (PCB) and internal logic of ICs.

**Architecture (Block Diagram):**

The architecture consists of:
1. **TAP Controller:** A 16-state FSM that decodes TMS and TCK to generate internal control signals.
2. **Instruction Register (IR):** Holds the currently active JTAG instruction (BYPASS, EXTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, INTEST, IDCODE, USERCODE).
3. **Data Registers:** Includes the **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)**, **Bypass Register (BYR)**, and the **Device Identification Register (IDR)**.
4. **Boundary Scan Cells (BSC):** A transparent latch inserted between each pin and the internal core logic.

**Mandatory Pins (4):**
- **TDI** — Test Data In (serial input to shift registers)
- **TDO** — Test Data Out (serial output)
- **TMS** — Test Mode Select (controls TAP FSM transitions)
- **TCK** — Test Clock (dedicated test clock)

**Optional Pin (1):**
- **TRST*** — Test Reset (asynchronous active-low reset of TAP controller)

**Operation Modes:**
- **EXTEST:** Tests inter-chip PCB interconnects by driving values via BSCs.
- **INTEST:** Tests internal core logic by applying stimuli via BSCs at the chip's inputs.
- **SAMPLE/PRELOAD:** Snapshots the normal functional data without disrupting operation.
- **BYPASS:** Reduces the BSR to a 1-bit register to speed up board-level chain shifting.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a) Explain Built-In Self-Test (BIST) architecture. Describe the function of LFSR and MISR with diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Built-In Self-Test (BIST) is a DFT technique in which the **test pattern generator** and the **response compactor** are placed **on-chip**, allowing the IC to test itself without an expensive external ATE.

**Architecture (Block Diagram):**

The key components of a BIST architecture are:
1. **LFSR (Linear Feedback Shift Register):** Acts as the on-chip test pattern generator (PRPG).
2. **MISR (Multiple Input Signature Register):** Acts as the on-chip response compactor.
3. **BIST Controller:** A finite-state machine that orchestrates the BIST sequence.
4. **ROM (Optional):** Stores multiple seeds for the LFSR to vary test patterns.
5. **Comparator:** Compares the final MISR signature with the golden signature stored on-chip.

**LFSR Function:**
An LFSR is a shift register with XOR feedback taps at specific bit positions. With $m$ flip-flops, it can generate $2^{m} - 1$ distinct pseudo-random patterns before repeating. Two main topologies:
- **External-XOR LFSR (Standard):** XOR gates are on the feedback path.
- **Internal-XOR LFSR (Modular):** XOR gates are inserted between FFs, providing faster operation.

**MISR Function:**
A MISR is a shift register with multiple XOR-input stages (one per CUT output). As test responses arrive in parallel, they are XORed with the current register state. After all patterns are applied, the final contents — the **signature** — are compared to the pre-computed **fault-free signature**. A mismatch indicates a defective chip.

**Advantages of BIST:**
- Eliminates the need for expensive ATE.
- Enables in-field testing (e.g., automotive ECUs testing themselves at startup).
- Reduces test data volume.
- Enables at-speed testing (functional clock speeds).

**Disadvantages:**
- Area overhead from on-chip BIST hardware.
- Risk of **aliasing** (faulty output producing the correct signature by coincidence).
- Higher design complexity.

---

**Q.B (b) A digital circuit has 80,000 flip-flops. The designer plans to implement full scan design with 8 parallel scan chains. The ATPG tool generates 1200 test patterns. The tester clock period is 8 ns. Calculate:**
**(i) Scan chain length (2 marks)**
**(ii) Total test application time in milliseconds (3 marks)**
**(iii) If the yield is 0.92 and the required fault coverage is 99.5%, what is the defect level in ppm? (2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Scan Chain Length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \left\lceil \frac{N_{FF}}{k} \right\rceil \\
  &= \left\lceil \frac{80{,}000}{8} \right\rceil \\
  &= 10{,}000 \text{ flip-flops per chain}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Test Application Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{app} &= (2L + 1) \times P_{ATPG} \times T_{clk} \\
        &= (2 \times 10{,}000 + 1) \times 1200 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \\
        &= 20{,}001 \times 1200 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \\
        &= 192{,}009{,}600 \times 10^{-9} \\
        &= 0.192 \text{ seconds} \\
        &= 192 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii) — Defect Level:**

Using the Williams-Brown formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL &= 1 - Y^{(1 - FC)} \\
   &= 1 - (0.92)^{(1 - 0.995)} \\
   &= 1 - (0.92)^{0.005} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Calculating $(0.92)^{0.005}$ using the approximation $(1 - x)^n \approx e^{-nx}$ for small $x$:

$$
(0.92)^{0.005} \approx e^{-0.08 \times 0.005} \approx e^{-0.0004} \approx 0.9996
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL &\approx 1 - 0.9996 = 0.0004 \\
   &= 400 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Watch Out for These Valuation Deductions!**
> 1. **Forgetting the capture cycle:** Many students compute $T_{app} = 2L \times P \times T_{clk}$ instead of $(2L+1) \times P \times T_{clk}$. You lose **2 marks** for this mistake.
> 2. **Misapplying Williams-Brown:** The formula is $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$, **not** $DL = (1 - Y)(1 - FC)$. The latter is the *escape probability*, not the *defect level*. Examiner will deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Units Confusion:** Always express DL in **ppm** (parts per million) or **%**. Writing $0.0004$ without units may be marked as incomplete. Multiply by $10^6$ for ppm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Design for Testability**

- [x] **DFT Definition:** Design modifications that improve *controllability* and *observability* of internal nodes.
- [x] **Controllability** = ability to set node values from PIs. **Observability** = ability to read node values at POs.
- [x] **Ad-hoc DFT** = manual, designer-dependent (test points, one-hot encoding, feedback-breaking muxes). Largely obsolete.
- [x] **Full Scan:** Every FF replaced with a scan FF (mux-DFF). Provides $95\%$–$100\%$ stuck-at fault coverage.
- [x] **Scan FF** = D-FF + 2:1 MUX at data input. Controlled by global **SE** signal.
- [x] **Partial Scan:** Subset of FFs scanned. Lower area, lower FC ($70\%$–$95\%$).
- [x] **Scan Chain Length:** $L = \lceil N_{FF}/k \rceil$, where $k$ = number of parallel chains.
- [x] **Test Application Time:** $T_{app} = (2L + 1) \times P_{ATPG} \times T_{clk}$.
- [x] **Boundary Scan (IEEE 1149.1 / JTAG):** 4 mandatory pins (TDI, TDO, TMS, TCK) + optional TRST*.
- [x] **JTAG Instructions:** EXTEST, INTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, BYPASS, IDCODE.
- [x] **BIST:** Test logic on-chip. Uses LFSR for pattern generation, MISR for response compaction.
- [x] **LFSR Period:** $n = 2^m - 1$ for an $m$-bit LFSR (maximal-length sequence).
- [x] **Aliasing Probability (BIST):** $P_{alias} = 1/2^m$ for an $m$-bit MISR.
- [x] **Williams-Brown Defect Level:** $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$.
- [x] **Area Overhead (Full Scan):** Typically $5\%$–$15\%$ of total die area.
- [x] **Performance Penalty:** MUX adds $\leq 5\%$ delay on the critical path.
- [x] **At-Speed Testing:** Performed at functional clock rate to detect delay faults (transition faults).
- [x] **Modern Trends:** Compression (EDT — Embedded Deterministic Test), LBIST, Hierarchical DFT for 3D-IC and chiplets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook:** Remember the **"3-5-15 Rule"** for DFT metrics: target **$\geq 95\%$** fault coverage, accept **$\leq 5\%$** performance penalty, and tolerate up to **$15\%$** area overhead. If you remember just this, you can reason about most exam questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DFT and Scan Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** DFT and Scan Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Design for Testability (DFT) — Formal Academic Definition

**Design for Testability (DFT)** refers to a collection of integrated circuit (IC) design techniques and structural modifications applied during the synthesis and physical design phases of a VLSI chip, which deliberately simplify the process of applying manufacturing tests and achieving high **fault coverage** on the fabricated silicon. The principal goal of DFT is to ensure that the chip's internal nodes are both easily *controllable* (i.e., settable to a desired logic value from the primary inputs) and easily *observable* (i.e., readable out to the primary outputs).

Mathematically, **fault coverage (FC)** is defined as:

$$FC = \frac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_{detected}$ is the number of modeled faults detected by the test set, and $N_{total}$ is the total number of modeled (typically single stuck-at) faults in the circuit under test (CUT).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: DFT is the integration of testability features directly into the hardware structure of the IC so that post-manufacturing tests can be generated economically and applied with high efficiency. It bridges the gap between functional design and post-silicon validation.

## 1.2 Scan Design — The Cornerstone of Structured DFT

**Scan Design** is a structured DFT methodology in which the flip-flops (or memory elements) of a sequential circuit are temporarily reconfigured into one or more long **shift registers** called *scan chains*. In normal (functional) mode, the flip-flops operate as ordinary memory elements connected to the combinational logic of the design. In *test mode*, they are daisy-chained together so that arbitrary test vectors can be serially shifted into the chip, and the resulting responses can be serially shifted out for comparison with expected values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scan Design fundamentally solves the sequential ATPG (Automatic Test Pattern Generation) problem by converting the sequential test generation problem into a much simpler combinational test generation problem.** This is the single most important insight for KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Airport Security Screening Analogy"

Imagine a large international airport with hundreds of passengers boarding a flight. To ensure security, every passenger's luggage must be individually X-rayed and inspected.

- **Without DFT (Ad-hoc approach)**: Inspectors must individually hand-search each bag while the passenger is in transit. This is slow, error-prone, and many bags may slip through unchecked.
- **With DFT (Structured approach — Scan Design)**: All bags are first placed on a single conveyor belt that moves them one-by-one through an X-ray scanner in a *controlled, serialized* fashion. Every bag is now systematically inspected.

In this analogy:
- The **passengers' bags** represent the **internal flip-flops** of the chip.
- The **conveyor belt** represents the **scan chain**.
- The **X-ray scanner** represents the **automatic test equipment (ATE)**.
- The **pass rate** of inspection corresponds to **fault coverage**.

This is exactly what scan design achieves — it gives the test engineer a deterministic, repeatable "conveyor belt" to inspect every internal memory element of the chip.

## 1.4 Why DFT and Scan Design Are Indispensable

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Motivations in Modern VLSI:**
> 1. **Complexity Explosion**: Modern SoCs contain **billions of transistors**, making functional testing infeasible.
> 2. **Deep Sub-micron Defects**: Manufacturing defects (bridges, opens, delay faults) require structural testing.
> 3. **Time-to-Market Pressure**: DFT enables *at-speed testing* and reduces test development cost.
> 4. **Quality and Yield**: Higher fault coverage translates to lower **Defective Parts Per Million (DPPM)**.

The standard industry-accepted fault coverage target for shipping VLSI products is **≥ 95%**, with mission-critical (automotive, medical) chips requiring **≥ 99%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of DFT Techniques

DFT techniques are broadly divided into two categories:

### 2.1.1 Ad-hoc (or Non-Structured) DFT Techniques
These are simple, localized design rules applied early in the design flow. They are *point solutions* and do not provide a systematic, automated test methodology.

Common ad-hoc techniques include:
- **Test Point Insertion**: Adding extra primary inputs (PIs) and primary outputs (POs) to improve controllability and observability.
- **Initialize-on-Power-up**: Using global reset to set all flip-flops to a known state.
- **Partitioning the Circuit**: Breaking a large design into smaller, independently testable blocks.
- **Avoiding Asynchronous Loops (combinational feedback)**: Asynchronous feedback creates races and unpredictable behavior.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Ad-hoc DFT methods are *not scalable*. They become impractical for designs with more than a few thousand gates. They also do not integrate cleanly with ATPG tools.

### 2.1.2 Structured DFT Techniques
Structured DFT provides a **uniform, algorithmic, automated** method to test any sequential circuit. The two dominant structured DFT approaches are:

1. **Scan Design** (most widely used in industry)
2. **Built-In Self-Test (BIST)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus**: Approximately **70% of Module 4 marks** are allocated to Scan Design. BIST is covered briefly. Ad-hoc techniques appear only as 2- or 3-mark questions.

## 2.2 Architecture of a Scan Flip-Flop (Muxed-D Scan Cell)

The fundamental building block of scan design is the **multiplexed flip-flop (Muxed-D FF)**. The standard cell consists of:

- A **2:1 multiplexer** placed in front of the D input of the data flip-flop.
- The multiplexer selects between:
  - **D (functional data)**: The original data input from the combinational logic during normal operation.
  - **SI (Scan Input)**: The data input from the previous flip-flop in the scan chain during test mode.
- The select line is the **Scan Enable (SE)** signal:
  - `SE = 0` → Functional mode (D is selected)
  - `SE = 1` → Scan-shift mode (SI is selected)

The output **Q** drives the combinational logic and also feeds the **Scan Output (SO)** to the next flip-flop in the chain.

## 2.3 Scan Design Operation — Three Modes

A scan-based circuit operates in **three distinct modes**:

| Mode | Scan Enable (SE) | Clock | Function |
|------|-----------------|-------|----------|
| **Normal (Functional)** | `0` | System Clock | Circuit behaves as designed |
| **Scan-In (Shift)** | `1` | Test Clock | Test vectors shifted into scan chains via SI |
| **Capture (Test)** | `0` | Test Clock (one pulse) | Circuit captures response into FFs |
| **Scan-Out (Shift)** | `1` | Test Clock | Captured responses shifted out via SO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: The Capture phase is critical — it is the *single clock pulse* during which the combinational logic's response to the applied test pattern is captured into the flip-flops. During capture, SE must be de-asserted (`SE = 0`) so the functional D-input path is used.

## 2.4 Types of Scan Design

| Scan Style | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Muxed-D Scan** | Mux added before D-input of each FF | Simple, minimal area overhead | Scan-shift speed limited, glitches on Mux |
| **Clocked Scan** | Two clocks used (A, B) — independent clocks during shift | Avoids hold-time issues, robust | Requires two independent clock pins |
| **LSSD (Level-Sensitive Scan Design)** | Uses polarity-hold latches (SRLs) — shift register latch pair | Race-free, level-sensitive, very robust | More complex, higher area, requires multiple clocks |
| **Random-Access Scan** | Each FF individually addressable via row/column decoders | Fast access, low-power scan | Not widely used; complex addressing |
| **Boundary Scan (IEEE 1149.1)** | Scan cells placed at I/O pads for board-level testing | Enables board and system test | Different application than internal scan |

> [!NOTE]
> **LSSD was pioneered by IBM** and is the gold standard for robust scan design. The LSSD cell uses two non-overlapping clocks: **C1** (used for shifting), **C2** (used for updating the master latch from the slave), and a **System Clock (SC)**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas listed below are mathematically verifiable and consistently appear in KTU 2024 scheme university examinations.**

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Fault Coverage (FC) | $FC = \dfrac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as percentage |
| Test Application Time (TAT) | $T_{test} = \dfrac{(N_{patt} + N_{ff}) \times N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds |
| Scan Chain Length (L) | $L = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}} \right\rceil$ | Number of flip-flops |
| Total Shift Cycles per Pattern | $N_{shift} = L + 1$ | Shift-in + 1 capture + shift-out |
| At-Speed Test Frequency | $f_{at\_speed} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clock\_period}}$ | Hz |
| Scan Power Dissipation | $P_{scan} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts |
| Defect Level (DL) | $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ | Y = yield, FC = fault coverage |
| Sequential Depth (effective) with Scan | Reduced from $N_{ff}$ cycles to **1 cycle** | Scan converts sequential to combinational |
| Number of Test Patterns (combinational logic) | $N_{patt} \approx N_{gates} \text{ to } 5 \cdot N_{gates}$ | Empirically determined |
| Scan Cell Area Overhead | $Area_{overhead} \approx \dfrac{N_{ff}}{N_{gates} + N_{ff}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defect Level (DL) Formula Derivation Context**: The DL formula $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ is derived under the assumption that the yield $Y$ follows a binomial distribution of defective chips. A **100% FC** gives **DL = 0** (zero defects escape), while **0% FC** gives **DL = 1 - Y** (all defective chips escape).

## 2.6 Controllability, Observability, and Testability

Three key metrics quantify the "testability" of a node:

1. **Controllability (C)**: The ease with which a node can be set to a specific logic value (0 or 1) from the primary inputs. Higher controllability = easier to set.
2. **Observability (O)**: The ease with which the logic value of a node can be propagated to a primary output. Higher observability = easier to read.
3. **Testability (T)**: A composite metric, often defined as $T = (C_0 \cdot C_1 \cdot O)$ or similar product terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Question**: "Why does scan design improve testability?" — Answer: Scan design provides **100% controllability and 100% observability** for all scan flip-flops, because any FF can be directly loaded with any value via the scan chain (controllability) and its state can be directly read out via the scan chain (observability).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive ICs** (ISO 26262): Use scan + BIST to achieve **ASIL-D** functional safety compliance.
- **Aerospace and Defense** (DO-254): Strict requirement for **≥ 99.9% fault coverage**.
- **Mobile SoCs** (Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon): Use **hierarchical scan compression** to manage billion-gate designs.
- **Memory Test**: Scan + March algorithms test embedded SRAM.
- **FPGA Testing**: Built-in scan and configuration readback used for verification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Scan Chain Length and Shift Cycles

**Problem Statement**: A VLSI design contains $N_{ff} = 4096$ flip-flops. The designer partitions them into $N_{chains} = 4$ scan chains. Determine the scan chain length and the total number of shift cycles per test pattern.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Identify the inputs.
$$N_{ff} = 4096, \quad N_{chains} = 4$$

**Step 2**: Apply the scan chain length formula.
$$L = \left\lceil \frac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3**: Substitute the values.
$$L = \left\lceil \frac{4096}{4} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4**: Perform the division.
$$\frac{4096}{4} = 1024$$

**Step 5**: Apply the ceiling function.
$$L = 1024 \text{ flip-flops per chain}$$

**Step 6**: Compute the shift cycles per pattern. Note that one extra cycle is needed for the capture operation.
$$N_{shift} = L + 1 = 1024 + 1 = 1025 \text{ cycles per pattern}$$

**Step 7**: Comment on the result. The scan chain length determines the dominant portion of test time. A shorter chain (more parallel chains) reduces shift time but requires more ATE channels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fault Coverage Calculation and Defect Level

**Problem Statement**: A manufactured IC has a yield $Y = 0.85$ and a test achieves fault coverage $FC = 0.95$. Compute the Defect Level (DL). If FC is improved to 0.99, what is the new DL?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Recall the DL formula.
$$DL = 1 - Y^{(1 - FC)}$$

**Step 2**: Compute $1 - FC$ for the first case.
$$1 - FC = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$

**Step 3**: Compute $Y^{(1-FC)}$.
$$Y^{0.05} = (0.85)^{0.05}$$

**Step 4**: Evaluate using logarithms.
$$\ln(0.85) = -0.16252$$
$$0.05 \times (-0.16252) = -0.008126$$
$$e^{-0.008126} = 0.99191$$

**Step 5**: Compute DL.
$$DL = 1 - 0.99191 = 0.00809 \approx 0.81\%$$

**Step 6**: Repeat for FC = 0.99.
$$1 - FC = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01$$
$$Y^{0.01} = (0.85)^{0.01}$$
$$\ln(0.85) = -0.16252$$
$$0.01 \times (-0.16252) = -0.0016252$$
$$e^{-0.0016252} = 0.99838$$

**Step 7**: Compute new DL.
$$DL_{new} = 1 - 0.99838 = 0.00162 \approx 0.16\%$$

**Step 8**: Interpret the result.
- At **FC = 95%**, roughly **8,090 defective parts per million (DPPM)** escape.
- At **FC = 99%**, only **1,620 DPPM** escape.
- This is a **5× reduction** in defective parts, justifying the engineering effort to improve fault coverage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Test Application Time

**Problem Statement**: A design has $N_{ff} = 8192$ flip-flops organized into 8 scan chains of equal length. The ATPG tool generates $N_{patt} = 2500$ test patterns. The ATE clock frequency is $f_{clk} = 100$ MHz. Calculate the total test application time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Compute scan chain length.
$$L = \frac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}} = \frac{8192}{8} = 1024 \text{ flip-flops}$$

**Step 2**: Compute cycles per pattern.
$$N_{shift} = L + 1 = 1025 \text{ cycles per pattern}$$

**Step 3**: Compute total clock cycles.
$$N_{cycles}^{total} = N_{patt} \times N_{shift} = 2500 \times 1025 = 2,562,500 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 4**: Compute test application time.
$$T_{test} = \frac{N_{cycles}^{total}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{2,562,500}{100 \times 10^{6}}$$

**Step 5**: Evaluate.
$$T_{test} = 25.625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ seconds} = 25.625 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6**: Verify and interpret. The test takes about 25.6 ms, which is a very small fraction of the total chip test budget. This demonstrates why scan-based testing is preferred for high-volume production.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Scan Cell Area Overhead

**Problem Statement**: A combinational circuit contains $N_{gates} = 100,000$ gates and $N_{ff} = 5000$ flip-flops. Each flip-flop occupies the area equivalent of 6 gates, and the scan multiplexer adds 2 gates per FF. Compute the area overhead due to scan.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Compute original area in gate equivalents.
$$A_{orig} = N_{gates} + N_{ff} \times 6 = 100,000 + 5000 \times 6 = 100,000 + 30,000 = 130,000 \text{ gate equivalents}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the area added by the scan multiplexers.
$$A_{mux} = N_{ff} \times 2 = 5000 \times 2 = 10,000 \text{ gate equivalents}$$

**Step 3**: Compute new total area.
$$A_{new} = A_{orig} + A_{mux} = 130,000 + 10,000 = 140,000 \text{ gate equivalents}$$

**Step 4**: Compute area overhead percentage.
$$Area_{overhead} = \frac{A_{mux}}{A_{new}} \times 100\% = \frac{10,000}{140,000} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5**: Evaluate.
$$Area_{overhead} = 7.14\%$$

**Step 6**: Interpret. A 7.14% area overhead is typical for scan design and is considered an acceptable cost given the dramatic improvement in testability.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Scan vs. Non-Scan Test Generation Complexity

| Parameter | Without Scan (Sequential ATPG) | With Scan (Combinational ATPG) | Improvement Factor |
|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| Test Generation Complexity | $O(2^{N_{ff}})$ | $O(N_{gates})$ | Exponential |
| Required Test Patterns | 10× to 100× more | 1× (baseline) | 10×–100× |
| Sequential Depth | Up to $N_{ff}$ cycles | **1 cycle** | $N_{ff} \times$ faster |
| Fault Coverage Achievable | 60%–80% | 95%–99%+ | ~30% increase |
| ATPG Tool Complexity | Very high | Moderate | Significant |
| Test Application Time | Long | Moderate | Depends on chain length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept**: This matrix shows why scan design is considered *the* cornerstone of modern digital testing. The reduction of test generation complexity from **exponential in the number of flip-flops** to **linear in the number of gates** is the central theoretical justification for scan.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Scan Chain Depth vs. Test Time Trade-off

**Problem Statement**: Show mathematically how the number of scan chains affects test time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Express the total scan chain length as a function of the number of chains.
$$L(N_{chains}) = \frac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}}$$

**Step 2**: Express the cycles per pattern.
$$N_{shift}(N_{chains}) = \frac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}} + 1$$

**Step 3**: Express the total cycles.
$$N_{cycles}^{total}(N_{chains}) = N_{patt} \times \left( \frac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}} + 1 \right)$$

**Step 4**: Express the total test time.
$$T_{test}(N_{chains}) = \frac{N_{patt}}{f_{clk}} \times \left( \frac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}} + 1 \right)$$

**Step 5**: Analyze the limit.
- As $N_{chains} \to N_{ff}$: $L \to 1$, $T_{test} \to \frac{N_{patt} \cdot 2}{f_{clk}}$ (minimum).
- As $N_{chains} \to 1$: $L \to N_{ff}$, $T_{test} \to \frac{N_{patt}(N_{ff} + 1)}{f_{clk}}$ (maximum).

**Step 6**: Interpretation. The test time decreases approximately as $\frac{1}{N_{chains}}$ until the constant "+1" capture cycle dominates. This is why modern designs use **scan compression** to effectively achieve hundreds of internal scan chains with limited ATE channels.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Muxed-D Scan Flip-Flop Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeD[D - Functional Data] --> nodeMux1{MUX 2 to 1}
    nodeSI[SI - Scan Input] --> nodeMux1
    nodeSE[SE - Scan Enable] --> nodeMux1
    nodeMux1 --> nodeFF[D FF]
    nodeClk[System Clock] --> nodeFF
    nodeFF --> nodeQ[Q - Output]
    nodeQ --> nodeSO[SO - Scan Output]
    nodeQ --> nodeCombLogic[Combinational Logic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: When `SE = 0`, the multiplexer selects the **D (functional data)** input, allowing the flip-flop to operate normally. When `SE = 1`, the multiplexer selects the **SI (scan input)**, allowing the previous flip-flop in the scan chain to drive this flip-flop. The output **Q** simultaneously drives the combinational logic and the next scan flip-flop via **SO**.

## 4.2 Scan Chain Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeATE[Automatic Test Equipment ATE] --> nodeSI1[Scan In Pin SI]
    nodeSI1 --> nodeFF1[Scan FF 1]
    nodeFF1 --> nodeFF2[Scan FF 2]
    nodeFF2 --> nodeFF3[Scan FF 3]
    nodeFF3 --> nodeFFN[Scan FF N]
    nodeFFN --> nodeSO[Scan Out Pin SO]
    nodeSO --> nodeATE
    
    nodeFF1 --> nodeCombA[Combinational Logic Block A]
    nodeFF2 --> nodeCombA
    nodeFF3 --> nodeCombB[Combinational Logic Block B]
    nodeFFN --> nodeCombB
    
    nodeCombA --> nodeFF1
    nodeCombA --> nodeFF2
    nodeCombB --> nodeFF3
    nodeCombB --> nodeFFN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Question Expected**: Students are typically asked to draw the scan chain architecture. This diagram demonstrates the *daisy-chain connection* of flip-flops and the bidirectional connection to combinational logic. The bold flow from ATE → SI → FFs → SO → ATE captures the test data path.

## 4.3 Scan Test Application Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startTest([Start Test]) --> shiftIn1[Set SE 1]
    shiftIn1 --> shiftIn2[Apply Scan Clock N times to shift in test vector]
    shiftIn2 --> capture1[Set SE 0]
    capture1 --> capture2[Apply one functional clock pulse for capture]
    capture2 --> capture3[Combinational logic responds to stimulus]
    capture3 --> capture4[Response stored in Scan FFs]
    capture4 --> shiftOut1[Set SE 1]
    shiftOut1 --> shiftOut2[Apply Scan Clock N times to shift out response]
    shiftOut2 --> compare[ATE compares response with expected]
    compare --> nextPattern{More patterns?}
    nextPattern -- Yes --> shiftIn2
    nextPattern -- No --> endTest([End Test])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **This flow is the canonical scan test sequence.** Memorize it for the 14-mark KTU question on "Explain the scan test methodology with a suitable diagram."

## 4.4 LSSD (Level-Sensitive Scan Design) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSI[Scan Input SI] --> nodeL1[L1 Latch - Polarity Hold]
    nodeC1[Clock C1 - Shift] --> nodeL1
    nodeL1 --> nodeL2[L2 Latch - Polarity Hold]
    nodeC2[Clock C2 - Update] --> nodeL2
    nodeL2 --> nodeD[D Data to Combinational Logic]
    nodeD --> nodeComb[Combinational Logic]
    nodeComb --> nodeSO[Scan Output SO]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LSSD cells use two non-overlapping clocks C1 and C2.** The L1 latch is the shift-capture latch, and L2 is the output (functional) latch. Data is transferred L1 → L2 by activating C2. This architecture is *inherently race-free* and *level-sensitive*, making it ideal for high-reliability designs (aerospace, automotive).

## 4.5 Hierarchical Scan Compression Architecture (Modern SoC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeATEin[ATE Scan In] --> nodeCompIn[Compressor Decompressor]
    nodeCompIn --> nodeSc1[Internal Scan Chain 1]
    nodeCompIn --> nodeSc2[Internal Scan Chain 2]
    nodeCompIn --> nodeSc3[Internal Scan Chain N]
    nodeSc1 --> nodeCompOut[Compactor]
    nodeSc2 --> nodeCompOut
    nodeSc3 --> nodeCompOut
    nodeCompOut --> nodeATEout[ATE Scan Out]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Scan compression** allows a small number of ATE channels to drive a large number of internal scan chains. For example, 8 ATE channels may control 100 internal scan chains, achieving a **12.5× compression ratio** and dramatically reducing test data volume and test time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define DFT and explain its necessity in VLSI design. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer**:
**Design for Testability (DFT)** is a set of design techniques that incorporate testability features into the hardware structure of an IC during the design phase itself, so that post-manufacturing testing becomes easier, faster, and achieves higher fault coverage.

**Necessity of DFT**:
1. **Increased Circuit Complexity**: Modern ICs contain billions of transistors; manual test generation is impossible.
2. **Manufacturing Defects**: Sub-micron processes introduce defects (bridges, opens, shorts) that must be detected.
3. **Sequential Test Difficulty**: Sequential circuits have state-dependent behavior; without DFT, ATPG tools struggle to achieve high coverage.
4. **Cost Reduction**: DFT enables automated ATPG, reducing time-to-market and test cost.
5. **Quality Assurance**: Industry standards (ISO 26262, DO-254) mandate high fault coverage, achievable only with structured DFT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU 2024)**: Definition = 1 mark, Necessity points = 2 marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Ad-hoc DFT and Structured DFT. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Ad-hoc DFT | Structured DFT |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| Definition | Localized, manual design rules | Systematic, algorithmic methodology |
| Automation | Not automated | Fully automatable (ATPG tools) |
| Scalability | Poor for large designs | Excellent for billion-gate designs |
| Fault Coverage | Variable (60%–85%) | High and predictable (95%–99%+) |
| Examples | Test points, partitioning | Scan, BIST |
| Tool Support | Limited | Full commercial tool support |

**Conclusion**: Structured DFT (especially scan design) is the industry standard for modern VLSI.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Scan Design — Detailed Explanation [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Scan Design in detail with a neat diagram of a scan flip-flop. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VLSI chip has 16,384 flip-flops partitioned into 16 scan chains. ATPG generates 5,000 test patterns. If the test clock frequency is 50 MHz, calculate the total test application time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Scan Flip-Flop (Muxed-D)**:
The scan flip-flop is constructed by adding a 2:1 multiplexer in front of the D-input of a standard data flip-flop.

- **Inputs**: `D` (functional data), `SI` (scan input), `SE` (scan enable), `CLK`
- **Output**: `Q`
- **Operation**:
  - When `SE = 0`: Mux selects `D` → flip-flop operates normally.
  - When `SE = 1`: Mux selects `SI` → flip-flop is part of the scan chain.

**Scan Chain Architecture**: Multiple scan flip-flops are connected in series. The scan output `Q` of one FF becomes the scan input `SI` of the next FF, forming a long shift register.

**Test Procedure**:
1. **Shift-In**: `SE = 1`, apply `L` shift clocks to load test vector serially.
2. **Capture**: `SE = 0`, apply 1 functional clock to capture combinational response.
3. **Shift-Out**: `SE = 1`, apply `L` shift clocks to unload response.
4. **Compare**: ATE compares shifted-out response with the expected response.

**Advantages**:
- 100% controllability and observability of scan flip-flops.
- Converts sequential ATPG into combinational ATPG (exponential → linear complexity).
- Achieves fault coverage typically ≥ 95%.
- Fully compatible with commercial ATPG tools (Synopsys TetraMAX, Cadence Modus).

**Limitations**:
- Area overhead (~5%–10%) due to multiplexers.
- Increased routing congestion in physical design.
- Higher dynamic power during scan shift (many FFs toggle simultaneously).
- Scan-shift speed limited by clock skew and ATE channels.
- Testability of clock-domain crossings and memory elements remains challenging.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given**:
$$N_{ff} = 16,384, \quad N_{chains} = 16, \quad N_{patt} = 5000, \quad f_{clk} = 50 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 1**: Compute the scan chain length.
$$L = \frac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}} = \frac{16,384}{16} = 1024$$

**Step 2**: Compute cycles per pattern (shift + 1 capture cycle).
$$N_{shift} = L + 1 = 1024 + 1 = 1025$$

**Step 3**: Compute total clock cycles.
$$N_{cycles}^{total} = N_{patt} \times N_{shift} = 5000 \times 1025 = 5,125,000$$

**Step 4**: Compute test application time.
$$T_{test} = \frac{N_{cycles}^{total}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{5,125,000}{50 \times 10^{6}}$$
$$T_{test} = 0.1025 \text{ seconds} = 102.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Answer**: Total test application time is **102.5 ms**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): LSSD and Fault Coverage [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the LSSD (Level-Sensitive Scan Design) technique. How does it differ from Muxed-D scan? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a manufactured IC with yield $Y = 0.90$, calculate the Defect Level when fault coverage is (i) 90%, (ii) 95%, (iii) 99%. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**LSSD Overview**:
LSSD was developed by IBM and uses **polarity-hold latches (SRLs)** as the basic storage element. Each LSSD cell consists of **two latches (L1 and L2)** and uses **two non-overlapping clocks (C1 and C2)**.

**LSSD Operation**:
- **L1 Latch**: Used during scan shift; clocked by C1.
- **L2 Latch**: Holds data for the combinational logic; clocked by C2.
- **Shift Operation**: C1 is active; data propagates from SI → L1 → L2 (when C2 also active).
- **Functional Operation**: System clock drives the latches.

**Comparison Table**:

| Aspect | Muxed-D Scan | LSSD |
|--------|--------------|------|
| Storage Element | Edge-triggered D-FF | Polarity-hold latches (SRL pair) |
| Number of Clocks | 1 (System Clock) | 3 (C1, C2, System Clock) |
| Race Conditions | Possible | Inherently race-free |
| Area Overhead | Lower | Higher (two latches per cell) |
| Robustness | Moderate | Very high |
| Adoption | Industry standard | Niche (aerospace, mission-critical) |
| Tool Support | Universal | Limited |

**Key Advantage**: LSSD is *level-sensitive*, meaning data is valid as long as the clock is held active, making it tolerant of clock skew and noise.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given**: $Y = 0.90$

**Formula**:
$$DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$$

**(i) For FC = 0.90**:
$$1 - FC = 0.10$$
$$Y^{0.10} = (0.90)^{0.10}$$
$$\ln(0.90) = -0.10536$$
$$0.10 \times (-0.10536) = -0.010536$$
$$e^{-0.010536} = 0.98952$$
$$DL = 1 - 0.98952 = 0.01048 \approx 1.05\%$$

**(ii) For FC = 0.95**:
$$1 - FC = 0.05$$
$$Y^{0.05} = (0.90)^{0.05}$$
$$0.05 \times (-0.10536) = -0.005268$$
$$e^{-0.005268} = 0.99475$$
$$DL = 1 - 0.99475 = 0.00525 \approx 0.525\%$$

**(iii) For FC = 0.99**:
$$1 - FC = 0.01$$
$$Y^{0.01} = (0.90)^{0.01}$$
$$0.01 \times (-0.10536) = -0.0010536$$
$$e^{-0.0010536} = 0.99895$$
$$DL = 1 - 0.99895 = 0.00105 \approx 0.105\%$$

**Comment**:
- At FC = 90%, about **10,500 DPPM** escape.
- At FC = 95%, about **5,250 DPPM** escape.
- At FC = 99%, only **1,050 DPPM** escape.
- Each 1% improvement in fault coverage beyond 95% yields significant DPPM reduction. This justifies the engineering investment in DFT to push coverage from 95% to 99%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in DFT and Scan Design Questions (KTU 2024)**:
>
> 1. **Confusing SE polarity**: Many students write `SE = 1` for functional mode. **Correct**: `SE = 0` selects functional D-input; `SE = 1` selects scan-input. Losing **1–2 marks** if wrong.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1 capture cycle"**: When calculating test time, students often use only the shift-in cycles, forgetting the 1 capture cycle and the shift-out cycles. The correct formula is $N_{shift} = L + 1$ for each test pattern.
>
> 3. **Mixing LSSD clocks**: In LSSD questions, students confuse C1, C2, and System Clock roles. **Correct**: C1 shifts, C2 updates, System Clock is for functional mode.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the Mux in scan FF diagram**: When asked for a diagram, omitting the 2:1 multiplexer before the D-input loses **2–3 marks** immediately. The mux is the *defining feature* of the scan FF.
>
> 5. **Fault Coverage vs. Defect Level confusion**: FC is a *test quality metric*; DL is a *manufacturing quality metric*. They are related but not the same. Use the DL formula $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ only when explicitly required.
>
> 6. **Sign errors in exponents**: When computing $Y^{(1-FC)}$, students often confuse $1 - FC$ and $FC$. Remember: **uncovered faults escape**, so the exponent is $1 - FC$.
>
> 7. **Skipping the units**: Always specify the units in the final answer (seconds, milliseconds, percentage, gate equivalents).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 High-Yield Rapid Revision — DFT and Scan Design**
>
> - [ ] **DFT Definition**: Integration of testability features during design to enable high fault coverage.
> - [ ] **Ad-hoc vs Structured DFT**: Ad-hoc = manual, point solutions. Structured = systematic, automated (Scan, BIST).
> - [ ] **Scan Design Core Idea**: Convert sequential ATPG → combinational ATPG by re-purposing FFs as shift registers.
> - [ ] **Muxed-D Scan Cell**: 2:1 Mux before D-FF. `SE = 0` → functional. `SE = 1` → scan.
> - [ ] **Three Modes of Scan**: Normal, Scan-Shift (SI), Capture.
> - [ ] **LSSD**: Two latches (L1, L2), two non-overlapping clocks (C1, C2). Race-free, level-sensitive.
> - [ ] **Fault Coverage Formula**: $FC = \dfrac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$. Industry target ≥ 95%.
> - [ ] **Defect Level Formula**: $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$.
> - [ ] **Scan Chain Length**: $L = \dfrac{N_{ff}}{N_{chains}}$ (use ceiling if not divisible).
> - [ ] **Test Application Time**: $T_{test} = \dfrac{N_{patt} \times (L + 1)}{f_{clk}}$.
> - [ ] **Controllability = 100%**: All scan FFs can be set to any value via scan-in.
> - [ ] **Observability = 100%**: All scan FFs can be read out via scan-out.
> - [ ] **Area Overhead**: Typically 5%–10% (muxers per FF).
> - [ ] **Power Issue**: Scan shift causes high toggle activity → power-aware scan methods exist.
> - [ ] **Scan Compression**: Reduces ATE channel count and test data volume by 10×–100×.
> - [ ] **At-Speed Testing**: Scan enables transition-delay and path-delay fault testing using capture clocks.
> - [ ] **Boundary Scan (IEEE 1149.1)**: Different from internal scan — used for board-level interconnect testing (JTAG).
> - [ ] **BIST**: Built-In Self-Test — internal pattern generator and response compactor (LFSR, MISR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip**: For 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer as: (i) Diagram, (ii) Working Principle, (iii) Advantages, (iv) Limitations, (v) Comparison (if comparison is asked), and (vi) Numerical solution (if asked). This complete structure ensures full marks allocation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial-Scan Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Partial-Scan Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partial-Scan Design** is a Design-for-Testability (DFT) methodology in which only a **carefully selected subset** of the sequential elements (flip-flops/latches) in a sequential circuit are replaced with **scan flip-flops**, while the remaining sequential elements retain their normal functional (non-scan) configuration. The primary objective is to achieve a favorable trade-off between **testability** (fault coverage, ATPG complexity) and **design overhead** (area, timing, power, and routing congestion).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEVT635)**, Partial-Scan Design is positioned as a *compromise solution* between the high overhead of **Full-Scan** and the poor testability of **Non-Scan (Ad-Hoc)** designs. It is governed by the principle that **breaking all feedback loops** in the sequential circuit is *not always necessary* to achieve acceptable Automatic Test Pattern Generation (ATPG) performance; strategically selecting cycle-breaking flip-flops can yield near-full-scan testability at a fraction of the overhead.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Highway Inspection Analogy** 🛣️
>
> Imagine inspecting **10,000 km of national highways** for potholes. Installing CCTV cameras on **every single road** (Full-Scan) would cost a fortune, but checking **none** (Non-Scan) leaves critical blind spots.
>
> A smart engineer installs cameras **only at strategic chokepoints** — major junctions, bridges, and interchanges that, once monitored, give visibility into the *flow* of traffic on the connecting roads. The remaining smaller roads are still testable because traffic patterns from the monitored chokepoints can be **propagated and observed** through them.
>
> In VLSI terms: the **monitored junctions are the scan flip-flops**, the **unmonitored roads are the non-scan flip-flops**, and the **traffic flow** is the **combinational logic data path**. Partial-Scan Design is the engineering decision of *where* to place the cameras so that you get maximum fault visibility with minimum cost.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

The following **standard KTU/industry metrics** are used to evaluate partial-scan designs:

- **Sequential Depth (k)**: The maximum number of non-scan flip-flops along any combinational path. Also called the *testability window*.
- **Scan Ratio (α)**: The fraction of flip-flops selected for scan conversion.
- **Cycle Breaking**: The minimum set of flip-flops whose scan conversion breaks all feedback cycles in the sequential graph.
- **Test Coverage (TC)**: Percentage of detectable faults covered by the generated test set.
- **Overhead Penalty (β)**: Additional area, delay, and power cost introduced by scan insertion.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, Partial-Scan Design is typically preceded by *Full-Scan Design* and *Scan Architecture*, and is a frequent **14-mark question** in the KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) because it requires students to justify *which* flip-flops to scan, not just *how* to scan them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Framework — How Partial-Scan Design Works

The design methodology proceeds in a structured sequence of phases:

**Phase 1 — Circuit Modeling as a Sequential Graph**
- The sequential circuit is modeled as a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$, where:
  - Each vertex $v_i \in V$ represents a flip-flop (or primary input/output).
  - Each edge $e_{ij} \in E$ represents a **combinational dependency** from the output of flip-flop $i$ to the input of flip-flop $j$.

**Phase 2 — Identification of Cyclic Structures**
- A **feedback cycle** (or feedback loop) is a closed path $v_1 \to v_2 \to \dots \to v_k \to v_1$ in $G$.
- Cycles create *sequential dependencies* that make ATPG extremely difficult because they prevent the circuit from being modeled as a combinational network.

**Phase 3 — Selection of Scan Flip-Flops**
- A *cut-set* of flip-flops is selected such that **all cycles** in $G$ are broken when those FFs are converted to scan mode.
- Common selection criteria:
  1. **Minimum Feedback Vertex Set (MFVS)** — selects the fewest FFs that break all cycles.
  2. **Testability-based selection** — prioritizes FFs that lie on low-controllability/observability paths.
  3. **Cost-driven selection** — prefers FFs with low area/power impact for conversion.

**Phase 4 — Scan Insertion and Test Generation**
- Selected FFs are replaced with **muxed scan flip-flops** (typically Multiplexed-D FF or Clocked-Scan FF).
- **Combinational ATPG** is applied to the *partially scanned* circuit. Because the scan FFs provide direct controllability/observability, the ATPG complexity drops drastically.
- The remaining non-scan FFs form short **bounded-depth** sequential paths (depth $\leq k$), which can be handled by **time-frame expansion** for $k$ clock cycles.

### 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason |
|------|--------|
| Graph modeling | Enables formal cycle analysis (a graph-theoretic problem with polynomial solutions). |
| Cycle breaking | Cycles are the **root cause** of ATPG intractability in sequential circuits. |
| MFVS selection | Guarantees the *minimum-overhead* partial-scan solution. |
| Time-frame expansion | Bounded sequential depth $k$ means ATPG can unroll $k$ time frames and use combinational algorithms. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE for Module 4.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Test Coverage (TC)** | $\text{TC} = \dfrac{N_{det}}{N_{tot}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{det}$ = detected faults, $N_{tot}$ = total faults |
| 2 | **Sequential Depth (k)** | $k = \max(\text{length of longest non-scan FF chain})$ | Integer; ideally $k \le 3$ for tractable ATPG |
| 3 | **Scan Ratio (α)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{N_{scan}}{N_{FF}} \times 100\%$ | Lower α = less overhead |
| 4 | **Test Application Time** | $T_{app} = (N_{pat} + N_{scan}) \cdot f_{clk}$ | $N_{pat}$ = # test patterns, $f_{clk}$ = clock freq. |
| 5 | **Scan Shift Cycles** | $T_{shift} = N_{scan} \cdot S_{len}$ | $S_{len}$ = scan chain length |
| 6 | **Area Overhead** | $A_{ovh} = \dfrac{A_{mux} \cdot N_{scan}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $A_{mux}$ = 2:1 mux area per scan FF |
| 7 | **Cycle-Breaking Condition** | A scan set $S$ is valid iff $G \setminus S$ is **acyclic** | Graph-theoretic constraint |
| 8 | **Fault Coverage with Partial Scan** | $FC_{partial} = FC_{full} - \delta(k)$ | $\delta(k)$ = coverage penalty due to depth $k$ |
| 9 | **ATPG Complexity Bound** | $O(2^{k})$ for non-scan path of depth $k$ | Exponential in $k$ |
| 10 | **Minimum Scan FFs (MFVS)** | $N_{scan}^{min} = \vert \text{MFVS}(G) \vert$ | Cardinality of minimum feedback vertex set |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Pitfall in LaTeX**: When writing inline absolute values like $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert S \vert$ for set cardinality, **always use `\vert`** (not `|`) inside markdown to prevent table-rendering errors. KTU examiners will instantly downgrade answers with broken formatting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
