- **Industry Adoption**: Partial-scan is widely used in **high-performance CPUs, GPUs, and DSPs** where the area/timing penalty of full-scan is unacceptable (e.g., critical-path registers, pipeline-stage FFs in GHz designs).
- **Modern EDA Tools**: Synopsys DFT Compiler, Cadence Modus, and Mentor Tessent all support **partial-scan synthesis** with MFVS-based and testability-driven selection algorithms.
- **Trade-off Visualization**: In a typical SoC, full-scan may add **15–25% area overhead**, while partial-scan (with ~40–60% scan ratio) achieves **95–98% fault coverage** at only **6–10% overhead**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bounding ATPG Complexity via Sequential Depth

**Given**: A partial-scan design with sequential depth $k$ along the longest non-scan path.

**To Prove**: The ATPG complexity for the partial-scan circuit is bounded by $O(2^{k} \cdot N_{comb})$, where $N_{comb}$ is the number of combinational gates.

**Step 1 — Modeling Time-Frame Expansion**

When a sequential circuit has bounded depth $k$, the ATPG algorithm can unroll the circuit into $k$ consecutive time frames. Each non-scan flip-flop $i$ in time frame $t$ holds a value that depends on time frame $t-1$.

Let $n_s$ denote the number of non-scan flip-flops. The combined state-space dimension of the unrolled circuit is:

$$
D_{state} = 2^{n_s \cdot k}
$$

> This is the **search-space size** that a sequential ATPG must explore.

**Step 2 — Reducing Search Space via Scan Controllability**

The scan flip-flops (controlling $n_{scan}$ FFs) provide **direct controllability** in the current time frame, effectively reducing the implicit state-space dimension. The effective ATPG search becomes:

$$
D_{eff} = 2^{(n_s - n_{scan}) \cdot k}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost of Combinational ATPG on Unrolled Circuit**

For each unrolled time frame, the combinational ATPG has complexity $O(N_{comb})$. Across $k$ frames, the total complexity is:

$$
C_{ATPG} = O(k \cdot N_{comb} \cdot 2^{(n_s - n_{scan}) \cdot k})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification under Partial-Scan Assumption**

In a well-designed partial-scan circuit, $n_s \cdot k$ is small (since $k \le 3$ for most industrial designs and $n_s$ is reduced by scan insertion). Thus, the term $2^{(n_s - n_{scan}) \cdot k}$ can be bounded by a small constant $c$. The complexity reduces to:

$$
C_{ATPG} = O(2^{k} \cdot N_{comb}) \quad \text{(where the constant factors are absorbed)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\therefore \quad C_{ATPG}^{partial-scan} = O(2^{k} \cdot N_{comb})}
$$

> This is why the KTU syllabus emphasizes: **"The key design goal of partial-scan is to bound $k$ to a small value (typically $\le 3$)."**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Test Application Time for Partial-Scan

**Given**: A partial-scan circuit with $N_{scan}$ scan FFs forming a single scan chain of length $S_{len} = N_{scan}$, and $N_{pat}$ test patterns.

**Step 1 — Identify the Test Phases**

Each test application consists of:
- **Shift-In Phase**: $S_{len}$ clock cycles to load the next pattern through the scan chain.
- **Capture Phase**: 1 clock cycle (or 2 for at-speed testing) to capture the response.
- **Shift-Out Phase**: $S_{len}$ clock cycles to unload the response.

**Step 2 — Total Cycles per Pattern**

For each test pattern, the total clock cycles required are:

$$
T_{pat} = S_{len} + T_{cap} + S_{len}
$$

where $T_{cap}$ is the capture clock cycles (typically $T_{cap} = 1$). For pipelined scan (commonly used in industry):

$$
T_{pat} = S_{len} + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Total Test Application Time**

For $N_{pat}$ patterns, the total time is:

$$
T_{app} = N_{pat} \cdot (S_{len} + 1) \text{ cycles}
$$

Converting to seconds using clock period $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$:

$$
T_{app}^{sec} = \dfrac{N_{pat} \cdot (N_{scan} + 1)}{f_{clk}} \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: Partial-scan reduces $N_{scan}$ compared to full-scan, hence **reducing shift cycles** and improving test throughput. For example, if full-scan has 1000 scan FFs and partial-scan has 500, the test time is roughly halved.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Area Overhead Calculation

**Given**: A circuit with $N_{FF}$ total flip-flops, $N_{scan}$ converted to scan FFs, each requiring an additional 2:1 multiplexer.

**Step 1 — Gate Equivalent of Scan Multiplexer**

A 2:1 mux requires approximately **3 NAND gates** (or 4 transmission gates) in standard-cell implementation. Let $G_{mux}$ be the gate-equivalent area of one mux.

**Step 2 — Additional Area for Scan Routing**

Beyond the mux, scan insertion also requires:
- One extra scan-input (SI) pin per scan chain.
- Scan enable (SE) signal routing.
- Scan-output (SO) connection.

Let $A_{routing}$ be the routing overhead per scan FF. Total overhead:

$$
A_{ovh} = N_{scan} \cdot (G_{mux} + A_{routing}) + A_{chain\_{routing}}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalized Overhead Percentage**

$$
\boxed{\beta = \dfrac{N_{scan} \cdot (G_{mux} + A_{routing})}{A_{total}} \times 100\%}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Exam Style):**

A circuit has $N_{FF} = 2000$ flip-flops, total area $A_{total} = 500{,}000$ gate-equivalents. A partial-scan design selects $N_{scan} = 800$ FFs. Each scan FF adds $G_{mux} + A_{routing} = 5$ gate-equivalents. Routing overhead for scan chains is $A_{chain} = 2000$ GE.

$$
A_{ovh} = 800 \cdot 5 + 2000 = 4000 + 2000 = 6000 \text{ GE}
$$

$$
\beta = \dfrac{6000}{500{,}000} \times 100\% = 1.2\%
$$

Compared to full-scan ($N_{scan} = 2000$):
$$
\beta_{full} = \dfrac{2000 \cdot 5 + 4000}{500{,}000} \times 100\% = 2.8\%
$$

> **Conclusion**: Partial-scan achieves **less than half** the area overhead of full-scan in this example.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Partial-Scan

| Design Criterion | Full-Scan | Partial-Scan | Non-Scan |
|------------------|:---------:|:------------:|:--------:|
| **Fault Coverage** | 98–100% | 90–98% | 70–85% |
| **Area Overhead** | 15–25% | 5–12% | 0–2% |
| **Performance Penalty** | High (mux in FF path) | Moderate (only on selected FFs) | None |
| **ATPG Complexity** | Combinational ($O(N_{comb})$) | Bounded ($O(2^k \cdot N_{comb})$) | Sequential ($O(2^{n_s})$) |
| **Test Application Time** | High (long scan chains) | Moderate | Low |
| **Power During Test** | High (toggle in scan) | Moderate | Low |
| **Design Effort** | Low (automated) | High (selection algorithm) | High (ad-hoc) |
| **Best For** | Test-critical ASICs | High-performance CPUs/SoCs | Legacy / low-cost designs |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked to compare partial-scan vs full-scan, *always* mention the **sequential depth $k$** as the key differentiator. Examiners reward answers that explicitly state "partial-scan is preferred when $k$ can be bounded to a small value (≤3)."

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Partial-Scan Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Original Sequential Circuit] --> A1[Build Sequential Graph G]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Feedback Cycles]
    A2 --> A3[Select Scan Flip Flops via MFVS]
    A3 --> A4[Convert Selected FFs to Scan FFs]
    A4 --> A5[Insert Scan Chains and SE Signal]
    A5 --> A6[Run Combinational ATPG]
    A6 --> A7[Validate Fault Coverage FC]
    A7 --> A8[FC meets target]
    A8 --> A9[Done Partial Scan Design]

    style A0 fill:#1a1a2e,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#16213e,color:#fff
    style A2 fill:#0f3460,color:#fff
    style A3 fill:#533483,color:#fff
    style A4 fill:#e94560,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#e94560,color:#fff
    style A6 fill:#0f3460,color:#fff
    style A7 fill:#16213e,color:#fff
    style A8 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style A9 fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Sequential Graph with Cycle-Breaking Scan Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FF1[FF1 Non-Scan] -->|comb| FF2[FF2 Non-Scan]
    FF2 -->|comb| FF3[FF3 SCAN]
    FF3 -->|comb| FF4[FF4 Non-Scan]
    FF4 -->|comb| FF5[FF5 SCAN]
    FF5 -->|comb| FF1

    FF6[FF6 SCAN] -->|comb| FF2
    FF3 -->|comb| FF6

    FF1 -.feedback cycle.-> FF1

    style FF1 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style FF2 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style FF3 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style FF4 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style FF5 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    style FF6 fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
```

**Legend**: 🟥 **Red (Non-Scan FFs)** remain as functional flip-flops. 🟩 **Green (Scan FFs)** break the feedback cycle and provide direct controllability/observability. Note how selecting **FF3, FF5, and FF6** breaks the cycle `FF1 → FF2 → FF3 → FF4 → FF5 → FF1` at two points (FF3 and FF5), yielding an acyclic partial-scan graph.

### 4.3 Partial-Scan vs Full-Scan — Functional Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Full["FULL-SCAN DESIGN"]
        F1[All FFs replaced] --> F2[Single long scan chain]
        F2 --> F3[Combinational ATPG only]
        F3 --> F4[FC 98-100 percent]
        F4 --> F5[High area overhead]
    end

    subgraph Partial["PARTIAL-SCAN DESIGN"]
        P1[Selected FFs replaced] --> P2[Short scan chain]
        P2 --> P3[Combinational ATPG plus bounded time-frame]
        P3 --> P4[FC 90-98 percent]
        P4 --> P5[Moderate area overhead]
    end

    subgraph Non["NON-SCAN DESIGN"]
        N1[No FFs replaced] --> N2[No scan structure]
        N2 --> N3[Full sequential ATPG]
        N3 --> N4[FC 70-85 percent]
        N4 --> N5[Low area overhead]
    end

    style Full fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style Partial fill:#4ecdc4,color:#fff
    style Non fill:#95a5a6,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Test Application Flow for a Partial-Scan Circuit

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ATE as ATE Tester
    participant SI as Scan In Pin
    participant SC as Scan Chain Nscan FFs
    participant NS as Non Scan FFs depth k
    participant SO as Scan Out Pin

    Note over ATE,SO: Shift-In Phase
    ATE->>SI: Apply test pattern bit by bit
    SI->>SC: Serial shift Nscan cycles
    SC->>NS: Apply parallel stimulus to combinational logic

    Note over ATE,SO: Capture Phase
    NS->>SC: Capture response into scan FFs
    SC->>NS: Functional FFs capture via combinational logic

    Note over ATE,SO: Shift-Out Phase
    SC->>SO: Serial shift out response
    SO->>ATE: Compare with expected response

    ATE->>ATE: Increment pattern counter repeat
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Partial-Scan Design. List its two main advantages over Full-Scan Design.**

**Model Answer:**
Partial-Scan Design is a DFT technique in which only a selected subset of flip-flops in a sequential circuit is replaced with scan flip-flops, while the rest remain as ordinary (functional) flip-flops.

**Two main advantages over Full-Scan:**
1. **Lower area and timing overhead** — fewer scan FFs mean less additional hardware and reduced impact on critical paths.
2. **Lower power dissipation during test** — shorter scan chains result in reduced switching activity during shift operations.

> **Marks Distribution (per KTU pattern):** Definition (1.5 marks) + Two advantages (0.75 + 0.75 marks).

---

**Q2. What is the role of the Minimum Feedback Vertex Set (MFVS) in Partial-Scan Design?**

**Model Answer:**
The MFVS is a graph-theoretic construct that identifies the *smallest set of flip-flops* whose removal (i.e., conversion to scan FFs) breaks **all feedback cycles** in the sequential dependency graph. The role of MFVS in Partial-Scan Design is to:
- **Minimize the number of scan FFs** required to break all cyclic dependencies.
- **Guarantee acyclic structure** of the remaining non-scan FFs, which enables bounded-depth time-frame expansion for ATPG.
- **Optimize the trade-off** between testability (high) and overhead (low).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Testability-Based Partial-Scan Selection*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the sequential graph model used for partial-scan flip-flop selection. How are feedback cycles identified in this model? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A sequential circuit is modeled as a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$ where:
- Each vertex $v_i \in V$ represents a flip-flop or a primary input/output.
- Each directed edge $(v_i, v_j) \in E$ represents a **combinational path** from the Q-output of flip-flop $i$ to the D-input of flip-flop $j$ (or from a primary input to a FF, or from a FF to a primary output).

**Feedback cycles** are identified using standard graph algorithms:
1. **Depth-First Search (DFS)** — A back edge in the DFS tree indicates a cycle.
2. **Strongly Connected Components (SCC)** — All vertices in a non-trivial SCC belong to at least one cycle (Tarjan's algorithm, $O(|V| + |E|)$).
3. **Cycle Enumeration** — Johnson's algorithm enumerates all elementary cycles.

Once cycles are identified, the MFVS is computed by selecting one FF per cycle (preferably the FF with the lowest *testability cost*) until all cycles are broken.

**(b)** Compare the test coverage, area overhead, and ATPG complexity of Full-Scan, Partial-Scan, and Non-Scan designs using a suitable table or analytical expressions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Full-Scan | Partial-Scan | Non-Scan |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|----------|
| **Test Coverage** | 98–100% | 90–98% | 70–85% |
| **Area Overhead ($\beta$)** | 15–25% | 5–12% | 0–2% |
| **ATPG Complexity** | $O(N_{comb})$ | $O(2^k \cdot N_{comb})$ | $O(2^{n_s})$ |
| **Sequential Depth $k$** | $k = 0$ | $k \le 3$ (bounded) | $k$ = full path length |
| **Test Application Time** | High (long chain) | Moderate | Low |
| **Design Effort** | Low | High | High |

**Analytical Expressions:**
- Full-Scan: $C_{ATPG} = O(N_{comb})$, $FC \approx 100\%$
- Partial-Scan: $C_{ATPG} = O(2^k \cdot N_{comb})$, $FC = FC_{full} - \delta(k)$
- Non-Scan: $C_{ATPG} = O(2^{n_s})$, $FC$ limited by sequential depth

> **Examiner Note:** For full marks, students must show the relationship $FC_{partial} = FC_{full} - \delta(k)$ and explain that $\delta(k) \to 0$ as $k \to 0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Cost-Benefit Analysis of Partial-Scan*

**(a)** A VLSI circuit has $N_{FF} = 5000$ flip-flops. A partial-scan design selects 1500 flip-flops for scan conversion. Each scan FF requires an additional 2:1 multiplexer with 4 gate-equivalents (GE) area. The routing overhead is 1 GE per scan FF, plus 3000 GE for scan chain routing. The total chip area is $A_{total} = 1{,}200{,}000$ GE. Calculate the area overhead percentage. Compare this with full-scan overhead. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Partial-Scan Overhead:**

$$
A_{ovh,partial} = N_{scan} \cdot (G_{mux} + A_{routing}) + A_{chain}
$$

$$
A_{ovh,partial} = 1500 \cdot (4 + 1) + 3000 = 7500 + 3000 = 10{,}500 \text{ GE}
$$

$$
\beta_{partial} = \dfrac{10{,}500}{1{,}200{,}000} \times 100\% = 0.875\%
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Full-Scan Overhead:**

For full-scan, $N_{scan} = N_{FF} = 5000$:

$$
A_{ovh,full} = 5000 \cdot (4 + 1) + 9000 = 25{,}000 + 9000 = 34{,}000 \text{ GE}
$$

(Assuming chain routing scales linearly: $3000 \times (5000/1500) = 10{,}000$, using 9000 as a conservative estimate.)

$$
\beta_{full} = \dfrac{34{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 2.83\%
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

$$
\text{Savings} = \dfrac{\beta_{full} - \beta_{partial}}{\beta_{full}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{2.83 - 0.875}{2.83} \times 100\% \approx 69\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Partial-scan achieves approximately **69% area overhead reduction** compared to full-scan in this design.

**(b)** Discuss the testability criteria used to select flip-flops for partial-scan conversion. Why is bounded sequential depth ($k \le 3$) critical for ATPG? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Testability Criteria for FF Selection:**

1. **Cycle-Breaking Priority** — FFs that participate in feedback cycles are top candidates (they directly reduce $k$).
2. **Low SCOAP Controllability** — FFs with high *0-controllability* or *1-controllability* (difficult to set) are preferred for scan conversion.
3. **Low Observability** — FFs feeding many fan-out points but having low observability are prime candidates.
4. **Critical Path Avoidance** — FFs on timing-critical paths should be *avoided* for scan conversion to minimize performance penalty.
5. **High Fan-in/Fan-out** — FFs with high connectivity to other FFs have higher impact on cycle breaking.

**Why $k \le 3$ is Critical:**

- The ATPG complexity grows as $O(2^k)$. For $k=3$, the search space is $2^3 = 8$ per non-scan FF — tractable.
- For $k=5$ or higher, the search space explodes to $32+$ — making ATPG infeasible.
- Empirical data from EDA tools shows that **$k \le 3$ yields >95% fault coverage**, while $k > 5$ drops coverage below 85%.

$$
\boxed{\therefore \text{Bounding } k \le 3 \text{ ensures ATPG tractability and high fault coverage.}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing partial-scan with full-scan**: Students often describe scan chain architecture (which is a full-scan topic) instead of *selection methodology* for partial-scan. Examiners deduct 3–4 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Skipping the graph model**: Failing to mention the sequential dependency graph $G=(V,E)$ and cycle-breaking concept costs the **definition marks** (typically 2 marks).
>
> 3. **Ignoring sequential depth $k$**: Any partial-scan answer *must* include the concept of $k$ and the time-frame expansion technique. Omitting this loses 3–5 marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the MFVS**: The Minimum Feedback Vertex Set is the *most-tested concept* in partial-scan questions. Always include it.
>
> 5. **Not providing numerical examples**: KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *analytical problem-solving*. A well-labeled numerical calculation (like the area overhead example) typically earns **full 7 marks** for that sub-part.
>
> 6. **Mixing up BIST with partial-scan**: BIST (Built-In Self-Test) is a *different* DFT technique. Do not confuse the two in your answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Partial-Scan Design** ✅

- **Definition** (✓): Partial-Scan = selected subset of FFs converted to scan FFs to break feedback cycles while minimizing overhead.
- **Sequential Graph Model** (✓): $G = (V, E)$ where V = FFs, E = combinational dependencies.
- **Cycle Breaking** (✓): The fundamental mechanism. Use DFS, SCC, or Johnson's algorithm for cycle detection.
- **MFVS** (✓): Minimum Feedback Vertex Set = optimal scan-FF selection for minimum overhead.
- **Sequential Depth $k$** (✓): Maximum length of non-scan FF chain. **Bound $k \le 3$** for tractable ATPG.
- **ATPG Complexity** (✓): $O(2^k \cdot N_{comb})$ — exponential in $k$, hence the need to bound $k$.
- **Test Application Time** (✓): $T_{app} = N_{pat} \cdot (N_{scan} + 1)$ clock cycles.
- **Area Overhead Formula** (✓): $\beta = \dfrac{N_{scan} \cdot (G_{mux} + A_{routing})}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$
- **Test Coverage** (✓): Partial-scan achieves 90–98% vs full-scan's 98–100%.
- **Selection Criteria** (✓): Cycle-breaking > low controllability > low observability > avoid critical paths.
- **Comparison Table** (✓): Always include the full-scan vs partial-scan vs non-scan comparison in 14-mark answers.
- **Real-World Usage** (✓): High-performance CPUs, GPUs, SoCs where full-scan overhead is unacceptable.
- **Industry Tools** (✓): Synopsys DFT Compiler, Cadence Modus, Mentor Tessent support partial-scan synthesis.
- **KTU-Specific Keywords** (✓): "Bounded sequential depth," "time-frame expansion," "feedback vertex set," "ATPG complexity reduction."

> **One-Line Takeaway for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> *"Partial-Scan Design achieves near-full-scan testability at reduced overhead by strategically breaking feedback cycles using the Minimum Feedback Vertex Set, while bounding sequential depth to $k \le 3$ for tractable ATPG."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Logic BIST
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Random Logic BIST

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Built-In Self-Test (BIST)** is a Design-for-Testability (DFT) technique in which a digital circuit is equipped with on-chip hardware that enables it to **generate its own test patterns** and **analyze its own output responses**, eliminating or significantly reducing the dependence on expensive external Automatic Test Equipment (ATE). When this BIST architecture is specifically applied to **Random Logic** (irregular, non-structured, gate-level logic such as the data-path of a processor, control logic, glue logic, or custom ASIC blocks), it is referred to as **Random Logic BIST**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Random Logic BIST is the application of BIST methodology to unstructured, irregular combinational and sequential logic networks by embedding Test Pattern Generators (TPGs), Response Analyzers (Compactors), and a BIST Controller directly onto the chip.*

The core challenge — and the reason it is a *distinct* sub-topic within Module 4 — is that random logic contains **feedback loops, reconvergent fan-out, bidirectional I/O, and uncontrolled state-elements** that the canonical memory BIST approaches cannot handle. Random Logic BIST therefore relies on **scan-based access** (essentially turning random logic into a temporarily scan-equivalent structure) plus **Linear Feedback Shift Registers (LFSRs)** for pattern generation and **Multiple Input Signature Registers (MISRs)** for response compaction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Self-Examining Hospital Lab"**
> 
> Imagine a hospital pathology lab that receives blood samples. In the *traditional model* (External ATE), the lab ships every sample to a distant city for testing — slow, expensive, and prone to logistical errors. In the *BIST model*, the hospital builds a tiny lab inside its own building. It has a **machine that synthesizes all possible test reagents on demand** (TPG), a **device that mixes them with the blood and produces a coded color signature** (Compactor/MISR), and a **foreman that orchestrates the entire process** (BIST Controller). The doctor then simply checks the signature against a known "healthy" code stored in a register.
> 
> **Random Logic BIST** is the same idea, but the "blood sample" is the *irregular, messy logic network* — the data-path of a CPU, a custom cryptographic engine, an automotive ECU. The "lab" cannot use simple grid-like testing reagents (which is what memory BIST does), so it must **inject pseudo-random stimuli** (LFSR) and **record the response as a compressed signature** (MISR). The on-chip lab then declares the chip "healthy" or "faulty" in milliseconds.

### 1.3 Why Random Logic BIST is a Unique Engineering Problem

Random logic differs from memories and regular structures (PLAs, RAMs, ROMs) in three critical ways:

| Property | Regular Structure (Memory) | Random Logic (Data-path/Control) |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Highly regular, repeating cells | Irregular, custom, application-specific |
| Fault model | Cell-array faults (stuck-at, coupling, transition) | Stuck-at, bridging, delay, IDDQ |
| Direct access | Trivial (address decoder exists) | Non-trivial (deeply embedded) |
| Controllability/Observability | High | **Low** — requires scan |
| Test generation | Deterministic, short | Long, requires pseudo-random |

This is why **scan + LFSR + MISR** is the canonical Random Logic BIST triad, and every formula in this module orbits this triad.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Random Logic BIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Building Blocks You Must Memorize for KTU:**
> 1. **TPG (Test Pattern Generator):** LFSR or Cellular Automaton — generates pseudo-random vectors.
> 2. **Response Compactor (Output Response Analyzer — ORA):** MISR or comparator — compresses output to a short signature.
> 3. **BIST Controller:** FSM that sequences the test, compares the final signature with a golden signature, and signals PASS/FAIL.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of Random Logic BIST

The generic Random Logic BIST architecture operates in **three modes** selected by a Test Mode (TM) pin:

1. **Normal Mode** — TM = 0 → BIST circuitry is bypassed; chip functions normally.
2. **Shift Mode (Scan Mode)** — TM = 1 → Scan chains act as shift registers, loading LFSR-generated patterns.
3. **Capture Mode** — LFSR holds the pattern, scan chains apply it to the Combinational Logic Under Test (CLUT), and the responses are shifted into the MISR.

A typical execution flow is:

$$\text{LFSR}_{\text{seed}} \xrightarrow{\text{Scan-In}} \text{Scan Chains} \xrightarrow{\text{Apply}} \text{CUT} \xrightarrow{\text{Capture}} \text{MISR} \xrightarrow{\text{Compare}} \text{Go/No-Go}$$

### 2.2 Test Pattern Generation (TPG) — The LFSR

The **Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR)** is the workhorse of Random Logic BIST. It is an n-stage shift register with feedback taps chosen according to a **characteristic polynomial** that is **primitive over GF(2)** (Galois Field of order 2). When primitive, the LFSR cycles through **$2^n - 1$** unique non-zero states before repeating, producing a **maximal-length pseudo-random sequence (m-sequence)**.

**Two canonical LFSR topologies exist:**

- **Standard (External-XOR) LFSR** — feedback XORs are placed at the inputs of selected stages.
- **Galois (Internal-XOR) LFSR** — feedback XORs are placed *between* stages; faster because only one XOR is in any cut-set, making it preferred for high-speed BIST.

The **characteristic polynomial** for an n-bit LFSR is expressed as:

$$P(x) = x^n + c_{n-1}x^{n-1} + c_{n-2}x^{n-2} + \cdots + c_1 x + c_0$$

where $c_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and at least two $c_i$ must be 1 (otherwise the LFSR degenerates). For maximal length, $P(x)$ must be **primitive** over GF(2).

### 2.3 Response Compaction — The MISR

Direct storage of $2^n - 1$ response vectors is impossible on-chip. The **Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR)** compacts every response vector into a fixed-width signature. An n-bit MISR has the same structure as a Galois LFSR but with **n parallel input bits** XORed into the feedback path.

**Why use a MISR instead of a simple counter?**
A plain counter loses all bitwise information (multiple distinct outputs can yield the same count). The MISR preserves a polynomial signature in GF(2), so unique outputs map to unique signatures with overwhelming probability.

### 2.4 Aliasing — The Inherent Compaction Error

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing is the #1 cause of false-pass in Random Logic BIST.** Understand this deeply for KTU 2024.

**Aliasing** occurs when a faulty circuit produces a response that, after compaction, yields the **same signature as the fault-free circuit**. The faulty chip is then wrongly declared "PASS." The probability of aliasing for a sufficiently long random input stream in an n-bit MISR is:

$$P_{\text{alias}} \approx \frac{1}{2^n}$$

For $n = 16$, $P_{\text{alias}} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-5}$, which is generally acceptable for production test. This is why MISRs are chosen to be **wide (16–32 bits)** in industrial practice.

### 2.5 Fault Coverage Metric

The test quality is quantified by the **Fault Coverage (FC)**:

$$FC = \frac{N_{\text{detected}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

Random Logic BIST aims to achieve **FC ≥ 95%** for stuck-at faults, with the residual 5% handled by deterministic ATPG patterns stored in ROM (a hybrid scheme called *Mixed-Mode BIST*).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Engineering Use | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $2^n - 1$ | Length of m-sequence from n-bit primitive LFSR | Number of unique pseudo-random vectors | Dimensionless |
| $P(x) = x^n + c_{n-1}x^{n-1} + \cdots + c_0$ | Characteristic polynomial of LFSR/MISR | Tapped for feedback configuration | Coefficients in $\{0,1\}$ |
| $P_{\text{alias}} \approx 1 / 2^n$ | Probability of aliasing in an n-bit MISR | Determines MISR width | $0 < P_{\text{alias}} \le 0.5$ |
| $FC = N_{\text{det}} / N_{\text{total}} \times 100\%$ | Fault coverage metric | Quality of test | $0 \le FC \le 100\%$ |
| $t_{\text{test}} = N \cdot (n + m) \cdot T_{clk}$ | Total BIST test time | Scheduling during production | Seconds |
| $A_{\text{h}} = 1 - (1 - p)^N$ | Hit probability (single stuck-at coverage per vector) | Determines required pattern count $N$ | $0 \le A_h \le 1$ |
| $N \approx \ln(1 - A_h) / \ln(1 - p)$ | Required number of random vectors | Sizing LFSR run length | Integers |

Where in the time formula: $N$ = number of test vectors, $n$ = scan chain length, $m$ = number of scan chains, $T_{clk}$ = clock period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — Where This System Is Used in the Real World:**
> Random Logic BIST is the **de-facto DFT standard** in safety-critical industries: automotive ECUs (ISO 26262), avionics (DO-254), medical implants, and high-volume ASICs/SoCs. Companies like Intel, NVIDIA, Qualcomm, and Texas Instruments embed Random Logic BIST in virtually every modern processor die. It dramatically reduces ATE cost (often by 70–90% on high-volume parts), enables **in-field self-test** for car ECUs, and supports **power-on self-test (POST)** in servers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Aliasing Probability of an n-bit MISR

We will derive the *aliasing probability* of an n-bit MISR for a uniformly random fault response stream. The MISR is treated as a **linear machine over GF(2)**, mapping an input sequence $\mathbf{R}(t)$ to a state $S(t)$.

**Step 1 — Define the MISR state recursion.**

Let the MISR have $n$ stages with state vector $\mathbf{S}(t) = [s_{n-1}(t), \ldots, s_0(t)]^T$ and let the response vector from the CUT at time $t$ be $\mathbf{R}(t) = [r_{n-1}(t), \ldots, r_0(t)]^T$. The MISR state update in matrix form over GF(2) is:

$$\mathbf{S}(t+1) = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{S}(t) + \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{R}(t)$$

Here $\mathbf{A}$ is the $n \times n$ companion matrix of the characteristic polynomial, and $\mathbf{B}$ is an input mapping matrix (typically the identity matrix for a standard MISR). All arithmetic is **modulo 2**.

**Step 2 — Unroll the recursion.**

Iterating $N$ times from an initial state $\mathbf{S}(0)$:

$$\mathbf{S}(N) = \mathbf{A}^N \mathbf{S}(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \mathbf{A}^{N-1-i} \mathbf{B} \mathbf{R}(i)$$

**Step 3 — Apply superposition (linearity).**

Because the system is linear, the final signature decomposes into the *fault-free* contribution and the *fault-induced* contribution:

$$\mathbf{S}(N) = \mathbf{S}_{\text{ff}}(N) + \mathbf{S}_{\text{fault}}(N)$$

The fault-free signature is:

$$\mathbf{S}_{\text{ff}}(N) = \mathbf{A}^N \mathbf{S}(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \mathbf{A}^{N-1-i} \mathbf{B} \mathbf{R}_{\text{ff}}(i)$$

The faulty signature replaces $\mathbf{R}_{\text{ff}}(i)$ with $\mathbf{R}_{\text{fault}}(i)$.

**Step 4 — Define the aliasing condition.**

Aliasing occurs when the fault-free and faulty final states are equal, i.e.:

$$\mathbf{S}_{\text{ff}}(N) = \mathbf{S}_{\text{fault}}(N) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \mathbf{A}^{N-1-i} \mathbf{B} \Delta \mathbf{R}(i) = \mathbf{0}$$

where $\Delta \mathbf{R}(i) = \mathbf{R}_{\text{fault}}(i) \oplus \mathbf{R}_{\text{ff}}(i)$ is the bit-wise XOR of faulty and fault-free responses.

**Step 5 — Count valid aliasing inputs.**

For each non-zero fault difference sequence $\Delta \mathbf{R}(i)$, the sum is a linear function of $n \cdot N$ binary input bits. The sum equals zero if and only if the linear function evaluates to zero on the specific input. For a uniform random stream, each bit of the sum is equally likely to be 0 or 1.

**Step 6 — Compute the probability.**

There are $2^{nN}$ possible error-vector sequences. Exactly $2^{nN - n} = 2^{n(N-1)}$ of them map to the zero sum (the rank of the linear map is $n$ for a non-degenerate MISR). Hence:

$$P_{\text{alias}} = \frac{2^{n(N-1)}}{2^{nN}} = \frac{1}{2^n}$$

This is **independent of $N$** — a powerful result! It means that for $N \ge 1$, the aliasing probability is dominated by the MISR width.

**Step 7 — Numerical example for KTU 2024.**

For $n = 16$ (a typical industrial MISR width):

$$P_{\text{alias}} = \frac{1}{2^{16}} = \frac{1}{65536} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is $0.00153\%$, comfortably below the $0.01\%$ acceptance threshold used in production test.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Number of Random Patterns for Target Coverage

We will derive the expected number $N$ of random patterns needed to detect a target fault with hit probability $p$ per pattern, achieving overall detection probability $A_h$.

**Step 1 — Probability that a single random vector misses the fault.**

A fault is *activated and propagated* by a random vector with probability $p$ (for random logic, $p$ is roughly the controllability/observability product, often $0.01$ to $0.1$). The miss probability per vector is:

$$P_{\text{miss,1}} = 1 - p$$

**Step 2 — Probability that $N$ vectors all miss.**

Because the LFSR sequence has long autocorrelation, consecutive vectors are approximately independent for fault detection purposes. So:

$$P_{\text{miss},N} = (1 - p)^N$$

**Step 3 — Probability that the fault is detected (hit) by at least one of $N$ vectors.**

$$A_h = 1 - P_{\text{miss},N} = 1 - (1 - p)^N$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$ given target $A_h$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - A_h &= (1 - p)^N \\
\ln(1 - A_h) &= N \ln(1 - p) \\
N &= \frac{\ln(1 - A_h)}{\ln(1 - p)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for KTU 2024.**

Suppose $p = 0.05$ (a moderately hard-to-detect fault) and the target is $A_h = 0.99$:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\ln(1 - 0.99)}{\ln(1 - 0.05)} = \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(0.95)} = \frac{-4.6052}{-0.05129} \approx 89.78
\end{aligned}$$

Round up: $N = 90$ random vectors. So a maximal-length LFSR of $n = 7$ (giving $2^7 - 1 = 127$ vectors) is sufficient for this fault.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In exam questions, you will often be given $p$, $A_h$, or $N$ and asked to compute the missing parameter. Memorize the formula $A_h = 1 - (1-p)^N$ and its inverse.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LFSR Topologies

| Parameter | Standard (External-XOR) LFSR | Galois (Internal-XOR) LFSR |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback tap location | External, between $Q_n$ and $Q_1$ | Internal, between stages |
| Number of XORs in critical path | $n$ (worst case) | **1** (best case) |
| Speed | Slower | **Faster** |
| Power consumption | Higher glitch rate | Lower |
| Area | Slightly higher | Slightly lower |
| Pattern quality | Identical (same characteristic polynomial) | Identical |
| KTU recommended choice | When speed is not critical | **Preferred for high-speed BIST** |

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Random Logic BIST vs. Deterministic ATPG

| Parameter | External ATPG (Deterministic) | Random Logic BIST (Pseudo-Random) |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern source | Off-chip ATE | On-chip LFSR |
| Pattern length | Short, hand-crafted | Long, exhaustive |
| Coverage of random-resistant faults | Excellent (targeted) | Poor (residual faults) |
| ATE cost | High (memory depth needed) | Low (only signature needed) |
| In-field test | Impossible (no ATE in field) | **Possible** (e.g., car ECU) |
| Test time | Short | Long (for high coverage) |
| Hybrid Mixed-Mode BIST | — | **Industry standard** (BIST for bulk + ROM-stored deterministic for residue) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Random Logic BIST Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[BIST CONTROLLER FSM]
        ctrlA[IDLE STATE]
        ctrlB[SHIFT MODE]
        ctrlC[CAPTURE MODE]
        ctrlD[COMPARE AND REPORT]
        ctrlA --> ctrlB
        ctrlB --> ctrlC
        ctrlC --> ctrlB
        ctrlB --> ctrlD
    end

    subgraph TPG_BLOCK[TEST PATTERN GENERATOR]
        lfsrA[N STAGE LFSR]
        lfsrB[SEED REGISTER]
        lfsrC[CHARACTERISTIC POLYNOMIAL TAP NETWORK]
        lfsrB --> lfsrA
        lfsrC --> lfsrA
    end

    subgraph CUT_BLOCK[CIRCUIT UNDER TEST]
        sc1[SCAN CHAIN 1]
        sc2[SCAN CHAIN 2]
        scN[SCAN CHAIN M]
        clutA[COMBINATIONAL LOGIC UNDER TEST]
        ffA[SCAN FLIP FLOPS]

        sc1 --> clutA
        sc2 --> clutA
        scN --> clutA
        clutA --> ffA
        ffA --> sc1
        ffA --> sc2
        ffA --> scN
    end

    subgraph ORA_BLOCK[OUTPUT RESPONSE ANALYZER]
        misrA[N BIT MISR]
        misrB[EXPECTED GOLDEN SIGNATURE ROM]
        misrC[COMPARATOR XOR TREE]
        misrA --> misrC
        misrB --> misrC
        misrC --> ctrlD
    end

    lfsrA -- Parallel Load --> sc1
    lfsrA -- Parallel Load --> sc2
    lfsrA -- Parallel Load --> scN
    ffA -- Shift Out Serial Response --> misrA
    CTRL -- Clock and Mode Control --> TPG_BLOCK
    CTRL -- Scan Enable and Shift Capture --> CUT_BLOCK
    CTRL -- Capture Clock and Reset --> ORA_BLOCK
    ctrlD -- PASS FAIL Output --> GO[GO NO GO SIGNAL TO PAD]
```

### 4.2 State Diagram of BIST Controller FSM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> SHIFT : TestMode goes high
    SHIFT --> CAPTURE : ShiftComplete asserted
    CAPTURE --> SHIFT : OneCaptureCycleDone
    SHIFT --> COMPARE : PatternCount equals N
    COMPARE --> PASS : Signature equals GoldenROM
    COMPARE --> FAIL : Signature differs from GoldenROM
    PASS --> IDLE : Reset or TestMode low
    FAIL --> IDLE : Reset or TestMode low
    IDLE --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Detailed Data Flow of a Single BIST Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 LFSR Generates Next Pattern]
    stepB[Step 2 ScanIn Patterns to Scan Chains N clocks]
    stepC[Step 3 Apply Pattern to Combinational Logic]
    stepD[Step 4 Capture Response into Scan Flip Flops]
    stepE[Step 5 ShiftOut Response to MISR M plus N clocks]
    stepF[Step 6 MISR Updates Internal Signature State]
    stepG[Step 7 Increment Pattern Counter]
    stepH[Step 8 Pattern Count equals 2 to N minus 1]
    stepI[Step 9 Compare Final MISR Signature with GoldenROM]
    stepJ[Step 10 Emit Go NoGo Result]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH -- No --> stepA
    stepH -- Yes --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
```

### 4.4 LFSR vs MISR Structural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LFSR_DIAGRAM[N BIT LFSR]
        d1[D1] --> d2[D2]
        d2 --> d3[D3]
        d3 --> d4[D4]
        d4 --> dN[DN]
        dN --> x1[XOR GATE 1]
        x1 --> d1
        d3 --> x2[XOR GATE 2]
        x2 --> x1
    end

    subgraph MISR_DIAGRAM[N BIT MISR]
        m1[M1] --> m2[M2]
        m2 --> m3[M3]
        m3 --> m4[M4]
        m4 --> mN[MN]
        mN --> y1[XOR GATE 1]
        y1 --> m1
        m3 --> y2[XOR GATE 2]
        y2 --> y1
        r1[RESPONSE INPUT 1] --> y1
        r2[RESPONSE INPUT 2] --> y2
        rN[RESPONSE INPUT N] --> mN
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide:** The LFSR and MISR are topologically *identical* in their feedback network. The single structural difference is that the MISR has **n additional input XOR gates** that inject the response bits. If you disable these inputs (tie them to 0), the MISR degenerates into a pure LFSR.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Built-In Self-Test (BIST). List and briefly explain the three primary on-chip blocks required for implementing Random Logic BIST.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Built-In Self-Test (BIST) is a Design-for-Testability technique in which additional circuitry is embedded within an IC to enable the chip to test itself, generating test patterns internally and analyzing responses internally with minimal external Automatic Test Equipment (ATE) support.

**Three Primary On-Chip Blocks:**

1. **Test Pattern Generator (TPG):** A hardware block, typically an LFSR or a Cellular Automaton, that generates pseudo-random test vectors on-chip. The TPG replaces the external ATE's pattern generator.

2. **Output Response Analyzer (ORA):** A compactor, typically a Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR), that compresses the long stream of CUT output responses into a short, fixed-width signature. The final signature is compared against a pre-computed *golden signature* to decide PASS/FAIL.

3. **BIST Controller:** A finite-state machine (FSM) that sequences the test: loads the LFSR seed, drives scan chains, applies patterns, captures responses, and finally triggers the comparator. It is the "foreman" of the on-chip test process.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is aliasing in the context of MISR-based response compaction? Mention the formula for aliasing probability and one technique to reduce it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Aliasing:**
Aliasing is the phenomenon in which a *faulty* circuit produces an output response stream that, after MISR compaction, yields the *same final signature* as the fault-free circuit. Consequently, the BIST controller declares the faulty chip as "PASS" — a false positive.

**Aliasing Probability Formula:**
For an n-bit MISR, the aliasing probability (assuming long random input streams) is:

$$P_{\text{alias}} \approx \frac{1}{2^n}$$

**Technique to Reduce Aliasing:**
- **Increase the MISR width $n$:** Doubling $n$ reduces $P_{\text{alias}}$ by a factor of 4.
- **Use Double-Capture (Two-Pattern Testing):** Capture twice per pattern and combine.
- **Use Parity-bit Augmented MISR (P-MISR):** Add an extra parity bit to break linear dependencies.
- **Use a comparator with multiple check-points** rather than a single final comparison.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of Random Logic BIST. Identify and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of an n-bit LFSR used as a Test Pattern Generator. Derive the expression for the number of distinct test patterns generated by an n-bit LFSR with a primitive characteristic polynomial. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to (a):

The Random Logic BIST architecture consists of four major blocks:

**Block 1 — Test Pattern Generator (TPG):** Implemented as an n-bit Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR). The LFSR is initialized with a non-zero seed and clocked to produce a sequence of $2^n - 1$ unique pseudo-random patterns (assuming a primitive polynomial). The patterns are applied to the CUT either directly (in a *parallel* or *boundary-scan* scheme) or, more commonly, by *serial scan-load* into scan chains.

**Block 2 — Scan Chain Network (CUT Interface):** A set of m scan chains embedded in the CUT. During BIST, the LFSR-generated pattern is shifted into all scan chains in parallel (one bit per chain per clock). After shift-in, a single capture clock applies the pattern to the combinational logic, and the response is captured into the scan flip-flops. The captured response is then shifted out serially into the MISR while the next pattern is shifted in.

**Block 3 — Output Response Analyzer (ORA):** Implemented as an n-bit Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR). As response bits are shifted out of the scan chains, they are XORed into the MISR feedback path. After $N \cdot (n+m)$ clocks (where $N$ is the number of test patterns), the MISR holds a single n-bit signature.

**Block 4 — BIST Controller (FSM):** A finite-state machine that orchestrates the entire process: (i) initialize the LFSR with the seed, (ii) start the shift-capture cycle, (iii) count patterns, (iv) trigger the comparator when the pattern count reaches the target, and (v) report PASS/FAIL via an output pin.

A comparator XORs the final MISR signature with a *golden signature* stored in on-chip ROM. If the XOR result is all zeros, the test is declared PASS; otherwise, FAIL.

### Model Solution to (b):

An n-bit LFSR consists of n D-flip-flops connected in a shift-register chain, with selected stages feeding back through XOR gates to the input. The placement of these XOR gates follows a **characteristic polynomial**:

$$P(x) = x^n + c_{n-1}x^{n-1} + c_{n-2}x^{n-2} + \cdots + c_1 x + c_0, \quad c_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Operation:**
1. Initially, the LFSR is loaded with a non-zero seed (e.g., 00…01).
2. On each clock edge, all bits shift one position to the right, and the feedback XOR sum is loaded into the leftmost stage.
3. The state evolves as: $\mathbf{S}(t+1) = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{S}(t) \pmod 2$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is the companion matrix of $P(x)$.

**Derivation of pattern count:**
For an LFSR of degree $n$, the state space has $2^n$ possible values. The all-zero state is a *fixed point* (it maps to itself), and it is excluded from the maximal-length sequence to avoid trivial cycling. Hence, the cycle visits at most $2^n - 1$ distinct non-zero states.

**Claim:** *If $P(x)$ is primitive over $\text{GF}(2)$, the LFSR cycle length is exactly $2^n - 1$.*

**Proof sketch:**
A primitive polynomial of degree $n$ over $\text{GF}(2)$ is one whose multiplicative order is $2^n - 1$. Equivalently, the smallest positive integer $k$ such that $P(x)$ divides $x^k - 1$ is $k = 2^n - 1$. The LFSR state polynomial at time $t$ is $x^t \cdot P_{\text{seed}}(x) \pmod{P(x)}$. The state returns to the seed when $x^t \equiv 1 \pmod{P(x)}$, i.e., when $t$ is the multiplicative order of $x$ modulo $P(x)$. This order is exactly $2^n - 1$ for primitive polynomials. $\blacksquare$

**Example:** For $n = 4$ and $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$ (primitive), the LFSR generates $2^4 - 1 = 15$ distinct patterns.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of an n-bit Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR). Derive the aliasing probability of an MISR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-bit LFSR is used to test a combinational circuit. The fault detection probability per random vector is $p = 0.1$. Calculate: (i) the number of vectors required to achieve 99% fault coverage, and (ii) the aliasing probability if a 16-bit MISR is used for response compaction. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to (a):

A **Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR)** is structurally similar to a Galois LFSR, but with **n parallel input lines** that XOR response bits into the feedback network during the compaction process. As the scan chains shift out response bits, the MISR simultaneously shifts and compacts them into a single n-bit signature.

**State recursion:**
$$\mathbf{S}(t+1) = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{S}(t) + \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{R}(t) \pmod 2$$

**Derivation of Aliasing Probability:**

Let $\Delta \mathbf{R}(i) = \mathbf{R}_{\text{fault}}(i) \oplus \mathbf{R}_{\text{ff}}(i)$ be the bit-wise error between faulty and fault-free responses. The aliasing condition is:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \mathbf{A}^{N-1-i} \mathbf{B} \Delta \mathbf{R}(i) = \mathbf{0} \pmod 2$$

For a non-degenerate MISR (primitive characteristic polynomial), the linear map from $\Delta \mathbf{R}$ to the final-state difference has full column rank $n$. There are $2^{nN}$ possible error-vector sequences, and exactly $2^{n(N-1)}$ map to the zero sum. Therefore:

$$P_{\text{alias}} = \frac{2^{n(N-1)}}{2^{nN}} = \frac{1}{2^n}$$

This result is *independent of $N$* — a remarkable property of MISR compaction.

### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $p = 0.1$, target coverage $A_h = 0.99$, MISR width $n = 16$.

**(i) Number of vectors for 99% coverage:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_h &= 1 - (1 - p)^N \\
1 - A_h &= (1 - p)^N \\
\ln(1 - 0.99) &= N \cdot \ln(1 - 0.1) \\
\ln(0.01) &= N \cdot \ln(0.9) \\
-4.6052 &= N \cdot (-0.10536) \\
N &= \frac{-4.6052}{-0.10536} \approx 43.7
\end{aligned}$$

Rounding up, **$N = 44$ vectors** are required. A 4-bit LFSR can produce up to $2^4 - 1 = 15$ vectors per full cycle, so multiple cycles (or a longer LFSR) are needed. Practical recommendation: use a 6-bit LFSR (63 vectors) to comfortably exceed 44 with a single pass.

**(ii) Aliasing probability with a 16-bit MISR:**

$$P_{\text{alias}} = \frac{1}{2^{16}} = \frac{1}{65536} \approx 1.5259 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is approximately **$0.00153\%$**, well within industry-acceptable bounds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing LFSR with MISR:** Students frequently describe a MISR as "an LFSR with inputs." While technically true, examiners expect you to emphasize the **parallel input injection** and its effect on the state-transition equation.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the all-zero state:** When asked "how many patterns does an n-bit LFSR generate?", the wrong answer is $2^n$. The correct answer is $2^n - 1$ because the all-zero state is a fixed point and must be excluded (a primitive polynomial cycle never visits it).
> 
> 3. **Forgetting log when solving for $N$:** Many students write $N = (1 - A_h)/(1 - p)$ which is dimensionally inconsistent (a probability divided by a probability is a ratio, not a count). The correct formula involves $\ln$ on both sides.
> 
> 4. **Conflating aliasing with fault coverage:** Aliasing is a property of the *compactor*, not the *pattern generator*. They are independent design choices. Examiners will deduct marks if you mix them up.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting scan chains:** Random Logic BIST *requires* scan-based access. Stating that the LFSR drives the CUT directly (without scan) is incorrect for random logic — it only works for memory BIST.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Density Revision Checklist for Random Logic BIST**
> 
> ✅ **Three Building Blocks:** TPG (LFSR) + ORA (MISR) + BIST Controller (FSM).
> 
> ✅ **Why BIST for Random Logic?** Random logic has low controllability/observability — cannot be directly addressed like memory.
> 
> ✅ **Scan is Mandatory:** Random Logic BIST requires scan chains to access internal flip-flops.
> 
> ✅ **LFSR generates $2^n - 1$ patterns:** When characteristic polynomial is primitive over GF(2).
> 
> ✅ **LFSR Topologies:** Standard (external XOR) vs. Galois (internal XOR). Galois is faster.
> 
> ✅ **Characteristic Polynomial:** $P(x) = x^n + c_{n-1}x^{n-1} + \cdots + c_0$, with $c_i \in \{0,1\}$, and at least two $c_i = 1$ for non-degeneracy.
> 
> ✅ **MISR Operation:** Galois LFSR + n parallel input XORs. Compresses response stream into an n-bit signature.
> 
> ✅ **Aliasing Probability:** $P_{\text{alias}} = 1 / 2^n$ — independent of pattern count $N$.
> 
> ✅ **Hit Probability Formula:** $A_h = 1 - (1 - p)^N$, where $p$ is per-vector fault-detection probability.
> 
> ✅ **Solving for $N$:** $N = \ln(1 - A_h) / \ln(1 - p)$. Always round up.
> 
> ✅ **Fault Coverage Target:** $\geq 95\%$ from random patterns, residual handled by deterministic patterns in Mixed-Mode BIST.
> 
> ✅ **Golden Signature:** Pre-computed fault-free signature stored in on-chip ROM for final comparison.
> 
> ✅ **False PASS Risk:** Aliasing → use wide MISR ($n \geq 16$), double-capture, or parity-augmented MISR.
> 
> ✅ **Industrial MISR Width:** 16 to 32 bits in practice.
> 
> ✅ **Test Time:** $t_{\text{test}} = N \cdot (n + m) \cdot T_{clk}$, where $m$ = number of scan chains.
> 
> ✅ **Industrial Use Cases:** Automotive ECUs (ISO 26262), avionics (DO-254), medical implants, in-field POST for servers, automotive ignition self-checks.
> 
> ✅ **Key Advantage:** Drastically reduces ATE cost; enables in-field test without external equipment.
> 
> ✅ **Key Disadvantage:** Cannot easily detect random-resistant faults → requires Mixed-Mode BIST or deterministic test point insertion.
> 
> ✅ **KTU Favorite Exam Topics:** (1) LFSR pattern count, (2) MISR aliasing formula derivation, (3) $A_h$ formula numerical problems, (4) Block diagram of Random Logic BIST architecture, (5) Distinguish LFSR vs. MISR.

---

topics.title: Memory BIST
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory BIST

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory BIST (MBIST):** A built-in self-test technique in which dedicated on-chip hardware is permanently embedded within a memory (or memory array) to perform automated testing of memory cells, without requiring external Automated Test Equipment (ATE). The hardware autonomously generates address sequences, data backgrounds, and expected read responses, then compares the actual output against the expected response to declare **Pass** or **Fail**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MBIST is classified under **Design-for-Testability (DFT)** for embedded memories. It forms a mandatory component of System-on-Chip (SoC) test architecture because modern chips contain **70–90% SRAM** area and traditional external ATE cannot economically test the deep, high-speed embedded memory cores.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Self-Testing Notebook":** Imagine a classroom notebook that, every night, automatically rewrites each page, checks if the page is clean, erases it, rewrites the opposite content, checks again, and reports to the teacher whether every page is writable. The notebook never needs an external inspector; the test is built into the notebook itself. MBIST is that self-inspecting mechanism for silicon memory — the chip tests its own memory cells during power-up, in-field, or during manufacturing test.

## 1.3 Why Memory BIST is Mandatory

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Speed Mismatch** | ATE runs at ~200 MHz; embedded SRAM runs at 1–3 GHz. Direct ATE access is impossible. |
| **Pin Limitation** | An SoC has 10⁶ memory cells but only ~500 package pins. Direct access would take centuries. |
| **Cost Reduction** | ATE time per chip is expensive. MBIST runs at-speed, in-situ, and at zero ATE cost. |
| **In-Field Test** | Radiation/aging faults can be detected after deployment using periodic MBIST. |
| **Repair Enablement** | MBIST output feeds redundancy analyzers to replace faulty cells with spares. |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Recall for KTU Exams)

- **Fault Coverage:** Typically **> 95%** of classical memory fault models.
- **Test Time:** Expressed as **T = N × M × C** cycles, where N = number of addresses, M = data backgrounds, C = operations per cell.
- **Area Overhead:** Typically **2–5%** of memory area.
- **Power Dissipation:** Must be capped to avoid IR-drop during test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Fault Models (Foundational Concept)

Memory cells exhibit **dynamic, pattern-sensitive, and coupling faults** that are NOT covered by classic stuck-at tests. KTU frequently tests your knowledge of the following fault taxonomy:

### 2.1.1 Classical (Single-Cell) Faults
- **SAF (Stuck-At Fault):** Cell is permanently stuck at 0 or 1.
- **TF (Transition Fault):** Cell fails a 0→1 or 1→0 transition.
- **AF (Address Decoder Fault):** One address maps to multiple cells, or no cell is accessed, or some cells are inaccessible.

### 2.1.2 Two-Cell Coupling Faults
- **CF (Coupling Fault):** A transition in aggressor cell *i* forces victim cell *j* to a state.
- **CFid (Idempotent Coupling):** 0→1 in *i* forces *j* to 0 (or 1).
- **CFdyn (Dynamic Coupling):** Read of *i* flips *j*.

### 2.1.3 Pattern-Sensitive Faults
- **NPSF (Neighborhood Pattern Sensitive Fault):** The state of a victim cell depends on a base cell and its 4-neighbors (N, S, E, W).
- **Active NPSF:** A transition in neighbors causes victim change.
- **Passive NPSF:** A specific pattern forces victim to a state.
- **Static NPSF:** A specific pattern forces victim to a state without transition.

## 2.2 March Tests — The Heart of MBIST

A **March test** is a finite sequence of **March elements**. Each element consists of:
- A list of operations: `r0, r1, w0, w1`
- An address order: `⇑` (ascending), `⇓` (descending), `⇕` (either)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used in KTU:**
> - `r0` = read expecting 0
> - `w0` = write 0
> - `⇑` = address 0 → N−1
> - `⇓` = address N−1 → 0

## 2.3 Canonical March Tests (MUST memorize for 14-mark questions)

| Test Name | Algorithm | Complexity | Faults Detected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MATS** | `{⇑(w0); ⇑(r0, w1); ⇓(r1, w0)}` | 4N | All SAF, some AF |
| **MATS+** | `{⇑(w0); ⇑(r0, w1); ⇓(r1, w0); ⇑(r0)}` | 5N | All SAF, all AF |
| **MATS++** | `{⇑(w0); ⇑(r0, w1); ⇓(r1, w0); ⇑(r0, w1); ⇓(r1, w0)}` | 6N | All SAF, all AF, all TF |
| **March X** | `{⇑(w0); ⇑(r0, w1, r1, w0, r0, w1); ⇓(r1, w0, r1, w1, r0, w1); ⇑(r0, w1, r0)}` | 12N | All CFid |
| **March Y** | `{⇑(w0); ⇑(r0, w1, r1); ⇑(r1, w0, r0); ⇓(r0, w1, r1); ⇓(r1, w0, r0); ⇑(r0)}` | 8N | All CFid (fewer ops than March X) |
| **March C** | `{⇑(w0); ⇑(r0, w1, r1, w0, r0, w1); ⇑(r0, w1, r1, w1, r0, w1); ⇑(r1, w0, w1, r1, w0, w0); ⇓(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇓(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇓(r1, w0, w1, w1, r0, w0); ⇓(r1, w0, w1, w0, r0); ⇑(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇑(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇑(r1, w0, r1, w0, w0); ⇑(r0)}` | 11N | All CFdyn + all CFid + all SAF + all TF + all AF |
| **March SR** | `{⇑(w0); ⇑(r0, w1, r1, w0, r0, w1); ⇑(r0, w1, r1, w1, r0, w1); ⇑(r1, w0, w1, r1, w0, w0); ⇓(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇓(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇓(r1, w0, r1, w1, w0, w0); ⇓(r1, w0, r1, w0, r0); ⇑(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇑(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1); ⇑(r1, w0, r1, w0, r0); ⇑(r0)}` | 14N | All Read Disturb Faults (RDF) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Test Time | $T_{test} = N \times M \times O$ | N = addresses, M = data backgrounds, O = operations/cell. Unit: clock cycles. |
| March Test Complexity | $C = \sum_{i} n_i$ | $n_i$ = number of operations in $i$-th March element. |
| Fault Coverage | $FC = \frac{F_d}{F_t} \times 100\%$ | $F_d$ = detected faults, $F_t$ = total faults. Unit: %. |
| Yield Loss Probability | $Y_L = (1-Y)^N$ | Y = per-cell yield, N = cells. Exponential failure with size. |
| Area Overhead | $AO = \frac{A_{BIST}}{A_{MEM}}$ | Typical: 2–5%. |
| CAM Lookup (March X) | $6N$ | Total operations for March X. |
| Redundancy Repair | $P_{repair} = \sum_{k=0}^{R} \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k}$ | R = spare rows/cols, p = fault probability. |
| Address Counter Bits | $a = \log_2(N)$ | N = memory depth. |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Industrial Use:**
> - **Mobile SoCs (Qualcomm, Apple, MediaTek):** Run March C- on power-up to catch latent defects.
> - **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Periodic MBIST during mission mode for ASIL-D compliance.
> - **Aerospace & Defense:** Triple-Modular Redundancy (TMR) + MBIST for radiation hardening.
> - **DRAM Industry:** MBIST feeds Built-In Self-Repair (BISR) for row/column redundancy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Test Time for March C-

March C- is widely used in industry. We derive its test time from first principles.

**Given:**
- Memory depth: $N$ words
- Memory width: $W$ bits (we test bit-by-bit or word-by-word)
- Single operation time: $T_{clk}$ (one clock cycle)

**March C- Algorithm:**
$$\{ \Uparrow(w0);\, \Uparrow(r0, w1, r1, w0, r0, w1);\, \Uparrow(r0, w1, r1, w1, r0, w1);\, \Uparrow(r1, w0, w1, r1, w0, w0);\, \Downarrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1);\, \Downarrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1);\, \Downarrow(r1, w0, r1, w1, w0, w0);\, \Downarrow(r1, w0, r1, w0, r0);\, \Uparrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1);\, \Uparrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1);\, \Uparrow(r1, w0, r1, w0, w0);\, \Uparrow(r0) \}$$

**Step 1: Count operations per element**

Let us count the operations in each of the 11 March elements (M0 to M10):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &: \Uparrow(w0) &\Rightarrow& \ 1 \text{ op} \times N \\
M_1 &: \Uparrow(r0, w1, r1, w0, r0, w1) &\Rightarrow& \ 6 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_2 &: \Uparrow(r0, w1, r1, w1, r0, w1) &\Rightarrow& \ 6 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_3 &: \Uparrow(r1, w0, w1, r1, w0, w0) &\Rightarrow& \ 6 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_4 &: \Downarrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1) &\Rightarrow& \ 5 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_5 &: \Downarrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1) &\Rightarrow& \ 5 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_6 &: \Downarrow(r1, w0, r1, w1, w0, w0) &\Rightarrow& \ 6 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_7 &: \Downarrow(r1, w0, r1, w0, r0) &\Rightarrow& \ 5 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_8 &: \Uparrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1) &\Rightarrow& \ 5 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_9 &: \Uparrow(r0, w0, r0, w1, r1) &\Rightarrow& \ 5 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_{10} &: \Uparrow(r1, w0, r1, w0, w0) &\Rightarrow& \ 5 \text{ ops} \times N \\
M_{11} &: \Uparrow(r0) &\Rightarrow& \ 1 \text{ op} \times N \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sum total operations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MarchC-} &= N \times (1 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 5 + 5 + 6 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 1) \\
&= N \times 56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final test time in seconds**

$$
\boxed{T_{test} = 56N \cdot T_{clk}}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

For a **$4\text{ K} \times 8$ SRAM**, $N = 4096$ words, $T_{clk} = 1\,\text{ns}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{test} &= 56 \times 4096 \times 1\,\text{ns} \\
&= 229{,}376\,\text{ns} \\
&\approx 229.4\,\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Fault Coverage Bound for March Tests

**Theorem:** A March test of complexity $C$ can detect all unlinked faults of size $k$ if and only if every fault requires at least $k$ sensitization operations, and the test contains a sensitization-compatible March element pair.

For **CFid (Idempotent Coupling Faults)**, a $0 \rightarrow 1$ transition in aggressor *a* forces victim *v* to 0. To sensitize, we need:
1. Initialize both cells: `w0` to *a*, `w0` to *v* (or background 0)
2. Transition aggressor: `w1` to *a*
3. Read victim: `r0` should return 0

This requires **at least 2N operations with the right address order**, hence the lower bound for CFid is **5N (MATS+)** or higher.

For **CFdyn**, dynamic read coupling: a read on *a* flips *v*. To detect, we need:
1. Initialize *v* to known value
2. Perform the dynamic operation
3. Read *v* again

This forces March C- to have 10N operations minimum.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which March Test to Use?

| Application Domain | Recommended March Test | Complexity | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-cost MCU embedded SRAM** | MATS+ | 5N | Covers SAF, AF, low area overhead. |
| **Smartphone SoC L1/L2 cache** | March C- | 11N | Industry standard; covers CFdyn. |
| **Automotive safety-critical (ASIL-D)** | March SR | 14N | Detects Read Disturb Faults. |
| **DRAM with retention faults** | March C- + Retention Pause | 11N+pause | Retention test requires wait. |
| **CAM / Content Addressable Memory** | Modified March C- | 12N+ | Plus match-line fault tests. |
| **ML SRAM (in-memory compute)** | March SS | 22N | Detects computing-mode faults. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level MBIST Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Test Controller FSM] --> B[Address Generator]
    A --> C[Data Background Generator]
    A --> D[Read/Write Multiplexer]
    A --> E[Expected Response Generator]
    B --> F[Memory Under Test MUT]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G[Miscompare Comparator]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Fault Register]
    H --> I[Pass or Fail Output]
    A --> J[Clock and Reset Block]
    J --> A
    H --> K[BISR Redundancy Analyzer]
    K --> F
```

**Figure 4.1: Generic MBIST Block Architecture** — Shows the closed-loop comparison path: Data background and address are generated, fed to MUT, and the read response is XORed with the expected response. Any mismatch latches the fault register and signals fail.

## 4.2 MBIST Controller State Machine (Hardwired BIST)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> InitMemory: Start BIST
    InitMemory --> M0Element: M0 complete
    M0Element --> M1Element: Up count done
    M1Element --> M2Element: Read compare pass
    M2Element --> M3Element: Up count done
    M3Element --> M4Element: Up count done
    M4Element --> M5Element: Down count done
    M5Element --> M6Element: Down count done
    M6Element --> M7Element: Down count done
    M7Element --> M8Element: Down count done
    M8Element --> M9Element: Up count done
    M9Element --> M10Element: Up count done
    M10Element --> M11Element: Up count done
    M11Element --> CompareFinal
    CompareFinal --> Pass: No mismatch
    CompareFinal --> Fail: Mismatch detected
    Pass --> [*]
    Fail --> BISRRedundancy
    BISRRedundancy --> [*]
```

**Figure 4.2: Hardwired FSM-based MBIST Controller for March C-** — Each state corresponds to one March element. This is the **hardwired** approach: minimum area, but inflexible (cannot change test without re-spinning silicon).

## 4.3 Microcode-Based MBIST Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MC[Microcode ROM] --> IR[Instruction Register]
    IR --> DEC[Instruction Decoder]
    DEC --> AG[Address Generator]
    DEC --> DPG[Data Pattern Generator]
    DEC --> ERG[Expected Response Gen]
    AG --> MUT[Memory Under Test]
    DPG --> MUT
    MUT --> CMP[Comparator]
    ERG --> CMP
    CMP --> FR[Fault Register]
    FR --> CMP2[Signature Compressor]
    CMP2 --> OUT[Pass or Fail]
    PC[Program Counter] --> MC
    DEC --> PC
```

**Figure 4.3: Microcode-Programmable MBIST** — Stores the March test as instructions in ROM. Enables **flexibility**: the same silicon can run March C-, March SR, or a custom user-defined test simply by loading a different microcode image. Higher area, but standard for SoCs.

## 4.4 BISR (Built-In Self-Repair) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Power On or Test Trigger] --> MBIST[Run MBIST]
    MBIST --> Q1{Any Faults?}
    Q1 -- No --> P1[Pass: Memory Healthy]
    Q1 -- Yes --> ANA[Fault Location Analyzer]
    ANA --> MAP[Build Fault Map]
    MAP --> RA[Redundancy Algorithm]
    RA --> Q2{Repair Possible?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> REP[Activate Spare Row or Column]
    REP --> MBIST2[Re-run MBIST]
    MBIST2 --> Q3{Still Failing?}
    Q3 -- No --> P2[Pass: Repaired]
    Q3 -- Yes --> FAIL[Hard Fail: Mark Bad]
    Q2 -- No --> FAIL
    P1 --> End[Done]
    P2 --> End
    FAIL --> End
```

**Figure 4.4: BISR Pipeline** — Combines MBIST with redundancy. Critical for **DRAM and high-density SRAM** in production environments where post-package repair is mandatory for yield.

## 4.5 Address Generation LFSR (Pseudo-Random Address)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[D Flip-Flop 0] --> XOR[XOR Gate]
    D1[D Flip-Flop 1] --> D0
    D2[D Flip-Flop 2] --> D1
    D3[D Flip-Flop 3] --> D2
    XOR --> D3
    D0 --> ADDR[Memory Address Bus]
```

**Figure 4.5: 4-bit LFSR for Pseudo-Random Address Generation** — Used in **pseudo-random MBIST** where deterministic March traversal is replaced by LFSR addresses. Lower controller complexity but reduced CFid coverage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Memory BIST. List any four memory fault models detected by March C- test.**

**Model Answer:**

**Memory BIST (MBIST)** is a Design-for-Testability technique in which on-chip hardware autonomously tests embedded memories by generating addresses, data backgrounds, and expected read responses, then comparing them with the actual outputs to flag faults.

**Four fault models detected by March C-:**
1. **SAF (Stuck-At Fault)**
2. **TF (Transition Fault)**
3. **AF (Address Decoder Fault)**
4. **CFid / CFdyn (Coupling Faults — both static idempotent and dynamic)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often list only SAF and TF. Examiners award full marks only when you mention **CFdyn** (dynamic coupling) since that's March C-'s distinguishing capability.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between hardwired and microcode-based MBIST architectures.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hardwired MBIST | Microcode MBIST |
|---|---|---|
| **Test Logic** | FSM with fixed states | Stored microcode in ROM |
| **Flexibility** | Fixed at design time | Re-programmable per test |
| **Area Overhead** | Low (~1–2%) | Higher (~3–5%) |
| **Power** | Lower | Slightly higher |
| **Use Case** | Single fixed test (e.g., MATS+) | Multi-test SoC memories |
| **Test Reuse** | None | Same silicon, different tests |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the MBIST architecture with a neat block diagram. List the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a March test to detect all SA0, SA1, and TF faults in a 4×4 SRAM. Show the complete algorithm and compute its test complexity. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MBIST Architecture — Block Diagram Explanation (7 Marks)**

The Memory BIST architecture consists of the following functional blocks (refer Figure 4.1):

1. **Test Controller FSM (Finite State Machine):** The brain of MBIST. Orchestrates the sequence of March elements, generates the read/write control signals, and sequences address direction (`⇑` or `⇓`). It is a hardwired state machine with states corresponding to each March element.

2. **Address Generator:** Generates the address sequence to be applied to the memory. In deterministic March tests, it is a simple binary up/down counter. In pseudo-random MBIST, it is an LFSR. The generator must support both ascending and descending traversal.

3. **Data Background Generator (DBG):** Produces the data patterns written into the memory. Common patterns: all-0s, all-1s, checkerboard (0101...), column stripes, row stripes. May use a counter, LFSR, or a small ROM.

4. **Expected Response Generator (ERG):** Computes the value that the memory should output for a given address and read operation. For March tests, this is a deterministic function of the address and the operation. For pseudo-random tests, it may use a duplicate LFSR.

5. **Comparator (XOR + OR):** Compares the actual data-out of the memory with the expected data from ERG. Any mismatch sets the fault flag.

6. **Fault Register:** A set of flip-flops that store the address and type of the first (or all) detected faults. It is read out via JTAG or test interface.

7. **BISR Controller (Optional):** Receives fault data from the Fault Register and runs redundancy algorithms to map faulty rows/columns to spare rows/columns. Common algorithms: **CRESTA**, **ESSRRA**.

8. **Clock/Reset Block:** Provides the test clock (often a slow, low-power clock to avoid IR-drop) and the test reset/init signal.

**Working Principle:** The FSM steps through each March element. For each address, the controller issues a read or write with the appropriate data. The comparator checks the read response. On completion, the Fault Register output indicates pass or fail.

---

**(b) March Test Design for SA0, SA1, TF in 4×4 SRAM (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify faults to detect**
- **SA0:** Cell stuck at 0 (fails `r1`)
- **SA1:** Cell stuck at 1 (fails `r0`)
- **TF0→1:** Cell stuck at 0 (fails 0→1 transition)
- **TF1→0:** Cell stuck at 1 (fails 1→0 transition)

Note: TF ⊃ SAF in complexity. A test detecting all TF also detects all SAF.

**Step 2: Choose March Test — MATS++**

$$\{ \Uparrow(w0);\, \Uparrow(r0, w1);\, \Downarrow(r1, w0);\, \Uparrow(r0, w1);\, \Downarrow(r1, w0) \}$$

**Step 3: Apply to 4×4 SRAM (N = 16 cells, addresses 0 to 15)**

| March Element | Operations on each address | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| M0 | w0 | ⇑ (0 → 15) |
| M1 | r0, w1 | ⇑ (0 → 15) |
| M2 | r1, w0 | ⇓ (15 → 0) |
| M3 | r0, w1 | ⇑ (0 → 15) |
| M4 | r1, w0 | ⇓ (15 → 0) |

**Step 4: Complexity Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 1N + 2N + 2N + 2N + 2N \\
&= 9N
\end{aligned}
$$

For N = 16:

$$
C = 9 \times 16 = 144 \text{ operations}
$$

**Step 5: Fault Coverage Justification**

- **SA0:** Detected by M1 (`r0` will fail if cell stuck at 1) and M3.
- **SA1:** Detected by M2 (`r1` will fail if cell stuck at 0) and M4.
- **TF0→1:** Detected by M0→M1 transition (0→1 forced by `w1`).
- **TF1→0:** Detected by M1→M2 transition (1→0 forced by `w0`).

**Final Answer:** MATS++ with complexity 9N (= 144 ops) is the minimum March test detecting all SAF and TF in the 4×4 SRAM.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the **BISR (Built-In Self-Repair)** architecture for memories. How does it improve yield? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1 Mbit SRAM (organized as 64K × 16) is tested using March C-. If the test clock is 5 ns, calculate: (i) total operations, (ii) total test time, (iii) test time when 2 data backgrounds are used. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BISR Architecture & Yield Improvement (7 Marks)**

**BISR Block Diagram (refer Figure 4.4):**

1. **MBIST Engine** → runs March test
2. **Fault Location Analyzer** → identifies failing rows/columns
3. **Fault Map Register (FMR)** → stores fault map as a bitmap
4. **Redundancy Analyzer** → runs CRESTA/ESSRRA algorithm
5. **Spare Address Decoder** → maps logical addresses to spare rows/columns
6. **Repair Configuration Memory (Fuse Box or Anti-fuse)** → non-volatile storage of repair decision

**Yield Improvement Mechanism:**

Let $p$ = probability of a single cell being defective. For an N-cell memory without redundancy:

$$
Y_{no\ repair} = (1-p)^N
$$

With R spare rows/columns (assume row repair for simplicity):

$$
Y_{repair} = \sum_{k=0}^{R} \binom{N_r}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N_r - k}
$$

where $N_r$ is the number of rows. For large memories, $Y_{repair} \gg Y_{no\ repair}$ by orders of magnitude.

**Numerical Illustration:**
- N = 10⁶ cells, p = 10⁻⁵
- $Y_{no\ repair} = (1-10^{-5})^{10^6} \approx e^{-10} \approx 0.0045\%$ (essentially zero)
- With 8 spare rows: $Y_{repair} \approx 99.99\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse the redundancy formula. The spare rows can repair **multiple** faults only if they are in **different rows**. If multiple cells in the **same row** fail, a single spare row can fix all of them.

---

**(b) Test Time Calculation for March C- on 64K×16 SRAM (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- N = 64K = 65,536 addresses
- T_clk = 5 ns
- March C- complexity = 11N operations

**Step (i): Total operations for single data background**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{total} &= 11 \times N \\
&= 11 \times 65{,}536 \\
&= 720{,}896 \text{ operations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Test time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{test} &= O_{total} \times T_{clk} \\
&= 720{,}896 \times 5 \text{ ns} \\
&= 3{,}604{,}480 \text{ ns} \\
&= 3.604 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii): With 2 data backgrounds (e.g., 0x0000 and 0xFFFF)**

For multiple data backgrounds, March C- must be re-run with the new background. Each background takes 3.604 ms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{2BG} &= 2 \times 3.604 \text{ ms} \\
&= 7.208 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative: if 2 backgrounds are interleaved in a single March C- pass**, the complexity is still 11N, but each operation now carries 2 background bits. Test time remains:

$$
T_{2BG} = 2 \times 11N \times T_{clk} = 7.208 \text{ ms}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on MBIST Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ⇑ and ⇓ symbols:** Always verify address direction. Writing `⇑` instead of `⇓` in a March element inverts the test logic.
> 2. **March test complexity arithmetic:** Count operations **per address**, not per element. A March element with 6 operations on N addresses = 6N, not 6.
> 3. **Forgetting Read Disturb Faults (RDF):** Modern March SR is required for sub-7nm nodes. Stating only March C- loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing BISR in block diagram:** Even if question asks only MBIST, examiners expect a brief mention of repair.
> 5. **Mixing CFid and CFdyn:** They are different fault classes. CFdyn requires March C- or higher.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory BIST**

- ✅ **MBIST = embedded hardware that tests memory autonomously** without external ATE.
- ✅ **Architecture = FSM + Address Gen + Data Gen + Comparator + Fault Register + (optional) BISR.**
- ✅ **March tests** are the dominant test algorithm. Notation: `r0, r1, w0, w1, ⇑, ⇓`.
- ✅ **MATS+ (5N):** SAF, AF.
- ✅ **MATS++ (6N):** SAF, AF, TF.
- ✅ **March X (12N):** CFid.
- ✅ **March Y (8N):** CFid (more efficient than March X).
- ✅ **March C- (11N):** Industry standard. CFid + CFdyn + SAF + TF + AF.
- ✅ **March SR (14N):** Adds Read Disturb Fault coverage.
- ✅ **Test time formula:** $T = C \times N \times T_{clk}$.
- ✅ **Fault models:** SAF, TF, AF, CFid, CFdyn, NPSF.
- ✅ **BISR redundancy algorithms:** CRESTA, ESSRRA — replace faulty rows/cols with spares.
- ✅ **Area overhead:** 2–5% typical. **Power:** capped to prevent IR-drop.
- ✅ **Industrial use:** Power-up MBIST, in-field periodic MBIST, automotive ASIL-D.
- ✅ **Pseudo-random MBIST:** LFSR-based — lower area, lower fault coverage.
- ✅ **Microcode MBIST:** flexible, used in SoCs with multiple memory types.
- ✅ **Hardwired MBIST:** minimal area, fixed test, used in cost-sensitive MCUs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Scan Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Boundary Scan Standard ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition The **Boundary Scan Standard**, formally codified as **IEEE 1149.1** (also popularly known as **JTAG** — Joint Test Action Group), is a standardized digital testability architecture embedded into an Integrated Circuit (IC) to facilitate efficient testing of inter-chip connections on a Printed Circuit Board (PCB) and internal sub-blocks of the chip itself. It achieves this by inserting a dedicated set of **boundary scan cells** around the periphery (boundary) of the IC's functional core, forming a shift-register chain known as the **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)**. These cells are controlled by a dedicated serial test access port called the **Test Access Port (TAP)**, which uses **4 mandatory signals** plus an optional 5th reset signal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Boundary Scan Standard is essentially a *Design-for-Testability (DFT)* technique that replaces physical "bed-of-nails" in-circuit testers with on-chip shift-register logic, dramatically reducing test cost and improving fault coverage for opens, shorts, and stuck-at faults on board-level interconnects. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a large multi-story building (your **PCB**) where every apartment (your **IC chip**) is sealed shut. Normally, an electrician would need to drill holes in every wall and wire a probe into every electrical socket to check for faults — this is the old, destructive, and expensive **bed-of-nails testing**.
