Now, suppose every apartment was built with a **secret internal wiring inspection tunnel** that loops around the inside of every wall, with checkpoints (test points) at every socket. An external inspector (the **TAP controller**) walks through this tunnel using a single entry door (**TDI**) and exits from another door (**TDO**), inspecting each checkpoint in sequence. The inspector's movement is choreographed by a walkie-talkie signal (**TMS**) timed by a clock (**TCK**). This is exactly what Boundary Scan does: - **TDI** = Entry door of the inspection tunnel - **TDO** = Exit door - **TMS** = Mode control walkie-talkie - **TCK** = Clock pacing the walk - **TRST\*** = Emergency reset button | Element | Standard Reference | Full Form | |:---|:---|:---| | **TDI** | Test Data In | Serial data input to the test chain | | **TDO** | Test Data Out | Serial data output from the test chain | | **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Controls TAP state machine transitions | | **TCK** | Test Clock | Synchronizes the test logic | | **TRST\*** | Test Reset (optional) | Asynchronous TAP controller reset | > [!NOTE] > The standard mandates **TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO** as the four compulsory pins. **TRST\*** is optional. The active-low notation (\*) indicates the reset is asserted when the signal is pulled LOW. ### 1.3 Purpose and Engineering Significance The Boundary Scan Standard addresses three critical engineering problems: 1. **Loss of Physical Access:** With Ball Grid Array (BGA), Chip-Scale Packages (CSP), and flip-chip packaging, traditional probe-based testing has become physically impossible. 2. **Interconnect Complexity:** Modern PCBs contain thousands of nets — manual testing is economically unviable. 3. **Hierarchical Testing:** Enables testing at multiple levels — chip, board, and system — using the same infrastructure. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Boundary Scan chain is **serially scannable**, meaning data is shifted bit-by-bit through a chain of cells, similar to a shift register. This serial nature is the key reason it can be accessed from only a few external pins regardless of internal complexity. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architecture of a Boundary Scan Compliant Device A Boundary Scan compliant IC consists of the following key hardware blocks: - **Test Access Port (TAP):** 4 (or 5) dedicated pins for test access. - **TAP Controller:** A 16-state finite state machine governing all test operations. - **Instruction Register (IR):** Holds the current test instruction. Minimum length is **2 bits**. - **Bypass Register (BR):** A 1-bit register that routes TDI directly to TDO, bypassing the chip. - **Boundary Scan Register (BSR):** A shift register chain formed by boundary scan cells around the I/O pins. - **Optional Registers:** Device Identification Register, USERCODE register. ### 2.2 Boundary Scan Cell — The Fundamental Unit Every functional I/O pin of the IC is equipped with a **boundary scan cell** that typically contains: - A **shift register flip-flop** (for serial shifting) - A **parallel output flip-flop** (for applying test data to the pin) - A **parallel input multiplexer** (for capturing pin data) - A **2-to-1 mode multiplexer** (selects between Normal and Test mode) The cell operates in four primary modes: - **Normal Mode:** The cell is transparent — functional data flows normally. - **Sample Mode:** The cell captures the current value on the functional pin/line. - **Preload Mode:** The cell loads a value into the parallel output flip-flop without affecting the pin. - **Extest Mode:** The parallel output drives the test value onto the external interconnect. ### 2.3 The TAP Controller — 16-State FSM The TAP controller is a synchronous FSM clocked by **TCK**, with state transitions controlled by **TMS**. The 16 states are organized into two parallel paths: **Data Register Path (DR-Path):** - Run-Test/Idle - Select-DR-Scan → Capture-DR → Shift-DR → Exit1-DR → Pause-DR → Exit2-DR → Update-DR **Instruction Register Path (IR-Path):** - Select-IR-Scan → Capture-IR → Shift-IR → Exit1-IR → Pause-IR → Exit2-IR → Update-IR **Common:** - Test-Logic-Reset ### 2.4 Mandatory and Optional Instructions (IEEE 1149.1) | Instruction | Opcode (Example) | Function | Mandatory? | |:---|:---|:---|:---:| | **BYPASS** | All 1's | Routes TDI → BR → TDO (1-bit bypass) | Yes | | **EXTEST** | 0000 | Drives BSR contents onto external pins | Yes | | **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** | 0010 | Samples pin data / Preloads BSR | Yes | | **INTEST** | 1100 | Applies tests to internal core logic | Optional | | **IDCODE** | 0001 | Shifts out 32-bit device ID | Optional | | **USERCODE** | 1110 | Shifts out user-programmable ID | Optional | | **HIGHZ** | 0111 | Places all outputs in high-impedance | Optional | | **CLAMP** | 0110 | Drives BSR onto pins, bypasses DR | Optional | | **RUNBIST** | 1000 | Activates on-chip self-test | Optional | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Critical Point:** The opcode `111...1` (all ones) is **RESERVED for BYPASS** in every compliant device. This is a standardized escape pattern ensuring that unknown devices in a chain do not block test data flow. ### 2.5 Test Modes — Board-Level and Chip-Level **Boundary Scan Test (BST) Modes:** 1. **Interconnect Test (EXTEST):** Tests wiring between ICs. Sends test patterns from one chip's BSR, captures them on the receiving chip's BSR, and compares for shorts/opens. 2. **Sample Test:** Non-intrusive — observes functional data while the system runs normally. 3. **Built-In Self-Test (BIST):** Uses internal pattern generators (PRPG) and signature analyzers (MISR) with the BSR as observation points. 4. **Cluster Test:** Tests logic clusters (non-boundary-scan ICs) connected between boundary-scan devices. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Specification | Notes | |:---|:---|:---| | Minimum TAP pins | **4 (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) + TRST\*** | TRST\* is optional | | TAP Controller states | **16 states** | Two parallel state paths | | Minimum Instruction Register width | **2 bits** | Supports at least 4 instructions | | Bypass Register length | **1 bit** | Connects TDI to TDO | | IDCODE Register length | **32 bits** | Optional but standard | | Test Clock frequency | $\leq \frac{1}{2} \cdot f_{sys}$ | TCK is typically slower than system clock | | Boundary scan cells per pin | **$\geq 1$ cell per I/O pin** | Multi-cell for bidirectional pins | | Interconnect test time | $T_{test} = N_{nets} \cdot (L_{BSR} \cdot T_{TCK})$ | $N_{nets}$ = nets, $L_{BSR}$ = chain length | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** In production board testing, the **BSR chain length** dramatically affects test time. A board with 10 ICs, each having 200 boundary scan cells, has a 2000-bit shift register — and test patterns must be shifted through it. This is why TAP clock frequency and chain partitioning are critical design decisions in real SoCs. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Performing an EXTEST Operation The **EXTEST** (External Test) is the most commonly used board-level interconnect test. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step procedure: **Step 1 — Power-On and TAP Controller Reset** Upon power-up, the TAP controller must enter the **Test-Logic-Reset** state. This is achieved by holding **TMS = 1** for at least **5 consecutive rising edges of TCK**. $$ \text{Initial State: } TMS = 1, \quad \text{for } 5 \text{ rising edges of } TCK $$ Result: TAP controller enters **Test-Logic-Reset** state, which forces all boundary scan cells into their functional (Normal) mode, ensuring the chip operates normally until a test is initiated. **Step 2 — Instruction Register Loading (Shifting in EXTEST opcode)** The TAP controller must navigate: Test-Logic-Reset → Run-Test/Idle → Select-DR-Scan → Select-IR-Scan → Capture-IR → Shift-IR → Exit1-IR → Update-IR. During the **Shift-IR** state, the **EXTEST opcode** (e.g., `0000` for a 4-bit IR) is shifted serially into the Instruction Register through TDI, synchronized to TCK. $$ \text{Shift sequence: } TMS = 0, \quad \text{for } L_{IR} \text{ TCK cycles} $$ where $L_{IR}$ is the Instruction Register length. Each TCK rising edge shifts one bit. **Step 3 — Instruction Update** When TMS transitions to drive the FSM from **Exit1-IR → Update-IR**, the value currently in the IR shift register is latched into the **IR parallel output**, which is decoded by the instruction decoder. $$ \text{IR update: } IR_{out} \leftarrow IR_{shift} $$ **Step 4 — Data Register Selection and Test Pattern Loading** The FSM navigates back to **Select-DR-Scan → Capture-DR → Shift-DR**. With EXTEST active, the decoder selects the **Boundary Scan Register (BSR)** as the active data register. The desired test vector is shifted into the BSR via TDI: $$ \text{Shift sequence: } TMS = 0, \quad \text{for } L_{BSR} \text{ TCK cycles} $$ where $L_{BSR}$ is the total length of the boundary scan chain. **Step 5 — Test Application (Update-DR)** On transition to **Update-DR**, the loaded test vector is transferred from the BSR's shift flip-flops to the BSR's **parallel output flip-flops**, which now drive the IC's external pins. $$ BSR_{out} \rightarrow \text{Pin drivers} $$ These driven values are propagated across the PCB interconnects to the input pins of the receiving IC. **Step 6 — Test Capture (Capture-DR)** The TAP controller is navigated back to **Capture-DR** state. The boundary scan cells of the receiving IC capture the values present on their input pins. $$ \text{Captured data: } BSR_{in, captured} \leftarrow \text{Pin input values} $$ **Step 7 — Result Extraction and Comparison** The captured response is shifted out through TDO while a new test vector is shifted in (overlapping operation), and the values are compared against the expected response, which is pre-computed by an **Automatic Test Pattern Generator (ATPG)** tool. $$ \text{Pass condition: } BSR_{captured} \stackrel{?}{=} BSR_{expected} $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: TAP State Transitions The following table shows the **next state** for each current state based on the TMS value at the rising edge of TCK. | Current State | TMS = 0 | TMS = 1 | |:---|:---|:---| | Test-Logic-Reset | Run-Test/Idle | Test-Logic-Reset | | Run-Test/Idle | Run-Test/Idle | Select-DR-Scan | | Select-DR-Scan | Capture-DR | Select-IR-Scan | | Capture-DR | Shift-DR | Exit1-DR | | Shift-DR | Shift-DR | Exit1-DR | | Exit1-DR | Pause-DR | Update-DR | | Pause-DR | Pause-DR | Exit2-DR | | Exit2-DR | Shift-DR | Update-DR | | Update-DR | Run-Test/Idle | Select-DR-Scan | | Select-IR-Scan | Capture-IR | Test-Logic-Reset | | Capture-IR | Shift-IR | Exit1-IR | | Shift-IR | Shift-IR | Exit1-IR | | Exit1-IR | Pause-IR | Update-IR | | Pause-IR | Pause-IR | Exit2-IR | | Exit2-IR | Shift-IR | Update-IR | | Update-IR | Run-Test/Idle | Select-DR-Scan | > [!NOTE] > **Decoding the matrix:** Reading from left → right, the columns indicate the state the FSM transitions to on the next rising TCK edge. To traverse a path, set TMS = 0 (for downward in the diagram) or TMS = 1 (for moving sideways/upward to reset states). ### 3.3 Worked Example: Time Calculation for Boundary Scan Test **Problem:** A PCB contains 4 ICs, each with a 200-bit boundary scan chain. The TAP clock is set to 10 MHz. The interconnect test uses 16 different test patterns. Calculate the total time to load and capture all patterns. **Given:** - $N_{IC} = 4$ chips - $L_{BSR,per\ chip} = 200$ bits - $f_{TCK} = 10\ MHz \Rightarrow T_{TCK} = 100\ ns$ - $N_{patterns} = 16$ **Step 1 — Total chain length** $$ L_{total} = N_{IC} \cdot L_{BSR,per\ chip} $$ $$ L_{total} = 4 \cdot 200 = 800\ bits $$ **Step 2 — Time per pattern shift (one full chain scan)** $$ T_{shift} = L_{total} \cdot T_{TCK} $$ $$ T_{shift} = 800 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}\ s = 80\ \mu s $$ **Step 3 — Time per pattern (shift-in + update + capture + shift-out)** For simplicity, assuming the shift-in and shift-out overlap, the dominant cost is one full chain scan per pattern. $$ T_{pattern} = T_{shift} + T_{update/capture} \approx T_{shift} = 80\ \mu s $$ **Step 4 — Total test time for all patterns** $$ T_{total} = N_{patterns} \cdot T_{pattern} $$ $$ T_{total} = 16 \cdot 80\ \mu s = 1280\ \mu s = 1.28\ ms $$ **Result:** The total interconnect test time is **1.28 ms**, demonstrating the efficiency of boundary scan for high-speed production testing. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TAP Controller State Machine (Mermaid) The following Mermaid diagram renders the **16-state TAP Controller FSM** as a directed graph, showing transitions on TMS = 0 and TMS = 1. ```mermaid graph TD resetState[Test Logic Reset]:::reset idle[Run Test Idle] selDR[Select DR Scan] capDR[Capture DR] shiftDR[Shift DR] exit1DR[Exit1 DR] pauseDR[Pause DR] exit2DR[Exit2 DR] updDR[Update DR] selIR[Select IR Scan] capIR[Capture IR] shiftIR[Shift IR] exit1IR[Exit1 IR] pauseIR[Pause IR] exit2IR[Exit2 IR] updIR[Update IR] resetState -- TMS 0 --> idle resetState -- TMS 1 --> resetState idle -- TMS 0 --> idle idle -- TMS 1 --> selDR selDR -- TMS 0 --> capDR selDR -- TMS 1 --> selIR capDR -- TMS 0 --> shiftDR capDR -- TMS 1 --> exit1DR shiftDR -- TMS 0 --> shiftDR shiftDR -- TMS 1 --> exit1DR exit1DR -- TMS 0 --> pauseDR exit1DR -- TMS 1 --> updDR pauseDR -- TMS 0 --> pauseDR pauseDR -- TMS 1 --> exit2DR exit2DR -- TMS 0 --> shiftDR exit2DR -- TMS 1 --> updDR updDR -- TMS 0 --> idle updDR -- TMS 1 --> selDR selIR -- TMS 0 --> capIR selIR -- TMS 1 --> resetState capIR -- TMS 0 --> shiftIR capIR -- TMS 1 --> exit1IR shiftIR -- TMS 0 --> shiftIR shiftIR -- TMS 1 --> exit1IR exit1IR -- TMS 0 --> pauseIR exit1IR -- TMS 1 --> updIR pauseIR -- TMS 0 --> pauseIR pauseIR -- TMS 1 --> exit2IR exit2IR -- TMS 0 --> shiftIR exit2IR -- TMS 1 --> updIR updIR -- TMS 0 --> idle updIR -- TMS 1 --> selDR classDef reset fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Boundary Scan Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TB TAP[Test Access Port
TCK TMS TDI TDO TRST_N]:::tap TAPCtrl[TAP Controller
16-State FSM]:::ctrl IR[Instruction Register]:::ir IRDec[Instruction Decoder]:::dec BR[Bypass Register
1-bit]:::br BSR[Boundary Scan Register
Chain of cells]:::bsr IDR[Device ID Register
Optional]:::idr Core[Functional Core Logic]:::core Pins[IC I/O Pins]:::pins TAP --> TAPCtrl TAPCtrl --> IR TAPCtrl --> BR TAPCtrl --> BSR TAPCtrl --> IDR IR --> IRDec IRDec -.Selects active DR.-> BR IRDec -.Selects active DR.-> BSR IRDec -.Selects active DR.-> IDR BSR <--> Pins Pins <--> Core classDef tap fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px classDef ctrl fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px classDef ir fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:2px classDef dec fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px classDef br fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px classDef bsr fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#0000cc,stroke-width:2px classDef idr fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px classDef core fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px classDef pins fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Boundary Scan Cell Internal Structure ```mermaid graph LR SI[Serial In TDI]:::in SO[Serial Out]:::out PI[Parallel In
From Core]:::in PO[Parallel Out
To Pin]:::out PININ[Pin Input Value]:::in MODE[Mode Select
Normal/Sample/Extest]:::ctrl MUX1[2:1 MUX]:::mux MUX2[2:1 MUX]:::mux SRFF[Shift Register FF]:::ff POFF[Parallel Output FF]:::ff OC[Output Cell
3-state driver]:::out SI --> SRFF SRFF --> MUX1 PI --> MUX1 MUX1 --> POFF POFF --> MUX2 POFF --> OC PININ --> SRFF MUX2 --> SO MODE -.Controls.-> MUX1 MODE -.Controls.-> MUX2 MODE -.Controls.-> OC POFF --> PO OC --> PO classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px classDef out fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px classDef ctrl fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:1px classDef mux fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:1px classDef ff fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#0000cc,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.4 PCB-Level Interconnect Test Flow ```mermaid graph LR A[IC1 BSR
Drives pattern]:::bsr B[PCB Net
Interconnect]:::net C[IC2 BSR
Captures response]:::bsr D[Test Controller
Compares results]:::ctrl A -- Test Vector --> B B -- Response --> C C -- Captured Data --> D D -- Pass/Fail --> E[Result] classDef bsr fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#0000cc,stroke-width:2px classDef net fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px classDef ctrl fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Boundary Scan. List the four mandatory pins of the Test Access Port (TAP).** **Model Answer:** **Boundary Scan** is a Design-for-Testability technique standardized under **IEEE 1149.1** that integrates test logic (boundary scan cells) around the periphery of an IC to enable testing of inter-chip interconnects on a PCB without requiring physical probe access. The **four mandatory TAP pins** are: 1. **TCK** — Test Clock 2. **TMS** — Test Mode Select 3. **TDI** — Test Data Input 4. **TDO** — Test Data Output (One additional optional pin **TRST\*** provides asynchronous reset.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the function of the BYPASS instruction in the Boundary Scan standard? Why is its opcode standardized as all 1's?** **Model Answer:** The **BYPASS** instruction selects the 1-bit Bypass Register, creating a direct serial path from **TDI to TDO**, effectively removing the chip from the boundary scan chain. This is essential when testing other devices in a multi-chip chain, as it drastically reduces the shift time (from $L_{BSR}$ to just 1 bit per bypassed chip). The opcode is standardized as **all 1's** (e.g., `1111` for a 4-bit IR) because this is the default value loaded into the Instruction Register upon power-up or TAP reset. This guarantees that any unknown or non-compliant device in a chain will default to BYPASS mode, preventing it from blocking the test data flow. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing the IR and DR scan paths in the TAP state machine, (2) failing to mention that **EXTEST is for inter-chip interconnect testing** (not internal logic), and (3) omitting the role of **TRST\*** or stating it as mandatory. --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Boundary Scan compliant IC with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Instruction Register, Bypass Register, and Boundary Scan Register. **(b) [7 Marks)** With the help of a state diagram, explain the operation of the TAP controller. List any four states from the data register path and instruction register path. --- **Model Solution for (a):** A Boundary Scan compliant IC consists of the following key architectural blocks: 1. **Test Access Port (TAP):** 4 (or 5) dedicated pins (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, optional TRST\*) that serve as the only test interface. 2. **TAP Controller:** A synchronous finite state machine with **16 states** that decodes TMS and TCK signals to control all test operations. 3. **Instruction Register (IR):** A shift register (minimum 2 bits) that holds the current test instruction. The IR has a **shift portion** (for serial loading) and a **parallel output portion** (latched during Update-IR). It is decoded by the **Instruction Decoder** to select which data register is active. 4. **Data Registers:** - **Boundary Scan Register (BSR):** A serial chain of boundary scan cells, one per functional I/O pin. It is used to capture functional data (SAMPLE) or apply test data to external interconnects (EXTEST). - **Bypass Register (BR):** A 1-bit register that provides a short path from TDI to TDO when the chip is not being tested. - **Device Identification Register (optional):** A 32-bit register containing the manufacturer's ID, part number, and version. 5. **Boundary Scan Cells:** Inserted between the functional core logic and the I/O pins, these cells contain multiplexers and flip-flops to switch between normal and test modes. *(A neat block diagram showing the above should be drawn — refer to Section 4.2.)* **Model Solution for (b):** The **TAP Controller** is a 16-state finite state machine, synchronously clocked by TCK, with state transitions governed by TMS. The states are organized into two parallel paths (data register path and instruction register path), connected at the top and bottom. **Data Register Path States:** - **Select-DR-Scan:** Decides whether to enter DR or IR scan. - **Capture-DR:** Loads parallel data into the selected DR's shift register. - **Shift-DR:** Serial shifting of data through TDI to TDO. - **Update-DR:** Transfers the shifted data to the DR's parallel output. **Instruction Register Path States:** - **Select-IR-Scan:** Entry point to the IR scan path. - **Capture-IR:** Loads the default instruction (all 1's = BYPASS) into the IR. - **Shift-IR:** Serial shifting of new instruction opcode into IR. - **Update-IR:** Transfers the new instruction to the IR's parallel output, activating the selected DR. **Other Important States:** Test-Logic-Reset (forces normal operation), Run-Test/Idle (idle state). *(Refer to the state diagram in Section 4.1 for the complete FSM.)* --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the EXTEST instruction in detail. Describe how it is used to detect opens and shorts on PCB interconnects. **(b) [7 Marks]** With an example, explain the SAMPLE/PRELOAD and INTEST instructions. Why is the all-1's opcode reserved for BYPASS? --- **Model Solution for (a):** **EXTEST (External Test)** is the primary instruction used for **board-level interconnect testing**. It allows the boundary scan cells of one IC to drive test patterns onto the PCB nets, which are then captured by the boundary scan cells of the receiving IC. **Procedure:** 1. **Instruction Loading:** The EXTEST opcode (e.g., `0000`) is shifted into the IR. 2. **Test Vector Application:** A test pattern is shifted into the BSR of the driving IC, then transferred to the parallel output flip-flops (during Update-DR). These outputs drive the PCB interconnects. 3. **Response Capture:** The BSR of the receiving IC captures the values arriving at its input pins (during Capture-DR). 4. **Response Analysis:** The captured response is shifted out through TDO and compared with the expected (fault-free) response. **Fault Detection:** - **Short Faults (Bridging Faults):** If two adjacent nets are shorted, the driving values from both nets will appear simultaneously at the receiving input. The captured response will deviate from the expected pattern. - **Open Faults:** If a net is broken, the receiving input will float to an indeterminate value (or a stuck-at value depending on termination), again deviating from the expected pattern. - **Stuck-At Faults:** If a net is permanently shorted to VDD or GND, the captured value will be a constant 1 or 0 regardless of the driven value. **Model Solution for (b):** **SAMPLE/PRELOAD Instruction:** - **SAMPLE Mode:** The boundary scan cell captures (samples) the current value on the functional pin or internal core line *without disturbing normal operation*. This is used for non-intrusive debugging and observation of system behavior. - **PRELOAD Mode:** The boundary scan cell loads a value into its parallel output flip-flop *without driving it onto the pin yet*. This preloads a known initial state into the BSR so that the chip enters the next test (e.g., EXTEST) with a defined starting condition. **INTEST Instruction:** - The **INTEST** instruction allows testing of the **internal core logic** of the IC. Test patterns are applied to the internal core via the BSR (driven from the parallel output flip-flops), and the responses are captured back into the BSR for shifting out. This is useful for **chip-level testing** without external test equipment. **Why All-1's Opcode for BYPASS:** The all-1's pattern is reserved for BYPASS because: 1. **Power-On Default:** When the IR is reset (Test-Logic-Reset state), it loads the all-1's pattern. This ensures that any device — known or unknown — defaults to BYPASS mode at startup, preventing interference in a chain containing multiple devices. 2. **Fault Tolerance:** In a long boundary scan chain, if one device's IR is corrupted, it will likely end up in BYPASS, allowing the rest of the chain to be tested. 3. **Standardization:** Ensures interoperability between ICs from different manufacturers. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boundary Scan Standard (IEEE 1149.1)** - **Full Name:** IEEE 1149.1 Standard Test Access Port and Boundary Scan Architecture (popularly called **JTAG**). - **Mandatory TAP Pins (4):** TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO. **Optional:** TRST\*. - **TAP Controller States:** **16** — arranged as 2 parallel paths (DR-path and IR-path) plus Test-Logic-Reset and Run-Test/Idle. - **Boundary Scan Cell Modes:** Normal, Sample, Preload, Extest (and Intest for internal logic). - **Mandatory Instructions:** BYPASS (all-1's opcode), EXTEST, SAMPLE/PRELOAD. - **Bypass Register Length:** **1 bit** — minimizes test time when chip is not under test. - **Instruction Register Minimum Width:** **2 bits**. - **EXTEST** tests **inter-chip interconnects** on a PCB. - **INTEST** tests **internal core logic** of the IC. - **SAMPLE** is **non-intrusive** — does not disturb normal system operation. - **All-1's Opcode = BYPASS** — universal default for chain compatibility and fault tolerance. - **TCK** operates **asynchronously** to the system clock — test logic is decoupled from functional logic. - **TAP Reset:** Hold TMS = 1 for **5 rising edges of TCK** to enter Test-Logic-Reset. - **Architecture Blocks:** TAP, TAP Controller, IR, BSR, BR, optional IDCODE register. - **Real-World Use:** Replaces "bed-of-nails" testing for BGAs, CSPs, and high-density boards. - **Test Time:** Dominated by shift operations; $T_{test} \propto L_{BSR} \cdot T_{TCK} \cdot N_{patterns}$. - **TCK Tristate:** TDO is high-impedance except during the Shift-DR and Shift-IR states. ---

topics.title: Analog Test Bus Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT635 - VLSI VERIFICATION & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analog Test Bus Standard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Analog Test Bus Standard** refers formally to the **IEEE 1149.4 Standard for a Mixed-Signal Test Bus**. It is a superset of the digital boundary-scan standard (IEEE 1149.1) that extends the scan-based testing paradigm into the analog and mixed-signal domain. The standard defines a standardized architecture, comprising dedicated analog pins, an **Analog Test Access Port (ATAP)**, **Test Bus Interface Circuits (TBICs)**, and **Analog Boundary Modules (ABMs)**, to facilitate the testing of both **interconnects** (parametric faults like opens and shorts) and **parametric analog components** (resistors, capacitors, inductors, and active device parameters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The Analog Test Bus (IEEE 1149.4) bridges the gap between purely digital scan testing and the real-world analog nature of most SoC (System-on-Chip) pins. It enables **DC parametric testing** of analog pins via a minimum of **two analog test pins (AT1 and AT2)** without requiring expensive external analog test equipment to be directly connected to every node.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large apartment complex with hundreds of rooms (the chip pins). A regular postman (digital boundary scan, IEEE 1149.1) can only deliver letters (digital 1s and 0s) to verify if doors are locked or unlocked. He cannot check the water pressure in the showers, gas leaks, or electrical current in the wiring.

The **Analog Test Bus (IEEE 1149.4)** acts like a **specialized maintenance engineer** equipped with a small toolkit who travels along a dedicated corridor (the analog test bus). Using just two master pipes (AT1 and AT2), the engineer can:

1. **Inject** a precise test current or voltage.
2. **Route** it to any specific pin in the complex using internal switches.
3. **Sense** the resulting voltage or current from a different pin.
4. **Diagnose** whether the "plumbing" (interconnect wires) is intact, shorted, or broken, and also measure the "health" of components (resistance, capacitance) connected to those wires.

In short, the standard converts complex analog measurements into a simple, digital-controllable switching matrix that can be driven by an inexpensive Automatic Test Equipment (ATE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Architectural Constants / Metrics:**
> - **Pins Added to Digital TAP:** **2 dedicated analog test pins** (AT1, AT2) + **1 optional pin (ATN)** for internal analog testing.
> - **Internal Analog Bus Lines:** Exactly **2 lines** (AB1, AB2) routed throughout the chip.
> - **Maximum DC Test Voltage:** Limited to standard supply rails (typically **±5 V to ±15 V** depending on process).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of IEEE 1149.4

The IEEE 1149.4 architecture is built around four primary hardware components that work synergistically to enable mixed-signal testability.

#### 2.1.1 Test Bus Interface Circuit (TBIC)
The TBIC is the "gateway" between the external world and the internal analog bus. It resides near the chip pins (AT1 and AT2) and is responsible for:
- **Connecting/Disconnecting** the internal analog bus (AB1, AB2) to the external AT1/AT2 pins based on boundary-scan control signals.
- **Providing a high-impedance state** during normal operation so that it does not load the analog circuits.
- **Supporting differential measurements** by taking inputs from AT1 and routing them to AB1/AB2.

#### 2.1.2 Analog Boundary Module (ABM)
The ABM is the per-pin analog equivalent of the digital boundary-scan cell. Each analog I/O pin has an ABM that contains switches to:
- **Disconnect** the analog pin from the internal core logic (Functional mode).
- **Connect** the analog pin to the internal analog bus AB1 or AB2 (Test mode).

#### 2.1.3 Internal Analog Bus (AB1, AB2)
Two dedicated analog wires routed across the entire chip. They are controlled by the ABM switches and the TBIC. They carry:
- **DC test stimuli** for parametric tests.
- **Sensed analog responses** back to the TBIC.

#### 2.1.4 TAP (Test Access Port) Controller
The standard IEEE 1149.1 TAP controller (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST*) is reused to drive both digital and analog test logic. New instructions like `EXTEST`, `INTEST`, `RUNBIST`, and analog-specific instructions (like `PROBE`) are decoded.

### 2.2 Operational Modes of the Test Bus

| Mode | Function | Bus State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal Mode** | Chip operates functionally; test bus is invisible. | AT1, AT2, AB1, AB2 are **high-impedance**. |
| **DC Interconnect Test** | Detects shorts/opens between pins. | ABMs connect pins to AB1/AB2 to measure resistance. |
| **DC Parametric Test** | Measures component values (R, C). | TBIC drives known current/voltage; senses response. |
| **Functional Test** | Standard digital boundary scan (1149.1). | Only digital boundary cells are active. |

### 2.3 Why and How the Architecture Works

**The "Why":** Pure digital scan can only detect logic faults (stuck-at-0, stuck-at-1). It cannot detect an analog pin being shorted to VDD with a **50 Ω resistance**, or a capacitor that has lost 60% of its nominal value. These **analog parametric faults** are dominant in modern mixed-signal ICs.

**The "How":** The IEEE 1149.4 standard reduces the massive **test point problem**. Without it, an ATE would need a physical analog probe for every single pin (often 100s of pins). With 1149.4, only the two AT1/AT2 pins need to be contacted, and **internal analog switches (transmission gates)** route the signal internally — a concept known as **test resource partitioning**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation, symbol, boundary condition, and unit you must memorize for KTU ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Symbol Description | Boundary Condition | Engineering Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ohmic Test Current Injection | $I_{test} = \dfrac{V_{AT1} - V_{AT2}}{R_{load} + R_{AB}}$ | $V_{AT1}, V_{AT2}$: Test pin voltages; $R_{load}$: Pin-under-test resistance; $R_{AB}$: Analog bus parasitic resistance | $R_{load} \gg R_{AB}$ for accuracy | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | Sensed Differential Voltage | $V_{sense} = V_{AB1} - V_{AB2}$ | Voltage drop measured across the Device-Under-Test (DUT) | $\vert V_{sense} \vert \le V_{supply}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Measured Resistance (DUT) | $R_{DUT} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{test}}$ | Effective resistance of interconnect/component | Excludes $R_{AB}$ if compensated | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 4 | Bus Capacitance Time Constant | $\tau = R_{sw} \cdot C_{AB}$ | $R_{sw}$: Switch ON-resistance; $C_{AB}$: Bus line capacitance | $\tau \ll T_{test}$ for DC assumption | Seconds (s) |
| 5 | Interconnect Short Detection Threshold | $\Delta R = R_{shorted} - R_{nominal}$ | Change in resistance between adjacent pins | $\vert \Delta R \vert \ge R_{th}$ (threshold) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | Attofarad Resolution (Cap Test) | $C_{min} = \dfrac{I_{test}}{2\pi f \cdot V_{sense}}$ | Minimum detectable capacitance via AC excitation | $f$ = test frequency (typ. 10 kHz–100 kHz) | Farads (F) |
| 7 | Boundary Scan Path Length | $L_{scan} = N_{cells} + 1$ | $N_{cells}$: Number of digital + analog boundary cells | Includes BSR + Bypass register | Bits |
| 8 | Test Coverage Estimation | $C_{test} \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{N_{test\_points}}$ | Empirical coverage gain per analog test point | $N_{test\_points} \ge 4$ | Dimensionless ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> The IEEE 1149.4 standard is widely deployed in **automotive MCUs**, **audio codecs**, **power management ICs (PMICs)**, and **RF transceivers**. By unifying analog and digital test access, it slashes ATE test time (which costs roughly **\$0.05–\$0.20 per second** in production) and enables **in-system self-test** during board-level burn-in.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DC Parametric Resistance Measurement Using the 1149.4 Bus

The goal of this derivation is to show how a **single external measurement** at AT1/AT2 yields the resistance of a component located deep inside the chip, despite the presence of bus parasitics.

#### Step 1 — Setup of the Equivalent Circuit
Consider a pin $P_x$ connected through its ABM to internal bus line AB1. The current flows from external pin AT1 → internal bus AB1 → ABM switch → Pin $P_x$ → external pin $P_y$ → AB2 → AT2.

The total path resistance is:

$$
R_{total} = R_{AT1} + R_{AB1} + R_{sw,ABM} + R_{DUT} + R_{sw,ABM} + R_{AB2} + R_{AT2}
$$

> *Explanation:* This is a simple series resistance model where each physical segment (bond wire, bus trace, MOS switch, and the Device-Under-Test) contributes an additive resistive term.

#### Step 2 — Apply Known Test Current
The ATE forces a known precision current $I_{test}$ between AT1 and AT2. The current is identical through every element in the series loop (Kirchhoff's Current Law).

$$
I_{AT1} = I_{AB1} = I_{sw} = I_{DUT} = I_{sw} = I_{AB2} = I_{AT2} = I_{test}
$$

> *Explanation:* Because the loop is purely series, the injected current acts as a constant current source stimulus, unaffected by intermediate resistance values.

#### Step 3 — Measure the Sensed Voltage
The TBIC differentially senses the voltage between AB1 and AB2. The ABM switches are configured such that the sensed voltage drops across the **DUT and any interconnect in series with it**.

$$
V_{sense} = V_{AB1} - V_{AB2}
$$

#### Step 4 — Decompose the Sensed Voltage
Applying Ohm's law to the sensed section:

$$
V_{sense} = I_{test} \cdot (R_{DUT} + R_{interconnect})
$$

> *Explanation:* The voltage at AB1 is the voltage at AT1 minus the drop across the bus and switch. The voltage at AB2 is the voltage at AT2 plus the drop on the return path. The bus and switch drops cancel out because of differential sensing.

#### Step 5 — Solve for the DUT Resistance
Rearranging for the unknown:

$$
R_{DUT} + R_{interconnect} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{test}}
$$

$$
R_{DUT} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{test}} - R_{interconnect,known}
$$

> *Explanation:* By calibrating the known interconnect resistance (e.g., a bonding wire is typically 50 mΩ to 200 mΩ), the actual DUT resistance is extracted. This is the **virtual removal** of bus parasitics — a key advantage of differential sensing in IEEE 1149.4.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Test Mode vs. Functional Mode

The following matrix explicitly compares the state of every critical signal during the three most important modes of operation. This is a **high-frequency KTU question** type.

| Signal / Node | Functional Mode | DC Interconnect Test | DC Parametric Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AT1 Pin** | High-Z (floating) | Driven by ATE ($V_{source}$) | Driven by ATE ($I_{source}$) |
| **AT2 Pin** | High-Z (floating) | Connected to ATE ground/return | Connected to ATE voltmeter |
| **AB1 Bus** | High-Z | Routed via ABM to pin under test | Routed via ABM to DUT terminal 1 |
| **AB2 Bus** | High-Z | Routed via ABM to adjacent pin | Routed via ABM to DUT terminal 2 |
| **ABM Switch State** | Open (disconnected) | Closed on selected pins | Closed on selected pins |
| **TBIC Mode** | Transparent (disabled) | Differential voltage sense | Differential current/voltage sense |
| **TAP Controller** | Free-running or Reset | In `EXTEST` or `PROBE` instruction | In custom analog instruction |
| **Core Logic** | Active and operating | Isolated from pins (boundary cells disconnect) | Isolated; only test path active |

### 3.3 Numerical Verification Example

**Problem Statement:** A chip implements IEEE 1149.4. A precision current source forces **$I_{test} = 100 \, \mu A$** through pin pair (P1, P2). The TBIC measures **$V_{sense} = 4.85 \, mV$**. The known interconnect (trace + bondwire) between the ABM and the pin pad has $R_{interconnect} = 8.5 \, \Omega$. Find the resistance of the DUT.

**Solution:**

$$
R_{total} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{test}} = \dfrac{4.85 \times 10^{-3} \, V}{100 \times 10^{-6} \, A} = 48.5 \, \Omega
$$

$$
R_{DUT} = R_{total} - R_{interconnect} = 48.5 \, \Omega - 8.5 \, \Omega = 40.0 \, \Omega
$$

> *Interpretation:* The component under test is exactly **40 Ω**, with the bus parasitics successfully subtracted out by differential measurement.

### 3.4 Derivation: AC Parametric (Capacitance) Test Principle

For capacitive measurements, an AC stimulus is required. The bus injects a sinusoidal current at frequency $f$. The complex impedance of the DUT is:

$$
Z_{DUT}(f) = R_{DUT} + \dfrac{1}{j \cdot 2\pi f \cdot C_{DUT}}
$$

The magnitude of the sensed voltage is:

$$
\vert V_{sense}(f) \vert = I_{test} \cdot \sqrt{R_{DUT}^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{2\pi f \cdot C_{DUT}}\right)^2}
$$

By sweeping $f$ and fitting the response, $C_{DUT}$ can be extracted down to **attofarad ($10^{-18}$ F)** resolution, which is essential for testing MEMS devices and on-chip filter capacitors.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of IEEE 1149.4

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the **TAP Controller**, **TBIC**, **Analog Bus**, and **ABM cells** interact during a parametric test. This is rendered using safe alphanumeric node identifiers and clean uppercase labels to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["ATE AUTOMATIC TEST EQUIPMENT"] --> nodeB["AT1 AT2 EXTERNAL PINS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TBIC TEST BUS INTERFACE CIRCUIT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["AB1 AB2 INTERNAL ANALOG BUS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ABM 1 PIN P1 BOUNDARY MODULE"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["ABM 2 PIN P2 BOUNDARY MODULE"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["ABM 3 PIN P3 BOUNDARY MODULE"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["DUT DEVICE UNDER TEST"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI["DISCRETE COMPONENT R OR C"]
    nodeJ["IEEE 1149.1 TAP CONTROLLER TCK TMS TDI TDO"] --> nodeC
    nodeJ --> nodeE
    nodeJ --> nodeF
    nodeJ --> nodeG
    nodeK["BOUNDARY SCAN REGISTER BSR"] --> nodeE
    nodeK --> nodeF
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeL["ANALOG TEST INSTRUCTION REGISTER"] --> nodeJ
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **ATE** injects stimulus via two pins AT1/AT2.
- The **TBIC** receives this stimulus and routes it onto the **internal analog bus (AB1/AB2)**.
- The **TAP controller** (IEEE 1149.1) commands each **ABM** to open or close its switches.
- Closed ABMs connect specific pins (P1, P2, P3) to the bus, allowing current to flow through the **DUT**.
- The voltage drop is differentially sensed and returned to the ATE through the reverse path.

### 4.2 Functional Mode vs. Test Mode State Transition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NORMAL_MODE
    NORMAL_MODE --> TEST_RESET : TRST ASSERTED
    TEST_RESET --> DC_INTERCONNECT_TEST : INSTRUCTION EXTEST
    TEST_RESET --> DC_PARAMETRIC_TEST : INSTRUCTION PROBE MEASURE
    TEST_RESET --> AC_PARAMETRIC_TEST : INSTRUCTION AC_TEST
    DC_INTERCONNECT_TEST --> TEST_RESET : UPDATE_DR
    DC_PARAMETRIC_TEST --> TEST_RESET : UPDATE_DR
    AC_PARAMETRIC_TEST --> TEST_RESET : UPDATE_DR
    TEST_RESET --> NORMAL_MODE : TEST COMPLETE
```

### 4.3 Internal Switch Realization within an Analog Boundary Module (ABM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePad["PIN PAD"] --> nodeSw1["TRANSMISSION GATE TG1"]
    nodeSw1 --> nodeCore["INTERNAL CORE"]
    nodeSw1 -.Switch Control.-> nodeCtrl["ABM CONTROL FROM BSR"]
    nodePad --> nodeSw2["TRANSMISSION GATE TG2"]
    nodeSw2 --> nodeAB1["AB1 INTERNAL BUS"]
    nodeSw2 -.Switch Control.-> nodeCtrl
    nodeCore --> nodeSw3["TRANSMISSION GATE TG3"]
    nodeSw3 --> nodeAB2["AB2 INTERNAL BUS"]
    nodeSw3 -.Switch Control.-> nodeCtrl
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each ABM contains **three transmission gates (TG1, TG2, TG3)**. TG1 isolates the core from the pad. TG2 connects the pad to AB1. TG3 connects the core to AB2 (for internal node probing without touching the pad). This tri-state arrangement provides maximum flexibility for both interconnect and parametric testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the IEEE 1149.4 standard. Mention any two key advantages it offers over the IEEE 1149.1 digital boundary scan standard.*

**Model Answer:**
The **IEEE 1149.4** is a mixed-signal test bus standard that extends the digital boundary-scan architecture (IEEE 1149.1) to support testing of analog and mixed-signal components. It introduces an internal **2-wire analog bus (AB1, AB2)** controlled by **Analog Boundary Modules (ABMs)** and a **Test Bus Interface Circuit (TBIC)** connected to external pins **AT1 and AT2**.

**Key advantages over 1149.1:**
1. Enables **DC and AC parametric testing** of analog components (R, L, C) and interconnects, which pure digital scan cannot detect.
2. Reduces **ATE pin count** drastically — only two analog test pins are needed regardless of how many analog pins the chip has.
3. Supports **differential measurement** that virtually eliminates bus parasitic resistance errors.

*(Any two clearly stated advantages fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of an Analog Boundary Module (ABM) in the IEEE 1149.4 architecture? Briefly explain its functional states.*

**Model Answer:**
An **Analog Boundary Module (ABM)** is the per-pin analog equivalent of a digital boundary-scan cell. It contains analog switches (typically CMOS transmission gates) that selectively connect each chip pin to the internal analog bus lines (AB1 and AB2).

**Functional States:**
1. **Transparent (Normal) Mode:** All switches are open. The ABM does not load the analog pin; the chip operates normally.
2. **Interconnect Test Mode:** Switches close to route the pin onto AB1 or AB2 for short/open detection with neighboring pins.
3. **Parametric Measure Mode:** Switches close to inject current through a discrete component (resistor/capacitor) connected to the pin and sense the resulting voltage differentially.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the architecture of the **IEEE 1149.4 Mixed-Signal Test Bus**. Clearly identify the roles of the **TBIC, ATAP, AB1/AB2 bus, and ABMs**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A precision current source applies **$I_{test} = 250 \, \mu A$** between AT1 and AT2 in an IEEE 1149.4 test setup. The TBIC measures a differential voltage of **$V_{sense} = 12.0 \, mV$** across the device under test. The bond-wire + trace resistance between the ABM and the pin pad is known to be **$R_{interconnect} = 6.0 \, \Omega$**.
   (i) Compute the total measured resistance.
   (ii) Compute the actual DUT resistance after parasitic compensation.
   (iii) If the test threshold for a "pass" is **$R_{DUT} \in [45 \, \Omega, 55 \, \Omega]$**, decide whether the DUT passes. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Architecture Explanation (7 marks):**

The IEEE 1149.4 architecture comprises the following blocks:

1. **Test Access Port (TAP):** Reuses the IEEE 1149.1 pins — TCK (Test Clock), TMS (Test Mode Select), TDI (Test Data In), TDO (Test Data Out), and optional TRST*. The TAP controller is a 16-state finite state machine that sequences test instructions.

2. **Analog Test Access Port (ATAP):** Two dedicated external analog pins **AT1 and AT2** (plus optional ATN) serve as the gateway between the ATE and the internal analog bus.

3. **Test Bus Interface Circuit (TBIC):** Located near the AT1/AT2 pins. It contains analog switches that connect/disconnect the external pins to/from the internal analog bus AB1/AB2 based on TAP commands. During functional mode, TBIC presents high impedance.

4. **Internal Analog Bus (AB1, AB2):** Two analog wires routed throughout the entire chip, providing a uniform test infrastructure.

5. **Analog Boundary Modules (ABMs):** One per analog (or mixed-signal) pin. Each ABM contains three transmission gates that can isolate the pin from the core (TG1), connect the pin to AB1 (TG2), or connect the core to AB2 (TG3).

6. **Instruction Register and BSR:** The BSR is extended to control both digital and analog cells.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total measured resistance:**

$$
R_{total} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{test}} = \dfrac{12.0 \times 10^{-3} \, V}{250 \times 10^{-6} \, A}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 48.0 \, \Omega
$$

**(ii) Actual DUT resistance:**

$$
R_{DUT} = R_{total} - R_{interconnect} = 48.0 \, \Omega - 6.0 \, \Omega = 42.0 \, \Omega
$$

**(iii) Pass/Fail Decision:**

The pass window is $[45 \, \Omega, 55 \, \Omega]$. Since $R_{DUT} = 42.0 \, \Omega < 45 \, \Omega$, the DUT **FAILS** the parametric test (the resistance is below the lower limit, indicating a possible short or a manufacturing defect that lowered the resistance).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **IEEE 1149.1** and **IEEE 1149.4** standards in terms of test capability, pin overhead, and applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **DC interconnect test procedure** using the IEEE 1149.4 bus. Show mathematically how a **short between two adjacent pins** is detected. *(7 marks)*

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Differentiation Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | IEEE 1149.1 (Digital) | IEEE 1149.4 (Mixed-Signal) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test Capability** | Stuck-at fault detection on digital nets only. | Detects digital faults **plus** analog shorts/opens and parametric component values. |
| **External Pin Overhead** | 4 mandatory (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) + optional TRST*. | 4 mandatory digital + **2 analog (AT1, AT2)** + optional ATN. |
| **Internal Bus** | None — uses boundary scan cells only. | **2 internal analog wires (AB1, AB2)** routed across the chip. |
| **Cell Type** | Digital Boundary Scan Cell (BSC). | Analog Boundary Module (ABM) with transmission gates. |
| **Stimulus Type** | Digital logic levels (0 / 1). | DC current/voltage **and** AC sinusoidal stimuli. |
| **Application Domain** | Purely digital ICs, microprocessors. | Mixed-signal SoCs, audio, RF, automotive, MEMS. |
| **Measurement Resolution** | Logic-level (binary). | Down to **attofarads** ($10^{-18}$ F) and milliohms. |
| **Standard Year** | 1990 (initial); revised 2001, 2013. | 1999; revised 2010. |

**(b) DC Interconnect Test Procedure (7 marks):**

**Procedure Steps:**

1. **Enter Test Mode:** Apply the `EXTEST` or analog-specific instruction via the TAP controller. All functional core logic is isolated.
2. **Configure ABMs:** The boundary-scan register is loaded with a pattern that closes the ABM switches of two adjacent pins (say P1 and P2), connecting P1 to AB1 and P2 to AB2.
3. **Inject Current:** The ATE forces a small DC current $I_{test}$ (typically 1 mA) through AT1 → AB1 → P1 → interconnect → P2 → AB2 → AT2.
4. **Measure Voltage:** The TBIC measures $V_{sense} = V_{AB1} - V_{AB2}$.
5. **Compute Resistance:**

$$
R_{interconnect} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{test}}
$$

6. **Compare with Threshold:**
   - **Normal:** $R_{interconnect} \approx R_{trace,nominal}$ (e.g., a few ohms).
   - **Open Circuit:** $R_{interconnect} \to \infty$ (no current flows, sense voltage saturates).
   - **Short to Adjacent Pin:** $R_{interconnect}$ is anomalously low because current bypasses the trace via the short.

**Mathematical Detection of a Short:**

Suppose a parasitic short of resistance $R_{short}$ bridges pins P1 and P2 (in addition to the intentional trace $R_{trace}$). The equivalent parallel resistance is:

$$
R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_{trace} \cdot R_{short}}{R_{trace} + R_{short}}
$$

If $R_{short} \ll R_{trace}$, then $R_{eq} \approx R_{short}$. The measured voltage drops sharply:

$$
V_{sense,short} = I_{test} \cdot R_{eq} \approx I_{test} \cdot R_{short} \ll V_{sense,nominal}
$$

Since the deviation $\Delta V = V_{sense,nominal} - V_{sense,short}$ exceeds the ATE detection threshold $V_{th}$, the short is flagged as a fault.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting parasitic subtraction:** Many students write $R_{DUT} = V_{sense} / I_{test}$ without subtracting $R_{interconnect}$. This loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing TBIC with ABM:** TBIC connects to the *external* test pins; ABM is *per internal analog pin*. Mixing up roles loses up to 3 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 3. **Missing the differential sensing concept:** Simply stating "measure voltage" is incomplete. You must mention **differential measurement between AB1 and AB2** to cancel bus parasitics.
> 4. **Wrong standard citation:** Always write **"IEEE 1149.4"** — students who write only "1149" or "1149.1" lose 1 mark immediately.
> 5. **Unit mistakes:** Mixing $mV$ with $V$ or $\mu A$ with $mA$ in numerical problems will be penalized 1 mark even if the logic is correct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Analog Test Bus (IEEE 1149.4):**

- [ ] **Full Standard Name:** IEEE 1149.4 — *Standard for a Mixed-Signal Test Bus*.
- [ ] **Year of Release:** **1999**, revised in 2010.
- [ ] **Pin Overhead:** Adds exactly **2 analog pins (AT1, AT2)** + optional ATN to the standard 1149.1 TAP.
- [ ] **Internal Bus:** Two wires — **AB1 and AB2** — routed globally across the die.
- [ ] **TBIC** = Test Bus Interface Circuit (one per chip, near external pins).
- [ ] **ABM** = Analog Boundary Module (one per analog/mixed pin).
- [ ] Each ABM contains **3 transmission gates** (TG1, TG2, TG3).
- [ ] **Key Strength:** Enables DC and AC parametric testing using only 2 external analog contacts.
- [ ] **Differential sensing** between AB1 and AB2 cancels out internal bus parasitic resistance.
- [ ] **Master Formula:** $R_{DUT} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{test}} - R_{interconnect,known}$.
- [ ] **AC Test:** Uses sinusoidal excitation; capacitance extracted from $|Z(f)| = \sqrt{R^2 + (1/2\pi f C)^2}$.
- [ ] **Interconnect Faults Detected:** Opens (∞ R), Shorts (↓ R), Bridging faults.
- [ ] **Resolution:** Down to **attofarads** for capacitance, **milliohms** for resistance.
- [ ] **Relationship to 1149.1:** 1149.4 is a **superset**; 1149.1 TAP and BSR are reused unchanged.
- [ ] **Most Common KTU Question Pattern:** Numerical problem on $R_{DUT}$ extraction (5–7 marks) + descriptive block diagram explanation (7 marks).
- [ ] **Applications:** Audio codecs, RF transceivers, automotive MCUs, PMICs, MEMS, sensor SoCs.
- [ ] **Cost Justification:** Eliminates expensive analog ATE channels; one analog pin replaces $N$ probe channels.
